topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Need for Regulatory Control and Standards - History of Medical Device Regulation - Classification of Medical Devices - Medical Device Design - Product Lifecycle of Medical Device

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Fundamental Need for Regulatory Control

### Formal KTU Definition
> **Regulatory Control** in the context of medical devices is the legally enforceable framework established by national or supranational authorities (e.g., **US FDA**, **European Commission**, **CDSCO India**, **PMDA Japan**, **NMPA China**) to govern the **design, manufacture, packaging, labelling, storage, installation, servicing, marketing, and post-market surveillance** of medical devices, with the primary objective of safeguarding **public health and patient safety** while ensuring devices perform as intended by the manufacturer.

### The Three Pillars of Regulatory Necessity
1. **Patient Safety & Risk Mitigation** — A medical device directly interfaces with the human body. Failure (e.g., a faulty defibrillator, contaminated catheter, or software glitch in an insulin pump) can lead to **death, serious injury, or chronic disability**.
2. **Performance & Efficacy Assurance** — The device must demonstrably achieve its **intended medical purpose** as claimed by the manufacturer, backed by clinical or non-clinical evidence.
3. **Trade Facilitation & Harmonization** — International standards (e.g., **ISO 13485:2016**, **IEC 60601-1**, **IMDRF GHTF guidance**) create a **common regulatory language**, enabling cross-border market access.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU module emphasises that regulation is **not a barrier to innovation** but a **trust architecture**. Without regulation, the market collapses due to information asymmetry between the manufacturer (who knows the device's limitations) and the patient/physician (who does not).

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Traffic Signal System"
Imagine a busy intersection with **no traffic lights**. Cars (medical devices) of different speeds, sizes, and braking capacities (Class I, II, III) share the road with pedestrians (patients). Chaos, collisions, and fatalities would be inevitable. The traffic signal system (regulatory framework) imposes:
- **Lane discipline** (Design Controls)
- **Speed limits** (Performance Standards)
- **Driver licensing** (Pre-market Approval)
- **Accident reporting** (Post-Market Surveillance)

> The **traffic light does not stop cars from moving**; it ensures that **everyone arrives alive**.

---

## 1.2 What is a "Standard" in the Medical Device Context?

### Formal Definition
> A **Standard** is a **documented technical specification** prepared by a recognised standards-developing organisation (e.g., **ISO**, **IEC**, **ASTM**, **BIS**, **CEN**) that provides **rules, guidelines, or characteristics** for activities or their results, aimed at the **achievement of the optimum degree of order** in a given context.

### Categories of Standards
| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal / Basic Standards** | Apply to all devices (foundation) | ISO 14971 (Risk Management), IEC 62366-1 (Usability) |
| **Vertical / Product Standards** | Specific to a particular device type | IEC 60601-2-4 (Defibrillators), ISO 14708 (Implants) |
| **Process Standards** | Govern the QMS itself | ISO 13485:2016 (QMS for Medical Devices) |
| **Regulatory Standards** | Cited by regulators as mandatory | 21 CFR Part 820 (US FDA QSR) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standards are voluntary in principle, but mandatory in practice** — Regulators (e.g., EU MDR Annex I) *presume conformity* when a device complies with harmonised standards, shifting the **burden of proof** to the manufacturer if they deviate.

---

## 1.3 Definition of a Medical Device (Comparative Jurisdictions)

A "Medical Device" is not a universal, fixed definition — it varies by jurisdiction. KTU students must internalise these distinctions.

**United States (FD&C Act, Section 201(h)):**
> An instrument, apparatus, implement, machine, contrivance, implant, in vitro reagent… intended for use in the diagnosis, cure, mitigation, treatment, or prevention of disease… and which does not achieve its primary intended purposes through chemical action within or on the body.

**European Union (MDR 2017/745, Article 2(1)):**
> Any instrument, apparatus, appliance, software, implant, reagent, material, or other article… intended by the manufacturer to be used for human beings… for specific medical purposes including diagnosis, prevention, monitoring, prediction, prognosis, treatment, or alleviation of disease.

**India (Medical Device Rules, 2017):**
> Devices classified under the rules and notified by the Central Government, including instruments, appliances, implants, software, etc., intended for internal or external use in diagnosis, treatment, mitigation, or prevention of disease or disorder.

### The Three Legal Tests (Common to All)
1. **Intended Purpose Test** — Documented claims by the manufacturer.
2. **Mode of Action Test** — Does it act *physically/electrically/mechanically* (device) or *pharmacologically/immunologically/metabolically* (drug)?
3. **Risk-to-Benefit Profile Test** — Does the claimed benefit justify the inherent risk?

---

## 1.4 Quick-Reference Concept Map (Topic Architecture)

| Sub-Topic | One-Line Essence |
|---|---|
| Need for Regulatory Control | Protection of public health through enforceable rules |
| History of Regulation | Evolution from reactive to proactive frameworks (1906 → 2017 EU MDR) |
| Classification of Devices | Risk-based stratification (Class I/II/III or I/IIa/IIb/III) |
| Medical Device Design | Structured, documented design control per ISO 13485 + 21 CFR 820.30 |
| Product Lifecycle | TPLC: Concept → Design → Verification → Market → PMS → Obsolescence |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Genesis and Evolution of Medical Device Regulation (Historical Timeline)

### Pre-Regulation Era (Before 1900)
- Medical devices were sold as **patent medicines and unverified contraptions**.
- The **US Pharmacopeia (1820)** set early drug standards, but devices were largely unregulated.
- Patient harm was common — e.g., **Emanuel Snook's Röntgen ray tubes (1896)** delivered massive radiation doses.

### The Landmark Era (1906–1976)
| Year | Regulation | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **1906** | **Pure Food and Drug Act** (USA) | First federal law prohibiting adulteration/misbranding. Devices not yet explicitly covered. |
| **1938** | **Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic (FD&C) Act** | Extended to medical devices. Required pre-market notification. |
| **1962** | **Kefauver-Harris Amendment** | Post-thalidomide disaster; required *efficacy* proof for drugs. |
| **1976** | **Medical Device Amendments** | **Most pivotal law** — introduced the **3-tier classification** (Class I/II/III), **510(k)** clearance, and **PMA**. |

### The Globalisation Era (1990–2017)
| Year | Event | Region |
|---|---|---|
| **1990** | Safe Medical Devices Act (SMDA) | USA — Mandatory adverse event reporting |
| **1992** | EU Medical Device Directive (MDD 93/42/EEC) | Europe — First harmonised device framework |
| **2002** | ISO 13485 (2nd Ed.) | Global — QMS for medical devices |
| **2010** | India recognises devices under Drugs & Cosmetics Act | India |
| **2017** | EU MDR 2017/745 adopted | Europe — Replaced MDD with stricter rules |
| **2017** | Indian Medical Device Rules, 2017 | India — Dedicated device rules under CDSCO |
| **2021** | EU MDR Date of Application (DoA) | Europe — All new devices must comply |
| **2025** | Transition deadlines for legacy devices | Europe — MDD certificates expire |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU 2024 module traces regulation as a reactive response to disasters** (thalidomide, Dalkon Shield IUD, PIP breast implants, metal-on-metal hips). Each regulatory milestone is a **policy autopsy** of past failures.

### Three Catastrophic Drivers of Regulation
1. **Thalidomide Disaster (1957–1962)** — ~10,000 children born with phocomelia. Resulted in **Kefauver-Harris Amendment** and global pharmacovigilance.
2. **Dalkon Shield IUD (1970s)** — Faulty intrauterine device caused 300,000+ injuries, 18 deaths. Resulted in the **1976 Medical Device Amendments**.
3. **PIP Breast Implant Scandal (2010)** — French manufacturer used industrial-grade silicone. Resulted in **EU MDR 2017/745** and the **EUDAMED database**.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Medical Devices — The Risk Stratification Model

### Why Classify?
> The regulatory burden (documentation, testing, oversight) should be **proportionate to the risk**. A tongue depressor and a cardiac pacemaker cannot have the same regulatory pathway. Classification is the **risk-coefficient multiplier** applied to the regulatory process.

### FDA (United States) Classification

| Class | Risk Level | Examples | Regulatory Pathway |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | **Low** | Elastic bandages, stethoscopes, examination gloves | **General Controls** (510(k) exempt for ~74% of devices) |
| **Class II** | **Moderate** | Infusion pumps, surgical drapes, blood pressure cuffs | **General Controls + Special Controls + 510(k)** (substantial equivalence) |
| **Class III** | **High** | Heart valves, implantable pacemakers, stents | **General Controls + Premarket Approval (PMA)** — clinical trials mandatory |

> **Key Insight for KTU:** Most Class I devices are **exempt from 510(k)**, most Class II require **510(k)**, and most Class III require **PMA** (clinical evidence + bench + animal + human studies).

### EU MDR (2017/745) Classification — 22 Rules

| Class | Risk Level | Examples | Conformity Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | Low | Non-sterile bandages, corrective glasses | Self-declaration (Annex II/III partly) |
| **Class IIa** | Low-Medium | Hearing aids, dental fillings, surgical gloves (sterile) | Notified Body involvement (Annex IX/X) |
| **Class IIb** | Medium-High | Ventilators, orthopaedic implants, defibrillators | Notified Body mandatory |
| **Class III** | High | Pacemakers, coronary stents, heart valves | Notified Body + Design Examination Certificate + Clinical Investigation |

### The Four Classification Parameters (Universal)
$$
\text{Risk Score} = f(\text{Duration}, \text{Invasiveness}, \text{Active vs. Non-Active}, \text{Body Contact Type})
$$

**Parameter 1 — Duration of Use**
$$
\text{Duration Tier} = \begin{cases} \text{Transient} & \text{if} \; t < 60 \text{ minutes} \\ \text{Short-term} & \text{if} \; 60 \text{ min} \leq t \leq 30 \text{ days} \\ \text{Long-term} & \text{if} \; t > 30 \text{ days} \end{cases}
$$

**Parameter 2 — Invasiveness**
- Non-invasive (skin contact only)
- Invasive through body orifice (mouth, ear)
- Surgically invasive (incision, implantation)
- Implantable (partial or total, permanent or temporary)

**Parameter 3 — Active vs. Non-Active**
- **Active Device** = depends on a source of energy (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic) other than the human body or gravity.
- **Active Therapeutic Device** = delivers energy to the body (e.g., electrosurgical unit).
- **Active Diagnostic Device** = supplies information about a physiological state (e.g., ECG).

**Parameter 4 — Body Contact / Anatomical Site**
- Skin-intact contact → Lowest risk
- Mucous membrane → Moderate
- Blood/Internal tissue → Higher
- Central nervous system / Heart → Highest

### Risk Matrix (Used in ISO 14971 Risk Management)
$$
\text{Risk} = \text{Severity} \times \text{Probability of Occurrence}
$$

| | **Low Probability** | **Medium Probability** | **High Probability** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Catastrophic Severity (death)** | Medium | High | **Unacceptable** |
| **Serious (irreversible injury)** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Minor (reversible injury)** | Negligible | Low | Medium |
| **Negligible (no injury)** | Negligible | Negligible | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Cheat:** A device's class is **not optional or chosen** by the manufacturer. It is **derived from the intended purpose and design** via the regulatory rule-set. Misclassification is a **regulatory non-conformity** that can lead to **refusal of market authorisation**.

---

## 2.3 Medical Device Design — Design Control Framework

### What is Design Control?
> **Design Control** is a systematic, documented set of procedures applied to the design process to ensure that device design translates user needs into a safe, effective, manufacturable product. It is mandated under **21 CFR 820.30** (US FDA) and **ISO 13485:2016, Clause 7.3**.

### The 8-Element Design Control Waterfall
1. **Design and Development Planning** — Project plan, deliverables, responsibilities, review milestones.
2. **Design Input** — Functional, performance, regulatory, user, and safety requirements (the "voice of the customer").
3. **Design Output** — Drawings, specifications, software code, manufacturing procedures (the "voice of the designer").
4. **Design Review** — Formal, documented, multi-disciplinary evaluation at each stage.
5. **Design Verification** — "Did we make the product **right**?" — Confirms outputs meet inputs.
6. **Design Validation** — "Did we make the **right** product?" — Confirms device meets user needs and intended use.
7. **Design Transfer** — Handoff of design to manufacturing (DFM, DFA, process validation).
8. **Design Changes** — Controlled change management after market release.

> **KTU Mnemonic: "P-I-O-R-V-V-T-C"** — Planning, Input, Output, Review, Verification, Validation, Transfer, Changes.

### Companion Standards to Design Control

| Standard | Scope |
|---|---|
| **ISO 14971:2019** | Application of risk management to medical devices |
| **IEC 62366-1:2015** | Usability engineering (Human Factors) |
| **IEC 60601-1** | Safety & essential performance of medical electrical equipment |
| **ISO 10993 series** | Biological evaluation of medical devices (biocompatibility) |
| **IEC 62304:2006** | Software life cycle processes for medical device software |
| **ISO 11607** | Packaging for terminally sterilised medical devices |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Control is a Process, not a Document.** The **Design History File (DHF)** is the *output* of effective design control, not a substitute for it. KTU examiners commonly fail students who treat the DHF as a paperwork exercise.

---

## 2.4 The Total Product Life Cycle (TPLC) of a Medical Device

### Definition
> The **TPLC** is a holistic framework promoted by the **US FDA CDRH** and the **IMDRF** that integrates **pre-market** and **post-market** regulatory activities, treating the device as a continuous, evolving entity from concept to obsolescence.

### The Six Phases of TPLC

**Phase 1 — Concept & Feasibility**
- Clinical need identification, market analysis, user research.
- Initial risk assessment per ISO 14971.
- **Output:** Feasibility report, business case, regulatory strategy.

**Phase 2 — Design & Development**
- Design control per ISO 13485 Clause 7.3.
- Iterative prototyping, design reviews.
- **Output:** DHF, Technical File (EU), 510(k)/PMA dossier.

**Phase 3 — Verification & Validation (V&V)**
- Design verification (bench testing, software V&V per IEC 62304).
- Design validation (clinical evaluation per EU MDR Annex XIV; usability per IEC 62366).
- **Output:** V&V reports, Clinical Evaluation Report (CER), Biocompatibility report.

**Phase 4 — Regulatory Submission & Market Authorisation**
- **US:** 510(k) or PMA.
- **EU:** CE marking via Notified Body.
- **India:** CDSCO licence (MD-5 to MD-15 forms depending on class).
- **Output:** Marketing authorisation, listing/registration.

**Phase 5 — Manufacturing & Distribution**
- Production under QMS (ISO 13485 / 21 CFR 820).
- Labelling per 21 CFR 801 / EU MDR Chapter III.
- Distribution controls (chain of custody, traceability).
- **Output:** Finished device, certificates of conformity.

**Phase 6 — Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) & End-of-Life**
- Adverse event reporting (MDR for US, vigilance for EU).
- Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR) for EU MDR.
- Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF) studies.
- Field Safety Corrective Actions (FSCA), recalls.
- **Output:** PMS reports, complaint files, trend analyses.
- Eventually: **Obsolescence management**, **decommissioning**, **retirement**.

### The Three Key Documents of TPLC
$$
\begin{cases} \text{Technical File (EU)} / 510(\text{k}) \text{ or PMA (US)} & \text{— Pre-market} \\ \text{Design History File (DHF)} & \text{— Throughout design} \\ \text{Post-Market Surveillance Report} & \text{— Post-market} \end{cases}
$$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk = Severity × Probability** | $\text{Risk} = S \times P$ | ISO 14971 risk scoring |
| **Class Determination** | $\text{Class} = f(\text{Duration}, \text{Invasiveness}, \text{Energy Source}, \text{Anatomy})$ | EU MDR Rule 1–22 |
| **Conformity Assessment Stringency** | $\text{Class III} > \text{IIb} > \text{IIa} > \text{I}$ | EU MDR Annex IX–XI |
| **PMA vs 510(k)** | $\text{PMA} : \text{Class III} \;;\; 510(\text{k}) : \text{Class II (most)}$ | US FDA pathway |
| **DHF Traceability** | $\text{Input} \to \text{Output} \to \text{Verification} \to \text{Validation}$ | ISO 13485 Clause 7.3 |
| **V&V Distinction** | $\text{Verification} = \text{Inputs met?} \;;\; \text{Validation} = \text{User needs met?}$ | 21 CFR 820.30 |
| **TPLC Loop** | $\text{PMS} \to \text{Risk Update} \to \text{Design Change} \to \text{Re-Verification}$ | Continuous improvement |
| **Notified Body Involvement** | $\text{Required for} \geq \text{Class IIa (sterile/measuring)}$ | EU MDR |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrices & Worked Models

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Classification of a Medical Device (EU MDR Method)

### Problem Statement
**Device X** is a **non-invasive, battery-powered, transcutaneous oxygen saturation monitor** (pulse oximeter) used continuously on the fingertip for patients in ICUs (continuous use > 60 minutes, up to 30 days). The device has a **software-based algorithm** for SpO₂ computation and **does not** contact the central nervous system or cardiovascular system directly.

**Classify Device X under EU MDR 2017/745 using Rule 11.**

### Analytical Walk-through

> **Step 1 — Identify the device's functional category.**

Device X is an **active device** (depends on electrical energy from a battery) intended for **monitoring** a physiological parameter (SpO₂).

> **Step 2 — Apply Rule 11 (Software & Active Monitoring Devices, EU MDR Annex VIII).**

Rule 11 states:
> *"Software intended to provide information which is used to take decisions with diagnosis or therapeutic purposes is classified as Class IIa, except if such decisions have an impact on a patient's health that may lead to:*
> *— death or irreversible deterioration → Class III*
> *— serious deterioration or surgical intervention → Class IIb."*

> **Step 3 — Evaluate clinical impact.**

SpO₂ readings are used to titrate oxygen therapy. Incorrect readings can lead to **hypoxia** (serious deterioration) or **hyperoxia** (oxygen toxicity). In ICU patients (critically ill), this is **serious deterioration** territory → not Class III (not death-imminent) but **Class IIb**.

> **Step 4 — Cross-check with Rule 10 (Active devices for monitoring).**

Rule 10: Active devices intended for **monitoring** of physiological parameters are **Class IIa**, *unless* the nature of the monitoring is **vital** (where variations could result in immediate danger) → then **Class IIb**.

> **Step 5 — Final Determination.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Device X} \rightarrow \text{Class IIb}}
$$

The conformity assessment will require a **Notified Body** (Annex IX or X), **Technical Documentation review**, and a **Clinical Evaluation Report (CER)** per EU MDR Article 61.

### Worked Summary Matrix
| Step | Question | Answer |
|---|---|---|
| Is the device **active**? | Depends on battery? | Yes |
| Is it for **monitoring**? | Measures SpO₂? | Yes |
| Is the parameter **vital**? | Could variation cause immediate danger? | Yes (ICU context) |
| Rule 10 outcome | Vital monitoring | Class IIb |
| Rule 11 outcome | Software for therapeutic decisions | Class IIb (confirmed) |
| **Final Class** | | **IIb** |

---

## 3.2 Risk Assessment Worked Example (ISO 14971)

### Problem
A **syringe pump** has two identified hazards:
- **Hazard A:** Software glitch causes over-infusion of medication. Severity: **Serious (4)**. Probability: **Occasional (3)**.
- **Hazard B:** Battery failure during transport. Severity: **Negligible (1)**. Probability: **Probable (5)**.

Calculate the Risk Priority Number (RPN) and determine acceptability.

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Define the RPN formula.**
$$
\text{RPN} = \text{Severity (S)} \times \text{Probability (P)}
$$

**Step 2 — Score each hazard.**

**Hazard A (Over-infusion):**
$$
\text{RPN}_A = 4 \times 3 = 12
$$

**Hazard B (Battery failure):**
$$
\text{RPN}_B = 1 \times 5 = 5
$$

**Step 3 — Map to risk acceptability matrix.**

| RPN Range | Risk Level | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| 1 – 4 | **Acceptable** | No action required |
| 5 – 9 | **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) | Risk control measures required |
| 10 – 16 | **Unacceptable** | Design change mandatory |

**Step 4 — Apply matrix.**

Hazard A (RPN = 12) → **Unacceptable** → Design change mandatory (e.g., add maximum-dose limit alarm, redundant flow sensor).
Hazard B (RPN = 5) → **ALARP** → Document the residual risk; add low-battery alarm.

> **Step 5 — Risk Control Hierarchy (ISO 14971 Clause 5.4):**
1. **Inherent Safety by Design** (preferred)
2. **Protective measures in the device** (e.g., alarms, interlocks)
3. **Information for Safety** (e.g., warnings, training)

> **Step 6 — Residual Risk Evaluation.**

After implementing controls, recalculate RPN. If RPN drops to ≤ 4 (Acceptable), proceed. If still unacceptable, **reiterate** the risk control process.

### Final Outcome
$$
\boxed{\text{Hazard A: RPN} = 12 \rightarrow \text{Unacceptable} \;;\; \text{Hazard B: RPN} = 5 \rightarrow \text{ALARP}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Design Control Traceability Matrix (DCTM) — Worked Template

### Purpose
A **Design Control Traceability Matrix (DCTM)** links User Needs → Design Inputs → Design Outputs → V&V activities, satisfying the **traceability** requirement of **21 CFR 820.30(j)** and **ISO 13485 Clause 7.3.9**.

### Worked Example — A Digital Thermometer

| User Need | Design Input (DI) | Design Output (DO) | Verification (V) | Validation (VL) | Risk Link |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| UN-1: Measure oral temperature in adults | DI-1: Accuracy ± 0.1°C between 35–42°C | DO-1: Sensor spec, ADC resolution ≥ 12-bit | V-1: Bench test at 35, 37, 42°C | VL-1: Clinical study on 100 subjects | R-1: Inaccuracy → misdiagnosis |
| UN-2: Battery life ≥ 1 year | DI-2: Avg. current ≤ 5 µA standby | DO-2: Low-power MCU, sleep mode firmware | V-2: Battery endurance test (1000 hr) | VL-2: User trial over 12 months | R-2: Failure → no reading |
| UN-3: Display legible in low light | DI-3: OLED display, 50 nits minimum | DO-3: Display module schematic, GUI code | V-3: Photometric test | VL-3: User feedback questionnaire | R-3: Misread → dosing error |

### Analytical Logic of the DCTM
$$
\text{UN}_i \;\xrightarrow{\text{derives}}\; \text{DI}_i \;\xrightarrow{\text{implements}}\; \text{DO}_i \;\xrightarrow{\text{verifies}}\; V_i \;\xrightarrow{\text{validates}}\; \text{VL}_i
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Verification answers "Did we build the device to the *specifications*?" and Validation answers "Did we build the *right* device for the *user*?" Students frequently interchange these. Memorise:
> - **V**erification = **V**s **S**pecs (Specification match)
> - **V**a**L**idation = rea**L**-world (User/Intended use)

---

## 3.4 Conformity Assessment Pathway Comparison

### US FDA vs. EU MDR (Step-by-Step)

| Stage | US FDA (Class II) | EU MDR (Class IIb) |
|---|---|---|
| 1. QMS | 21 CFR Part 820 (QSR) | ISO 13485:2016 (Notified Body audit) |
| 2. Pre-clinical | Bench + animal + biocompatibility (ISO 10993) | Same + risk management (ISO 14971) |
| 3. Clinical | Often not required for 510(k) | Required: Clinical Investigation (ISO 14155) |
| 4. Submission | 510(k) dossier to FDA | Technical Documentation to Notified Body |
| 5. Review | FDA review (90 days) | Notified Body audit + certification |
| 6. Authorisation | 510(k) clearance letter | CE Certificate + EU Declaration of Conformity |
| 7. Post-Market | MDR (Medical Device Reporting) | Vigilance + PSUR + PMCF |

### Key Compliance Equation (Conceptual)
$$
\text{Market Access} = \text{QMS} \cap \text{Technical Documentation} \cap \text{Clinical Evidence} \cap \text{PMS Plan}
$$

All four must be satisfied **simultaneously**. A device cannot be legally placed on the market with **any one** of these missing.

---

## 3.5 India CDSCO — Classification & Pathway (Specific to KTU Context)

### Medical Device Rules 2017 (India) — Class System

| Class | Risk | Examples | Licence Form | Audit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Low | Sterile dressings, non-electric thermometers | MD-5 (Mfg), MD-6 (Sale) | Notified Body audit (if sterile/measuring) |
| **Class B** | Low-Moderate | Hypodermic needles, suction equipment | MD-7/MD-8 (Import/Manufacture) | Notified Body audit |
| **Class C** | Moderate-High | Bone cements, ventilators | MD-9/MD-10 | Notified Body + CDSCO review |
| **Class D** | High | Heart valves, stents, implantable defibrillators | MD-11/MD-12 | Notified Body + CDSCO review |

> **Indian Regulator: CDSCO (Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation)** under the **Ministry of Health & Family Welfare**, with **State Drug Controllers** as the field enforcement arm.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specificity:** Since this is a Kerala-based university, students must understand the **State Licensing Authority (SLA)** role of the **Kerala State Drugs Controller** for manufacturing licences and the **CDSCO** (Zonal office in Chennai) for new device approvals.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Total Product Life Cycle (TPLC) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Concept and Feasibility] --> B[Phase 2: Design and Development]
    B --> C[Phase 3: Verification and Validation]
    C --> D[Phase 4: Regulatory Submission and Market Authorisation]
    D --> E[Phase 5: Manufacturing and Distribution]
    E --> F[Phase 6: Post-Market Surveillance]
    F --> G{Adverse Events or Risks Detected?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Field Safety Corrective Action or Recall]
    H --> B
    G -- No --> I[Continuous PMS and Trend Analysis]
    I --> J{End of Life Decision}
    J -- Continue --> E
    J -- Retire --> K[Obsolescence and Decommissioning]
    
    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style C fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style D fill:#8c564b,color:#fff
    style E fill:#e377c2,color:#fff
    style F fill:#17becf,color:#000
    style H fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style K fill:#7f7f7f,color:#fff
```

### Diagram Interpretation
The TPLC is **not linear**; the post-market phase (Phase 6) **feeds back** to Design and Development (Phase 2) when new risks emerge. This feedback loop is the **core philosophy** of Total Product Life Cycle approach (US FDA CDRH 2018+).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — EU MDR 2017/745 Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Device X]) --> Q1{Is the device active?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Non-invasive and contact with skin only?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q3{Is it for monitoring a vital physiological parameter?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> C1[Class I]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Transient use through body orifice?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> C2[Class IIa]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Surgically invasive, short term?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6{Contact with central nervous system or heart?}
    Q5 -- No --> C3[Class I or IIa]
    Q6 -- Yes --> C4[Class III]
    Q6 -- No --> Q7{Long-term implantable?}
    Q7 -- Yes --> C5[Class IIb or III]
    Q7 -- No --> C6[Class IIa]
    Q3 -- Yes --> C7[Class IIb]
    Q3 -- No --> Q8{Is it software for diagnostic or therapeutic decisions?}
    Q8 -- Yes --> Q9{Could error lead to death?}
    Q9 -- Yes --> C8[Class III]
    Q9 -- No --> Q10{Could error lead to serious deterioration?}
    Q10 -- Yes --> C9[Class IIb]
    Q10 -- No --> C10[Class IIa]
    Q8 -- No --> C11[Class I or IIa]
    
    style Start fill:#000,color:#fff
    style C1 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style C2 fill:#bcbd22,color:#000
    style C3 fill:#bcbd22,color:#000
    style C4 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style C5 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style C6 fill:#bcbd22,color:#000
    style C7 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style C8 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style C9 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style C10 fill:#bcbd22,color:#000
    style C11 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
```

### Diagram Interpretation
This flowchart operationalises **EU MDR Annex VIII, Rules 1–22**. A device is **always** evaluated against **all** applicable rules, and the **highest class prevails** (Rule 3 fallback: in case of doubt, the higher classification applies).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Design Control Waterfall (ISO 13485 Clause 7.3)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[1. Design and Development Planning] --> P2[2. Design Input]
    P2 --> P3[3. Design Output]
    P3 --> P4[4. Design Review]
    P4 --> P5{Verification passed?}
    P5 -- No --> P2
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[5. Design Verification]
    P6 --> P7{Validation passed?}
    P7 -- No --> P2
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[6. Design Validation]
    P8 --> P9[7. Design Transfer to Manufacturing]
    P9 --> P10[8. Design Changes Control]
    P10 --> P11([Design History File DHF Compiled])
    
    style P1 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style P2 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style P3 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style P4 fill:#8c564b,color:#fff
    style P5 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style P6 fill:#e377c2,color:#fff
    style P7 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style P8 fill:#17becf,color:#000
    style P9 fill:#bcbd22,color:#000
    style P10 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style P11 fill:#7f7f7f,color:#fff
```

### Diagram Interpretation
The design control waterfall is **iterative** — failures at Verification or Validation send the process **back** to Design Input, not merely to the failed step. This is the **iterative refinement principle**.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Global Regulatory Framework Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USA[United States Regulatory Framework]
        A1[FD and C Act 1938] --> A2[1976 Medical Device Amendments]
        A2 --> A3[21 CFR Part 820 QSR]
        A2 --> A4[510k Clearance]
        A2 --> A5[PMA Approval]
    end
    
    subgraph EU[European Union Framework]
        B1[MDD 93/42/EEC 1993] --> B2[MDR 2017/745]
        B2 --> B3[Annex I General Safety and Performance Requirements]
        B2 --> B4[Annex VIII Classification Rules]
        B2 --> B5[Annex IX Conformity Assessment]
    end
    
    subgraph IND[India Framework]
        C1[Drugs and Cosmetics Act 1940] --> C2[Medical Device Rules 2017]
        C2 --> C3[CDSCO Central Authority]
        C2 --> C4[State Licensing Authority SLA]
    end
    
    subgraph INTL[International Harmonisation]
        D1[IMDRF GHTF] --> D2[ISO 13485 QMS]
        D1 --> D3[ISO 14971 Risk Management]
        D1 --> D4[IEC 60601 Electrical Safety]
    end
    
    USA --> INTL
    EU --> INTL
    IND --> INTL
```

### Diagram Interpretation
Three regional regulatory frameworks (US, EU, India) all **converge** on international standards. The **IMDRF** (International Medical Device Regulators Forum) is the **harmonisation platform** that aligns these regions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a "Medical Device" as per the US Federal Food, Drug and Cosmetic (FD&C) Act. Distinguish it from a "Drug" using the primary mode of action test.**

#### Model Answer
As per Section 201(h) of the US FD&C Act (1938), a **Medical Device** is defined as an instrument, apparatus, implement, machine, contrivance, implant, in vitro reagent, or other similar or related article, including a component part, or accessory which is:
- Recognised in the official National Formulary, or the US Pharmacopoeia, or any supplement to them;
- Intended for use in the diagnosis of disease, or in the cure, mitigation, treatment, or prevention of disease, in man or other animals; or
- Intended to affect the structure or any function of the body of man or other animals; and
- **Which does not achieve its primary intended purposes through chemical action within or on the body of man or other animals** and is not dependent upon being metabolised for the achievement of its primary intended purposes.

**Distinction from a Drug (Primary Mode of Action Test):**
- A **Medical Device** achieves its primary intended purpose through **physical, mechanical, electrical, or thermal means** (e.g., a pacemaker delivers electrical impulses to regulate heartbeat).
- A **Drug** achieves its primary intended purpose through **chemical, pharmacological, metabolic, or immunological action** within the body (e.g., aspirin reduces pain via cyclooxygenase enzyme inhibition).
- The distinction is critical because the **regulatory pathway** (FDA's Center for Devices and Radiological Health — CDRH — vs. Center for Drug Evaluation and Research — CDER) and **testing requirements** differ significantly.
- **Borderline cases** (e.g., drug-eluting stents, combination products) are resolved by the **FDA's Office of Combination Products** based on the **primary mode of action**.

> **Word count:** ~180 words | **Marks:** 3 | **Time:** 5–6 min

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain in brief the three-tier risk-based classification of medical devices as per the US FDA, with one example each.**

#### Model Answer
The **US FDA** classifies medical devices into three regulatory classes based on the **level of risk** the device poses to the patient and the **level of regulatory control** required to provide reasonable assurance of safety and effectiveness.

**Class I — Low Risk:**
- Examples: Elastic bandages, examination gloves, stethoscopes, tongue depressors.
- Regulatory Controls: Subject to **General Controls** (e.g., establishment registration, device listing, good manufacturing practices, labelling). Approximately **74%** of Class I devices are **exempt from 510(k) pre-market notification**.

**Class II — Moderate Risk:**
- Examples: Infusion pumps, blood pressure cuffs, surgical drapes, pregnancy test kits, powered wheelchairs.
- Regulatory Controls: Subject to **General Controls + Special Controls** (e.g., performance standards, post-market surveillance, patient registries, special labelling) and **510(k) pre-market notification** demonstrating **substantial equivalence** to a legally marketed predicate device.

**Class III — High Risk:**
- Examples: Heart valves, implantable pacemakers, coronary stents, cochlear implants, automated external defibrillators.
- Regulatory Controls: Subject to **General Controls + Premarket Approval (PMA)**. The manufacturer must submit **scientific evidence** (bench, animal, and human clinical trial data) demonstrating **reasonable assurance of safety and effectiveness**.

> **Word count:** ~180 words | **Marks:** 3 | **Time:** 5–6 min

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Discuss the **historical evolution of medical device regulation** with reference to the 1906 Pure Food and Drug Act, 1938 FD&C Act, 1976 Medical Device Amendments, and the EU MDR 2017/745.

topics.title: Medical Device Development Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Medical Device Development Process # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Medical Device Development Process (MDDP)** is a structured, regulated, and systematic engineering lifecycle that transforms an unmet clinical need into a safe, effective, and commercially viable medical device, in full compliance with international regulatory frameworks such as **ISO 13485:2016** (Quality Management Systems), **ISO 14971:2019** (Risk Management), **21 CFR Part 820** (US FDA Quality System Regulation), and the **EU Medical Device Regulation (MDR 2017/745)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > The Medical Device Development Process is **NOT** a single engineering activity. It is a **multi-stage, gated, and document-driven lifecycle** governed by *Design Controls* and *Risk Management* running in parallel. KTU examiners specifically look for your ability to link each stage to the corresponding regulatory clause (e.g., linking "Design Verification" to *21 CFR 820.30(f)* or *ISO 13485:2016 Clause 7.3.8*). The process typically follows the **Stage-Gate model** (Cooper's methodology) adapted for MedTech, comprising distinct phases separated by formal *Go/No-Go* decision gates. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "Building a Hospital-Grade Elevator" Imagine you are an engineer asked to build an **elevator for a hospital** that carries patients on stretchers, doctors, IV poles, and sometimes an MRI machine. | Stage | Hospital Elevator Analogy | Medical Device Parallel | |---|---|---| | **User Need** | "We need to move ICU patients quickly between floors." | Clinical Need / Voice of Customer (VOC) | | **Design Input** | Minimum door width = 1.2 m, Max load = 1600 kg, Battery backup required. | Functional, Performance, and Regulatory Requirements | | **Design Output** | Engineering drawings, Bill of Materials, Circuit diagrams. | Specifications, CAD models, Software code, Labels. | | **Design Verification** | Test the elevator prototype in the factory — *does it meet the design inputs?* | "Did we build the device right?" (Bench testing) | | **Design Validation** | Test the elevator *in a real hospital* with real patients — *does it meet the user need?* | "Did we build the right device?" (Clinical/Simulated Use) | | **Design Transfer** | Hand over the manufacturing manual to the production line. | Transfer design to production at commercial scale. | | **Post-Market** | Monitor breakdown reports, recalls, complaints. | Post-Market Surveillance (PMS), Vigilance. | > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU exams:** A very common 3-mark question is: *"Differentiate between Design Verification and Design Validation."* Memorize the elevator analogy — examiners love answers that demonstrate *intuitive* understanding, not just textbook definitions. ## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Highlighted for KTU Recall) - **Design Control (21 CFR 820.30 / ISO 13485 Clause 7.3):** A set of interrelated procedures and documentation practices that ensure the device design translates user needs into a safe, functional product. - **Design History File (DHF):** The complete, auditable compilation of all design control records for a finished device. - **Design Input → Design Output:** The "I" defines *what* the device must do; the "O" defines *how* it is realized. - **Verification vs Validation:** Verification = "Are we building the product **right**?" Validation = "Are we building the **right** product?" - **Go/No-Go Gate:** A formal, documented decision checkpoint where cross-functional leadership decides whether the project advances. ## 1.4 Visual Schematic of the Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Stage 0: Discovery
& Feasibility] --> B{Gate 1} B -- Go --> C[Stage 1: Concept
& User Needs] C --> D{Gate 2} D -- Go --> E[Stage 2: Design
& Development] E --> F{Gate 3} F -- Go --> G[Stage 3: Verification
& Validation] G --> H{Gate 4} H -- Go --> I[Stage 4: Regulatory
& Launch] I --> J[Stage 5: Post-Market
Surveillance] J -. Continuous Feedback .-> A ``` > [!TIP] > Notice the **circular arrow** from Post-Market back to Discovery. This represents **Total Product Life Cycle Approach (TPLC)** — a key philosophy adopted by the **US FDA CDRH**. Real-world problems found in the market feed back into the *next-generation* device design. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Foundational Phases of Design Control (ISO 13485 Clause 7.3) The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes a deep understanding of the **seven sub-clauses of Design Control** as defined in **ISO 13485:2016 Clause 7.3**. Below is a structured, exam-ready breakdown. ### 2.1.1 Design and Development Planning (Clause 7.3.1) Every medical device project must begin with a **Design and Development Plan** (often called the *Project Plan* or *PDP – Product Development Plan*). This document defines: - **Scope** of the design effort (device variants, accessories, software). - **Design stages**, their **review activities**, and **gate criteria**. - **Responsibilities and authorities** (RACI matrix). - **Resource needs** (budget, headcount, equipment). - **Interfaces** between different teams (R&D, QA, Regulatory, Manufacturing, Clinical). - **Update mechanism** as the project evolves. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that the Design Plan must be *reviewed and updated* — it is a **living document**, not a one-time file. A common exam question asks: *"What happens when the design plan changes during the project?"* The answer: it is re-reviewed, re-approved, and version-controlled. ### 2.1.2 Design and Development Inputs (Clause 7.3.3) Design Inputs are the **physical, functional, performance, regulatory, and usability requirements** that the device must satisfy. They are derived from: 1. **User Needs** (clinical, patient, physician, caregiver). 2. **Regulatory Requirements** (e.g., IEC 60601-1 electrical safety, IEC 62366-1 usability). 3. **Risk Control Measures** (output of ISO 14971 risk management). 4. **Standards** (ISO, IEC, ASTM, country-specific). 5. **Outputs of Management Review.** 6. **Historical data from similar devices** (complaints, CAPA, recalls). Design Inputs must be: - **Complete** - **Unambiguous** - **Verifiable** (measurable!) - **Traceable** to user needs - **Conflict-free** > [!NOTE] > **Verifiable is the keyword.** "The device must be safe" is NOT a valid input. "The device must limit leakage current to < 0.5 mA per IEC 60601-1" IS a valid input. ### 2.1.3 Design and Development Outputs (Clause 7.3.4) Outputs are the **results of the design effort** — they describe *how* the inputs are achieved. Common outputs include: - **Device Master Record (DMR)** precursor. - **Engineering Drawings** (CAD models, tolerance stacks). - **Specifications** (mechanical, electrical, software, packaging). - **Bill of Materials (BOM).** - **Software architecture** (for SaMD / SiMD). - **Labeling and IFU** (Instructions For Use). - **Packaging specifications.** - **Installation and servicing procedures.** > [!IMPORTANT] > Outputs must be released in a **form that allows verification against the inputs**. They must be **approved** before release. The relationship: *Input → Output → Verification* is a **closed loop** that auditors love to trace. ### 2.1.4 Design and Development Review (Clause 7.3.5) At planned intervals, the design is **systematically reviewed** to: - Evaluate the **ability of the design results to meet requirements**. - Identify **problems and propose corrective actions**. - Ensure **design transfer readiness**. Participants must include **representatives of functions concerned with the design stage being reviewed** (not just R&D — QA, Regulatory, Manufacturing, Clinical Affairs must be present). Results must be **recorded and maintained**. ### 2.1.5 Design and Development Verification (Clause 7.3.6) **Verification = Confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence that the design and development outputs meet the design and development inputs.** It is performed at the **sub-system or component level** in a **controlled environment** (lab, test bench). Examples: - Bench testing a syringe's burst pressure. - EMC testing per IEC 60601-1-2. - Software unit testing (IEC 62304). ### 2.1.6 Design and Development Validation (Clause 7.3.7) **Validation = Confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence that the device meets the needs of the user and the intended use.** It is performed on the **finished device** (or production-equivalent) in a **simulated or actual use environment**. Examples: - Usability testing (Human Factors per IEC 62366-1). - Clinical investigation per ISO 14155. - Animal studies (where applicable, e.g., implants). - Simulated use with cadaver or manikin. > [!NOTE] > **Validation requires the finished device + intended use conditions.** This is why the V&V timeline usually appears at the **end of design control** — you cannot validate a device that is not yet finalized. ### 2.1.7 Design and Development Transfer (Clause 7.3.8) The transfer of the design from R&D to **manufacturing** is itself a controlled process. The organization must ensure that: - Production specifications are **complete and approved**. - Manufacturing processes are **validated** (IQ/OQ/PQ — Installation, Operational, Performance Qualification). - Training of production personnel is documented. - The **Device Master Record (DMR)** is finalized. ### 2.1.8 Control of Design and Development Changes (Clause 7.3.9) All changes during and after design transfer must be: - **Reviewed** - **Verified** - **Validated** (where applicable) - **Approved** before implementation A **Change Control Board (CCB)** typically manages this. The change history forms part of the **Design History File**. ## 2.2 The V-Model of Medical Device Development A powerful alternative visualization, heavily used in regulated industries, is the **V-Model**. The left arm of the V represents *decomposition* (user need → user requirement → system requirement → sub-system requirement → module specification). The right arm represents *integration and confirmation* (module test → integration test → system test → acceptance test → clinical evaluation). ```mermaid flowchart TB A[User Need] --> B[User Requirement Spec] B --> C[System Requirement Spec] C --> D[Sub-system Spec] D --> E[Module Spec] E --> F[Implementation] F --> G[Module Test] G --> H[Integration Test] H --> I[System Test] I --> J[Acceptance Test] J --> K[Clinical Evaluation] A -. Validation Link .-> K B -. Validation Link .-> J C -. Validation Link .-> I D -. Verification Link .-> H E -. Verification Link .-> G ``` > [!TIP] > **Verification arrows** are on the *lower* (deeper) part of the V, while **Validation arrows** are on the *upper* (broader) part. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction in diagram-based questions. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Unit / Boundary | Purpose | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Risk Priority Number (RPN) | $RPN = Severity \times Occurrence \times Detection$ | Score 1–10 each; max $RPN = 1000$ | FMEA risk ranking (ISO 14971 derived) | | 2 | Risk Acceptability | $Risk = f(Harm, Probability)$ | ISO 14971 Risk Matrix | Go/No-Go decision on risk controls | | 3 | Sample Size for Verification (binomial) | $n = \frac{\ln(1-C)}{\ln(1-p)}$ where $C$=confidence, $p$=reliability target | Dimensionless | Reliability demonstration testing | | 4 | Statistical Process Control | $UCL = \mu + 3\sigma$, $LCL = \mu - 3\sigma$ | Process parameter units | Manufacturing process control | | 5 | Time-to-Market | $T_{tm} = T_{concept} + T_{design} + T_{V\&V} + T_{regulatory}$ | Months/Years | Project planning metric | | 6 | Design Control Coverage | $\% DC = \frac{N_{controlled\_activities}}{N_{total\_activities}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Audit readiness score | | 7 | Cost of Quality (CoQ) | $CoQ = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\_failure} + C_{external\_failure}$ | Currency units | Quality economics | | 8 | Usability Error Rate | $UER = \frac{N_{use\_errors}}{N_{use\_opportunities}}$ | Percentage | IEC 62366-1 validation | | 9 | Biocompatibility Margin of Safety | $MOS = \frac{NoAEL}{Exposure}$ | Dimensionless ratio (>1 acceptable) | ISO 10993-1 evaluation | | 10 | Design Transfer Readiness Index | $DRI = \frac{\sum w_i \cdot S_i}{N_{criteria}}$ where $w$=weight, $S$=score (0–1) | Score 0–1 (>0.85 typical Go) | Gate 4 decision metric | > [!NOTE] > The RPN formula $RPN = S \times O \times D$ is from **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019)** and is widely applied under the umbrella of **ISO 14971:2019** for medical device risk management. Note that the updated **AIAG-VDA methodology** uses *Action Priority (AP)* instead of pure RPN, but the formula above is what KTU exams expect. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Medical Device Development Process is the **backbone of the entire medical device industry**. It governs: - **Cardiovascular implants** (pacemakers, stents) — 5–10 year development cycles. - **In-vitro diagnostics (IVDs)** — reagent + instrument co-development. - **Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)** — IEC 62304 + agile adaptation. - **Combination products** (drug-eluting stents) — FDA OCP and EU MDR Article 117. - **Personalized devices** (3D-printed implants) — Annex XIII of EU MDR. For a KTU biomedical engineer, this process is the **single most important framework** to master — it is the language spoken by Quality, Regulatory, Clinical, and Manufacturing teams worldwide. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Traceability Matrix & Analytical Walkthroughs ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Risk Priority Number (RPN) Computation for a Design Hazard > **Background Scenario (typical KTU problem):** During the design of a *portable infusion pump*, a Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) team identifies the hazard: *"Occlusion in the fluid line may cause under-infusion of critical medication."* The team assigns: > - Severity (S) = **8** (serious injury, life-threatening potential) > - Occurrence (O) = **4** (occasional failure, ~1 in 1,000) > - Detection (D) = **5** (moderate detectability by current controls) ### Step-by-Step Solution: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Recall the canonical RPN formula (AIAG-VDA, ISO 14971 aligned).} \\ RPN &= Severity \times Occurrence \times Detection \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Substitute the assigned scores.} \\ RPN &= 8 \times 4 \times 5 \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Perform the multiplication in the standard order.} \\ 8 \times 4 &= 32 \\ 32 \times 5 &= 160 \\ \\ \text{Step 4: Interpret the RPN value.} \\ RPN &= 160 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation Logic:** $$ \begin{aligned} RPN_{threshold} &= 100 \quad (\text{typical KTU/industry threshold for action}) \\ 160 &> 100 \implies \text{Action required.} \\ \\ \text{Step 5: Propose a risk control measure.} \\ &\text{Add an air-in-line pressure sensor (D drops from 5 to 2) and} \\ &\text{a redundant occlusion alarm (O drops from 4 to 2).} \\ \\ RPN_{new} &= 8 \times 2 \times 2 = 32 \\ RPN_{reduction} &= \frac{160 - 32}{160} \times 100\% = 80\% \\ \\ \text{Step 6: Verify residual risk acceptability per ISO 14971.} \\ RPN_{new} &= 32 \ll 100 \implies \text{Residual risk ACCEPTABLE.} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Insight:** A 14-mark question may give you a scenario and three hazard tables and ask you to *compute RPNs, rank them, and recommend which one requires immediate design change*. Always show the formula, the substitution, the arithmetic, and the decision logic. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sample Size for Design Verification (Reliability Confidence) > **Scenario:** A manufacturer claims the *single-use syringe* will have a 99.5% reliability ( $p = 0.995$ ) with 95% confidence ( $C = 0.95$ ). How many units must be tested with **zero failures** to substantiate this claim? ### Step-by-Step Solution: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Write the binomial-based zero-failure reliability formula.} \\ n &= \frac{\ln(1 - C)}{\ln(1 - p)} \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Substitute the values.} \\ n &= \frac{\ln(1 - 0.95)}{\ln(1 - 0.995)} \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Compute the numerator.} \\ 1 - 0.95 &= 0.05 \\ \ln(0.05) &= -2.9957 \\ \\ \text{Step 4: Compute the denominator.} \\ 1 - 0.995 &= 0.005 \\ \ln(0.005) &= -5.2983 \\ \\ \text{Step 5: Divide the numerator by the denominator.} \\ n &= \frac{-2.9957}{-5.2983} \\ n &= 0.5654 \\ \\ \text{Step 6: Round UP to the next integer (statistical rule for zero-failure tests).} \\ n &= 1 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result Interpretation:** Testing just **1 unit** with zero failures is *not* practically meaningful — it yields only 50% confidence. The correct industry practice is to either **(a) increase $C$ to 0.95 and accept a higher $n$**, or **(b) use a Weibull/Cremer-model approach**. KTU examiners accept the formula, but expect you to comment on **practical limitations**. > [!TIP] > If the problem had been $C = 0.95$ and $p = 0.99$, then $n = \frac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.01)} = \frac{-2.9957}{-4.6052} = 0.65 \rightarrow 1$. For $p = 0.90$, $n = \frac{-2.9957}{\ln(0.10)} = \frac{-2.9957}{-2.3026} = 1.30 \rightarrow 2$. Higher reliability targets require exponentially more units. ## 3.3 Design Traceability Matrix (DTM) Construction The **Design Traceability Matrix** is a *mandatory* document that links every **User Need** → **Design Input** → **Design Output** → **Verification** → **Validation**. It is the single most powerful evidence during a regulatory audit (FDA, Notified Body). ### 3.3.1 Generic Template | User Need ID | User Need (UN) | Design Input ID | Design Input (DI) | Design Output ID | Design Output (DO) | Verification ID | Verification Method | Validation ID | Validation Method | Residual Risk | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | UN-001 | Device shall deliver insulin accurately | DI-001 | Flow rate accuracy: ±5% | DO-001 | Micro-pump spec v1.2 | V-001 | Bench flow test per ISO 8536-9 | VL-001 | Clinical simulation per IEC 62366 | Low | | UN-002 | Device shall be safe for use on pediatric skin | DI-002 | Biocompatible per ISO 10993-5 | DO-002 | Silicone housing, Material M-77 | V-002 | Cytotoxicity test report #R-44 | VL-002 | Skin irritation study | Low | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** *"Prepare a Design Traceability Matrix for a digital thermometer showing at least three user needs. Explain the importance of traceability in design control."* — Marks breakdown: 3 marks for the table structure, 4 marks for at least 3 fully-populated rows, 4 marks for the importance explanation, 3 marks for regulatory linkage (e.g., 21 CFR 820.30). ## 3.4 Stage-Gate Decision Logic Derivation > **Problem:** A medical device company is at **Gate 3** (end of Design & Development stage). Define the *Go/No-Go* criteria and apply them to a project where: $Design Inputs Met = 92\%$, $V\&V Completion = 88\%$, $Open High-Risk Items = 5$, $Regulatory Submission Ready = No$. ### Step-by-Step Solution: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Define the Go Criteria vector.} \\ G &= \{g_1, g_2, g_3, g_4\} \quad \text{where:} \\ g_1 &: \text{Design Inputs Met} \geq 100\% \\ g_2 &: \text{V\&V Completion} \geq 95\% \\ g_3 &: \text{Open High-Risk Items} = 0 \\ g_4 &: \text{Regulatory Submission Ready} = Yes \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Evaluate the project against each criterion.} \\ g_1 &: 92\% < 100\% \quad \rightarrow \text{FAIL} \\ g_2 &: 88\% < 95\% \quad \rightarrow \text{FAIL} \\ g_3 &: 5 > 0 \quad \rightarrow \text{FAIL} \\ g_4 &: \text{No} \neq \text{Yes} \quad \rightarrow \text{FAIL} \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Apply the logical conjunction (AND-gate logic).} \\ Decision &= g_1 \land g_2 \land g_3 \land g_4 \\ Decision &= \text{FAIL} \land \text{FAIL} \land \text{FAIL} \land \text{FAIL} \\ Decision &= \text{FAIL} \\ \\ \text{Step 4: Issue the decision.} \\ \textbf{Decision} &= \textbf{NO-GO} \\ \\ \text{Step 5: Recommended actions before re-submission.} \\ &\text{1. Close the 8\% gap in Design Inputs.} \\ &\text{2. Complete remaining 7\% of V\&V activities.} \\ &\text{3. Mitigate or accept the 5 high-risk items.} \\ &\text{4. Finalize regulatory submission package.} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **logical foundation of a Stage-Gate management system**. The model is *conjunctive* — failure of *any one* criterion results in a No-Go. This is a deliberate design to **protect patient safety**. ## 3.5 Integration of ISO 14971 Risk Management into the Design Process Risk management is **not a one-time activity**. It runs **in parallel** with the design process. The diagram below (and its text-based version here) summarizes the linkage. **Iteration 1: Risk Analysis (after User Needs, before Design Inputs)** - Identify hazards. - Estimate and evaluate risks. - Implement risk control measures (design changes). - Re-evaluate residual risk. **Iteration 2: Risk Control Verification (during Verification phase)** - Confirm that the implemented risk control measures are *effective* in the design outputs. **Iteration 3: Overall Residual Risk Evaluation (during Validation phase)** - Confirm that the *overall* residual risk is acceptable when weighed against benefits. **Iteration 4: Production and Post-Production Information (Post-Market)** - Monitor complaints, CAPA, vigilance reports. - Update the Risk Management File. > [!TIP] > The deliverable is a **Risk Management File (RMF)** and a **Risk Management Report (RMR)**, mandated by **ISO 14971:2019 Clause 3.4 and Clause 9** respectively. ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example: Project Timeline Calculation > **Problem:** A Class III implantable cardiac device has the following estimated phase durations: Concept = 6 months, Design = 18 months, V&V = 12 months, Regulatory Review = 9 months, Scale-up = 6 months. Compute the **Total Time-to-Market** and the **percentage of time spent in V&V**. ### Step-by-Step Solution: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Apply the time-to-market formula.} \\ T_{tm} &= T_{concept} + T_{design} + T_{V\&V} + T_{regulatory} + T_{scaleup} \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Substitute the values.} \\ T_{tm} &= 6 + 18 + 12 + 9 + 6 \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Sum the terms.} \\ T_{tm} &= 51 \text{ months} \\ \\ \text{Step 4: Compute the V\&V percentage.} \\ \% T_{V\&V} &= \frac{T_{V\&V}}{T_{tm}} \times 100\% \\ &= \frac{12}{51} \times 100\% \\ \\ \text{Step 5: Calculate the ratio.} \\ \frac{12}{51} &= 0.2353 \\ 0.2353 \times 100\% &= 23.53\% \\ \\ \text{Step 6: Conclude with engineering interpretation.} \\ &\text{Approximately 24% of the project duration is dedicated to V\&V,} \\ &\text{which is typical for a Class III implant. This justifies why} \\ &\text{parallel V\&V planning is recommended to compress the timeline.} \end{aligned} $$ # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: End-to-End Medical Device Development Lifecycle (TPLC) ```mermaid flowchart TD stage0[Stage 0: Discovery
and Feasibility] stage1[Stage 1: Concept
and User Needs] stage2[Stage 2: Design
and Development] stage3[Stage 3: Verification
Validation and Regulatory] stage4[Stage 4: Launch
and Scale Up] stage5[Stage 5: Post Market
Surveillance and Vigilance] gate1{Gate 1
Feasibility Review} gate2{Gate 2
Design Freeze} gate3{Gate 3
V and V Complete} gate4{Gate 4
Regulatory Approval} stage0 --> gate1 gate1 -- Go --> stage1 stage1 --> gate2 gate2 -- Go --> stage2 stage2 --> gate3 gate3 -- Go --> stage3 stage3 --> gate4 gate4 -- Go --> stage4 stage4 --> stage5 stage5 -. Feedback and Lessons Learned .-> stage0 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Design Control Process Block Architecture (ISO 13485 Clause 7.3) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[User Needs] --> B[Design Input] B --> C[Design Output] C --> D[Design Review] C --> E[Design Verification] E --> F{Outputs meet Inputs?} F -- Yes --> G[Design Validation] F -- No --> H[Design Change Control] H --> C G --> I{User Need Met?} I -- Yes --> J[Design Transfer] I -- No --> H J --> K[Device Master Record] K --> L[Manufacturing and Launch] L --> M[Post Market Surveillance] M -. Continuous Improvement .-> A ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Risk Management Integration with Design Control (ISO 14971) ```mermaid flowchart TB R1[Risk Analysis] --> R2[Risk Evaluation] R2 --> R3{Risk Acceptable?} R3 -- No --> R4[Implement Risk Control] R4 --> R5[Verify Risk Control] R5 --> R6[Residual Risk Evaluation] R6 --> R7{Residual Acceptable?} R7 -- No --> R4 R7 -- Yes --> R8[Benefit Risk Analysis] R8 --> R9{Overall Acceptable?} R9 -- No --> R10[Design Change] R10 --> R1 R9 -- Yes --> R11[Risk Management Report] R11 --> R12[Production and Post Production] R12 -. Update .-> R1 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Traceability and Document Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB UN[User Needs Document] --> DI[Design Input Spec] DI --> DO[Design Output Spec] DO --> VR[Verification Protocols and Reports] UN --> VL[Validation Protocols and Reports] DI --> VL VR --> DHF[Design History File] VL --> DHF DO --> DHF DI --> DHF UN --> DHF DO --> DMR[Device Master Record] DMR --> MFG[Manufacturing] MFG --> PMS[Post Market Surveillance] PMS --> CAPA[CAPA and Vigilance] CAPA --> DHF ``` ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Cross-Functional Team Interaction ```mermaid flowchart LR RD[Research and Development] -->|Design Inputs| RA[Regulatory Affairs] RD -->|V and V Results| QA[Quality Assurance] QA -->|Audit Feedback| RD RA -->|Regulatory Strategy| MFG[Manufacturing] CA[Clinical Affairs] -->|User Needs| RD CA -->|Clinical Data| RA MFG -->|Design Transfer Input| RD MFG -->|Process Data| QA PMS[Post Market Team] -->|Complaint Data| QA PMS -->|User Feedback| RD QA -->|CAPA| All[All Functions] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why Block Diagrams instead of physical drawings:** The Medical Device Development Process is fundamentally a *process*, not a physical object. Hence, **process flow diagrams** (Mermaid) are the most accurate representation. Physical device drawings (e.g., a syringe) would not capture the **regulatory and lifecycle dimensions** that KTU examiners test. ## 4.6 Diagram: Waterfall vs Agile vs Hybrid for MedTech ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Waterfall W1[User Needs] --> W2[Design] --> W3[V and V] --> W4[Regulatory] --> W5[Launch] end subgraph Agile A1[Sprint 1: UN1 to D1] --> A2[Sprint 2: UN2 to D2] --> A3[Sprint 3: UN3 to D3] --> A4[Final V and V] end subgraph Hybrid H1[Design Input Phase] --> H2[Agile Sprints for Output] --> H3[Formal V and V Gate] --> H4[Regulatory Submission] end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > For **traditional hardware medical devices**, Waterfall is still dominant due to the need for *frozen design* before V&V and regulatory submission. For **Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)**, **IEC 62304** explicitly supports Agile/Scrum. KTU 2024 expects you to know that the **Hybrid model** is now the industry best practice for combination products and connected devices. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between Design Verification and Design Validation. Provide one example for each in the context of a blood pressure monitor.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Design Verification | Design Validation | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Confirmation that design *outputs* meet design *inputs* | Confirmation that the device meets the *user needs* and *intended use* | | **Question Asked** | "Are we building the device **right**?" | "Are we building the **right** device?" | | **Stage** | Sub-system or component level | Finished device in actual/simulated use | | **Environment** | Laboratory / bench | Clinical / simulated use environment | | **Example (BP Monitor)** | Bench test to confirm pressure sensor accuracy of ±3 mmHg per IEC 80601-2-30 | Clinical study with 30 patients to confirm cuff comfort, ease-of-use, and accurate BP readings across all age groups | > [!TIP] > Always present answers in **tabular form** when asked for differentiation. Examiners award easy partial marks. ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is a Design History File (DHF)? List any four documents it must contain as per 21 CFR 820.30.** **Model Answer:** A **Design History File (DHF)** is a compilation of all records that describe the design history of a finished device, maintained by the manufacturer to demonstrate that the device was developed in accordance with the approved design plan and design controls. **Four mandatory contents of the DHF:** 1. **General Description of the Device, its Intended Use, and Intended Users.** 2. **Design Inputs** (functional, performance, regulatory, usability requirements). 3. **Design Outputs** (drawings, specifications, software code, BOM, labeling). 4. **Design Verification and Validation Protocols and Reports.** 5. **Design Review Records** (meeting minutes, attendees, decisions). 6. **Design Transfer Records** (DMR, production transfer documentation). 7. **Design Change History.** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse the **DHF** with the **DMR** (Device Master Record). Remember: **DHF = "how we designed it"**, **DMR = "how to build it"**. The DHF includes the design rationale; the DMR is the manufacturing blueprint. Mixing these up will cost you 1 full mark. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Note:** As per the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with an internal choice. You must answer **ONE of the two alternatives** (either OR). Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Paper]` **Design and describe the complete Medical Device Development Process for a Class II wearable ECG monitor. Your answer must cover the following:** **(a)** Explain the **five design control phases** as per ISO 13485:2016 Clause 7.3, with specific examples for the wearable ECG monitor. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Construct a **Design Traceability Matrix** showing at least three user needs mapped to design inputs, outputs, verification, and validation methods. Discuss the role of **risk management (ISO 14971)** in each phase. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Five Design Control Phases for Wearable ECG Monitor (7 marks):** **1. Design and Development Planning (Clause 7.3.1) — 1.5 marks** A formal **Design and Development Plan** is created. It defines: - **Scope:** Wearable single-lead ECG monitor with Bluetooth connectivity, Android/iOS app, USB-C charging. - **Stages:** Concept (3 mo) → Design (9 mo) → V&V (6 mo) → Regulatory (3 mo). - **Gates:** Stage reviews at the end of each stage. - **Responsibilities:** R&D (hardware, firmware, app), Regulatory (US FDA 510(k) + EU MDR Class IIa), Quality (ISO 13485 audit), Clinical (usability). - **Resources:** Budget ₹3.5 Cr, 12 engineers, 2 clinicians. **2. Design and Development Inputs (Clause 7.3.3) — 1.5 marks** - **User Need:** Continuous cardiac monitoring for ambulatory patients. - **Design Inputs:** - Battery life: ≥ 7 days continuous. - Sampling rate: ≥ 250 Hz per IEC 60601-2-47. - Ingress protection: IP67. - Biocompatibility: ISO 10993-1. - Wireless: Bluetooth 5.0, FCC Part 15 compliant. - Cybersecurity: FDA cybersecurity guidance. **3. Design and Development Outputs (Clause 7.3.4) — 1.5 marks** - Hardware schematic and PCB layout. - Firmware (IEC 62304 Class B). - Mobile app (SaMD component). - Mechanical enclosure (CAD). - Labeling and IFU. - Packaging design. **4. Design and Development Verification (Clause 7.3.6) — 1.5 marks** - Bench testing: signal-to-noise ratio ≥ 40 dB, sampling rate verification. - EMC testing per IEC 60601-1-2. - Electrical safety per IEC 60601-1. - Battery cycle testing: 500 cycles. - Firmware unit and integration testing per IEC 62304. **5. Design and Development Validation (Clause 7.3.7) — 1 mark** - Usability testing per IEC 62366-1 with 15 representative users. - Clinical evaluation: comparison with Holter monitor on 30 patients. - Validation of full system in actual home environment. **(b) Design Traceability Matrix + ISO 14971 Integration (7 marks):** **Traceability Matrix (4 marks):** | UN ID | User Need | DI ID | Design Input | DO ID | Design Output | V ID | Verification | VL ID | Validation | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | UN-01 | Monitor heart rhythm continuously | DI-01 | Sampling rate ≥ 250 Hz, 24-bit ADC | DO-01 | Analog front-end ADS1292R | V-01 | Bench ECG signal acquisition test | VL-01 | Clinical study vs Holter | | UN-02 | Comfortable for 7-day wear | DI-02 | Weight ≤ 30 g, biocompatible silicone strap | DO-02 | Enclosure CAD, Strap M-101 | V-02 | ISO 10993-5 cytotoxicity test | VL-02 | Wear-comfort study, n=15 | | UN-03 | Secure wireless data transfer | DI-03 | BLE 5.0, AES-128 encryption | DO-03 | Firmware v1.4 (BT stack) | V-03 | Cybersecurity penetration test | VL-03 | Field data integrity audit | **ISO 14971 Risk Management Integration (3 marks):** - **After User Needs:** Conduct preliminary hazard analysis. Identify risks like skin irritation, misdiagnosis, data breach. - **During Design Inputs:** Translate risk control measures into specific design inputs (e.g., biocompatible material, encryption). - **During Design Outputs / Verification:** Confirm that risk controls are *implemented* (e.g., penetration test passes). - **During Validation:** Confirm that residual risk is *acceptable* against benefits (e.g., clinical study shows acceptable false-positive rate). - **Post-Market:** Update risk file from complaints and CAPA. > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Losing Pitfalls in Question A:** > 1. **Do NOT skip the regulatory linkage.** A 1-mark deduction applies if you fail to mention *which ISO/IEC standard* governs each test. > 2. **Do NOT confuse verification and validation examples.** Bench test = verification; clinical/home-use test = validation. > 3. **Do NOT present the traceability matrix as a generic template.** Fill it with **device-specific** content (e.g., "ADS1292R" for the ECG, not "generic sensor"). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Model Paper]` **As a regulatory affairs engineer at a MedTech startup, you are tasked with designing the development strategy for a Class III active implantable (a cochlear implant). Your answer must address the following:** **(a)** Describe the **Stage-Gate model** with all five stages and four gates, explaining the *Go/No-Go criteria* at each gate for a cochlear implant. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compute the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** for the following three hazards identified during the FMEA of the cochlear implant. Recommend the highest-priority risk control measure. Justify using **ISO 14971:2019** benefit-risk analysis. **(7 marks)** **Hazard Data:** | Hazard ID | Description | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) | |---|---|---|---|---| | H-01 | Electrode migration causing facial nerve stimulation | 9 | 3 | 6 | | H-02 | Battery premature depletion | 7 | 5 | 4 | | H-03 | Firmware lock-up causing loss of stimulation | 8 | 4 | 7 | --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Stage-Gate Model for Cochlear Implant (7 marks):** **1. Stage 0 – Discovery & Feasibility (2 months)** - Define clinical need: restoration of hearing in severe-to-profound sensorineural hearing loss. - Preliminary IP landscape and market analysis. - **Gate 1 – Feasibility Review:** Go criteria = (i) Clinical need validated by ≥ 3 KOLs (Key Opinion Leaders), (ii) Preliminary IP clearance, (iii) Regulatory pathway identified (FDA PMA + EU MDR Class III). **2. Stage 1 – Concept & User Needs (4 months)** - Capture user needs from ENT surgeons, audiologists, patients, caregivers. - Define use environment, patient population, contraindications. - **Gate 2 – Design Freeze:** Go criteria = (i) User Needs Document approved, (ii) Preliminary Risk Analysis complete, (iii) Design Input draft complete. **3. Stage 2 – Design & Development (18 months)** - Detailed design: electrode array, hermetic package, sound processor, firmware, fitting software. - **Gate 3 – V&V Complete:** Go criteria = (i) 100% of Design Inputs verified, (ii) ≥ 95% of V&V tests complete, (iii) 0 open high-risk items, (iv) Biocompatibility tests passed per ISO 10993. **4. Stage 3 – Regulatory & Validation (12 months)** - FDA PMA submission, EU MDR Technical File. - Clinical investigation per ISO 14155. - **Gate 4 – Regulatory Approval:** Go criteria = (i) FDA approval, (ii) CE Mark issued, (iii) Manufacturing process validated (IQ/OQ/PQ). **5. Stage 4 – Launch & Post-Market** - Limited market release (LMN), physician training, PMS plan active. **(b) RPN Computation (7 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Recall:} \quad RPN &= S \times O \times D \\ \\ \text{For H-01 (Electrode migration):} \\ RPN_{H01} &= 9 \times 3 \times 6 = 162 \\ \\ \text{For H-02 (Battery depletion):} \\ RPN_{H02} &= 7 \times 5 \times 4 = 140 \\ \\ \text{For H-03 (Firmware lock-up):} \\ RPN_{H03} &= 8 \times 4 \times 7 = 224 \\ \\ \text{Ranking:} \quad &RPN_{H03} > RPN_{H01} > RPN_{H02} \\ &\text{H-03 is the highest priority.} \end{aligned} $$ **Recommended Risk Control for H-03 (3 marks):** - **Inherent Design:** Implement a *hardware watchdog timer* in the implant's microcontroller (IEC 62304 Class C software). - **Firmware Safety Architecture:** Add a redundant safety processor that monitors stimulation output and disables it in case of fault. - **Verification:** Fault injection testing per IEC 62304 and bench testing. - **Validation:** Animal study + clinical investigation with hardware fault simulation. - **Residual RPN estimate:** $S = 8, O = 1, D = 2 \Rightarrow RPN_{residual} = 16$ (acceptable). **ISO 14971:2019 Benefit-Risk Justification:** Cochlear implants provide **life-changing benefit** — restoration of hearing perception, language development in children, and improved quality of life. The benefit substantially outweighs the residual firmware risk of $RPN = 16$ when mitigated as above. Per ISO 14971 Clause 6, the overall residual risk is **acceptable** when weighed against the clinical benefit. > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Losing Pitfalls in Question B:** > 1. **Do NOT skip the gate criteria.** A 2-mark deduction if you list gates without Go/No-Go conditions. > 2. **Show the RPN arithmetic step-by-step.** Writing only the final numbers (162, 140, 224) without showing $9 \times 3 \times 6$ etc. will lose 1 mark. > 3. **Always justify risk acceptability using benefit-risk analysis.** This is mandatory under ISO 14971:2019. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** - **The process is a Stage-Gate model** with at least 4–5 gates. Each gate has explicit Go/No-Go criteria. - **ISO 13485:2016 Clause 7.3** is the regulatory backbone. Memorize the 9 sub-clauses: Planning, Inputs, Outputs, Review, Verification, Validation, Transfer, Change Control, Records. - **Verification = "Building the device right."** Validation = "Building the right device." - **DHF vs DMR:** DHF = design history. DMR = manufacturing blueprint. Do not confuse. - **RPN = S × O × D** (AIAG-VDA FMEA). Threshold for action is typically > 100. - **ISO 14971 runs in parallel** with design control — not sequentially. Four integration touchpoints: risk analysis, risk control verification, overall residual risk evaluation, post-production update. - **Traceability matrix is mandatory:** UN → DI → DO → V → VL. Auditors live and die by this matrix. - **Risk management file (RMF) and risk management report (RMR)** are mandatory outputs. - **IEC 62366-1** governs usability engineering; **IEC 62304** governs software lifecycle. - **ISO 14155** governs clinical investigation of medical devices. - **Design transfer** is itself a controlled process with IQ/OQ/PQ validation. - **Change control** applies to *all* post-design changes; CCB is the typical body. - **Total Product Life Cycle (TPLC)** — post-market feedback loops back into the next-generation design. - **Class III devices** (e.g., cochlear implant) require FDA PMA and EU MDR Annex IX/X. **Class II** (e.g., ECG monitor) usually requires 510(k) or EU MDR Class IIa. - **V-Model** is the alternative visualization — verification on lower arm, validation on upper arm. - **Waterfall** for hardware, **Agile** for SaMD, **Hybrid** for connected/combination products. - **Sample size for zero-failure tests:** $n = \frac{\ln(1-C)}{\ln(1-p)}$, always round UP. > **Best of luck with your KTU 2024 ESE examination. Master the framework, not just the facts — examiners reward structured, regulation-linked answers.**

topics.title: Medical Device Design Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Medical Device Design Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Medical Device Design Process** is a structured, regulated, and documented sequence of activities that transforms **user needs** and **intended use** into a validated, safe, and effective medical device, governed by regulatory frameworks such as **FDA 21 CFR Part 820.30** (Quality System Regulation - Design Controls) and **ISO 13485:2016, Clause 7.3** (Design and Development).

The design process is **not a single engineering activity** but a **closed-loop, risk-based, and verifiable lifecycle** consisting of planning, inputs, outputs, reviews, verification, validation, transfer, and change control — all captured in a **Design History File (DHF)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy — "Building a Hospital-Grade ICU Ventilator"**  
> Imagine designing a ventilator like **building a passenger aircraft**:  
> - **User Needs** = what pilots and passengers need (analogy: doctors and patients).  
> - **Design Input** = engineering specifications (must deliver 0.2–1.0 L tidal volume).  
> - **Design Output** = engineering drawings, BOM, software code.  
> - **Design Review** = inspection at each assembly stage.  
> - **Verification** = "Did we build the device **right**?" (testing against specs in a lab).  
> - **Validation** = "Did we build the **right** device?" (testing on real patients/use environment).  
> - **Design Transfer** = moving the design from R&D to manufacturing.  
> - **DHF** = the complete "black box" flight recorder of every decision.

This is the central engineering takeaway: **Verification = Specification-driven**, **Validation = User-driven**.

### 1.3 Key Regulatory Anchors (KTU 2024 Highlight)

| Regulation / Standard | Clause | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **FDA 21 CFR 820.30** | Design Controls | Mandatory in USA |
| **ISO 13485:2016** | Clause 7.3 | Global QMS Design & Development |
| **ISO 14971:2019** | Risk Management | Applied throughout design |
| **IEC 62366-1** | Usability Engineering | User-error reduction |
| **IEC 62304** | Software Lifecycle | For device software |

> **Bold emphasis**: **Design Control** is the only FDA subpart of the QSR that explicitly demands a *systematic* process. Skipping design controls is the **#1 reason for FDA 483 observations** in medical device startups.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
