## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 9 Mandatory Design Control Elements (FDA 21 CFR 820.30)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Design Control** is non-negotiable for **Class II and Class III** devices in the US and **all CE-marked devices** in the EU under **MDR 2017/745**.

1. **Design and Development Planning** — Defines responsibilities, phases, reviews, resources.
2. **Design Input** — Physical, performance, regulatory, and usability requirements.
3. **Design Output** — Drawings, specifications, BOM, software source code, labeling.
4. **Design Review** — Formal, documented, multi-disciplinary review at each phase.
5. **Design Verification** — Confirmation by examination and objective evidence that **design output meets design input**.
6. **Design Validation** — Confirmation that the device meets **user needs and intended use** in actual or simulated use conditions.
7. **Design Transfer** — Translation of design into production specifications.
8. **Design Changes** — Controlled change management with re-verification/validation.
9. **Design History File (DHF)** — The complete, auditable record proving the process was followed.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why Plan?** → Prevents scope-creep, assigns accountability, and aligns with regulatory milestones (e.g., Design Freeze, IDE submission, 510(k) submission).
- **Why Design Input?** → Prevents ambiguous engineering. Inputs must be **measurable, traceable, and unambiguous**.
- **Why Design Output?** → Outputs must **trace back to inputs** — this is the **traceability matrix** (often a spreadsheet mapping each input to its verification).
- **Why Verification AND Validation separately?** → Because a device can be **built right** (verification passes) but still be the **wrong device** (validation fails on the user).
- **Why Design Transfer?** → A working prototype is **not** a manufacturable product. Transfer ensures DFM (Design for Manufacturing) and process validation.
- **Why DHF?** → Auditors (FDA, Notified Body) will request it. If it does not exist, the product cannot be legally marketed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Meaning | Regulatory Source |
|---|---|---|
| $V$ | Design Verification | Confirms $DO \geq DI$ |
| $V_a$ | Design Validation | Confirms $DO \geq UN$ (User Needs) |
| $R$ | Risk Score | $R = S \times P$ (Severity × Probability) |
| $S$ | Severity (1–5) | ISO 14971 Table |
| $P$ | Probability of Occurrence (1–5) | ISO 14971 |
| $D$ | Detectability (1–5) | Used in FMEA |
| $RPN$ | Risk Priority Number | $RPN = S \times P \times D$ |
| $C_{stage}$ | Cost of Change at Stage $i$ | $\sum C_{stage_i} \uparrow$ as $i \rightarrow$ Post-Market |
| $TR$ | Traceability Index | $\frac{\text{Inputs traced to Outputs}}{\text{Total Inputs}} \times 100\%$ |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Rule of 10 in Medical Device Design**: The cost of fixing a design error **multiplies by 10×** at each successive phase (Concept → Design → Prototype → Pilot → Production → Post-Market).

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

This process underpins the development of:
- **Implantables** (pacemakers, stents) — Class III, highest scrutiny.
- **IVDs** (COVID-19 rapid test kits) — ISO 13485 + IVDR compliance.
- **Software as Medical Device (SaMD)** — IEC 62304 + FDA's "Predetermined Change Control Plan" (PCCP).
- **Combination products** (drug-eluting stents) — Both drug and device design controls apply.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Process Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Since the **Medical Device Design Process** is a procedural/regulatory subject, "derivations" here translate to the **step-by-step logical flow** of each design control element, with **analytical matrices** for traceability and risk evaluation.

### 3.1 Sequential Design Lifecycle (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — User Needs & Intended Use Determination**

$$
UN = \{ u_1, u_2, \dots, u_n \}
$$

where each $u_i$ is a documented user requirement derived from clinical literature, Voice of Customer (VoC), and regulatory predicates.

**Step 2 — Design Input (DI) Generation**

$$
DI_i = f(UN_i, \, ISO_{ref}, \, Risk_i)
$$

Each input must be:
- *Unambiguous* (only one interpretation),
- *Verifiable* (testable),
- *Traceable* (linked to a user need).

**Step 3 — Design Output (DO) Generation**

$$
DO_j = \{ Spec_j, \, Drawing_j, \, Code_j, \, Label_j \}
$$

Output must satisfy: $\forall j,\; DO_j \text{ traces back to a } DI_i$.

**Step 4 — Design Review**

Formal review with cross-functional team (Engineering, QA, Regulatory, Clinical, Manufacturing). Min 1 review at each design phase. Documented with action items.

**Step 5 — Design Verification**

$$
V_i: \text{Test}_{lab}(DO_i) \stackrel{?}{=} DI_i
$$

Conducted in **lab/controlled environment**. Methods: bench testing, dimensional inspection, software unit tests.

**Step 6 — Design Validation**

$$
V_{a,k}: \text{Test}_{use}(DO_k) \stackrel{?}{=} UN_k
$$

Conducted in **actual or simulated use environment**. Methods: clinical evaluation, usability testing (IEC 62366), animal studies (if required).

**Step 7 — Design Transfer**

Production specifications, DFM review, supplier qualification, process validation (IQ/OQ/PQ), and pilot production runs.

**Step 8 — Design Changes**

Each change triggers **Change Impact Assessment** → re-verification/validation if affected. Documented in **Change Control Records**.

**Step 9 — Design History File (DHF) Compilation**

$$
DHF = \bigcup_{i=1}^{9} \text{Records of Step}_i
$$

The DHF must be **complete, indexed, and retrievable** for the entire device lifecycle.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Verification vs. Validation

| Parameter | Design Verification (V) | Design Validation (Vₐ) |
|---|---|---|
| **Question Answered** | Are we building the product **right**? | Are we building the **right** product? |
| **Reference** | Design Input (DI) | User Needs (UN) & Intended Use |
| **Environment** | Lab / controlled | Actual or simulated use |
| **Sample** | Engineering units | Production-equivalent units |
| **Method Examples** | Bench test, dimensional, software unit test | Clinical study, usability test (summative) |
| **Timing** | After design output, before transfer | After verification, on finished device |
| **Document** | Verification Test Reports | Validation Reports (incl. clinical evaluation) |
| **Regulatory Basis** | 21 CFR 820.30(f) | 21 CFR 820.30(g) |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Risk Management Integration (ISO 14971)

The design process is **iterative with risk management**, not linear. Each iteration applies the loop:

$$
\text{Hazard Identification} \rightarrow \text{Estimate } S, P \rightarrow \text{Risk Evaluation} \rightarrow \text{Risk Control} \rightarrow \text{Residual Risk}
$$

| Risk Control Measure | Design Stage Applied | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Inherent Safety by Design** | Concept / Input | Architecture decisions |
| **Protective Measures** | Detailed Design | Guards, alarms, software interlocks |
| **Information for Safety** | Labeling / IFU | Warnings, contraindications |
| **Verification of Risk Control** | Verification Phase | Test reports |
| **Validation of Risk Control** | Validation Phase | Usability + clinical data |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Per **ISO 14971:2019**, the **Risk Management File** must be **cross-referenced** with the **Design History File** — they are companion documents, not standalone.

---

### 3.4 Design Traceability Matrix (DTM) Logic

The DTM is the **single most important audit artifact** in design controls. It links User Needs → Design Inputs → Design Outputs → Verification → Validation.

$$
\text{Mapping: } UN_i \;\rightarrow\; DI_i \;\rightarrow\; DO_j \;\rightarrow\; V_i \;\rightarrow\; V_{a,k}
$$

A complete DTM shows:
- Every user need is addressed by at least one design input.
- Every design input is satisfied by at least one design output.
- Every design output is verified.
- Every user need is validated.

> **Audit Failure Mode**: A common FDA 483 observation is *"Design inputs were not adequately verified"* — a DTM gap.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — FDA Design Control Flow (21 CFR 820.30)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[userNeeds] --> B[designInput]
    B --> C[designOutput]
    C --> D[designReview]
    D --> E[designVerification]
    E --> F{Verification OK}
    F -- No --> G[Design Iteration]
    G --> C
    F -- Yes --> H[designValidation]
    H --> I{Validation OK}
    I -- No --> G
    I -- Yes --> J[designTransfer]
    J --> K[designChanges Control]
    K --> L[designHistoryFile]
    L --> M[Post Market Surveillance]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading the flow**: The **outer feedback loop** (G — Design Iteration) represents the **iterative nature** of design. Each iteration must be **documented in the DHF**.

### 4.2 Mermaid — The V-Model of Medical Device Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U1[User Needs] -- decompose --> D1[Design Input]
    D1 -- define --> D2[Design Output]
    D2 -- left arm --> V1[Verification]
    V1 -- right arm --> U2[Intended Use Confirmed]
    U2 -- validate --> D3[Validation]
    D3 -- closing --> T1[Design Transfer]
    D1 -.- V1
    D2 -.- V1
    D3 -.- U1
```

> **Interpretation**: The **left descending arm** represents the **decomposition** of requirements; the **right ascending arm** represents the **integration and confirmation** through verification and validation.

### 4.3 Mermaid — Stage-Gate Medical Device Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Stage 0 Concept] --> G0{Gate 0 Feasibility}
    G0 -- Approved --> S1[Stage 1 Design Input Freeze]
    S1 --> G1{Gate 1 Input Review}
    G1 -- Approved --> S2[Stage 2 Design Output Development]
    S2 --> G2{Gate 2 Design Review}
    G2 -- Approved --> S3[Stage 3 V and Va]
    S3 --> G3{Gate 3 Design Freeze}
    G3 -- Approved --> S4[Stage 4 Design Transfer]
    S4 --> G4{Gate 4 Production Release}
    G4 -- Approved --> S5[Stage 5 Post Market]
```

> **Stage-Gate Reality**: Each **Gate** is a **formal decision point** where management, QA, and Regulatory **must sign off** before the design advances. Skipping a gate is a common 510(k) deficiency.

### 4.4 Mermaid — Risk Management ↔ Design Control Integration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Hazard Identification] --> R2[Risk Estimation S P]
    R2 --> R3{Risk Acceptable}
    R3 -- No --> R4[Risk Control Measure]
    R4 --> D1[Update Design Input]
    D1 --> R5[Verify Risk Control]
    R5 --> R6{Residual Risk Acceptable}
    R6 -- No --> R4
    R6 -- Yes --> R7[Document in Risk Mgmt File]
    R3 -- Yes --> R7
    R7 --> D2[Proceed to Next Design Stage]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
> *Define Design Verification and Design Validation. State the key difference between them in the context of FDA 21 CFR 820.30.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Design Verification (1 mark):** Confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence that the design output meets the design input requirements. *(Reference: 21 CFR 820.30(f))*
- **Design Validation (1 mark):** Confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence that the device meets user needs and intended use. *(Reference: 21 CFR 820.30(g))*
- **Key Difference (1 mark):** Verification ensures the device is built **right** (specification-driven, lab-based), while Validation ensures the **right** device is built (user-needs-driven, use-environment based).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**  
> *What is a Design History File (DHF)? List any four documents it must contain as per 21 CFR 820.30(j).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The DHF is a compilation of records which describes the design history of a finished device, demonstrating that the design was developed in accordance with the approved design plan and regulatory requirements.
- **Four DHF documents (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. Design and Development Plan
  2. Design Input records
  3. Design Output records (drawings, specs, code)
  4. Design Verification and Validation reports
  5. Design Review records and Design Transfer records
  6. Design Change records

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
> *Explain the Medical Device Design Process as per FDA 21 CFR 820.30. List and briefly describe the nine elements of design control. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The Medical Device Design Process, as mandated by **FDA 21 CFR 820.30**, is a systematic, documented approach ensuring device safety, efficacy, and regulatory compliance. The **nine elements of design control** are:

1. **Design and Development Plan (1 mark):** Defines activities, responsibilities, timelines, and review stages.
2. **Design Input (0.7 mark):** Physical, performance, regulatory, and usability requirements that address user needs.
3. **Design Output (0.7 mark):** Drawings, specifications, BOM, software code, and labeling — must be expressed in terms that allow verification.
4. **Design Review (0.7 mark):** Formal, documented, multi-disciplinary review at each phase.
5. **Design Verification (0.7 mark):** Objective evidence that design output meets design input.
6. **Design Validation (0.7 mark):** Objective evidence that the device meets user needs and intended use.
7. **Design Transfer (0.7 mark):** Translation of design into production-ready documentation.
8. **Design Changes (0.7 mark):** All changes reviewed, verified, validated, and approved before implementation.
9. **Design History File (0.7 mark):** Indexed record of all design activities and outputs, auditable throughout the device lifecycle.

---

> **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
> *Discuss the relationship between Design Verification and Design Validation with suitable examples for a Class II medical device (e.g., a digital blood pressure monitor). (7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** (1 mark) | Confirm design output matches design input | Confirm device meets user needs |
| **Reference** (1 mark) | Specifications (e.g., ±3 mmHg accuracy) | User needs (e.g., easy-to-read display for elderly patients) |
| **Environment** (0.5 mark) | Laboratory / bench test | Real-world / simulated use |
| **Sample** (0.5 mark) | Pre-production units | Production-equivalent / final device |
| **Example for BP Monitor** (3 marks) | Calibrating the pressure sensor against a reference manometer in the lab; software unit testing of the inflation algorithm | Usability study with 15 hypertensive patients using the device at home for 2 weeks; clinical comparison against a mercury sphygmomanometer per ISO 81060-2 |

**Key Point (1 mark):** A device may pass verification (correct pressure reading) but fail validation (display too dim for elderly users, leading to misreadings). Both are **mandatory and distinct**.

---

> **Q4(a). [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Alternative Choice]**  
> *With a neat flowchart, explain the Stage-Gate Medical Device Design Process. Discuss the importance of each gate in regulatory compliance. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The **Stage-Gate Process** divides design into discrete stages separated by formal decision points (gates).

**Flow (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stage 0 Concept] --> B{Gate 0 Feasibility}
    B --> C[Stage 1 Design Input]
    C --> D{Gate 1 Input Review}
    D --> E[Stage 2 Design Output]
    E --> F{Gate 2 Design Review}
    F --> G[Stage 3 V and Va]
    G --> H{Gate 3 Design Freeze}
    H --> I[Stage 4 Design Transfer]
    I --> J{Gate 4 Production Release}
```

**Importance of Gates (4 marks — 1 each):**
- **Gate 0 — Feasibility:** Confirms technical, regulatory, and economic viability before commitment.
- **Gate 1 — Input Review:** Freezes user needs and design inputs; basis for all downstream work.
- **Gate 2 — Design Review:** Confirms outputs satisfy inputs; risk controls verified.
- **Gate 3 — Design Freeze:** Locks the design for transfer; changes become expensive.
- **Gate 4 — Production Release:** Confirms process validation, supply chain, and post-market plan.

> Skipping gates is a frequent FDA 510(k) deficiency because the design plan was not followed.

---

> **Q4(b). [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Alternative Choice]**  
> *Explain the role of ISO 14971 risk management in the medical device design process. How is the Risk Management File linked to the Design History File? (7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

- **Role of ISO 14971 in Design (3 marks):**
  1. **Hazard Identification** at the concept stage.
  2. **Risk Estimation** ($R = S \times P$) for each hazard.
  3. **Risk Control** measures applied during design (inherent safety, protective measures, information for safety).
  4. **Verification and Validation of Risk Controls** during V&V phase.
  5. **Residual Risk Evaluation** before market release.

- **Linkage to DHF (4 marks):**
  1. The **Risk Management File (RMF)** is a **living document** maintained throughout the design lifecycle.
  2. Each **design input** addressing a hazard is **traced** in the **Design Traceability Matrix (DTM)** within the DHF.
  3. **Risk control verification reports** are stored within the DHF verification records.
  4. The **Risk Management Report** (final document) is referenced in the **DHF** as part of design transfer and post-market surveillance.
  5. ISO 14971 mandates that the RMF be **available for inspection** alongside the DHF during regulatory audits.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**  
> 1. **Conflating Verification with Validation** — They are NOT the same. V&V is the most-asked concept. State explicitly *what* is being confirmed and *against what*.  
> 2. **Skipping the "Why"** — Mere listing of 9 design controls gets only 50% marks. Examiners reward explanation of *why* each control exists.  
> 3. **Forgetting Risk Management** — Any design question without an ISO 14971 reference is incomplete in KTU 2024 evaluation.  
> 4. **No reference to traceability** — A traceability matrix mention (DTM) is a high-scoring differentiator.  
> 5. **Ignoring design changes** — Always mention that design changes trigger re-verification/re-validation; students often skip this.  
> 6. **Post-market phase absence** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes lifecycle thinking. End the answer at post-market surveillance, not at design transfer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Medical Device Design Process is **regulated** — it is not optional engineering.
- ✅ Anchored by **FDA 21 CFR 820.30** and **ISO 13485:2016 Clause 7.3**.
- ✅ **Nine Elements**: Plan, Input, Output, Review, Verification, Validation, Transfer, Changes, DHF.
- ✅ **Verification = right product; Validation = right product built right**.
- ✅ **Risk Management (ISO 14971)** is **integrated**, not parallel — formula $R = S \times P$.
- ✅ **Design History File (DHF)** is the *proof* of design control — auditors will request it.
- ✅ **Cost of change increases 10×** at each phase — motivates early verification.
- ✅ **Traceability Matrix (DTM)** links UN → DI → DO → V → Vₐ — single most important audit artifact.
- ✅ **Stage-Gate** with formal reviews at each gate is the practical implementation.
- ✅ **V-Model** visually represents the design-decomposition and integration philosophy.
- ✅ **Post-market surveillance** is the closing loop of the design lifecycle.
- ✅ For **SaMD**, additionally comply with **IEC 62304** and **FDA's Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP)**.
- ✅ For **usability**, comply with **IEC 62366-1** — summative testing is mandatory for high-risk devices.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing - Verification and Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Testing - Verification and Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definitions (Per ISO 13485:2016 & FDA 21 CFR 820)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification (Design Verification):** The process of confirming that **design and development outputs** meet the **design and development inputs**. It answers the question — *"Are we building the product RIGHT?"*
> *(Source: ISO 13485:2016, Section 8.3.4 / 21 CFR 820.30(f))*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation (Design Validation):** The process of confirming that the **resulting product** is capable of meeting the **requirements for the specified application** or **intended use**. It answers the question — *"Are we building the RIGHT product?"*
> *(Source: ISO 13485:2016, Section 8.3.5 / 21 CFR 820.30(g))*

| Term | Object of Comparison | Stage |
|---|---|---|
| **Verification** | Output vs Input (Spec-to-Spec) | Throughout design phase |
| **Validation** | Final Product vs User Need / Intended Use | Late design / Pre-launch |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Bakery Model 🍞

Imagine you are a baker developing a new gluten-free birthday cake for a client:

- **Verification** is the *kitchen test*: you check whether the cake you actually baked matches the *recipe specification* — Is the temperature 180 °C as written? Did you use 200 g of flour? Is the bake time 35 minutes? **You are checking the cake against the recipe on paper.**
- **Validation** is the *tasting party*: you invite real customers to eat the cake and ask — *"Is this what you actually wanted for your birthday? Does it taste good, look appealing, and is it safe to eat?"* **You are checking the cake against the user's needs.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A product can pass **Verification** (technically correct) but **FAIL Validation** (users don't want it). A medical device can be perfectly built to spec but still injure patients or be unusable by surgeons.

## 1.3 Testing — The Operational Bridge Between V & V

**Testing** is the *mechanism* by which verification and validation are operationally performed. It is the planned, documented, and traceable act of exercising a device (or its sub-system) to generate objective evidence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 1 explicitly positions *Testing* as the *instrument* that generates the *evidence* required by V&V. Without structured testing protocols, there is no defensible V&V.

### Why Both V and V Are Statutorily Required
1. **Patient Safety** — Faulty verification misses design defects; faulty validation misses use errors.
2. **Regulatory Compliance** — FDA, EU MDR (Article 61), and CDSCO/MHFW all mandate documented V&V.
3. **Liability Defense** — In product-liability litigation, the **Verification & Validation Master Report** is the first document the court examines.
4. **Cost of Rework** — A defect caught in **verification** costs ~10× less than the same defect caught in **validation**, and ~100× less than a defect caught in the field (post-market surveillance).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Medical Device V&V

### Pillar 1 — Design Verification (Spec → Spec)
Verification is decomposed into a hierarchy of tests:

1. **Unit / Sub-system Testing** — Individual components (e.g., a single sensor IC, an algorithm module).
2. **Integration Testing** — Interfaces between sub-systems (e.g., sensor → microcontroller → display).
3. **System-Level / Bench Testing** — Complete device against final design specification (e.g., IEC 60601-1 electrical safety, IPX7 water ingress, ISO 10993 biocompatibility at material level).
4. **Software Verification** — Per **IEC 62304**, includes unit, integration, and system software testing, plus static analysis.

### Pillar 2 — Design Validation (Product → User Need)
Validation encompasses:

1. **Clinical Evaluation / Simulated Use Testing** — Per **ISO 14155** (Good Clinical Practice for medical devices).
2. **Usability / Human Factors Validation** — Per **IEC 62366-1**, with summative testing on representative users.
3. **Performance Validation** — Bench or animal studies where clinical is not ethical.
4. **Packaging & Shelf-Life Validation** — Per **ASTM F1980** (accelerated aging) and **ISO 11607**.

### Pillar 3 — Test Lifecycle Management
A test goes through four states:
- **Test Plan** (what, why, how, when, who)
- **Test Protocol** (the procedural SOP with pass/fail criteria)
- **Test Execution** (raw data, calibration records, deviations)
- **Test Report** (conclusion, traceability matrix, sign-off)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regulatory Rule of Traceability:** Every requirement in the *Design Input* document must be linked to (a) a verification activity and (b) a validation activity. This linkage is called the **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** — the regulatory "spine" of any submission.

## 2.2 Verification vs Validation — The KTU High-Yield Comparison Table

| Parameter | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| **Question Answered** | Are we building the product *right*? | Are we building the *right* product? |
| **Compared Against** | Design Inputs / Specifications | User Needs / Intended Use |
| **Performed On** | Design output (drawings, code, prototypes) | Finished product, production-equivalent units |
| **Stage in Lifecycle** | Early → Mid (Design phase) | Late (Pre-market / Transfer to production) |
| **Test Environment** | Bench / Lab / Simulated | Actual / Simulated use environment |
| **Performed By** | Design Engineering | Design + Cross-functional (QA, Clinical, Marketing, End-users) |
| **Key Standard** | **ISO 13485 §8.3.4**, 21 CFR 820.30(f) | **ISO 13485 §8.3.5**, 21 CFR 820.30(g) |
| **Failure Implication** | Design defect (fixable) | Use-related or requirement defect (may need redesign) |
| **Document Output** | Verification Test Report | Validation Test Report / Clinical Evaluation Report |
| **Frequency** | Many times per design iteration | Usually once per design freeze |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Statistical & Risk-Based Testing Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are routinely asked in KTU numerical/short-answer questions on testing strategy.

| # | Formula / Metric | Meaning | Application in V&V |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sample Size for Proportion (Infinite Pop.):** $\displaystyle n = \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^{2}}$ | Minimum test units to estimate a reliability proportion $p$ within error $e$ at confidence $Z$ | Reliability demonstration testing |
| 2 | **Sample Size (Finite Pop.):** $\displaystyle n_{adj} = \frac{n}{1 + \frac{n-1}{N}}$ | Adjusted sample size for finite lot of $N$ units | Lot acceptance testing |
| 3 | **Test Effectiveness Ratio (TER):** $\displaystyle \text{TER} = \frac{\text{Defects found by test}}{\text{Total defects injected}}$ | How many of all latent defects a test catches | Test strategy optimization |
| 4 | **Defect Density (Post-Verification):** $\displaystyle D = \frac{\text{Defects found}}{\text{KLOC or Function Points}}$ | Software quality indicator | IEC 62304 verification |
| 5 | **Risk Priority Number (RPN):** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Per **ISO 14971**: Severity × Occurrence × Detection | Decides which tests are mandatory |
| 6 | **Confidence Interval (95%):** $\displaystyle \hat{p} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$ | Bound on true pass-rate from test data | Acceptance sampling plan |
| 7 | **Test Coverage:** $\displaystyle C = \frac{\text{Requirements tested}}{\text{Total requirements}} \times 100\%$ | RTM coverage percentage | Regulatory submission check |

> **Boundary Note:** The Z-value at 95% confidence is **1.96**; at 99% confidence it is **2.576**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | What V&V Prevents | Regulatory Driver |
|---|---|---|
| **Cardiac Pacemaker** | Fatal arrhythmia from undersensed signal | FDA PMA, EU MDR Class III |
| **Insulin Pump** | Incorrect bolus calculation | IEC 60601-1-10, FDA Class II/III |
| **MRI Scanner** | RF burns, projectile incidents | IEC 60601-2-33 |
| **Surgical Robot (Da Vinci-like)** | Tissue perforation from latency | FDA 510(k) + clinical |
| **AI Diagnostic Software** | Bias / false negatives in lesion detection | EU MDR Annex VIII, FDA PCCP |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Worked Derivations

## 3.1 The V-Model — The Canonical Engineering Derivation of V&V

The V-Model is **derived** from the Quality Management System requirement to ensure that *every design input has a corresponding verification/validation step*, while eliminating redundant testing. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step construction of the V-Model logic for medical devices.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests students' ability to *map* regulatory activities to V-Model legs. Memorize the left and right side correspondence.

### Step 1 — Define the *User Need* (Top of V)
The V-Model begins with the **User Need Statement** (e.g., "The device shall continuously monitor blood glucose in adult Type-1 diabetic patients for 7 days without calibration drift > 5%"). This is the apex of the V.

### Step 2 — Derive the *Design Input* Specifications
The User Need is decomposed into *measurable* design inputs:
- Accuracy: $\pm$5% vs reference
- Battery life: $\geq 7$ days
- Skin contact temperature: $\leq 40 \text{ °C}$

### Step 3 — Architectural Decomposition (Left Descending Leg)
The design is progressively decomposed:

$$
\text{User Need} \rightarrow \text{System Requirements} \rightarrow \text{Sub-system Specs} \rightarrow \text{Module Specs}
$$

### Step 4 — Implementation (Bottom of V)
The lowest-level modules are implemented in hardware/firmware/software.

### Step 5 — Ascending Right Leg (Verification then Validation)
At each descending level, a corresponding **ascending** test is prescribed:

| Left-Descending Leg (Decomposition) | Right-Ascending Leg (Testing) | Type |
|---|---|---|
| User Need | **Clinical / User Validation** | **Validation** |
| System Requirements | **System-Level Verification** | Verification |
| Sub-system Specs | **Integration Testing** | Verification |
| Module Specs | **Unit Testing** | Verification |

### Step 6 — Closure
The two legs of the V close at the **Design Transfer** stage, where the product moves to manufacturing and **Process Validation** (per **ISO 13485 §7.5.6** or **21 CFR 820.75**) takes over.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Derivations

### 3.2.1 Sample-Size Derivation for a Reliability Test

**Problem (KTU-style):** A manufacturer of an infusion pump claims a reliability of **95%** with an acceptable error of **$\pm$3%** at **95% confidence**. Calculate the minimum test sample size required for an infinite production population.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters**

$$
Z = 1.96, \quad p = 0.95, \quad e = 0.03
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the infinite-population formula**

$$
n = \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values**

$$
n = \frac{(1.96)^{2} \cdot (0.95) \cdot (1 - 0.95)}{(0.03)^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the numerator and denominator**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 3.8416 \times 0.95 \times 0.05 = 3.8416 \times 0.0475 = 0.182476
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = (0.03)^{2} = 0.0009
$$

**Step 5 — Final division and round-up (since $n$ must be a whole number of test units)**

$$
n = \frac{0.182476}{0.0009} = 202.75
$$

$$
\boxed{n \approx 203 \text{ units}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always **round UP** the sample size to the next whole integer — you cannot test 0.75 of a medical device.

**Step 6 — Finite-lot correction (extension)**
If the production lot has $N = 1000$ units already built, apply the finite-population correction:

$$
n_{adj} = \frac{n}{1 + \frac{n - 1}{N}} = \frac{203}{1 + \frac{202}{1000}} = \frac{203}{1.202} = 168.88
$$

$$
\boxed{n_{adj} \approx 169 \text{ units}}
$$

### 3.2.2 Risk-Based Test Prioritization (RPN)

**Problem:** A new syringe safety device has three potential failure modes with the following scores:

| Failure Mode | Severity (S, 1–10) | Occurrence (O, 1–10) | Detection (D, 1–10) |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1: Needle-stick injury | 10 | 4 | 5 |
| F2: Plunger jam | 5 | 6 | 3 |
| F3: Label misprint | 2 | 8 | 7 |

**Step 1 — Compute RPN for each**

$$
\text{RPN}_{F1} = 10 \times 4 \times 5 = 200
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_{F2} = 5 \times 6 \times 3 = 90
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_{F3} = 2 \times 8 \times 7 = 112
$$

**Step 2 — Rank and prioritize testing**

$$
\text{Priority}: F1 \; (200) > F3 \; (112) > F2 \; (90)
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** F1 (needle-stick) demands the most rigorous *user-validation* testing on real clinicians; F2 (plunger jam) can be addressed with bench verification; F3 (label) is a process-validation issue at manufacturing, not a V&V issue at design.

### 3.2.3 Verification Coverage Computation

**Problem:** A design input document has **120** requirements. The V&V test protocols cover **108** of them. Compute Test Coverage.

$$
C = \frac{108}{120} \times 100\% = 90\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Regulatory submission threshold is **100%**. A 90% coverage means 12 requirements have *no objective evidence* — a guaranteed **FDA 483 observation** during audit.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What Test

| Test Type | Maps to | Performed on | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bench / Type Testing** | Verification | Production-equivalent unit | Electrical safety (IEC 60601-1), EMC (IEC 60601-1-2) |
| **Software Unit Test** | Verification | Code module | Algorithm correctness |
| **Integration Test** | Verification | Combined sub-systems | Signal chain, communication bus |
| **Animal (GLP) Study** | Verification (typically) | Pre-clinical | Per **ISO 10993** for new materials |
| **Simulated Use Test** | Validation | Realistic-use prototype | Per **IEC 62366-1** |
| **Clinical Investigation** | Validation | Patients/users | Per **ISO 14155**, **EU MDR Ch. VI** |
| **Usability Summative** | Validation | Representative users | HF validation, **FDA HF Guidance** |
| **Process Validation (IQ/OQ/PQ)** | Process V&V | Manufacturing line | Per **21 CFR 820.75** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IQ = Installation Qualification, OQ = Operational Qualification, PQ = Performance Qualification.** All three are required for any new manufacturing process under **21 CFR 820.75** and **GHTF/N43**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Medical Device V-Model (V-Model for V&V)

```mermaid
graph TD
    userNeed[USER NEED STATEMENT] --> sysReq[SYSTEM REQUIREMENTS]
    sysReq --> subReq[SUBSYSTEM SPECIFICATIONS]
    subReq --> modSpec[MODULE SPECIFICATIONS]
    modSpec --> impl[IMPLEMENTATION HW FW SW]

    impl --> unitT[UNIT TESTING VERIFICATION]
    unitT --> intT[INTEGRATION TESTING VERIFICATION]
    intT --> sysT[SYSTEM LEVEL TESTING VERIFICATION]
    sysT --> clinT[CLINICAL USER VALIDATION]

    clinT --> designXfer[DESIGN TRANSFER TO MANUFACTURING]
    designXfer --> procV[PROCESS VALIDATION IQ OQ PQ]
    procV --> prodRelease[PRODUCTION RELEASE AND POST MARKET]

    userNeed -.-> clinT
    sysReq -.-> sysT
    subReq -.-> intT
    modSpec -.-> unitT
```

> **Reading the Dotted Arrows:** Each dotted line represents the **traceability link** (the RTM) that proves every input has a corresponding output and a corresponding test. These dotted links are what regulatory auditors *always* ask for first.

## 4.2 The V&V Relationship & Document Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[DESIGN INPUT SPECIFICATIONS] -->|INPUT TO| B[DESIGN OUTPUT DRAWINGS CODE BOMS]
    B -->|VERIFIED AGAINST A| C[VERIFICATION TESTS BENCH TYPE]
    A -->|VALIDATED VIA| D[VALIDATION TESTS CLINICAL USABILITY]
    B -->|PRODUCES| E[FINAL DEVICE PRODUCTION EQUIVALENT]
    E -->|USED IN| D

    C --> F[VERIFICATION REPORT]
    D --> G[VALIDATION REPORT]
    F --> H[REQUIREMENTS TRACEABILITY MATRIX RTM]
    G --> H
    A --> H
    B --> H

    H --> I[REGULATORY SUBMISSION 510K PMA CE MARK]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style D fill:#ffe1e1
    style H fill:#e1ffe1
    style I fill:#f5e1ff
```

## 4.3 Risk-Based Test Selection Block Architecture (ISO 14971 → V&V)

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1[HAZARD ANALYSIS ISO 14971] --> R2[RISK ESTIMATION S O D]
    R2 --> R3{RPN THRESHOLD}

    R3 -->|RPN HIGH| R4[CLINICAL VALIDATION IEC 14155]
    R3 -->|RPN MEDIUM| R5[SUMMATIVE USABILITY IEC 62366]
    R3 -->|RPN LOW| R6[BENCH VERIFICATION IEC 60601]

    R4 --> R7[DESIGN HISTORY FILE DHF]
    R5 --> R7
    R6 --> R7

    R7 --> R8[REGULATORY DOSSIER]
    R8 --> R9[MARKET ENTRY]

    style R3 fill:#ffe1e1
    style R7 fill:#e1ffe1
```

## 4.4 The V&V Test Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PLAN : TEST PLAN CREATED
    PLAN --> PROTOCOL : TEST PROTOCOL APPROVED
    PROTOCOL --> EXECUTION : TEST RUN ON DEVICE
    EXECUTION --> REPORT : RAW DATA COLLECTED
    REPORT --> REVIEW : QA AND PEER REVIEW
    REVIEW --> APPROVED : PASS CRITERIA MET
    REVIEW --> PROTOCOL : FAILURE TRIGGERS NCR AND RE TEST
    APPROVED --> [*] : TEST CLOSURE IN DHF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **NCR = Non-Conformance Report.** Any failed test creates an NCR, which must be root-cause analyzed (e.g., 5-Why, Fishbone), corrected, and the *same protocol* re-executed until pass. Skipping the NCR loop is a **fatal regulatory audit finding**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1.a]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Verification and Validation in the context of medical device design controls.**

**Model Answer (≈ 60 words / 3 marks):**

*Verification* confirms that **design outputs meet design inputs** (e.g., bench test shows the sensor outputs 5.0 V as specified). *Validation* confirms that the **finished device meets the user's needs and intended use** (e.g., a surgeon successfully uses the device in a simulated procedure). Verification is *"building the product right"*; Validation is *"building the right product."* Both are mandatory under ISO 13485 §8.3.4 & §8.3.5.

> **Marking Scheme:** Definition V (1) + Definition Val (1) + One-line contrast (1) = **3 marks**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q1.b]**

**Q2. What is a Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) and why is it regulatorily important?**

**Model Answer:**

An **RTM** is a tabular document that links every *design input* to its *design output*, *verification test*, and *validation test*. It provides **objective evidence** that no requirement is left untested.

**Regulatory Importance:**
1. FDA / Notified Body auditors examine the RTM **first** during a QMS audit.
2. EU MDR Annex II mandates a complete RTM as part of the *Technical Documentation*.
3. Any gap in the RTM is a **Major Non-Conformance** (audit failure).
4. It enables **systematic coverage** of all design features for safety and performance.

> **Marking Scheme:** Definition (1) + Two regulatory uses (2) = **3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU ESE — Model Paper 2024, Q2 (Module 1)] — INTERNAL CHOICE**

---

### ⭐ Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **V-Model of medical device development**. How does each descending specification level map to a corresponding ascending test level? Identify the *validation* and *verification* activities on the V-Model.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturer of a Class III implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) must demonstrate **99% reliability** of the sensing circuit within an error of **$\pm$2%** at **95% confidence** to a Notified Body. Calculate the **minimum number of test samples** required for (i) an infinite production lot, and (ii) a finite lot of $N = 500$ pre-production units. Justify why FDA mandates sample size justification for Class III devices.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — V-Model Explanation [7 marks]**

| # | Step | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **User Need** (Top of V) | High-level patient/clinician requirement — apex of the V. | 1 |
| 2 | **System Requirements** | Decomposed into measurable system specs (e.g., battery, accuracy). | 1 |
| 3 | **Sub-system Specs** | Hardware modules, firmware blocks, mechanical sub-assemblies. | 1 |
| 4 | **Module Specs** | Smallest testable units — chips, code functions. | 0.5 |
| 5 | **Implementation** | Bottom of V — actual HW/FW/SW built. | 0.5 |
| 6 | **Ascending tests** | Unit (↔ Module), Integration (↔ Sub-system), System (↔ System req), Clinical/User Validation (↔ User Need). | 2 |
| 7 | **Distinction:** Verification on bottom 3 ascending levels; Validation only at top. | 1 |

> **Key sentence:** "Validation sits at the apex (user need), while verification sits on the middle/lower ascending legs."

---

**Part (b) — Sample Size Calculation [7 marks]**

**Given:**
$$
Z = 1.96, \quad p = 0.99, \quad e = 0.02
$$

**(i) Infinite-population sample size:**

$$
n = \frac{(1.96)^{2} \cdot (0.99) \cdot (1 - 0.99)}{(0.02)^{2}}
$$

$$
n = \frac{3.8416 \times 0.99 \times 0.01}{0.0004} = \frac{0.0380318}{0.0004} = 95.08
$$

$$
\boxed{n = 96 \text{ units (rounded up)}}
$$

**(ii) Finite-population correction for $N = 500$:**

$$
n_{adj} = \frac{n}{1 + \frac{n - 1}{N}} = \frac{96}{1 + \frac{95}{500}} = \frac{96}{1.19} = 80.67
$$

$$
\boxed{n_{adj} = 81 \text{ units (rounded up)}}
$$

**Justification for FDA mandate (3 lines, ~3 marks):**
Class III devices are *life-sustaining / life-supporting*; under-inflated sample sizes can mask rare failure modes. Hence FDA requires a *biostatistical justification* of the sample size in the **PMA submission**, typically using the formula above plus **Bayesian** or **sequential testing** methods for very rare events.

---

### ⭐ Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **role of testing in the Design Control process** as mandated by **21 CFR 820.30** and **ISO 13485 §8.3**. Include a description of the *four document types* that constitute a complete V&V record.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wearable pulse oximeter is being developed. The failure mode *F1* (false SpO₂ reading during motion) has Severity = 9, Occurrence = 6, Detection = 4. Failure mode *F2* (Bluetooth drop) has Severity = 3, Occurrence = 7, Detection = 5. Compute the **RPN** for each and recommend which one should be subjected to **summative usability validation** as opposed to bench verification, with justification.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Role of Testing in Design Control [7 marks]**

**21 CFR 820.30 Design Control Steps:**
1. Design & Development Planning
2. Design Input
3. Design Output
4. Design **Review**
5. Design **Verification**
6. Design **Validation**
7. Design Transfer
8. Design Changes
9. Design History File (DHF)

> **The Four Mandatory V&V Documents:**

| # | Document | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Test Plan** | Strategy, scope, responsibilities, schedule, sample size rationale |
| 2 | **Test Protocol** | Step-by-step procedure, pass/fail criteria, environmental conditions, equipment calibration |
| 3 | **Test Report** | Raw data, deviations, NCRs, photographic evidence, statistical analysis |
| 4 | **RTM** | Traceability from User Need → Design Input → Design Output → V&V test |

> **Mark split:** 21 CFR 820 steps (3) + four documents (3) + conclusion (1).

---

**Part (b) — RPN Computation [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute RPN for F1 and F2**

$$
\text{RPN}_{F1} = 9 \times 6 \times 4 = 216
$$

$$
\text{RPN}_{F2} = 3 \times 7 \times 5 = 105
$$

**Step 2 — Rank & Decide**

$$
\text{RPN}_{F1} = 216 \;\; (\text{HIGH}) \quad > \quad \text{RPN}_{F2} = 105 \;\; (\text{MEDIUM})
$$

**Step 3 — Recommendation**

> **F1 (false SpO₂ during motion)** → **Summative Usability Validation** under **IEC 62366-1** with representative patients performing *activities of daily living*. Justification: high severity (9 = potential misdiagnosis → patient harm), moderate-high occurrence, and poor detection (clinicians may not notice).

> **F2 (Bluetooth drop)** → **Bench verification** (radio-range test per **FCC Part 15** and **IEC 60601-1-2 EMC**). Justification: low severity (alarm can be visual), detection by firmware log is reliable.

> **Mark split:** RPN calculation (2) + ranking (1) + recommendation F1 (2) + recommendation F2 + justification (2).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on V&V questions — Examiner observations from 2022–2024 papers:**
> 1. **Confusing V with V** — Writing *"validation = building the product right"* is an automatic **zero** for that sub-question. Memorize the correct definitions.
> 2. **Forgetting to round up** the sample size $n$ — the examiner will deduct **1 mark** for $n = 95.08$ instead of $96$.
> 3. **Skipping the finite-population correction** when the question mentions a *lot size* $N$ — this is worth a full **3 marks** in Part B.
> 4. **Omitting the RTM** in the V-Model diagram — examiners want to see the *traceability arrow* between left and right legs.
> 5. **Not citing the standard** (ISO 13485, 21 CFR 820, IEC 62366, ISO 14971) — a 1-mark "professional language" bonus is lost.
> 6. **Conflating "Process Validation" with "Design Validation"** — Process V&V is at *manufacturing* (IQ/OQ/PQ); Design V&V is at *product* level.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Verification = "Are we building the product right?"** (Output vs Input) — ISO 13485 §8.3.4.
- **Validation = "Are we building the right product?"** (Product vs User Need) — ISO 13485 §8.3.5.
- **Testing** is the *mechanism* that generates V&V evidence.
- The **V-Model** is the canonical map: 4 descending specifications ↔ 4 ascending tests.
- **Validation** sits only at the **apex** (User Need) of the V; **Verification** occupies the **3 lower ascending legs** (Unit, Integration, System).
- The **RTM (Requirements Traceability Matrix)** is the regulatory "spine" — must be **100% complete** for submission.
- The four mandatory V&V documents: **Test Plan, Test Protocol, Test Report, RTM**.
- Key standards: **ISO 13485:2016, 21 CFR 820.30, IEC 62304 (SW), IEC 62366-1 (Usability), ISO 14971 (Risk), ISO 14155 (Clinical), IEC 60601-1 (Electrical Safety), ISO 10993 (Biocompatibility), ISO 11607 (Packaging)**.
- Sample size formula: $\displaystyle n = \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^{2}}$; **always round up**.
- Finite-lot correction: $\displaystyle n_{adj} = \frac{n}{1 + (n-1)/N}$.
- RPN = **S × O × D**; threshold decides *bench verification* vs *summative usability validation* vs *clinical investigation*.
- Process Validation = **IQ + OQ + PQ** (per 21 CFR 820.75); separate from Design V&V.
- **Test Coverage** must be **100%** of design inputs for FDA/EU submission.
- **NCR (Non-Conformance Report)** is mandatory for any test failure; re-test only after root-cause closure.
- Class III / implantable devices demand **biostatistical sample-size justification** in their PMA submission.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions of Medical Device Division
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Functions of Medical Device Division

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Medical Device Division (MDD)** is a specialized regulatory wing operating under the **Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO)**, which functions as the apex national regulatory authority for drugs, cosmetics, and medical devices in India. The MDD is headed by the **Drugs Controller General (India) [DCG(I)]** and is primarily responsible for safeguarding public health by ensuring that all medical devices marketed in India meet the prescribed standards of **safety, efficacy, and quality**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBMT861), the Medical Device Division is examined under the regulatory framework that governs classification, registration, import, manufacture, and post-market surveillance of medical devices in India — primarily under the **Medical Devices Rules, 2017** and the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 (Amended)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **Medical Device Division (MDD)** as the **"Quality Gatekeeper"** at a busy international airport. Every medical device (the "passenger") arriving from abroad or being assembled domestically must pass through a strict security checkpoint before it can be released into the "country" (the Indian market).

- The **gatekeeper checks the passport** → verifies the **import license** and **registration certificate**.
- The **gatekeeper scans the luggage** → inspects **manufacturing quality** and **conformity to standards** (ISO 13485, BIS).
- The **gatekeeper monitors the passenger after landing** → conducts **post-market surveillance** and **adverse event reporting**.
- The **gatekeeper keeps a black list** → recalls defective devices, suspends/cancels licenses, and issues **safety alerts**.

Just as a gatekeeper is essential to prevent threats from entering a country, the MDD prevents unsafe, substandard, or non-compliant medical devices from reaching patients, doctors, and hospitals.

### 1.3 Key Regulatory Anchors

The MDD operates under the following statutory and regulatory anchors:

| Anchor | Full Form | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **DCA, 1940** | Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 | Parent legislation |
| **MDR, 2017** | Medical Devices Rules, 2017 | Operational ruleset |
| **CDSCO** | Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation | Apex authority |
| **DCG(I)** | Drugs Controller General (India) | Head of MDD |
| **BIS** | Bureau of Indian Standards | Standards body |
| **NIB** | Notified Body | Certification audits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric / Benchmark:** As per the KTU 2024 scheme, the MDD's regulatory jurisdiction currently covers **37 categories of regulated medical devices** notified under the Medical Devices Rules, 2017, which expanded from the original **15 notified device categories**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Summary Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Functions of the Medical Device Division — Logic Breakdown

The MDD performs **seven (7) primary functions**, each tied to a specific phase of the medical device lifecycle. These are:

#### 2.1.1 Policy Formulation & Regulatory Development
- **Why:** The medical device industry evolves rapidly (AI, IoT, software-as-a-medical-device), and the law must keep pace.
- **How:** The MDD drafts new rules, amendments, and guidelines under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 and the Medical Devices Rules, 2017. It consults stakeholders (industry, clinicians, patients) before notifying changes.

#### 2.1.2 Classification of Medical Devices
- **Why:** Risk determines regulatory scrutiny — a tongue depressor (Class A) cannot be regulated like an implantable pacemaker (Class D).
- **How:** Devices are classified based on the **risk-based approach** into four classes:
  - **Class A** — Low risk (e.g., surgical dressings, tongue depressors)
  - **Class B** — Low-moderate risk (e.g., hypodermic needles, suction equipment)
  - **Class C** — Moderate-high risk (e.g., orthopaedic implants, blood bags)
  - **Class D** — High risk (e.g., heart valves, implantable defibrillators)

> [!NOTE]
> The classification follows a **set of 18 rules** outlined in the **First Schedule of the Medical Devices Rules, 2017**, considering factors like duration of contact with the body, invasiveness, and the part of the body affected.

#### 2.1.3 Registration & Licensing
- **Why:** To ensure only legally authorized entities can manufacture, import, distribute, or sell medical devices.
- **How:** The MDD issues:
  - **Registration Certificates** for foreign manufacturers (import).
  - **Manufacturing Licenses** (Form MD-5 / MD-9) for domestic manufacturers.
  - **Import Licenses** (Form MD-15) for importers.
  - **Loan Licenses** for contract manufacturing arrangements.

#### 2.1.4 Pre-Market Evaluation (Quality & Performance Assessment)
- **Why:** Every regulated device must prove it is safe and effective **before** reaching the patient.
- **How:** The MDD reviews:
  - **Technical documentation** (design, materials, performance testing).
  - **Clinical evaluation reports** (clinical investigation data, literature review).
  - **Conformity to BIS / ISO 13485** quality management standards.
  - For high-risk devices (Class C and D), an on-site audit by a **Notified Body** is required.

#### 2.1.5 Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) & Vigilance
- **Why:** Risks not visible during pre-market evaluation (e.g., rare adverse events, long-term device failures) only emerge after mass deployment.
- **How:** The MDD mandates:
  - **Medical Device Adverse Event Reporting (MADE):** Manufacturers must report adverse events within **15 days** (serious events) and **30 days** (non-serious events).
  - **Field Safety Corrective Actions (FSCA)** for product recalls.
  - **Periodic Safety Update Reports (PSURs)** for Class C and D devices — at least **once a year** for the first two years, then every **two years**.

#### 2.1.6 Enforcement & Compliance
- **Why:** Rules without enforcement are merely suggestions.
- **How:** The MDD conducts:
  - **Audits and inspections** of manufacturing sites.
  - **Suspension or cancellation** of licenses for non-compliance.
  - **Criminal prosecution** under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act (with penalties up to imprisonment and fines).
  - **Import alerts** at port offices for non-conforming shipments.

#### 2.1.7 Coordination with State Drug Controllers & Stakeholders
- **Why:** Medical device regulation is a **Concurrent Subject** (Central + State shared responsibility) in India.
- **How:** The MDD liaises with **State Licensing Authorities (SLAs)** for grant of manufacturing licenses, and with international bodies (IMDRF, WHO) for harmonization of standards.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Summary Table

| Function | Lifecycle Phase | Key Rule / Form | Critical Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| Policy Formulation | Pre-regulation | MDR 2017, Amendments | All device classes |
| Classification | Pre-market | First Schedule (18 rules) | Class A → D |
| Registration & Licensing | Pre-market | Form MD-5, MD-9, MD-15 | Foreign & domestic |
| Pre-Market Evaluation | Pre-market | ISO 13485, Clinical Eval. | Class C & D audit required |
| Post-Market Surveillance | Post-market | MADE, PSUR | 15 days (serious), 30 days (non-serious) |
| Enforcement | Post-market | DCA 1940, Section 18A | Imprisonment + fines |
| Coordination | All phases | Concurrent List | SLAs, IMDRF, WHO |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **biomedical industry**, the MDD's functions directly affect:

- **Product Development Strategy:** Engineers must design devices considering the target **risk class**, which dictates documentation and testing requirements.
- **Quality Management Systems:** Manufacturing facilities must align with **ISO 13485**, the global QMS standard referenced by the MDD.
- **Time-to-Market:** Understanding the **MDD's approval pathways** helps firms plan realistic regulatory timelines.
- **Career Pathways:** Biomedical engineers frequently work in **regulatory affairs departments** interfacing daily with the MDD.
- **Clinical Engineering:** Hospital-based clinical engineers interact with **post-market surveillance** and **adverse event reporting** for in-use devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: The MDD's Decision-Making Workflow

The Medical Device Division follows a structured, step-by-step approach when processing a medical device application. Below is the **analytical workflow matrix** for an **import license application** (the most common case in KTU exam questions):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This walkthrough is exhaustive — every transition from receipt of application to final decision is explicitly mapped.

#### Step 1: Submission of Application
- The importer/authorized agent (Indian representative) submits **Form MD-14** along with prescribed fees and technical dossier to the **MDD at CDSCO (HQ), New Delhi**.

#### Step 2: Preliminary Scrutiny
- The MDD examines the application for **completeness** (all required documents attached).
- If incomplete → **rejection at gate** with reason letter within **30 days**.
- If complete → forward to the **Medical Device Officer (MDO)** for substantive review.

#### Step 3: Risk-Based Classification Verification
- The MDO verifies whether the device is correctly classified.
- If mis-classified (e.g., a Class C device submitted under Class B) → **reclassification request** sent to applicant.

#### Step 4: Technical Dossier Review
- Review of:
  - **Quality Management Certificate (ISO 13485)** of the foreign manufacturer.
  - **Free Sale Certificate (FSC)** from country of origin.
  - **CE Certificate / USFDA 510(k) / PMA** as evidence of safety.
  - **Clinical evaluation and risk analysis (ISO 14971)** documents.
  - **Labeling and IFU (Instructions for Use)** compliance with the MDR 2017 labelling requirements (Sixth Schedule).

#### Step 5: Notified Body Audit (For Class C and D only)
- For high-risk devices, the MDD mandates an audit by an **Indian Notified Body** (e.g., BIS, TUV, Intertek) registered with CDSCO.
- The audit verifies the manufacturer's QMS and production processes.

#### Step 6: Expert Committee Review (if required)
- For **new, novel, or high-risk devices**, the MDD refers the case to a **Subject Expert Committee (SEC)**.
- The SEC reviews clinical data and risk-benefit profile and gives recommendations.

#### Step 7: Grant or Rejection of Import License
- If approved → **Form MD-15 Import License** is granted, valid for a period of **3 years** initially, renewable every **5 years**.
- If rejected → reason communicated in writing; applicant may appeal.

#### Step 8: Post-License Obligations
- The licensee must:
  - **Appoint an Indian Authorised Agent** (for foreign manufacturers).
  - **Maintain a complaint handling system**.
  - **Submit Adverse Event reports** within 15 / 30 days as per MADE guidelines.
  - **Submit PSURs** for Class C and D devices.
  - **Allow unannounced inspections** by CDSCO officers.

### 3.2 Decision Logic Flow (Numerical-Aided Example)

Consider the following **KTU-style analytical question** worked out exhaustively:

> **Question:** A foreign manufacturer applies for an import license for an **implantable cardiac pacemaker** in India. Identify the risk class, applicable forms, audit requirements, and post-market obligations.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Determine the Risk Class**
- The pacemaker is a **Class D (high risk)** device — it is an active implantable medical device, intended to sustain or support life, and classified under the **First Schedule, Rule 11** of the MDR 2017.

**Step 2: Identify the Forms**
- **Form MD-14** — Application for Import License.
- **Form MD-15** — Import License (if approved).
- **Form MD-26** — For registration of foreign manufacturing site (pre-requisite for Class C and D).

**Step 3: Identify the Audit Requirement**
- Since the device is **Class D**, an on-site audit of the **foreign manufacturing facility** by an **Indian Notified Body** is **mandatory**.
- The audit verifies **ISO 13485** compliance and **Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP)**.

**Step 4: Post-Market Obligations**
- **MADE reporting** within **15 days** (serious) / **30 days** (non-serious).
- **PSUR** submission **annually** for first two years, then **every 2 years**.
- **Field Safety Corrective Action (FSCA)** if any defect is detected.
- **Indian Authorised Agent** must be appointed.
- **Labeling** must be in **English** and include UDI (Unique Device Identifier) once implemented.

**Step 5: License Validity**
- Initial license validity: **3 years**.
- Renewal: every **5 years**.

> [!NOTE]
> This structured workflow is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question pattern**. Memorize the risk classes, forms, time-lines, and audit rules.

### 3.3 Key Thresholds & Numbers to Memorize for KTU

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Risk Classes | 4 (A, B, C, D) |
| Rules for Classification | 18 (First Schedule) |
| Serious Adverse Event Reporting | Within **15 days** |
| Non-Serious Adverse Event Reporting | Within **30 days** |
| Initial License Validity | **3 years** |
| Renewal Validity | **5 years** |
| PSUR Frequency (Class C/D) | Annually (first 2 years) → Biennial |
| Notified Body Audit | Mandatory for Class C & D |
| Labelling Language | English |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Functional Architecture of the Medical Device Division

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app[Applicant: Foreign Manufacturer or Indian Importer] --> form[Submission: Form MD-14 / MD-5 / MD-9]
    form --> scrutiny[Preliminary Scrutiny by MDD]
    scrutiny -->|Incomplete| reject1[Rejection with Reason within 30 days]
    scrutiny -->|Complete| classify[Risk Classification Verification: Class A to D]
    classify -->|Misclassified| reclass[Reclassification Request to Applicant]
    classify -->|Correct| tech[Technical Dossier Review: ISO 13485, FSC, Clinical Data]
    tech -->|Class C or D| audit[Notified Body Audit of Manufacturing Site]
    tech -->|Class A or B| sec[Optional SEC Review]
    audit --> sec
    sec -->|Recommended| grant[Grant of License: Form MD-15 or MD-5 or MD-9]
    sec -->|Not Recommended| reject2[Rejection with Written Reason]
    grant --> post[Post-Market Obligations: MADE within 15 to 30 days, PSUR, FSCA]
    post --> monitor[Continuous Monitoring by MDD: Audits, Inspections, Recalls]
    monitor --> enforce[Enforcement: Suspension, Cancellation, Prosecution under DCA 1940]
    enforce --> coord[Coordination with SLAs, IMDRF, WHO]
    coord --> app
```

### 4.2 Lifecycle Mapping: MDD Functions Across the Device Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    policy[Policy Formulation] --> pre[Pre-Market Phase]
    classify[Classification] --> pre
    license[Licensing: Form MD-5 / MD-9 / MD-15] --> pre
    pmeval[Pre-Market Evaluation: ISO 13485, ISO 14971, Clinical Data] --> pre
    pre --> launch[Market Authorization: Device Released to Indian Market]
    launch --> post[Post-Market Phase]
    pms[Post-Market Surveillance: MADE, PSUR] --> post
    fsca[Field Safety Corrective Actions: Recalls, Alerts] --> post
    enforce[Enforcement: Inspections, Suspension, Prosecution] --> post
    post --> renew[Renewal: Every 5 Years]
    renew --> pre
```

### 4.3 Risk-Based Decision Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Class A: Low Risk] --> A1[Self-Declaration: No Audit by NB]
    B[Class B: Low-Moderate Risk] --> B1[State Licensing + ISO 13485 Certificate]
    C[Class C: Moderate-High Risk] --> C1[State Licensing + Notified Body Audit + Clinical Data]
    D[Class D: High Risk] --> D1[Central Licensing by MDD + Notified Body Audit + Comprehensive Clinical Evidence + SEC Review]
    D1 --> D2[Annual PSUR + 15-Day Adverse Event Reporting]
    C1 --> C2[Annual PSUR + 15-Day Adverse Event Reporting]
    B1 --> B3[30-Day Adverse Event Reporting]
    A1 --> A3[Self-Reporting of Adverse Events]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The block diagrams show the **escalation of regulatory scrutiny** as device risk increases. Class A and B are largely handled at the **State level**, while Class C and D require **centralized oversight by the MDD at CDSCO (HQ)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define the Medical Device Division. Mention any two of its primary functions.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The **Medical Device Division (MDD)** is a specialized regulatory wing under the **Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO)**, headed by the **Drugs Controller General (India) [DCG(I)]**. It is responsible for safeguarding public health by ensuring medical devices marketed in India are **safe, effective, and of acceptable quality** under the **Medical Devices Rules, 2017**.

**Two primary functions:**
1. **Risk-Based Classification & Registration** of medical devices into four classes (A, B, C, D) based on the First Schedule of the MDR 2017.
2. **Post-Market Surveillance and Vigilance** through adverse event reporting (MADE) within 15 days (serious) and 30 days (non-serious), and through PSURs for Class C and D devices.

---

#### **Question 2: What is the role of a Notified Body in the regulatory framework of the Medical Device Division?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A **Notified Body** is a third-party conformity assessment organization registered with the **CDSCO** and designated by the MDD to conduct **audits of manufacturing facilities** and verify compliance with the **Medical Devices Rules, 2017** and the relevant **ISO 13485** quality management standards.

For **Class C and Class D devices**, the audit by a Notified Body is **mandatory** before grant of a manufacturing or import license. The Notified Body issues a **certificate of conformity** which is a prerequisite for the MDD's final approval.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the risk-based classification of medical devices as per the Medical Devices Rules, 2017. List all four classes with two examples each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the **post-market surveillance** functions of the Medical Device Division, including the timelines for adverse event reporting and the submission of PSURs. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q. A (a):**

The **Medical Devices Rules, 2017** classify medical devices into **four risk-based classes** as outlined in the **First Schedule (Rules 1 to 18)**. The classification is based on:

- **Invasiveness** (surgical, non-invasive, implantable).
- **Duration of contact with the body** (transient, short-term, long-term).
- **Risk to the patient** (low to high).
- **Whether the device is active** (powered) or **non-active**.

| Class | Risk Level | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Low Risk | Tongue depressors, surgical dressings, alcohol swabs |
| **Class B** | Low-Moderate Risk | Hypodermic needles, suction equipment, infusion sets |
| **Class C** | Moderate-High Risk | Orthopaedic implants, blood bags, anaesthesia machines |
| **Class D** | High Risk | Heart valves, implantable pacemakers, intracranial stents |

The classification determines the **regulatory pathway**, the **level of audit required**, and the **post-market obligations** applicable to the device.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q. A (b):**

**Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)** is the MDD's mechanism to monitor the **real-world performance** of medical devices **after** market release. It includes the following functions:

**1. Medical Device Adverse Event Reporting (MADE):**
- **Serious adverse events** (death, serious injury, hospitalisation) → must be reported within **15 days**.
- **Non-serious adverse events** → must be reported within **30 days**.

**2. Periodic Safety Update Reports (PSURs):**
- Mandatory for **Class C and Class D** devices.
- **Annually** for the first **two years** of marketing.
- Subsequently, **once every two years**.

**3. Field Safety Corrective Actions (FSCA):**
- Includes **product recalls, modifications, and safety alerts** issued by the manufacturer in consultation with the MDD.

**4. Vigilance Inspections:**
- CDSCO officers may conduct **unannounced inspections** of manufacturing sites and warehouses.

**5. Record Keeping:**
- Manufacturers must maintain a **complaint handling file** and **traceability records** for at least **15 years** (or the device lifetime plus 2 years, whichever is longer).

**6. Public Database:**
- The MDD may publish safety alerts and recalls on the **CDSCO website** for public awareness.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Outline the **import licensing procedure** for a Class C medical device under the Medical Device Division. List the forms, audit requirements, and validity periods involved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **enforcement powers** of the Medical Device Division under the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940** and the **Medical Devices Rules, 2017**. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q. B (a):**

The import of a **Class C medical device** into India is regulated under **Chapter VII of the Medical Devices Rules, 2017** and follows the procedure below:

**Step 1:** The foreign manufacturer must obtain a **Registration Certificate** in **Form MD-26** from the MDD for the overseas manufacturing site.

**Step 2:** The **Indian Authorised Agent** or the importer submits an application in **Form MD-14** along with:
- Copy of ISO 13485 certificate.
- Free Sale Certificate from the country of origin.
- CE / USFDA approval (if available).
- Clinical evaluation report.
- Labelling and IFU (Sixth Schedule compliance).

**Step 3:** The MDD verifies the **risk classification** and the technical dossier.

**Step 4:** For a **Class C device**, an **on-site audit** of the foreign manufacturing site by an **Indian Notified Body** is **mandatory**.

**Step 5:** The MDD may refer the case to a **Subject Expert Committee (SEC)** for high-risk or novel devices.

**Step 6:** If approved, an **Import License in Form MD-15** is issued.

**Validity:** Initially **3 years**, renewable for **5 years** thereafter.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q. B (b):**

The MDD derives its enforcement powers from **Section 33** and **Section 18A** of the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940**, read with the **Medical Devices Rules, 2017**. Key powers include:

**1. Suspension and Cancellation of Licenses:** The MDD may suspend or cancel a manufacturing or import license if the licensee violates any provision of the MDR 2017.

**2. Seizure and Confiscation:** CDSCO officers can **seize** non-compliant devices, raw materials, and records.

**3. Prosecution and Penalties:** Violations may attract **imprisonment up to 3 years** (or more for repeat offences) and **fines**.

**4. Import Alerts:** The MDD can issue **port-of-entry alerts** to prevent non-conforming shipments from entering India.

**5. Mandatory Recalls:** The MDD can **order a product recall** if it is found to be unsafe or non-conforming.

**6. Publication of Notices:** The MDD may publish the **name of the offender** in the official gazette or on the CDSCO website.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> A **very common mistake** students make is to confuse the **enforcement powers under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940** with those under the **Medical Devices Rules, 2017**. Remember — the **DCA, 1940 is the parent legislation** that gives **penal teeth** (imprisonment, fines, prosecution), while the **MDR, 2017 is the operational ruleset** that gives **procedural powers** (classification, licensing, PMS). Examiners reward students who clearly distinguish these two layers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- The **Medical Device Division (MDD)** is a wing of the **CDSCO**, headed by the **DCG(I)**.
- The MDD operates under the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940** and the **Medical Devices Rules, 2017**.
- There are **four risk-based classes**: A, B, C, D, determined using **18 classification rules** in the First Schedule.
- **Class A** — Lowest risk, **Class D** — Highest risk.
- **Licenses:** Manufacturing (Form MD-5 / MD-9), Import (Form MD-14, MD-15), Registration (Form MD-26).
- **Notified Body audit** is **mandatory for Class C and D** devices.
- **Adverse event reporting:** Serious — **15 days**; Non-serious — **30 days**.
- **PSURs:** **Annual** (first 2 years) → **Biennial** thereafter (Class C and D).
- **Initial License Validity:** **3 years**; **Renewal:** **5 years**.
- **Labelling Language:** **English**; **Sixth Schedule** compliance mandatory.
- **Record keeping:** **15 years** or device lifetime + 2 years.
- **PMS functions:** MADE, PSUR, FSCA, vigilance inspections, public alerts.
- **Enforcement:** Suspension, cancellation, prosecution, import alerts, recalls.
- **Concurrent subject** — coordination between **MDD (Centre)** and **State Licensing Authorities**.
- **Key standards:** **ISO 13485** (QMS), **ISO 14971** (Risk Management).
- **Career linkages:** Regulatory affairs, clinical engineering, quality assurance, hospital-based biomedical engineering.
- **Exam mantra:** Always **quote the rule and the form number** for full marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering a 14-mark question, structure your answer as — *Definition → Rule → Form → Process → Timeline → Example*. This mirrors the **valuation key pattern** and helps the examiner award full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Registration and Licensing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Registration and Licensing ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Registration and Licensing **Medical Device Registration** is the formal, mandatory procedure by which a manufacturer, importer, or authorized representative submits comprehensive technical, scientific, and administrative documentation to a national or regional Regulatory Authority (RA) to obtain legal authorization for placing a specific medical device on the market of that jurisdiction. **Medical Device Licensing** is the subsequent grant of a legal permit, certificate, or authorization number issued by the Regulatory Authority after satisfactory review of the registration dossier, permitting the device to be manufactured, imported, distributed, sold, and used within the regulatory jurisdiction for the indications and intended purposes stated in the application. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** "Registration" is the *act of filing* the dossier; "Licensing" is the *act of approval* by the competent authority. They are sequential, not synonymous. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology Decoded:** > - **Establishment Registration** = Registering the *company/facility* (who you are). > - **Device Listing** = Listing the *specific products* (what you sell). > - **Premarket Authorization (PMA/510(k)/CE Mark)** = Approving the *device itself* (proof of safety/efficacy). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine you want to open a restaurant in a new city. - **Establishment Registration** is like getting a **Shop & Establishment Act license** — telling the city, "I exist, here is my address, here is my business name." - **Device Listing** is like **publishing your menu** — telling the city health department, "Here are the exact dishes I plan to serve." - **Premarket Licensing (510(k)/CE/MDR)** is like passing the **Food Safety Inspection** — proving that every dish is safe, hygienic, and meets the city's strict food code. You cannot serve a single customer (sell a device) until all three steps are completed, just like you cannot sell food without a license, a menu declaration, and a hygiene certificate. ### 1.3 Why Registration and Licensing Exist The core mandate of any medical device regulatory framework is rooted in the **3Ps Principle**: | Principle | Meaning | Real-World Manifestation | |-----------|---------|--------------------------| | **Patient Safety** | Prevent harm to the end user | Adverse event reporting, post-market surveillance | | **Public Health** | Protect population-level health outcomes | Risk-based classification (Class I → IV) | | **Product Efficacy** | Ensure the device performs as claimed | Clinical evidence, performance testing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Risk Metric:** The **Global Harmonization Task Force (GHTF)** (now **IMDRF** — International Medical Device Regulators Forum) risk-based classification uses four classes: **Class I (Low Risk)**, **Class IIa (Low-Moderate)**, **Class IIb (Moderate-High)**, **Class III (High Risk)** in EU; and **Class I, II, III** in US FDA. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework ### 2.1 The Universal 5-Stage Licensing Lifecycle Every major regulatory regime (US FDA, EU MDR, India CDSCO, Japan PMDA, Health Canada) follows a **5-stage lifecycle** for medical device registration and licensing: 1. **Classification** — Determining the device's risk class (I, IIa, IIb, III or A, B, C, D). 2. **Conformity Assessment** — Demonstrating compliance with Essential Principles / General Controls. 3. **Compilation of Technical Documentation** (Design Dossier, 510(k) submission, STED). 4. **Review by Regulatory Authority / Notified Body** — Scientific and administrative scrutiny. 5. **Issuance of License / Certificate** — Marketing authorization, CE Marking, 510(k) clearance, or import license. ### 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown by Jurisdiction #### A. United States — FDA Framework The **U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA)** operates under the **Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act, 1938)** and the **Medical Device Amendments of 1976**. The Center for Devices and Radiological Health (**CDRH**) is the primary review body. **Three Primary Premarket Pathways:** | Pathway | Risk Class | Evidence Required | Timeline | Outcome | |---------|-----------|------------------|----------|---------| | **Exempt (Class I)** | Low risk | General Controls only | Immediate | Listing only | | **510(k) Premarket Notification** | Class I/II (substantial equivalence) | Substantial Equivalence to a predicate device | **90 days** statutory review | "Clearance" letter | | **PMA (Premarket Approval)** | Class III (high risk) | Full clinical trial data, scientific validity | **180 days** minimum | "Approval" letter | > [!NOTE] > **De Novo Classification (FDA):** A novel low-to-moderate risk device with no predicate is filed as a De Novo request under **section 513(f)(2)** of the FD&C Act, creating a new device classification regulation. **Establishment Registration & Device Listing (21 CFR Part 807):** - All medical device establishments (manufacturers, contract manufacturers, contract sterilizers, specification developers, reprocessors, initial distributors, foreign manufacturers exporting to the US) must register annually. - The **FDA Establishment Registration (FURLS system)** requires Form FDA 2891. - Device Listing is done on Form FDA 2892. #### B. European Union — MDR 2017/745 Framework The **EU Medical Device Regulation 2017/745 (MDR)** replaced the MDD 93/42/EEC on **May 26, 2021**.
