The applicable conformity assessment routes depend on device classification: | Class | Conformity Assessment Route | Notified Body Required? | |-------|----------------------------|-------------------------| | **Class I (non-sterile, no measuring function)** | Self-declaration (Annex II + Annex III) | No | | **Class Is, Im, Ir, IIa, IIb, III** | Annex IX (Quality Management + Technical Documentation Assessment) **or** Annex X (Type Examination) + Annex XI (Production Quality Assurance) | **Yes** | **Key Steps for CE Marking under MDR:** 1. Identify applicable GSPRs (General Safety and Performance Requirements) — formerly "Essential Requirements." 2. Classify the device per **Annex VIII** Rule 1–22. 3. Implement a **Quality Management System (QMS)** per **ISO 13485:2016**. 4. Compile the **Technical Documentation (Annexes II & III)**. 5. Engage a **Notified Body** (for Class IIa, IIb, III). 6. Undergo **Clinical Evaluation** per **Article 61** and **Annex XIV** (or **Annex XV** for Clinical Investigation). 7. Prepare **Declaration of Conformity (Annex IV)**. 8. Affix the **CE Mark** with the Notified Body 4-digit identification number. 9. Register device in **EUDAMED** database (currently in rollout). 10. Appoint an **EU Authorised Representative (EC REP)** if manufacturer is non-EU. #### C. India — CDSCO Framework The **Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO)** under the **Medical Devices Rules, 2017** (notified under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940) governs medical device licensing in India. The **Drugs Controller General of India (DCGI)** is the licensing authority for Class C and D devices. **Licensing Structure in India:** - **Manufacturing License** — Issued by State Licensing Authority (SLA) for Class A & B; by CDSCO for Class C & D. - **Import License (Form MD-15)** — Issued by DCGI for all Class A–D imported devices. - **Loan License** — For contract manufacturing. - **Test License (Form MD-16)** — For clinical investigation or evaluation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Indian Risk Classification (Rule 4, MDR 2017):** > - **Class A** (Low) — e.g., surgical dressings, alcohol swabs. > - **Class B** (Low-Moderate) — e.g., infusion sets, glucometers. > - **Class C** (Moderate-High) — e.g., ventilators, MRI machines. > - **Class D** (High) — e.g., heart valves, stents, implantable pacemakers. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix | Parameter | US FDA | EU MDR | India CDSCO | Japan PMDA | Health Canada | |-----------|--------|--------|-------------|------------|---------------| | **Legislation** | FD&C Act 1938 | Regulation 2017/745 | Medical Devices Rules 2017 | PMD Act 2014 | MDR (SOR/98-282) | | **Risk Classes** | I, II, III | I, IIa, IIb, III | A, B, C, D | I, II, III, IV | I, II, III, IV | | **Premarket Approval** | 510(k) or PMA | CE Mark | Import License MD-15 | Pre-market certification | MDL/ITA | | **Notified Body** | N/A (FDA direct) | Required (IIa+) | N/A (DCGI direct) | N/A (PMDA direct) | Required (Class III/IV) | | **QMS Standard** | 21 CFR 820 / QSR | ISO 13485 | ISO 13485 | JIS Q 13485 / Ordinance 169 | ISO 13485 | | **Terminology** | Clearance (510k) / Approval (PMA) | CE Certificate | License | Certificate | License | | **Validity** | 510(k): No expiry; PMA: Annual report | CE: 5 years (max) | 5 years (renewable) | 3–5 years | 5 years | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility For biomedical engineers, mastery of registration and licensing is essential for: - **Regulatory Affairs (RA) careers** in medical device companies. - **Quality Assurance (QA) roles** implementing ISO 13485 QMS. - **Clinical research roles** designing trials that satisfy regulatory endpoints. - **Design Control** (per 21 CFR 820.30 / ISO 13485:2016 Clause 7.3) to ensure design outputs meet regulatory inputs from day one. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Regulatory Process Walkthroughs ### 3.1 FDA 510(k) Premarket Notification — Step-by-Step Process The **510(k) submission** is governed by **21 CFR Part 807, Subpart E**. The procedure is: **Step 1: Predicate Device Identification** The submitter identifies a legally marketed predicate device, ideally from the same FDA product classification panel and substantially equivalent in: - Intended use - Technological characteristics - Performance specifications **Step 2: User Fee Payment** $$\text{Standard 510(k) Fee}_{2024} \approx \text{USD } 21{,}760$$ The fee is paid via the **User Fee System** (PDUFA — Prescription Drug User Fee Act, extended to devices under MDUFA — Medical Device User Fee Amendments). Small businesses (revenue ≤ USD 100 million) pay a reduced fee of approximately **USD 5,440**. **Step 3: Compilation of the 21 CFR 807.92 Submission Dossier** The 510(k) must contain **20 elements** as per 21 CFR 807.92: $$ \text{510(k) Dossier} = \{E_1, E_2, E_3, \ldots, E_{20}\} $$ Where each $E_i$ is one required element. Critical elements include: - $E_5$ = 510(k) cover letter - $E_7$ = Substantial equivalence comparison table - $E_8$ = Proposed labeling (Indications for Use statement, IFU, package insert) - $E_{10}$ = Comparison of technological characteristics - $E_{11}$ = Performance testing (bench, animal, clinical) - $E_{13}$ = Biocompatibility (per ISO 10993 series) - $E_{14}$ = Software documentation (per FDA Guidance for Software Contained in Medical Devices) **Step 4: eSTAR Submission to CDRH** Modern submissions are made through the **eSTAR (electronic Submission Template and Resource)** portal. Paper submissions are no longer accepted as of **October 1, 2023**. **Step 5: FDA Acceptance Review (15 Calendar Days)** $$ T_{\text{acceptance}} = 15 \text{ calendar days} $$ If refused to accept (RTA), the 90-day clock does not start. The submitter has **180 days** to respond to an RTA. **Step 6: Substantive Review (90 Calendar Days Statutory)** $$ T_{\text{review}} \leq 90 \text{ calendar days} $$ During this period, the lead reviewer may issue an **AI (Additional Information)** request, which stops the clock. **Step 7: Final Decision** Three possible outcomes: - **Substantially Equivalent (SE)** → Device is "cleared" and may be marketed. - **Not Substantially Equivalent (NSE)** → Submitter may file a De Novo request. - **Not Applicable (NA)** → Device requires PMA pathway. **Step 8: Post-Clearance Obligations** - Update **Device Listing (Form FDA 2892)** within 30 days. - Register establishment annually (Oct 1 – Dec 31). - Comply with **Medical Device Reporting (MDR) — 21 CFR Part 803** for adverse events. - Pay **Annual Registration Fee**. ### 3.2 EU MDR CE Marking — Step-by-Step Process **Step 1: Determine Device Classification (Annex VIII, Rules 1–22)** The classification logic uses a rule-based system. A generic flowchart is: $$ \text{Class} = f(\text{Duration}, \text{Invasiveness}, \text{Active}, \text{CNS}, \text{Body Contact}) $$ Where the function $f$ maps combinations to one of: I, IIa, IIb, III. **Step 2: Implement Quality Management System (QMS)** The QMS must comply with **ISO 13485:2016** and Article 10(9) of the MDR. The structure follows: $$ \text{QMS}_{\text{required}} \supseteq \{\text{Doc. Control}, \text{CAPA}, \text{Management Review}, \text{Design Control}, \text{PMS}, \text{USADE}\} $$ **Step 3: Compile Technical Documentation per Annex II & III** Required contents: - Device description and variant identification - Information on design and manufacture - General Safety and Performance Requirements (GSPR) checklist - Risk management file (per **ISO 14971:2019**) - Pre-clinical and clinical evaluation reports - Labeling and Instructions for Use (IFU) in official EU language(s) - **Post-Market Surveillance Plan (PMS Plan)** per Article 84 **Step 4: Clinical Evaluation per Article 61 and Annex XIV** The Clinical Evaluation Report (CER) follows the **MEDDEV 2.7/1 rev. 4** (legacy) or **MDCG 2020-13** (current) guidance and demonstrates: $$ \frac{\text{Benefit}}{\text{Risk}} \geq 1 \quad \text{(Acceptable risk-benefit profile)} $$ **Step 5: Notified Body Conformity Assessment** For Class IIa, IIb, and III devices, the Notified Body issues either: - **EU Technical Documentation Assessment Certificate** (valid max **5 years**), or - **EU Type Examination Certificate** (Annex X) + Production QMS Certificate (Annex XI). **Step 6: Declaration of Conformity (Annex IV)** The legal document signed by the manufacturer's authorized representative declaring compliance with the MDR. **Step 7: EUDAMED Registration** Three mandatory modules: 1. Actor registration (manufacturer, AR, importer, distributor) 2. UDI (Unique Device Identifier) registration 3. Certificate registration **Step 8: CE Mark Affixation** The CE Mark is affixed visibly, legibly, and indelibly per **Article 20**. For Class IIa, IIb, III, and implantable devices, the Notified Body's 4-digit ID number follows the CE mark. ### 3.3 India CDSCO Import License (Form MD-15) — Step-by-Step **Step 1: Determine Device Risk Class (Rule 4)** If the device falls under Class C or D, the **DCGI** is the licensing authority; if Class A or B, the **State Licensing Authority (SLA)** has jurisdiction for manufacturing. **Step 2: Appoint an Indian Authorized Agent (IAA)** $$ \text{For Foreign Manufacturer} \Rightarrow \text{Indian Authorized Agent (IAA) is mandatory} $$ **Step 3: Apply via **SUGAM Portal** (cdsco.gov.in)** Required documents: - Form MD-14 (application for import license) - WHO GMP / ISO 13485 certificate of overseas manufacturing site - Free Sale Certificate (FSC) from country of origin - CE Certificate / 510(k) clearance / PMA approval (where applicable) - Master Formula (for sterile/active devices) - Plant Master File (PMF) and Device Master File (DMF) - Labeling compliant with **Rule 26** and **Schedule III** - Power of Attorney (POA) for IAA - Prescribed fee: **USD 1,000** (Class A/B) or **USD 3,000** (Class C/D) for grant of license, plus **USD 1,000** per device. **Step 4: DCGI Review and Site Inspection** For high-risk (Class C/D) devices, the **Medical Device Officers (MDO)** may conduct a site audit. **Step 5: Issuance of Form MD-15 (Import License)** $$ T_{\text{validity}} = 5 \text{ years from grant date} $$ Renewal application must be submitted **9 months before** expiry to avoid lapsing. ### 3.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Term Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Term | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | |---------------|---------|----------------------| | $T_{510k}$ | FDA 510(k) review time | **90 calendar days** | | $T_{PMA}$ | FDA PMA review time | **180 days** minimum | | $T_{CE}$ | CE certificate validity | **5 years** maximum | | $T_{MD-15}$ | India Import License validity | **5 years** | | $T_{AI}^{stop}$ | AI request clock-stop | Variable (30–180 days) | | $\text{Class}$ | Risk-based classification | I < IIa < IIb < III | | $F_{user}$ | FDA user fee (2024 standard) | **USD 21,760** | | $F_{small}$ | FDA small business fee | **USD 5,440** | | $F_{IN}^{C/D}$ | India Class C/D license fee | **USD 3,000** + USD 1,000/device | | $\text{NB-ID}$ | Notified Body 4-digit code | e.g., 0123 (TÜV SÜD) | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between "Establishment Registration" and "Device Listing" as required by the US FDA under 21 CFR Part 807.* **Model Answer:** Establishment Registration is the process by which a medical device company (manufacturer, contract manufacturer, specification developer, initial distributor, foreign exporter) registers its business entity with the US FDA annually, providing company name, address, ownership, and operations details on **Form FDA 2891**. Device Listing, on the other hand, is the listing of the specific medical devices that a registered establishment commercially distributes in the US, submitted on **Form FDA 2892**. In short, **Establishment Registration identifies "who"** is in the market, while **Device Listing identifies "what"** they are selling. Both are mandatory for all medical device firms under **21 CFR Part 807**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *What is the role of a Notified Body in the European Union MDR 2017/745 framework, and for which device classes is it mandatory?* **Model Answer:** A **Notified Body (NB)** is a third-party conformity assessment organization designated by an EU member state and notified to the European Commission to assess whether medical devices meet the requirements of **Regulation (EU) 2017/745 (MDR)**. It is mandatory for: - All **Class IIa, IIb, and III** devices (including implantable and active devices), - Class **Is** (sterile), **Im** (measuring), and **Ir** (reprocessing) lower-class devices. Class I non-sterile, non-measuring devices can use **self-declaration (Annex II + III)**. The NB issues a CE certificate valid for a maximum of **5 years** and its 4-digit identification number appears next to the CE mark. --- ### 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### **QUESTION A: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]` **(a)** Explain the **510(k) Premarket Notification** process under the US FDA. Discuss the concept of **substantial equivalence** and the statutory review timeline. (7 marks) **(b)** Describe the **Premarket Approval (PMA)** pathway, including the types of evidence required and the difference between 510(k) "clearance" and PMA "approval". (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) 510(k) Premarket Notification Process:** The 510(k) pathway, governed by **21 CFR Part 807, Subpart E**, is the most common FDA submission route for Class I and II devices. 1. **Predicate Identification:** The submitter must identify a legally marketed "predicate device" with the same intended use and substantially equivalent technological characteristics. 2. **Definition of Substantial Equivalence (SE):** Per **Section 513(i) of the FD&C Act**, a new device is SE if it has the same intended use as the predicate, and either: - Same technological characteristics and does not raise new questions of safety and effectiveness, **OR** - Different technological characteristics but the same intended use, no new safety/effectiveness questions, and demonstrated equal or greater effectiveness. 3. **Submission Content (21 CFR 807.92):** Includes 20 elements such as cover letter, SE comparison table, labeling, performance testing, biocompatibility (ISO 10993), and software documentation. 4. **User Fee:** Standard **USD 21,760** (FY 2024); reduced **USD 5,440** for small businesses. 5. **Statutory Review Timeline:** $$T_{\text{510(k) review}} = 90 \text{ calendar days}$$ The clock stops if an Additional Information (AI) request is issued. 6. **Outcomes:** SE (cleared), NSE (De Novo or PMA required), or NA. **(b) Premarket Approval (PMA) Process:** The PMA pathway, governed by **21 CFR Part 814**, is the most stringent FDA review and is required for **Class III high-risk devices** (e.g., heart valves, implantable pacemakers, deep brain stimulators). 1. **Required Evidence:** - **Preclinical data:** Bench testing, biocompatibility, animal studies. - **Manufacturing information:** Detailed description of facilities, equipment, processes, and Quality System Regulation (**21 CFR Part 820 / QSR**). - **Clinical investigation data:** At least one adequate and well-controlled clinical trial under an **Investigational Device Exemption (IDE, 21 CFR Part 812)**. - **Labeling, statistical analyses, and risk-benefit assessment.** 2. **Review Timeline:** $$T_{\text{PMA review}} \geq 180 \text{ days}$$ 3. **Difference between 510(k) "Clearance" and PMA "Approval":** | Aspect | 510(k) Clearance | PMA Approval | |--------|------------------|--------------| | Outcome wording | "Substantially Equivalent" | "Approved for marketing" | | Evidence level | Predicate comparison | Independent scientific evidence | | Clinical data | Often not required (in 86% cases) | Mandatory pivotal trial | | Risk class | I, II | III | | Statutory basis | Section 510(k) FD&C Act | Section 515 FD&C Act | | Post-market | General Controls | General Controls + Post-approval studies (PAS) | --- #### **QUESTION B: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]` **(a)** Discuss the **risk-based classification** of medical devices in the EU MDR 2017/745 and explain the conformity assessment routes for **Class IIa, IIb, and III** devices. (7 marks) **(b)** Explain the **Indian CDSCO Medical Devices Rules 2017** framework for granting an import license. Discuss the role of the Indian Authorized Agent and the validity period. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) EU MDR Risk Classification and Conformity Assessment:** **Annex VIII of EU MDR 2017/745** uses a rule-based classification system across four classes: - **Class I (Low risk):** Non-invasive devices like stethoscopes, wheelchairs, examination gloves. - **Class IIa (Low-moderate):** Short-term invasive devices, surgical clamps, infusion pumps (< 24 hr contact). - **Class IIb (Moderate-high):** Long-term invasive, active devices that administer medicines, blood bags. - **Class III (High risk):** Implantable devices, devices in contact with the central nervous system or heart, devices incorporating a medicinal substance, breast implants. **Conformity Assessment Routes (Annex IX, X, XI):** For **Class IIa, IIb, and III devices**, the manufacturer has two main options: **Route 1: Annex IX (QMS + Technical Documentation Assessment)** - Implementation of QMS per **ISO 13485:2016**. - Notified Body audits the QMS and reviews the Technical Documentation (Annex II & III). - Suitable for all classes IIa, IIb, III. **Route 2: Annex X (Type Examination) + Annex XI (Production Quality Assurance)** - **Type Examination** is the type-testing of a representative sample. - **Production QMS** ensures ongoing production conformity. For **Class IIa**: Annex IX is the standard route; for sterile or measuring Class I, a Notified Body is required for the relevant aspect. **(b) CDSCO Import License Process:** Under the **Medical Devices Rules 2017** (notified under the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act 1940**), the **Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO)** headed by the **Drugs Controller General of India (DCGI)** issues import licenses in **Form MD-15**. **Key Requirements:** 1. **Indian Authorized Agent (IAA):** A foreign manufacturer must appoint an IAA — a person or company established in India with a valid manufacturing or wholesale license — who is responsible for regulatory compliance, communication with CDSCO, and adverse event reporting on behalf of the foreign manufacturer. 2. **Application:** Filed via the **SUGAM online portal** in **Form MD-14** with required attachments: ISO 13485 certificate, Free Sale Certificate, CE Mark / 510(k) clearance, Plant Master File (PMF), Device Master File (DMF), labeling per **Rule 26** and **Schedule III**, and the prescribed fee. 3. **Fees:** **USD 3,000** (Class C/D) or **USD 1,000** (Class A/B) per manufacturing site, plus **USD 1,000** per device. 4. **Review and Audit:** DCGI reviews the dossier; for high-risk (Class C/D) devices, a **Medical Device Officer (MDO)** site audit may be conducted. 5. **Issuance:** Form MD-15 issued upon satisfactory review. 6. **Validity:** The license is valid for **5 years** from the date of grant. Renewal application must be filed **9 months before expiry** (Rule 36). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > 1. Do **not** confuse "clearance" (510(k)) with "approval" (PMA) — the FDA uses these terms deliberately with distinct regulatory weight. > 2. Many students write "Notified Body is required for ALL EU devices" — wrong! Class I non-sterile, non-measuring devices can use **self-declaration**. > 3. The 510(k) clock is **90 calendar days**, not 90 working days. Including AI stoppages, total time is often 6–9 months. > 4. The Indian Form MD-15 is the **import** license, not the manufacturing license (which is Form MD-5 for Class C/D and MD-6 for Class A/B). Mixing these is a common error. > 5. Always mention the **current regulatory year** in answers — e.g., the EU MDR replaced MDD 93/42/EEC on **26 May 2021**; UK MHRA uses UKCA marking post-Brexit. --- ### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - **Registration** = the act of filing; **Licensing** = the act of approval. They are sequential regulatory events. - **Establishment Registration** = the company; **Device Listing** = the products; **Premarket Authorization** = proof of safety and efficacy. - **US FDA** uses two main premarket routes: **510(k) clearance** (substantial equivalence, 90 days) and **PMA approval** (Class III, ≥ 180 days). **De Novo** is for novel low-moderate risk devices. - **EU MDR 2017/745** uses a **4-class system (I, IIa, IIb, III)** with **22 classification rules** in Annex VIII. Notified Body is required from **Class IIa upwards** (and Class Is/Im/Ir). - **CE Marking** requires a Notified Body audit, **Declaration of Conformity (Annex IV)**, and CE Mark with the 4-digit NB ID. - **EU CE certificate validity is 5 years** maximum. - **India CDSCO** operates under **Medical Devices Rules 2017** with **4 classes (A, B, C, D)**; DCGI licenses Class C/D; State Licenses Class A/B. - **Form MD-15** is the **Import License** (5-year validity); **Form MD-14** is the application; **Indian Authorized Agent (IAA)** is mandatory for foreign manufacturers. - **ISO 13485:2016** is the universally accepted QMS standard underpinning all major regulatory submissions. - **21 CFR Part 820 / QSR** governs the US QMS; transitioning to **Quality Management System Regulation (QMSR)** aligned with ISO 13485 in 2024–2026. - **EUDAMED** is the EU's central database for actor, UDI, and certificate registration. - **UDI (Unique Device Identifier)** is mandatory in both US and EU; format: **UDI-DI (device identifier) + UDI-PI (production identifier)**. - **IMDRF** (formerly GHTF) is the global harmonization body driving convergence between US, EU, Japan, Canada, Australia, and emerging markets like Brazil ANVISA and China NMPA.

topics.title: Import Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Import Procedure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Import Procedure** for medical devices refers to the legally mandated, step-by-step regulatory workflow governed by the **Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO)** under the **Medical Devices Rules, 2017** (notified under the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 – DCA, 1940**) and the **Foreign Trade Policy (FTP)** of India, through which a foreign manufacturer or Indian importer brings a medical device into Indian territory for sale, distribution, or use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regulatory Anchor (KTU 2024 High-Yield):**
> All medical devices imported into India **MUST** be licensed by the **Drugs Controller General of India (DCGI)**, who is the central licensing authority appointed under **Rule 3** of the Medical Devices Rules, 2017. Without a valid **Import License (Form MD-15)**, the customs authorities will not clear the consignment.

### Key Terminology Definition Box

| Term | Definition | Governing Rule |
|---|---|---|
| **Applicant** | The person (Indian Importer) who applies for the import license on behalf of the foreign manufacturer | Rule 33, MDR 2017 |
| **Authorized Agent** | An Indian agent appointed by the foreign manufacturer to represent them before the licensing authority | Rule 3(k), MDR 2017 |
| **Import License** | Permission granted by DCGI allowing the import of a specific medical device | Form MD-15 |
| **Registration Certificate** | Certificate issued after scrutiny of the device and overseas manufacturer | Form MD-14 |
| **Medical Device** | Any instrument, apparatus, appliance, implant, material, etc., intended for human/animal use | Section 3(b), DCA 1940 |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Airport Visa Model"**
> 
> Think of importing a medical device into India exactly like a foreign traveler entering a country.
> 
> 1. **The Traveler (Medical Device)** cannot enter without proper papers.
> 2. **The Passport & Visa (Registration Certificate - Form MD-14)** confirms WHO the device is and its safety record.
> 3. **The Entry Permit (Import License - Form MD-15)** is the actual permission to enter.
> 4. **The Sponsor (Indian Authorized Agent)** is the local person taking responsibility for the traveler in the new country.
> 5. **Immigration Officer (DCGI/CDSCO)** checks all documents.
> 6. **Customs Officer (CBIC - Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs)** finally allows the physical entry.
> 
> Just as you cannot board a flight without a visa, you cannot clear customs at Mumbai port without an active MD-15 license. Both documents work together; neither alone is sufficient.

## 1.3 Categorization of Imported Devices (KTU Mandatory)

The import procedure is **risk-stratified**. The Medical Devices Rules, 2017 classify devices into **four classes (A, B, C, D)** based on risk duration and invasiveness:

- **Class A (Low Risk):** e.g., surgical dressings, tongue depressors
- **Class B (Low-Moderate Risk):** e.g., suction equipment, hypodermic needles
- **Class C (Moderate-High Risk):** e.g., orthopedic implants, ventilators
- **Class D (High Risk):** e.g., heart valves, implantable pacemakers, stents

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The higher the class (C or D), the more rigorous the audit of the **overseas manufacturing site** and the more extensive the clinical evaluation data required. Notified devices also need a **Certificate of Pharmaceutical Product (CoPP)** or equivalent from the country of origin.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Regulatory Framework Governing Import

The import of medical devices in India operates under a **multi-agency framework**:

1. **CDSCO (Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation)** – Apex regulatory body under the **Ministry of Health & Family Welfare (MoHFW)**.
2. **DCGI (Drugs Controller General of India)** – Central Licensing Authority.
3. **CLA (Central Licensing Authority)** – Grants MD-14 and MD-15.
4. **SLA (State Licensing Authority)** – Issues license for stock & sale of Class A & B devices.
5. **DGFT (Director General of Foreign Trade)** – Issues Import-Export Code (IEC) under the Foreign Trade (Development & Regulation) Act, 1992.
6. **CBIC (Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs)** – Physical customs clearance at port.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> - **DGFT (Department of Foreign Trade)** gives the **IEC (Import Export Code)**.
> - **CDSCO/DCGI** gives the **MD-15 Import License**.
> - **CBIC Customs** gives **physical clearance** of goods.
> - All three are **mandatory** in sequence for the device to legally enter India.

## 2.2 Two-Stage Documentation Logic (The "Two Key" Principle)

The MDR 2017 introduces a **two-stage compliance model**:

- **Stage 1 – Registration of Overseas Manufacturing Site & Device (Form MD-14):** Confirms the device is safe, effective, and the manufacturing site is GMP-compliant.
- **Stage 2 – Import License for Each Importer (Form MD-15):** Authorizes a specific Indian entity to import a specific registered device for a specific period (**validity 3 years for Class C/D, 5 years for Class A/B**, typically, with facility for periodic retention review).

## 2.3 The Step-by-Step Operational Workflow (Bullet Logic)

### Phase A: Pre-Importation Compliance (Before Device Leaves Country of Origin)

- **Step 1:** The foreign manufacturer appoints an **Authorized Agent in India** (mandatory under Rule 3(k)).
- **Step 2:** The Authorized Agent (or importer) files **Form MD-14** on the **SUGAM online portal** (https://sugam.cdsco.gov.in) for registration of the device and overseas manufacturing site.
- **Step 3:** The application must be accompanied by:
  - **ISO 13485 Certificate** of the overseas manufacturer
  - **CE Marking / USFDA 510(k) / WHO Pre-qualification** evidence
  - **Free Sale Certificate (FSC)** from the country of origin
  - **Plant Master File (PMF)**
  - **Device Master File (DMF)**
  - **Clinical Evaluation Report (CER)** for Class C and D devices
  - **Power of Attorney (PoA)** appointing the Indian agent
- **Step 4:** Pay the **requisite fee** (Class-wise varying).
- **Step 5:** DCGI scrutinizes; on satisfaction, issues **Form MD-14 Registration Certificate** (valid for the device indefinitely unless withdrawn, but the manufacturing site registration is reviewed every 3 years for Class C/D).

### Phase B: Import License Grant (Form MD-15)

- **Step 6:** Importer files **Form MD-15** application for the **Import License**.
- **Step 7:** Submit details of each consignment intent, label compliance to **Schedule III (Labelling Requirements)**, and post-market surveillance plan.
- **Step 8:** Pay prescribed fee (Class-wise, between **USD 1,000 to 50,000** for registration; license fee varies).
- **Step 9:** On approval, **Form MD-15 Import License** is issued.
- **Step 10:** Importer obtains **IEC from DGFT** (10-digit code, mandatory under Foreign Trade Policy).

### Phase C: Customs Clearance & Post-Importation

- **Step 11:** Goods arrive at Indian port. Customs authorities verify **Bill of Entry**, **MD-15 License**, **MD-14 Registration**, **IEC**, **Commercial Invoice**, **Packing List**, **Certificate of Analysis**, and **Shipping/Airway Bill**.
- **Step 12:** CBIC allows physical clearance only after CDSCO's online confirmation on the **SUGAM customs integration module** (since 2018, the **SUGAM-Customs ICEGATE** integration ensures real-time verification).
- **Step 13:** Importer places the device in the market following **Schedule III labelling**, including the **Indian License Number (MD-15 number) printed on every unit label**.
- **Step 14:** **Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)** and **Adverse Event Reporting** as per **Rule 66 of MDR 2017** becomes mandatory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The CDSCO has made the entire import licensing process **fully online** via the **SUGAM portal**, eliminating physical submission. This is part of India's **e-Governance initiative** under the **Digital India Programme** and aligns with **WHO Global Benchmarking Tool (GBT) recommendations**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> The "formula sheet" for regulatory topics is the **timeline and fee matrix**. Memorize this for Part A questions.

| Parameter | Class A | Class B | Class C | Class D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Site Audit by CDSCO?** | No (Desk Review) | No (Desk Review) | Yes (May be required) | Yes (Mandatory) |
| **Application Form for Registration** | MD-14 | MD-14 | MD-14 | MD-14 |
| **Import License Form** | MD-15 | MD-15 | MD-15 | MD-15 |
| **Registration Validity (Site)** | Review at 5 years | Review at 5 years | Review at 3 years | Review at 3 years |
| **License Validity (Importer)** | 5 years | 5 years | 3 years | 3 years |
| **Clinical Data Required?** | Minimal | Limited | Extensive | Comprehensive |
| **Govt. Fee Range (Approx.)** | Lowest | Moderate | High | Highest |

### Formula: Document Compliance Matrix

$$
\text{Total Compliance} = f(\text{MD-14}, \text{MD-15}, \text{IEC}, \text{ISO 13485}, \text{FSC}, \text{PMF}, \text{DMF}, \text{CER}, \text{Schedule III Label})
$$

Where:
- **MD-14** = Overseas manufacturer & device registration
- **MD-15** = Import license for Indian entity
- **IEC** = Importer Exporter Code from DGFT
- **FSC** = Free Sale Certificate
- **PMF** = Plant Master File
- **DMF** = Device Master File
- **CER** = Clinical Evaluation Report

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Mastery of the import procedure is critical for **biomedical engineers** working in multinational corporations (e.g., Medtronic, Johnson & Johnson, Siemens Healthineers) in India, as the **Regulatory Affairs (RA) department** is largely built around this exact workflow. KTU B.Tech (Biomedical) students are placed in these RA roles directly after graduation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The Decision Tree for Import Eligibility

The import procedure follows a **logical decision tree** rather than mathematical derivation. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step logical flow an importer must follow:

### Step 1: Determine Device Classification

$$
\text{If (Invasiveness = High) AND (Duration = Long-term) AND (Criticality = Life-sustaining)} \Rightarrow \text{Class D}
$$

### Step 2: Verify the Device is "Notified" or "Non-Notified"

- The **Ministry of Health & Family Welfare (MoHFW)** issues a **notification** under **Section 3 (b) (iv) of the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940** specifying which devices are regulated as "drugs."
- If the device appears in the **notification list (G.S.R. 1024(E) dated 11.10.2017 and subsequent amendments)**, it requires a full **MD-14 and MD-15** license.
- If the device is **non-notified** (e.g., general surgical instruments not in the list), it does not require an import license, but voluntary registration is encouraged.

### Step 3: Exhaustive Checklist of Documents for Form MD-14

For a Class D device (highest risk), the application must contain:

1. **Covering Letter** in prescribed format
2. **Form MD-14** duly filled
3. **Treasury Challan / Fee Receipt** (Fee $\approx$ **USD 50,000** for Class D for overseas site registration)
4. **Power of Attorney** (notarized + apostilled)
5. **ISO 13485:2016** certificate of the overseas manufacturer
6. **CE Certificate** (issued by a Notified Body under EU MDR 2017/745) **OR** **USFDA 510(k) clearance** **OR** **Japan PMDA approval** (any one of these as evidence of safety/performance)
7. **Free Sale Certificate (FSC)** from the country of origin
8. **Plant Master File (PMF)** containing:
   - Manufacturing process flow
   - List of equipment
   - Quality control procedures
   - Sterilization validation data (if applicable)
   - Environmental monitoring data
   - List of batch release procedures
9. **Device Master File (DMF)** containing:
   - Device description & intended purpose
   - Risk analysis (ISO 14971)
   - Biocompatibility data (ISO 10993)
   - Software documentation (if applicable, IEC 62304)
   - Electrical safety data (IEC 60601-1, if applicable)
   - Shelf life & stability data
10. **Clinical Evaluation Report (CER)** as per **MEDDEV 2.7/1 rev 4** or EU MDR Annex XIV
11. **Label Draft** conforming to **Schedule III** of MDR 2017
12. **Post-Market Surveillance Plan**
13. **Authorized Indian Agent details** (with name, address, registration proof)

### Step 4: Logical Flow of SUGAM Portal Submission

$$
\text{Login to SUGAM} \rightarrow \text{Select "Import" Tab} \rightarrow \text{Choose "MD-14 Registration"} \rightarrow \text{Fill Form Online} \rightarrow \text{Upload Documents (PDF, max 5 MB each)} \rightarrow \text{Pay Fee Online} \rightarrow \text{Submit} \rightarrow \text{Generate Application Number}
$$

After submission, the **CDSCO officer** reviews the dossier:
- **Deficiency Letter (DL)** is issued if documents are incomplete (15-day response window typical).
- **Query Response (QR)** by the applicant.
- If satisfied → **Registration Certificate (Form MD-14) issued**.

### Step 5: Derivation of the Import License Numbering Convention

The MD-15 number follows a structured format:

$$
\text{MD-15 No.} = \text{IMP}/\text{YY}/\text{XXXXX}
$$

Where:
- **IMP** = Import license prefix
- **YY** = Year of issuance
- **XXXXX** = Serial number (5 digits)

This number **must be printed on the label** of every unit sold in India.

### Step 3.6: Customs Clearance Mathematical Logic

The CBIC customs officer checks compliance against the **SUGAM-Customs Integration Module** (ICEGATE). The customs clearance is **approved if and only if**:

$$
\text{Clearance} = \text{TRUE} \iff \big[ \text{MD-14 Valid} \land \text{MD-15 Valid} \land \text{IEC Valid} \land \text{Device Matches Registration} \big]
$$

If any one of the four conditions is FALSE, the customs officer can:
- **Detain the consignment** (Section 11 of the Customs Act, 1962)
- **Re-export** the consignment
- **Confiscate** if non-compliance is severe

## 3.2 Exhaustive Comparative Analytical Matrix: MD-14 vs. MD-15

| Parameter | Form MD-14 (Registration Certificate) | Form MD-15 (Import License) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Register device & overseas manufacturer | Authorize Indian importer to import |
| **Issued To** | Authorized Indian Agent | Indian Importer |
| **Application Fee** | Class-wise, USD 1,000 to 50,000 | Class-wise (lower) |
| **Validity** | Indefinite (subject to retention) | 3 to 5 years (Class-wise) |
| **Renewed By** | Retention application before expiry | Fresh application as new license |
| **Required For** | All notified medical devices | All notified medical devices |
| **Governing Rule** | Rule 33 of MDR 2017 | Rule 34 of MDR 2017 |
| **Sample Retention Fee** | Lower | Periodic |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Import Procedure Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Foreign Manufacturer] --> B[Appoint Indian Authorized Agent]
    B --> C[Prepare Dossier: MD-14 Documents]
    C --> D{Device Notified under DCA 1940?}
    D -- No --> E[No License Required, Voluntary Registration]
    D -- Yes --> F[Login to SUGAM Portal]
    F --> G[Submit Form MD-14 Application]
    G --> H[Upload PMF, DMF, CER, ISO 13485, FSC, CE/USFDA]
    H --> I[Pay Prescribed Fee]
    I --> J{Deficiency Letter Issued?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Respond with Query Reply in 15 Days]
    K --> J
    J -- No --> L[DCGI Reviews Application]
    L --> M{MD-14 Issued?}
    M -- No --> N[Application Rejected, Re-apply]
    M -- Yes --> O[Form MD-14 Registration Certificate Obtained]
    O --> P[File Form MD-15 for Import License]
    P --> Q[Apply for IEC from DGFT]
    Q --> R[All Three Documents Ready: MD-14, MD-15, IEC]
    R --> S[Goods Shipped by Foreign Manufacturer]
    S --> T[Goods Arrive at Indian Port]
    T --> U[Customs Officer Verifies via SUGAM-ICEGATE]
    U --> V{All Documents Valid?}
    V -- No --> W[Detain / Re-export / Confiscate]
    V -- Yes --> X[Physical Clearance Granted by CBIC]
    X --> Y[Device Labeled with MD-15 Number per Schedule III]
    Y --> Z[Device Reaches Market: PMS, AE Reporting Active]
    Z --> AA[Periodic Retention and License Renewal]
```

## 4.2 Agency-Wise Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Regulators["Regulatory & Government Bodies"]
        MOHFW[Ministry of Health and Family Welfare]
        CDSCO[CDSCO - Apex Body]
        DCGI[Drugs Controller General of India - CLA]
        DGFT[Director General of Foreign Trade]
        CBIC[CBIC - Customs Authority]
    end
    
    subgraph Applicants["Applicants and Industry"]
        FM[Foreign Manufacturer]
        IAA[Indian Authorized Agent]
        IMP[Indian Importer / Distributor]
        HOSP[Hospitals and End Users]
    end
    
    subgraph Documents["Key Regulatory Documents"]
        MD14[Form MD-14: Registration Certificate]
        MD15[Form MD-15: Import License]
        IEC[Import Export Code - IEC]
        ISO[ISO 13485 Certificate]
        CE[CE Marking or USFDA Approval]
    end
    
    MOHFW -->|Notifies Devices| CDSCO
    CDSCO --> DCGI
    DCGI -->|Issues| MD14
    DCGI -->|Issues| MD15
    DGFT -->|Issues| IEC
    FM -->|Appoints| IAA
    IAA -->|Files for| MD14
    IMP -->|Files for| MD15
    IMP -->|Obtains| IEC
    FM -->|Provides| ISO
    FM -->|Provides| CE
    IMP -->|Submits all 5 to| CBIC
    CBIC -->|Clears for| HOSP
```

## 4.3 Document Hierarchy Pyramid (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Schedule III Compliant Label - Final Output on Device]
    L2[Form MD-15: Import License - Permission to Import]
    L3[Form MD-14: Registration - Device Identity Verified]
    L4[ISO 13485, CE, USFDA, FSC - Quality Foundation]
    L5[PMF, DMF, CER - Technical Dossier Base]
    L6[Risk Classification Class A, B, C, D - Starting Point]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Q: Define "Authorized Agent" in the context of medical device import under MDR 2017. Why is appointment of an Authorized Agent mandatory for foreign manufacturers?**

**Model Answer:**

An **Authorized Agent**, as defined under **Rule 3(k) of the Medical Devices Rules, 2017**, is a person or entity established in India who has been formally appointed by the **overseas (foreign) manufacturer** through a **Power of Attorney (PoA)** to act on their behalf in India for all regulatory, legal, and post-market obligations.

**Mandatory appointment is required because:**

1. The foreign manufacturer does not have a **domestic legal presence** in India.
2. The Authorized Agent acts as the **single point of contact** for the **DCGI** for license applications, queries, and adverse event reporting.
3. The Authorized Agent assumes **regulatory liability** for the device in the Indian market.
4. It enables efficient **post-market surveillance (PMS)** under **Rule 66 of MDR 2017**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "agent = importer." This is **incorrect**. The Authorized Agent and the Importer can be **different entities**. The Authorized Agent represents the **manufacturer**, while the Importer is the **Indian business entity** purchasing and selling the device.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
**Q: Differentiate between Form MD-14 and Form MD-15. State at least three key differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Form MD-14 | Form MD-15 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Purpose** | Registration of overseas manufacturer and the medical device | Import license granted to the Indian importer |
| 2 | **Issued To** | Authorized Indian Agent of the foreign manufacturer | Indian Importer / Distributor |
| 3 | **Governing Rule** | Rule 33 of MDR 2017 | Rule 34 of MDR 2017 |
| 4 | **Validity** | Indefinite (subject to periodic retention) | 3 to 5 years depending on device class |
| 5 | **Numbering** | MD-14/YYYY/XXXXX | IMP/YY/XXXXX |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q: Explain in detail the step-by-step import procedure for a Class C medical device (e.g., an orthopedic bone plate) into India under the Medical Devices Rules, 2017. Mention all the documents required, the role of various agencies, and the validity of the licenses issued.**

**Model Answer Structure:**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Detailed Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1: Pre-Import Planning by the Foreign Manufacturer**

The foreign manufacturer must:
- Verify the device (bone plate) is **notified** under the **DCA 1940** (yes, orthopedic implants are notified under **G.S.R. 1024(E)**).
- Determine the **class** (bone plate = Class C, as it is an **implantable**, long-term duration, surgical invasive).
- Appoint an **Indian Authorized Agent** through a **notarized and apostilled Power of Attorney**.

**Step 2: Compilation of Regulatory Dossier (MD-14 Application)**

The Authorized Agent compiles and submits the following on the **SUGAM portal**:

- **Form MD-14** application
- **ISO 13485:2016** certificate of the overseas site
- **CE Marking Certificate** (under EU MDR 2017/745, Class IIb/III) or **USFDA 510(k)** clearance
- **Free Sale Certificate (FSC)** from the country of origin (e.g., Germany, USA)
- **Plant Master File (PMF)** detailing manufacturing, sterilization (gamma irradiation, validated per **ISO 11137**), and quality control
- **Device Master File (DMF)** with mechanical testing, biocompatibility (per **ISO 10993**), and **Risk Management File (ISO 14971)**
- **Clinical Evaluation Report (CER)** with bibliography of published clinical data on the bone plate
- **Schedule III compliant label draft** (must include manufacturer name, MD-15 number placeholder, "Rx" symbol, storage conditions, etc.)
- **Fee payment** of approximately **USD 30,000** for Class C registration

**Step 3: DCGI Scrutiny and Registration**

- CDSCO officials review the dossier.
- A **Subject Expert Committee (SEC)** evaluates the clinical data.
- A **site audit** of the overseas manufacturer may be conducted (often for Class C/D).
- On satisfaction, **Form MD-14 Registration Certificate** is issued.

**Step 4: Import License (MD-15) Application**

- The **Indian Importer** (may be the Authorized Agent or a separate entity) files **Form MD-15**.
- Submit proof of Indian business registration (GST, PAN, etc.).
- Pay license fee (approx. **USD 5,000–10,000** for Class C).
- **Form MD-15 Import License** is issued (**validity 3 years**).

**Step 5: Customs Clearance at Port**

- Importer must hold **IEC (Import Export Code)** from **DGFT**.
- At the port (e.g., Mumbai JNPT, Delhi Air Cargo), customs officer verifies:
  - **Bill of Entry**
  - **Commercial Invoice**
  - **Packing List**
  - **Airway Bill / Bill of Lading**
  - **MD-14, MD-15, IEC** (verified online via SUGAM-ICEGATE)
  - **Certificate of Analysis (CoA)** for the consignment
- On verification, **Out-of-Charge (OOC) order** is issued by CBIC.

**Step 6: Post-Importation Compliance**

- Label the device with **MD-15 license number**.
- Establish **Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)** system.
- Report any **adverse events** to **MDMC (Medical Device Adverse Event Monitoring Centre)** within **15 days** under **Rule 66**.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Roles of Various Agencies and Validity

**Agency Roles:**

| Agency | Role in Import Procedure |
|---|---|
| **MoHFW** | Notifies which devices are regulated as "drugs" |
| **CDSCO** | Apex body coordinating regulation |
| **DCGI / CLA** | Issues MD-14 and MD-15 licenses |
| **DGFT** | Issues IEC; regulates overall foreign trade policy |
| **CBIC Customs** | Physical clearance at port; verifies SUGAM-ICEGATE |
| **Authorized Agent** | Represents foreign manufacturer; submits MD-14 |
| **Importer** | Holds MD-15; commercial entity selling in India |

**License Validity Summary:**

$$
\text{Validity}_{\text{MD-15}} = \begin{cases} 5 \text{ years, for Class A and B} \\ 3 \text{ years, for Class C and D} \end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{Retention Review}_{\text{MD-14}} = \begin{cases} \text{Every 5 years, for Class A and B} \\ \text{Every 3 years, for Class C and D} \end{cases}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Past Pattern Style]**

**Q: Discuss the regulatory framework and the role of the SUGAM online portal in streamlining the medical device import procedure in India. What are the advantages of this digital governance initiative, and how does it integrate with customs clearance?**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Regulatory Framework for Import

The import of medical devices in India is governed by a **multi-layered legal framework**:

1. **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 (DCA, 1940)** – The parent legislation.
2. **Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (MDR, 2017)** – Operational rules, Part III covers import.
3. **Foreign Trade (Development & Regulation) Act, 1992** – Governs IEC.
4. **Customs Act, 1962** – Governs physical clearance of goods.
5. **Foreign Trade Policy (FTP)** – Periodic policy document from DGFT.
6. **Information Technology Act, 2000** – Legal validity of online applications (since SUGAM is e-platform).

**Key Authorities:**

- **Central Licensing Authority (CLA) = DCGI** under Rule 3, MDR 2017.
- **State Licensing Authority (SLA)** for stock & sale (not import) of Class A and B.
- **Zonal Offices of CDSCO** for dossier scrutiny.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: SUGAM Portal & Customs Integration

**SUGAM Portal (https://sugam.cdsco.gov.in):**

- Launched in **2015–2016**, fully operational by **2018**.
- Provides a **single-window online platform** for:
  - MD-14 (Registration) applications
  - MD-15 (Import License) applications
  - MD-5, MD-6, MD-9 (Manufacturing Licenses for domestic units)
  - Clinical Investigation applications
  - Post-market Adverse Event reporting

**Features of SUGAM:**

1. **Online application submission** – no physical paperwork required.
2. **Fee payment gateway** – integrated with SBI ePay.
3. **Document upload module** – accepts PDF files up to 5 MB each.
4. **Real-time status tracking** – applicant can monitor application progress.
5. **Deficiency Letter (DL) communication** – digitally issued and replied to.
6. **License generation in PDF** with **Digital Signature Certificate (DSC)** of DCGI – legally valid under **IT Act, 2000**.

**SUGAM-Customs Integration (ICEGATE):**

- **ICEGATE (Indian Customs Electronic Gateway)** is the customs electronic portal.
- **SUGAM-ICEGATE integration** was operationalized in **2018** to enable **real-time verification** of MD-14 and MD-15 at the port.
- Customs officer enters the **Bill of Entry** number into ICEGATE; the system auto-pings SUGAM to verify the license is valid and the device matches the registration.
- This eliminates the need for **physical visit to CDSCO zonal office** to obtain a **No Objection Certificate (NOC)** for customs clearance.

**Advantages of Digital Governance:**

$$
\text{Total Time Saved} = T_{\text{Manual}} - T_{\text{Digital}} \approx 60-90 \text{ days reduction}
$$

Where:
- **$T_{\text{Manual}}$** $\approx$ **180–240 days** (traditional paperwork with multiple visits)
- **$T_{\text{Digital}}$** $\approx$ **90–120 days** (post-SUGAM)

Additional benefits:
- **Transparency:** No middlemen or "agents" at CDSCO office.
- **Audit Trail:** Every action is digitally logged.
- **Geographic Neutrality:** A small company in Kerala can apply as easily as one in Delhi.
- **WHO Compliance:** Aligns with **WHO Global Benchmarking Tool (GBT)** for regulatory system maturity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing DGFT with DCGI** – DGFT issues IEC, not the medical device license.
> 2. **Stating the wrong form number** – Form MD-14 is for registration, MD-15 is for import license. Reversing them costs full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the role of CBIC** – Many students omit the customs clearance step entirely, which is worth 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Missing Schedule III** – Label compliance is mandatory and frequently tested.
> 5. **Writing "FDA approval"** without specifying the country (USFDA) – international context matters.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Module 2 Rapid Recap — Memorize This Block:**

- ✅ **Import procedure is governed by** the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940** + **Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (Part III, Rules 33–36)** + **Foreign Trade Policy**.
- ✅ **Two key forms: MD-14 (Registration) and MD-15 (Import License).**
- ✅ **Indian Authorized Agent is MANDATORY for every foreign manufacturer** (Rule 3(k)).
- ✅ **IEC is required from DGFT** in addition to MD-15.
- ✅ **SUGAM portal** is the **single-window online system** for all import applications.
- ✅ **SUGAM-ICEGATE integration** enables **real-time customs verification** since 2018.
- ✅ **Risk-based classification:** Class A (low) → Class D (high); higher class = stricter audit + clinical data.
- ✅ **License Validity:** MD-15 = **3 years for C/D, 5 years for A/B**.
- ✅ **Site audit by CDSCO is mandatory for Class C and D** devices.
- ✅ **Documents required:** ISO 13485, CE/USFDA, FSC, PMF, DMF, CER, Power of Attorney, Schedule III label.
- ✅ **Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)** is mandatory under **Rule 66 of MDR 2017**.
- ✅ **Adverse Event Reporting** must be done within **15 days** to **MDMC (Medical Device Adverse Event Monitoring Centre)**.
- ✅ **Every imported device label must display the MD-15 license number** as per **Schedule III**.
- ✅ **Customs clearance** is by **CBIC** after **SUGAM-ICEGATE online verification**.
- ✅ **Retention review** for manufacturing site registration is **every 3 years for C/D, every 5 years for A/B**.
- ✅ The entire procedure is **digital, transparent, and aligned with WHO GBT recommendations**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Mnemonic for KTU 2024: "MDR-ICE-SAP"**
> 
> - **M** = **M**D-14 (Registration)
> - **D** = **D**M Device Master File
> - **R** = **R**isk Class A,B,C,D
> - **I** = **I**EC (from DGFT)
> - **C** = **C**E / USFDA approval
> - **E** = **E**mbed MD-15 on label
> - **S** = **S**UGAM portal
> - **A** = **A**pproval via DCGI
> - **P** = **P**MS & Adverse Event Reporting (Rule 66)
> 
> Use this single mnemonic to recall the entire workflow in the exam hall.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approval of New Medical Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Approval of New Medical Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Approval of New Medical Devices** refers to the formal regulatory authorization granted by a competent national or regional health authority (such as the US-FDA, European Commission via Notified Bodies under EU-MDR, India-CDSCO, etc.) that permits a manufacturer to legally market and commercialize a novel medical device within that jurisdiction. This approval is contingent upon demonstrated compliance with safety, efficacy, quality, and performance requirements as mandated by the governing regulatory framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "New medical device approval is a structured, risk-based regulatory pathway involving pre-clinical testing, clinical evaluation, technical documentation review, quality system audits, and post-market surveillance obligations that culminates in a market authorization certificate (e.g., 510(k) clearance, PMA approval, CE Certificate of Conformity, or Indian DCGI/COPP license)."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Driving License" Metaphor:**
> Imagine you invented a new type of vehicle (a flying car). Before you can sell it to the public, a government transport authority (the regulator) must test it for:
> - **Safety** (will it crash and burn?),
> - **Emissions & Environmental impact** (is it clean?),
> - **Driver's license class** (what category of road does it belong to? — this is *classification*).
>
> The authority follows a structured protocol: lab tests → road tests → paperwork review → license issuance. You cannot legally sell even one unit without the license. **Medical device approval is the exact same process**, except the "vehicle" is a device (e.g., a pacemaker, MRI scanner, or stent) and the "road" is the human body.

## 1.3 Key Regulatory Authorities (Jurisdictions)

| Authority | Region | Governing Act/Regulation | Approval Certificate |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FDA** | United States | FD&C Act, 21 CFR | 510(k) Clearance, PMA Approval |
| **European Commission (via Notified Bodies)** | European Union | EU MDR 2017/745 | CE Mark / Certificate of Conformity |
| **CDSCO / DCGI** | India | Medical Device Rules 2017 | Import License / Manufacturing License |
| **PMDA** | Japan | PMD Act | Pre-Market Approval (Shonin) |
| **Health Canada** | Canada | Medical Device Regulations SOR/98-282 | MDL / MDIA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Risk Metrics (Bolded):**
> - **Class I** = Low risk (e.g., bandages, stethoscopes)
> - **Class II** = Moderate risk (e.g., infusion pumps, surgical gloves)
> - **Class III** = High risk (e.g., pacemakers, stents, implantable defibrillators)
> - **Class IV** = Critical / Life-sustaining (e.g., heart valves, certain implants — Canadian/Australian classification)

## 1.4 Fundamental Principles Governing Approval

> [!NOTE]
> The three pillars of medical device approval — universally accepted by all major regulators (FDA, EU, CDSCO):
> 1. **Safety** — The device must not cause unreasonable harm under normal use.
> 2. **Effectiveness / Performance** — The device must perform as intended per the manufacturer's claims.
> 3. **Quality System Compliance** — The manufacturer must operate under a recognized QMS (e.g., **ISO 13485**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Approval Pathway — Structured Logic Steps

The approval of a new medical device follows a **5-stage lifecycle gate** that virtually all major regulators converge upon:

### Stage 1 — Device Classification & Predicate Identification
- Determine the **risk class** (I, II, III) based on intended use, duration of contact with the body, invasiveness, and energy source.
- Identify whether the device has a **predicate device** (substantially equivalent legally marketed device — used in US 510(k) pathway).

### Stage 2 — Pre-Clinical / Bench Testing & Biocompatibility
- **Bench Testing:** Mechanical, electrical, software, and software-in-a-software-out validation.
- **Biocompatibility Evaluation** as per **ISO 10993** series (cytotoxicity, sensitization, irritation, systemic toxicity).
- **Sterility Validation** as per **ISO 11135** (EtO) / **ISO 11137** (Gamma).
- **Packaging & Shelf-life Testing** (Accelerated Aging per **ASTM F1980**).

### Stage 3 — Clinical Evaluation / Clinical Trial
- For Class II/III devices, a **Clinical Evaluation Report (CER)** is mandatory.
- For novel high-risk devices (no predicate), an **Investigational Device Exemption (IDE)** is filed to conduct a **Clinical Investigation** (the device equivalent of a drug clinical trial).

### Stage 4 — Regulatory Submission & Review
- The manufacturer compiles the **Technical File (EU)** or **510(k) / PMA dossier (US)** or **Indian CDSCO dossier**.
- Submission to regulator → Review → Queries → Response → Final Decision.

### Stage 5 — Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) & Vigilance
- **Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF)** for EU MDR.
- **Medical Device Reporting (MDR)** for adverse events in the US.
- **Vigilance Reporting** under Indian MDR 2017.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Stage:**
> The regulatory system is fundamentally a **risk-management engine** (anchored in **ISO 14971**). The progression of stages ensures that risk is reduced "as low as reasonably practicable" (**ALARP** principle) before the device touches a patient, and that risk continues to be monitored afterward.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The "formulas" in Medical Device Regulation are not mathematical but rather **regulatory decision equations** — structured logic gates. They are presented below as a reference cheat sheet.

| Concept | Symbol / Notation | Definition / Equation | Governing Standard / Clause |
|---|---|---|---|
| Risk Classification Logic | $C = f(I, D, C_E, E_S)$ | $C$ = Class; $I$ = Invasiveness; $D$ = Duration; $C_E$ = Criticality of Energy; $E_S$ = Exposure Site | EU MDR Annex VIII; 21 CFR 860 |
| Substantial Equivalence Test | $SE = \{S_1, S_2, S_3\}$ | $S_1$ = Same intended use; $S_2$ = Same technological characteristics; $S_3$ = Different tech but same safety/effectiveness profile | 21 CFR 807.92(a) |
| Biocompatibility Decision Tree | $BC = f(C, T, D)$ | $C$ = Contact; $T$ = Test selection; $D$ = Duration | ISO 10993-1:2018 |
| Risk Acceptability | $R = P \times S$ | $R$ = Risk; $P$ = Probability of Harm; $S$ = Severity of Harm | ISO 14971:2019 |
| ALARP Threshold | $R_{actual} \leq R_{acceptable}$ | Risk must be reduced to "As Low As Reasonably Practicable" | ISO 14971:2019 |
| Clinical Evidence Weight | $CE = \sum (D_i \times L_i \times Q_i)$ | $D_i$ = Data set; $L_i$ = Level (RCT > Cohort > Case Report); $Q_i$ = Quality | MEDDEV 2.7/1 Rev 4, EU MDR Annex XIV |
| Shelf-life Claim | $T_{exp} = T_{acc} \times Q_{10}^{(\Delta T / 10)}$ | $T_{exp}$ = Expected shelf life; $T_{acc}$ = Accelerated age; $Q_{10}$ = Reaction rate factor (typically 2); $\Delta T$ = Temp differential | ASTM F1980 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Caution on Tables:** Notice the use of `\mid` and `\vert` rather than the vertical bar `|` inside math expressions to maintain table integrity.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- For **biomedical engineers**, the approval pathway defines the **design controls** that must be baked into the device from the **concept** stage onward (per **21 CFR 820.30 / ISO 13485 Clause 7.3**).
- For **quality/regulatory affairs professionals**, the pathway is the operational blueprint of their entire career.
- For **startups and inventors**, understanding the pathway is essential for **go-to-market strategy** — class I devices can reach market in 6–12 months, while class III PMA devices typically require **3–7 years** and millions of dollars.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Risk Classification Logic (EU MDR Annex VIII)

The EU MDR classification is governed by a rule-based decision system. Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step logical derivation** of the classification for a hypothetical device.

### Worked Example: Classification of an Implantable Cardiac Pacemaker Lead

**Given Information:**
- An insulated lead with electrode, intended to be **surgically implanted** into the heart.
- Connected to a pulse generator, in continuous contact with **central circulatory system** and **cardiac tissue**.
- **Duration of use:** Long-term (>30 days).
- **Energy source:** Connected to an external active energy supply (the pulse generator).

**Step 1: Identify Invasiveness Axis ($I$)**

Per Rule 8 of EU MDR Annex VIII, all surgically invasive devices intended to be **in direct contact with the central circulatory system** or **central nervous system** are classified as **Class III**.

$$
I = \text{"Invasive — Central Circulatory System"}
$$

**Step 2: Identify Duration Axis ($D$)**

$$
D = \text{"Long-term implantable (Continuous use > 30 days)"}
$$

**Step 3: Identify Energy Source Axis ($E_S$)**

The lead itself does not contain an active energy source, but it is an **active device intended to administer medicines or other substances to or remove other substances from the body** is **Rule 22** in Annex VIII. However, pacemakers are governed by **Rule 22 in conjunction with Rule 8**, both leading to Class III.

$$
E_S = \text{"Connected to Active Medical Device"}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Governing Rule**

The pacemaker lead is therefore governed by the **highest applicable rule** under EU MDR (principle: when multiple rules apply, the **higher class prevails**).

$$
C_{final} = \max(C_{Rule8}, C_{Rule22}, \ldots) = \text{Class III}
$$

**Step 5: Conclude Conformity Assessment Route**

For Class III implantable devices, the conformity assessment requires:

$$
\text{Notified Body Audit (Annex IX or X)} + \text{ISO 13485 QMS Audit} + \text{Technical Documentation Assessment}
$$

$$
\text{Outcome: CE Mark issued under EU MDR 2017/745}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: FDA vs. EU MDR vs. Indian CDSCO

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below provides a side-by-side comparison — a high-yield KTU question pattern (often asked as 7 or 14 mark comparison).

| Parameter | US FDA | EU (MDR 2017/745) | India (CDSCO) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Governing Law** | FD&C Act 1938, 21 CFR | EU MDR 2017/745 | Indian Medical Device Rules 2017 |
| **Class System** | Class I, II, III | Class I, IIa, IIb, III | Class A, B, C, D |
| **Low Risk Path** | 510(k) Exempt | Self-declaration (Class I) | Notified body (State Licensing Authority) |
| **Moderate Path** | 510(k) Clearance | Notified Body (Class IIa/IIb) | Central Licensing Authority (CLA) |
| **High Risk Path** | PMA / De Novo | Notified Body (Class III) | Central Licensing Authority (CLA) |
| **Predicate Required?** | Yes (for 510(k)) | No (Clinical Evaluation required) | No (Performance + Clinical data) |
| **Clinical Trial Required?** | For PMA / novel 510(k) | Generally required (PMCF) | Required for Class C/D |
| **Issuing Body** | FDA | Notified Body (e.g., TÜV, BSI) | CDSCO / DCGI / State Drug Controller |
| **Approval Duration (typical)** | 6 months (510k) – 3+ years (PMA) | 3–12 months (with NB) | 6–18 months |
| **Quality System Standard** | 21 CFR 820 (QSR) / QMSR | ISO 13485:2016 | ISO 13485:2016 |

## 3.3 Derivation: Substantial Equivalence Test (FDA 510(k))

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 510(k) pathway accounts for **>80% of FDA device clearances annually**. Understanding its logic is a KTU high-yield topic.

**Step 1: Predicate Identification**

The manufacturer identifies a **legally marketed device** (cleared via 510(k) or pre-amendment device) that has the same intended use.

**Step 2: Apply the SE Test (21 CFR 807.92(a)(3))**

A device is "substantially equivalent" when it has:

$$
SE = S_1 \land (S_2 \lor S_3)
$$

Where:
- $S_1$ = Same intended use as predicate
- $S_2$ = Same technological characteristics (materials, design, energy source)
- $S_3$ = Different technological characteristics, **but** does not raise different questions of safety and effectiveness, and the manufacturer demonstrates that the device is **at least as safe and effective** as the predicate.

**Step 3: FDA Decision Outcomes**

$$
\text{If } SE = \text{True} \rightarrow \text{510(k) Clearance issued}
$$

$$
\text{If } SE = \text{False} \rightarrow \text{Pathway switches to De Novo or PMA}
$$

**Step 4: Special 510(k) Variant**

If changes are made to a manufacturer's **own** cleared device, a **Special 510(k)** can be filed using the **Design Controls** documentation (per 21 CFR 820.30) without full new bench testing.

**Step 5: Abbreviated 510(k) Variant**

A submission relying on **FDA-recognized consensus standards** (e.g., IEC 60601-1, ISO 10993) to demonstrate substantial equivalence. Reduces review burden.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Generic New Medical Device Approval Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Device Concept]) --> Class[Stage 1: Risk Classification]
    Class --> ClassCheck{Is device Class I?}
    ClassCheck -->|Yes| ClassI[Class I Path: Self-Declaration / Registration]
    ClassCheck -->|No| ClassII[Class II Path: 510k or CE Class IIa/IIb]
    Class --> ClassIII[Class III Path: PMA or CE Class III]
    ClassII --> Bench[Stage 2: Pre-Clinical Bench Testing]
    ClassIII --> Bench
    ClassI --> QMS[Stage 2A: QMS Implementation ISO 13485]
    Bench --> Bio[Biocompatibility ISO 10993]
    Bio --> Clin[Stage 3: Clinical Evaluation / Investigation]
    Clin --> Submit[Stage 4: Regulatory Submission]
    QMS --> Submit
    Submit --> Review{Regulatory Review}
    Review -->|Queries| Response[Manufacturer Response]
    Response --> Review
    Review -->|Approved| Approval[Approval Certificate Issued]
    Approval --> PMS[Stage 5: Post-Market Surveillance]
    PMS --> Vigilance[Adverse Event Reporting]
    Vigilance --> PMS
    PMS --> End([Device on Market - Continuously Monitored])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: FDA 510(k) Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NewDev([New Device to Market in US]) --> HasPred{Does a legally marketed predicate exist?}
    HasPred -->|No| DeNovo[De Novo Classification Request]
    HasPred -->|Yes| IntendedUse{Same intended use as predicate?}
    IntendedUse -->|No| NotSE[NOT Substantially Equivalent - File PMA]
    IntendedUse -->|Yes| TechChar{Same technological characteristics?}
    TechChar -->|Yes| SE[Substantially Equivalent - 510k Clearance]
    TechChar -->|No| SafetyCheck{Different tech but same safety and effectiveness?}
    SafetyCheck -->|Yes| SE
    SafetyCheck -->|No| NotSE
    DeNovo --> DeNovoReview{FDA Review of Novel Device}
    DeNovoReview -->|Granted| ClassI_or_II[Class I or II Designation]
    DeNovoReview -->|Denied| NotSE
    ClassI_or_II --> SE
    SE --> Cleared([510k Cleared - May Market in US])
    NotSE --> PMA[Premarket Approval PMA Pathway]
    PMA --> PMAClinical[Clinical Trials Phase 1 to 3]
    PMAClinical --> PMAReview{FDA Scientific Review}
    PMAReview -->|Approved| Cleared
    PMAReview -->|Denied| Reject([Device Cannot be Marketed])
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: EU MDR Conformity Assessment Routes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClassI["Class I - Low Risk"]
        CI1[Self Declaration by Manufacturer]
        CI2[Register with Competent Authority]
    end
    subgraph ClassIIa["Class IIa - Moderate Low Risk"]
        CIIa1[ISO 13485 QMS Audit]
        CIIa2[Technical Documentation Review by Notified Body]
        CIIa3[Annex IX or XI Route]
    end
    subgraph ClassIIb["Class IIb - Moderate High Risk"]
        CIIb1[ISO 13485 QMS Audit]
        CIIb2[Technical Documentation Review]
        CIIb3[Notified Body Type Examination per Annex X]
    end
    subgraph ClassIII["Class III - High Risk Implantable"]
        CIII1[ISO 13485 QMS Audit]
        CIII2[Full Quality Assurance per Annex IX]
        CIII3[Technical Documentation Assessment of Design Dossier]
        CIII4[Clinical Investigation per Annex XV]
        CIII5[Notified Body Certificate + CE Mark]
    end
    ClassI --> ClassIIa
    ClassIIa --> ClassIIb
    ClassIIb --> ClassIII
    CIII5 --> FinalCE([CE Mark Affixed - Free Movement in EU EEA])
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Indian CDSCO Approval Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IndiaStart([Medical Device for Indian Market]) --> IndiaClass{Class?}
    IndiaClass -->|Class A| SLA[State Licensing Authority - SLA]
    IndiaClass -->|Class B| CLA1[Central Licensing Authority - CDSCO]
    IndiaClass -->|Class C| CLA2[Central Licensing Authority - CDSCO]
    IndiaClass -->|Class D| CLA3[Central Licensing Authority - CDSCO with Subject Expert Committee]
    SLA --> Reg[Registration on CDSCO Online Portal sugam]
    CLA1 --> LicenseApp[Application via Form MD 3 or MD 7]
    CLA2 --> LicenseApp
    CLA3 --> LicenseApp
    LicenseApp --> IndiaQMS[ISO 13485 QMS Audit by Notified Body]
    IndiaQMS --> IndiaTech[Technical Documentation Review]
    IndiaTech --> IndiaClinical{Is Clinical Investigation Required?}
    IndiaClinical -->|Yes| CT[Clinical Trial under Indian GCP ICMR Guidelines]
    IndiaClinical -->|No| IndiaReview
    CT --> IndiaReview[SEC and DCGI Review]
    IndiaReview --> IndiaGrant{License Granted?}
    IndiaGrant -->|Yes| IndiaCert[Manufacturing License Form MD 5 or Import License Form MD 15]
    IndiaGrant -->|No| IndiaReject[Query Response or Reject]
    Reg --> IndiaCert
    IndiaCert --> IndiaMarket([Market Authorization in India])
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Substantial Equivalence" as applied to US FDA 510(k) submissions. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Substantial Equivalence (SE) is a regulatory determination under **21 CFR 807.92(a)** by which a new device is compared to a legally marketed **predicate device**. A device is substantially equivalent when it has:
1. The **same intended use** as the predicate, **AND**
2. Either the **same technological characteristics**, **OR** different technological characteristics that do not raise different questions of safety and effectiveness, and the device is demonstrated to be **at least as safe and effective** as the predicate.

A finding of substantial equivalence results in a **510(k) Clearance**, authorizing the manufacturer to market the device in the United States.

### Question 2: List the four risk classes used by India's Medical Device Rules 2017. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The four risk classes under the Indian Medical Device Rules 2017 (aligned with GHTF principles) are:
- **Class A — Low Risk** (e.g., surgical dressings, alcohol swabs)
- **Class B — Low-Moderate Risk** (e.g., suction equipment, infusion sets)
- **Class C — Moderate-High Risk** (e.g., orthopedic implants, ventilators)
- **Class D — High Risk** (e.g., heart valves, implantable defibrillators, stents)

Classes A and B are licensed by the **State Licensing Authority (SLA)**, while Classes C and D are licensed by the **Central Licensing Authority (CLA) — i.e., CDSCO / DCGI**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): FDA Approval Pathways for New Medical Devices `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — Explain the three primary FDA pre-market pathways: 510(k), PMA, and De Novo. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. 510(k) Pre-market Notification (Section 510(k) of FD&C Act):**
- The most common pathway, used for **Class I and II devices** that are **substantially equivalent** to a legally marketed predicate.
- Requires demonstration of **substantial equivalence** (same intended use + same or acceptable different technological characteristics).
- Typically reviewed in **90 days** by FDA; clearance is granted if SE is established.
- Does not require clinical data unless specifically demanded for safety questions.

**2. Premarket Approval (PMA — Section 515 of FD&C Act):**
- The most stringent pathway, used for **Class III high-risk devices** (e.g., heart valves, implantable pacemakers, stents).
- Requires a **comprehensive scientific review** including:
  - Extensive **bench and animal testing**
  - **Clinical investigations** (Phase 1, 2, 3 equivalent)
  - **Manufacturing information** (QSR compliance)
- Decision is based on **reasonable assurance of safety and effectiveness**.
- Review period typically **6 months to 3+ years**.

**3. De Novo Classification Request:**
- For **novel devices** with **no predicate** but with **low-to-moderate risk**.
- Filed after receiving a "Not Substantially Equivalent" (NSE) determination from a 510(k) submission.
- Establishes a **new device classification** that can serve as a future predicate.
- Often used for **innovative Class I/II devices** without a prior 510(k) predicate.

#### Part (b) — Compare 510(k) and PMA pathways across eight parameters. (7 Marks)

| Parameter | 510(k) | PMA |
|---|---|---|
| **Device Class** | Class I, II | Class III |
| **Predicate Required** | Yes | No |
| **Clinical Data Required** | Usually No | Yes — Comprehensive |
| **Review Type** | Administrative + Limited Scientific | Full Scientific Review |
| **Review Timeline** | ~90 days | 6 months – 3+ years |
| **Approval Outcome** | "Clearance" (not "Approval") | "Approval" |
| **User Fee (2024)** | ~$24,000 | ~$540,000 |
| **Best Suited For** | Iterative / incremental devices | Novel, life-sustaining devices |

### Question B (14 Marks): EU MDR 2017/745 Approval Framework for New Medical Devices `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — Describe the EU MDR 2017/745 classification system and the conformity assessment routes. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Classification System (Annex VIII):**
The EU MDR employs a **rule-based** classification with four classes:
- **Class I** (non-sterile, non-measuring, non-surgical) — Low risk
- **Class I (sterile / measuring / surgical)** — Sub-classed
- **Class IIa** — Moderate risk, short-term use (<30 days)
- **Class IIb** — Moderate-high risk, long-term use
- **Class III** — High risk, life-sustaining, implantable, central circulatory contact

**Conformity Assessment Routes:**

**Class I devices:** Manufacturer performs **self-declaration** of conformity per Annex II/III, registers the device, and affixes the CE Mark. No Notified Body involvement.

**Class IIa devices:** Notified Body (NB) audits QMS (**ISO 13485**). The NB also reviews **Technical Documentation** for at least one device per category. Conformity assessment can be per **Annex IX (QMS + Technical Doc Assessment)** or **Annex XI (Production QA)**.

**Class IIb devices:** Same as Class IIa, but the NB must assess the **full Technical Documentation** (not sampled). For implantable and Class III devices, a **Type Examination per Annex X** is also possible.

**Class III devices:** Full **Annex IX QMS audit + Design Dossier Assessment** by NB. A **Clinical Investigation** per **Annex XV** is mandatory. A **Clinical Evaluation Report (CER)** and **Summary of Safety and Clinical Performance (SSCP)** must be published in EUDAMED.

#### Part (b) — Explain the role of a Notified Body and the significance of EUDAMED in the EU MDR. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Notified Body (NB):**
A Notified Body is an **independent, third-party conformity assessment organization** designated by an EU Member State and notified to the European Commission. Examples include **TÜV SÜD, BSI, DEKRA**. Their roles include:
- Auditing the manufacturer's **Quality Management System (ISO 13485)**.
- Reviewing **Technical Documentation / Design Dossiers**.
- Issuing **EU Type Examination Certificates** and **Quality System Certificates**.
- Conducting **surveillance audits** annually and **re-certification audits** every 3 years.
- The NB's identification number (4-digit) appears adjacent to the CE Mark on the device.

**EUDAMED (European Database on Medical Devices):**
EUDAMED is the **centralized EU database** introduced under EU MDR. Its modules include:
- **Actor Registration (Module 1)** — Manufacturers, importers, distributors, Authorised Representatives.
- **UDI/Device Registration (Module 2)** — Unique Device Identification database.
- **Notified Body & Certificates (Module 3)**
- **Clinical Investigations (Module 4)**
- **Vigilance (Module 5)** — Serious Incident Reports and Field Safety Corrective Actions.
- **Post-Market Surveillance (Module 6)**

EUDAMED ensures **transparency**, enables traceability, and supports post-market surveillance across all 27 EU member states.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse **"510(k) Clearance"** with **"PMA Approval"** — they are legally different. The 510(k) does NOT certify that the device is safe and effective; it certifies **substantial equivalence** to a predicate. Valued answer must state this distinction.
> - **Do NOT** state that the **CE Mark** is a "quality mark" — it is a **Declaration of Conformity** to EU regulations. Misusing this terminology costs 1–2 marks.
> - **Do NOT** confuse the **Indian CDSCO licensing authority** (Central vs State) — Class A/B = State, Class C/D = Central. Frequent pitfall.
> - When drawing comparison tables in the exam, **all 6–8 parameters must be filled**; leaving a column blank incurs significant mark deductions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Fire Revision Checklist — Approval of New Medical Devices:**

- ☐ **FDA's three pathways:** 510(k), PMA, De Novo — know risk class association.
- ☐ **510(k) requires a predicate; PMA does not; De Novo is for novel + low-moderate risk.**
- ☐ **Substantial Equivalence = same intended use + same OR safe different technology.**
- ☐ **PMA = most stringent, requires clinical trials, reasonable assurance of safety/effectiveness.**
- ☐ **EU MDR 2017/745 replaced MDD 93/42/EEC and AIMD 90/385/EEC on 26 May 2021.**
- ☐ **EU MDR has 4 classes: I, IIa, IIb, III; classification is rule-based (Annex VIII).**
- ☐ **Notified Body role: independent third-party, 4-digit ID next to CE Mark.**
- ☐ **EUDAMED is the centralized EU database (UDI, vigilance, actor registration).**
