```mermaid
    O5[Objective 5: Ban Adulterated Misbranded Spurious] --> R5[Drugs Testing Laboratories]
    O6[Objective 6: Central Regulatory Authority] --> R6[CDSCO and DCGI]

    R1 --> OUT[Safe Quality Assured Drugs and Cosmetics for Indian Public]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
    R4 --> OUT
    R5 --> OUT
    R6 --> OUT

    style O1 fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style O2 fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style O3 fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style O4 fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style O5 fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style O6 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style OUT fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Comparative Categorization of Offences

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OFF[Drug Offence under D and C Act 1940] --> CAT{Category}
    CAT -->|Quality Issue| ADU[Adulterated Drug Sec 9 9A 9B]
    CAT -->|Labeling Issue| MIS[Misbranded Drug Sec 17]
    CAT -->|Identity Issue| SPU[Spurious Drug Sec 9C]

    ADU --> P1[Penalty Sec 13 Up to 3 years]
    MIS --> P2[Penalty Sec 18 Up to 2 years]
    SPU --> P3[Penalty Sec 27 Up to 7 to 10 years]
    SPU --> P4[Sec 27A Life or Death if causes death]

    style OFF fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style ADU fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style MIS fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style SPU fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style P4 fill:#000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State the primary objective of the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940.**

**Model Answer:**
The primary objective of the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 is to **regulate the import, manufacture, distribution, and sale of drugs and cosmetics** in India. The Act aims to ensure that all drugs and cosmetics available to the public are **safe, effective, and of standard quality**, conforming to the standards prescribed in the Indian Pharmacopoeia. It also aims to prevent the manufacture and sale of **adulterated, misbranded, and spurious drugs** by establishing a licensing and penal framework.

*(Word count: 60 words | 3 marks awarded for: Definition 1M + Purpose 1M + Scope 1M)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Adulterated, Misbranded, and Spurious drugs as defined under the Act.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Adulterated Drug | Misbranded Drug | Spurious Drug |
|--------|------------------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Section** | 9, 9A, 9B | 17 | 9C |
| **Cause** | Contamination or substitution | Wrong/misleading label | Identity fraud / counterfeiting |
| **Example** | Chalk powder in talc | Label claims cure for cancer | Lookalike of a branded drug |

*(3 marks awarded for: Correct sections 1M + Definition difference 1M + Example 1M)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students commonly lose marks by: (1) Writing only definitions without explaining the **regulatory mechanism** (license system, inspectorate), (2) Confusing the **Act (1940) and Rules (1945)**, and (3) Forgetting to mention the **penalty provisions**. Always include **Section numbers** and **the regulatory body** in your answers.

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a) Explain in detail the six main objectives of the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 has six primary objectives:

1. **Regulation of Import:** To regulate the import of drugs and cosmetics through a licensing system administered by the Central Licensing Authority, ensuring only quality products enter the Indian market.

2. **Regulation of Manufacture:** To ensure that drugs are manufactured only in licensed premises that comply with Schedule M (GMP) requirements, under the supervision of competent technical staff.

3. **Regulation of Distribution and Sale:** To control the sale and distribution chain, ensuring drugs are sold only by licensed dealers under the supervision of registered pharmacists.

4. **Quality and Safety Standards:** To mandate compliance with standards laid down in the Indian Pharmacopoeia and other official compendia, ensuring uniform quality across the country.

5. **Prohibition of Adulteration, Misbranding, and Spurious Drugs:** To define these offences (Sections 9, 9C, 17) and impose strict penalties to deter manufacturers from engaging in fraudulent practices.

6. **Establishment of Regulatory Authorities:** To create a structured regulatory framework with the CDSCO at the central level and State Drug Controllers at the state level, supported by testing laboratories like the Central Drugs Laboratory (CDL).

*(7 marks: 1 mark per objective with brief explanation)*

---

**(b) Discuss the role of the Drugs and Cosmetics Rules, 1945 in operationalizing the Act. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Drugs and Cosmetics Rules, 1945 were framed under **Sections 12 and 33** of the Act to provide the procedural and technical backbone for its implementation.

1. **Licensing Framework (Part II):** Prescribes forms, fees, conditions, and procedures for grant of manufacturing and import licenses.

2. **Quality Standards (Schedules):** Contains 26 schedules, of which **Schedule M (GMP)**, **Schedule T (AYUSH GMP)**, **Schedule Y (Clinical Trials)**, and **Schedule D (Cosmetic colors)** are the most important for a biomedical engineer.

3. **Manufacturing and Storage Conditions:** Specifies minimum requirements for premises, equipment, and personnel.

4. **Labeling and Packing Requirements:** Mandates display of batch number, manufacturing date, expiry date, MRP, and statutory warnings on labels.

5. **Sale Restrictions (Rules 65-71):** Prescribes conditions for sale of drugs, including the requirement of a registered pharmacist.

6. **Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (Part VIA):** A modern addition that classifies medical devices into Class A, B, C, D based on risk and establishes the **Medical Device Single Audit Program (MDSAP)** aligned approach. This is **directly relevant to PEBMT861 students**.

7. **Amendments and Updates:** The Rules are amended regularly to keep pace with scientific developments, most recently through the New Drugs and Clinical Trials Rules, 2019.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per major function with example)*

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**(a) Describe the regulatory authorities established under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Act establishes a **three-tier regulatory structure:**

**1. Central Government Level:**
- **Central Drugs Standard Control Organization (CDSCO):** Apex national body for drug regulation.
- **Drugs Controller General of India (DCGI):** Head of CDSCO, responsible for approval of new drugs, clinical trials, and import licenses.
- **Central Drugs Laboratory (CDL), Kasauli:** Apex testing laboratory for drug samples.
- **Indian Pharmacopoeia Commission (IPC), Ghaziabad:** Publishes the Indian Pharmacopoeia.

**2. State Government Level:**
- **State Drug Controllers:** Issue manufacturing and sale licenses for allopathic and AYUSH drugs within their jurisdiction.
- **State Drug Testing Laboratories:** Test drug samples drawn by state inspectors.
- **Drugs Inspectors:** Appointed under Section 21, empowered to inspect premises, draw samples, and initiate prosecution.

**3. Advisory Bodies:**
- **Drugs Technical Advisory Board (DTAB):** Advises Central and State Governments on technical matters.
- **Drugs Consultative Committee (DCC):** Ensures uniformity in administration of the Act across states.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per body with role)*

---

**(b) Explain the penalty provisions of the Act for manufacture of adulterated, misbranded, and spurious drugs. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Act prescribes a **graduated penalty structure** based on the severity of the offence:

| Section | Offence | Punishment |
|---------|---------|------------|
| **Section 13** | Manufacture of adulterated drug | Up to 3 years imprisonment + fine up to ₹5,000 |
| **Section 18** | Manufacture of misbranded drug | Up to 2 years + fine up to ₹2,000 |
| **Section 27** | Manufacture of spurious drug | First offence: up to 7 years; Subsequent: up to 10 years + fine up to ₹10,000 |
| **Section 27A** | Spurious drug causing death/grievous hurt | Imprisonment for life or death penalty (no upper fine limit) |
| **Section 28** | Sale without license | Up to 1 year + fine up to ₹1,000 |

**Key Points to Remember:**
- The punishment reflects the **gravity of harm** to public health.
- For **spurious drugs causing death**, the law allows for the most severe punishment, equating it to culpable homicide.
- Penalties were **enhanced in 2005** (Amendment Act) to act as a stronger deterrent.
- The **Drugs and Cosmetics (Amendment) Act, 2008** introduced stricter labeling norms.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per offence with proper section reference)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Act, 1940** = Primary statute; **Rules, 1945** = Subordinate legislation.
- **Six Objectives:** Import control, Manufacture control, Sale control, Quality standards, Ban on adulteration/misbranding/spurious, Regulatory authorities.
- **Adulterated (Sec 9, 9A, 9B)** = Quality issue; **Misbranded (Sec 17)** = Labeling issue; **Spurious (Sec 9C)** = Identity fraud.
- **Key Schedules:** M (GMP), T (AYUSH GMP), Y (Clinical Trials), H/H1/X (Prescription), D (Cosmetic colors).
- **Apex Body:** CDSCO headed by DCGI; Apex Lab: CDL Kasauli.
- **Harshest Penalty:** Section 27A (life/death) for spurious drugs causing death.
- **Medical Devices Connection:** Devices are regulated under the **Medical Devices Rules, 2017** (Part VIA of D&C Rules 1945) — classified as **Class A (low risk) to Class D (high risk)**.
- **Year Reminders:** 1940 (Act), 1945 (Rules), 1964 (Cosmetics added), 2005 (Penalty enhancement), 2017 (Medical Devices Rules), 2019 (New Drugs & Clinical Trials Rules).
- **Must-Use Terms in Answers:** Schedule M, GMP, CDL, CDSCO, DCGI, DTAB, DCC, IP, MDSAP.
- **Common Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse the Drugs and Cosmetics Act with the **Pharmacy Act, 1948** (which regulates pharmacists, not drugs).
- **Biomedical Relevance:** Any software, implant, diagnostic kit, or device you design as a B.Tech engineer will eventually need compliance with these rules to be legally marketed in India.

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip for KTU 2024:** If a 14-mark question asks about the Act, always structure your answer as: (1) Historical background (2) Six Objectives (3) Key Definitions (4) Regulatory Authorities (5) Schedules (6) Penalties. This 6-point structure fetches full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions of CDSCO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Functions of CDSCO

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO)** is the apex **National Regulatory Authority (NRA)** of India responsible for the regulation, approval, and quality assurance of **drugs, cosmetics, medical devices, and in-vitro diagnostics** in the country. It functions under the **Directorate General of Health Services (DGHS)**, which in turn operates under the **Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW)**, Government of India.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Headquarters:** FDA Bhawan, Kotla Road, New Delhi – 110002
> **Apex Official:** **Drugs Controller General of India (DCGI)**
> **Governing Act:** Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 (D&C Act) and Rules, 1945
> **Amended by:** Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (MDR-2017) for devices

The DCGI is the **statutory head** of CDSCO and is appointed by the **Central Government** to discharge the regulatory functions entrusted by the D&C Act.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine CDSCO as the **"Traffic Police of the Medical World"**:
- Every new drug, vaccine, or medical device entering the Indian market is like a vehicle trying to enter a busy intersection.
- Without a regulator, there would be **chaos, accidents, and deaths** — untested devices could harm patients.
- CDSCO acts as the **traffic controller**, ensuring every "vehicle" (product) has valid "documents" (licenses, safety data, clinical evidence) before being allowed on the "road" (market).
- Just as traffic police coordinate with regional units (state police), CDSCO works in tandem with **State Drug Controllers (SDCs)** to enforce rules across India.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to Remember CDSCO's Identity:**
> **"C-D-S-C-O" → Central Drug Standard Control Organisation**
> *It is the "FDA of India" — but unlike US-FDA, CDSCO primarily functions as a recommendatory and appellate body for state-level enforcement.*

## 1.3 Key Structural Anchors (Bold Highlights)

- **CDSCO is a Central Government body**, not a state body.
- **DCGI is the final approving authority** for new drugs, devices, and clinical trials.
- **MDR-2017 (Medical Devices Rules, 2017)** came into force on **1st January 2018** — a landmark shift in device regulation.
- CDSCO is supported by **Zonal Offices, Sub-Zonal Offices, Port Offices, and Central Drug Testing Laboratories (CDTLs)**.
- The **Central Licencing Approving Authority (CLAA)** for medical devices resides within CDSCO.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Any KTU question that mentions "regulatory body in India" expects the answer **CDSCO**, headed by the **DCGI**, operating under the **D&C Act, 1940**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The 18 Core Functions of CDSCO (Authoritative Classification)

The functions of CDSCO can be logically grouped into **seven functional domains** for clarity. This classification is the KTU-favoured "table-format" answer structure.

### Domain A: Regulatory Approvals (Pre-Market Functions)

1. **Approval of new drugs and clinical trials** — Evaluation of Investigational New Drug (IND) and New Drug Application (NDA) dossiers.
2. **Approval of new medical devices** — Reviewing safety, performance, and clinical evaluation data under MDR-2017.
3. **Granting licenses for import of drugs and devices** — Issued by the **CLAA** under Form MD-15 (import license).
4. **Granting licenses for manufacture of Class C & D devices** — Central licence for high-risk devices.

### Domain B: Standards & Quality Control

5. **Setting standards for drugs, devices, and cosmetics** — Including pharmacopoeial standards (Indian Pharmacopoeia).
6. **Coordination with Indian Pharmacopoeia Commission (IPC)** for quality monographs.
7. **Regulation of medical devices for safety, efficacy, and quality** under **MDR-2017**.

### Domain C: Vigilance & Post-Market Surveillance

8. **Pharmacovigilance** — Operating under the **Pharmacovigilance Programme of India (PvPI)**.
9. **Materiovigilance** — Adverse event reporting for medical devices (via **MvPI**, coordinated by **Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute, Trivandrum**).
10. **Recalls and adverse event management** for faulty devices.

### Domain D: Coordination & Advisory Role

11. **Advisory role to the Central Government** on matters of drug/device policy.
12. **Coordination with State Drug Controllers (SDCs)** for uniform enforcement.
13. **Liaison with WHO, US-FDA, EMA, PMDA (Japan), and other global regulators** for harmonisation.

### Domain E: Testing & Laboratory Oversight

14. **Supervision of Central Drug Testing Laboratories (CDTLs)** — Mumbai, Kolkata, Chennai, Hyderabad, Guwahati, Chandigarh.
15. **Approval of drug/device testing protocols** and analytical methods.

### Domain F: Cosmetics Regulation

16. **Regulation of cosmetics under Schedule S** of the D&C Rules (registration and quality standards).

### Domain G: Special & Strategic Functions

17. **Coordination of the National List of Essential Medicines (NLEM).**
18. **Emergency use authorisation (EUA)** — Exemplified by **COVAXIN, COVISHIELD, and DRDO's 2-DG** during the COVID-19 pandemic.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Table (Formula/Metrics Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Since the topic is regulatory (non-mathematical), the "formula sheet" is replaced by an authoritative **data & metric reference table** — this is the KTU-favoured substitute for engineering subjects.

| **Parameter** | **Specification** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|
| Statutory Authority | Drugs & Cosmetics Act, 1940 + Rules, 1945 | Parent legislation |
| Device-Specific Rules | Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (effective 1 Jan 2018) | Operational framework |
| Apex Official | Drugs Controller General of India (DCGI) | Statutory head |
| Headquarters | FDA Bhawan, New Delhi | Central location |
| Parent Ministry | Ministry of Health & Family Welfare (MoHFW) | Administrative control |
| Administrative Layer | Directorate General of Health Services (DGHS) | Linking authority |
| Medical Device Classes | Class A, B, C, D (Risk-based) | MDR-2017, Rule 4 |
| Import Licence Form | Form MD-15 | Issued by CLAA |
| Manufacture Licence – Class A/B | Form MD-5 (State authority) | Low-medium risk |
| Manufacture Licence – Class C/D | Form MD-9 (Central authority – CDSCO) | High risk |
| Test Licence | Form MD-11 | For clinical/investigational use |
| Clinical Trial Application | Form MD-22 (CTA) / Rule 47 | Mandatory for investigational devices |
| Medical Device Classification Basis | Rule 4, Schedule I (MDR-2017) | Risk + duration + invasiveness |
| Notified Bodies for Class A/B | State Drug Controllers | Licensing & audit |
| Notified Bodies for Class C/D | CDSCO itself (CLAA) | Central licensing |
| Materiovigilance Partner | Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute, Trivandrum | Adverse event reporting |
| Pharmacovigilance Partner | IPC, Ghaziabad (under PvPI) | Drug safety monitoring |
| Timeline – Import Licence | 6–9 months (typical) | Approximate processing |
| Timeline – Manufacture Licence (Class C/D) | 6–12 months | Approximate processing |
| New Drug Approval Committee | New Drugs Advisory Committees (NDAC) + Subject Expert Committees (SEC) | Internal review |
| International Counterparts | US-FDA, EMA (Europe), MHRA (UK), PMDA (Japan), TGA (Australia) | Global harmonisation partners |
| Quality Standards Adopted | ISO 13485, ISO 14971, IEC 60601, Indian Pharmacopoeia | Benchmarking |
| Risk Management Standard | ISO 14971:2019 | Mandatory adoption |
| Quality Management for Devices | ISO 13485:2016 | QMS requirement |
| Global Harmonisation Forum | IMDRF (International Medical Device Regulators Forum) | India is a member |

## 2.3 Why These Functions Matter (Engineering Relevance)

For **B.Tech Biomedical / Medical Electronics students**, the relevance of CDSCO's functions maps directly to:
- **Design Control**: ISO 14971 risk management must be embedded in device design.
- **Design History File (DHF)**: Required to obtain Form MD-9 / MD-15.
- **Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)**: Materiovigilance data feeds back into device iteration.
- **Standards Compliance**: ISO 13485 QMS is a precondition for Class C/D licensing.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** A B.Tech engineer designing a Class C implantable device (e.g., a pacemaker) must know that the final **manufacturing licence** will be granted by **CDSCO (CLAA)**, not the state — this dictates the documentation depth and clinical trial budget.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Logical Derivations

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: How CDSCO Executes Its Functions (Step-by-Step Logic)

This section breaks down the **regulatory execution chain** in exhaustive detail — the kind of structured flow expected in a 14-mark KTU answer.

### Step 1: Classification of the Medical Device (Rule 4, MDR-2017)

The first function CDSCO implicitly executes is **risk-based classification**, which determines the entire regulatory pathway.

**Logical Decision Tree:**

$$
\text{Medical Device} \xrightarrow{\text{Apply Rule 4 criteria}} \text{Class A / B / C / D}
$$

**Step 1.1 — Identify the device's intended use and risk profile.**

**Step 1.2 — Apply the four Rule 4 classification factors:**
- (i) Duration of use (transient, short-term, long-term)
- (ii) Invasiveness (non-invasive, invasive, surgically invasive, implantable)
- (iii) Anatomical contact (skin, mucosa, blood, CNS, vital organs)
- (iv) Energy source (active / non-active)

**Step 1.3 — Assign risk class using the matrix:**

$$
\text{Risk Class} = f(\text{Duration}, \text{Invasiveness}, \text{Contact Site}, \text{Energy})
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Class A = low risk (e.g., surgical gloves, tongue depressors); Class D = highest risk (e.g., heart valves, implantable defibrillators, intraocular lenses).

### Step 2: Determining the Licensing Authority

Once classified, the licensing pathway is determined:

$$
\text{Class A or B} \rightarrow \text{State Drug Controller (SDC)} \rightarrow \text{Form MD-5}
$$

$$
\text{Class C or D} \rightarrow \text{CDSCO (CLAA)} \rightarrow \text{Form MD-9}
$$

$$
\text{Import of any class} \rightarrow \text{CDSCO (CLAA)} \rightarrow \text{Form MD-15}
$$

### Step 3: Dossier Preparation (Technical File)

The manufacturer prepares a **Technical File / Common Submission Dossier (CSD)** containing:

1. **Device description and intended purpose**
2. **Risk management file (per ISO 14971)**
3. **Design verification and validation (V&V) reports**
4. **Biocompatibility data (per ISO 10993)**
5. **Software life cycle documents (per IEC 62304) — if applicable**
6. **Clinical Evaluation Report (CER) — per MEDDEV 2.7/1 or MDR EU equivalent**
7. **Quality Manual (per ISO 13485)**
8. **Labeling and IFU (Instructions for Use)**
9. **Post-Market Surveillance plan**
10. **Declaration of Conformity**

### Step 4: Submission and Review by CDSCO

**Step 4.1 — Applicant submits application** via the **SUGAM portal** (https://sugam.cdsco.gov.in).

**Step 4.2 — Scrutiny by CDSCO officer**: Completeness check (administrative).

**Step 4.3 — Technical review by Subject Expert Committee (SEC)** for Class C/D and new devices.

**Step 4.4 — Audit of manufacturing facility** by CDSCO inspectors (Class C/D only).

**Step 4.5 — Issuance of licence** (Form MD-9 or Form MD-15) or **rejection with reasons**.

### Step 5: Post-Market Obligations (Continuing Functions)

Once the product is in the market, CDSCO's **ongoing functions** kick in:

- **Materiovigilance reporting**: Manufacturer, importer, and hospital must report adverse events.
- **Periodic Safety Update Reports (PSURs)**: Submitted annually for high-risk devices.
- **Field Safety Corrective Actions (FSCA)**: Recalls, modifications, and user notifications.
- **Re-audits and surveillance inspections**: Every 2–3 years for licence retention.

## 3.2 Tabular Analytical Matrix — CDSCO Functions Mapped to Statutes

| **Function** | **Statute / Rule** | **Form Used** | **Authority Exercising** |
|---|---|---|---|
| New drug approval | Rule 122A, D&C Rules | Form 44 | DCGI |
| Clinical trial approval (drugs) | Rule 122DAA | Form CT-04 | DCGI |
| Clinical trial approval (devices) | Rule 47, MDR-2017 | Form MD-22 | DCGI |
| Manufacture licence (Class A/B) | Rule 20, MDR-2017 | Form MD-5 | State Licensing Authority |
| Manufacture licence (Class C/D) | Rule 21, MDR-2017 | Form MD-9 | CLAA (CDSCO) |
| Import licence (devices) | Rule 34, MDR-2017 | Form MD-15 | CLAA (CDSCO) |
| Test licence | Rule 24, MDR-2017 | Form MD-11 | CLAA (CDSCO) |
| Registration of foreign manufacturer | Rule 24A, MDR-2017 | Form MD-13 | CLAA (CDSCO) |
| Authorised Agent (Indian rep.) | Rule 25, MDR-2017 | Mandatory | CDSCO registered |
| Recall of defective device | Rule 75, MDR-2017 | Voluntary/Mandatory | CDSCO supervised |
| Adverse event reporting (devices) | Rule 76, MDR-2017 | Form MD-14 | MvPI / CDSCO |
| PMA / Post-Market Surveillance | Rule 64, MDR-2017 | PSURs | CDSCO monitored |

## 3.3 Derivation of the Classification Logic (Analytical Formulation)

While classification is **not a continuous mathematical function**, it can be expressed as a **deterministic rule-based decision system**:

$$
C = \arg\max_{i \in \{A, B, C, D\}} R_i
$$

where $C$ is the assigned class and $R_i$ is the risk score derived from:

$$
R = w_1 D + w_2 I + w_3 S + w_4 E
$$

Here:
- $D$ = duration score (0 = transient, 1 = short-term, 2 = long-term)
- $I$ = invasiveness score (0 = non-invasive, 1 = invasive, 2 = implantable)
- $S$ = site of contact score (0 = intact skin, 1 = mucosa, 2 = blood/CNS, 3 = vital organ)
- $E$ = energy score (0 = non-active, 1 = active diagnostic, 2 = active therapeutic)
- $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ are rule-weights prescribed in **Schedule I of MDR-2017**

**Class Assignment Thresholds:**

$$
R < 2 \Rightarrow \text{Class A}
$$

$$
2 \leq R < 5 \Rightarrow \text{Class B}
$$

$$
5 \leq R < 8 \Rightarrow \text{Class C}
$$

$$
R \geq 8 \Rightarrow \text{Class D}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **pedagogical reformulation** for KTU exam clarity. The actual classification follows the **deterministic 24-rule matrix in Schedule I of MDR-2017** — students should cite the rule number rather than the mathematical form in the final exam.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: From Concept to Market — The CDSCO Workflow

**Step 1:** Manufacturer identifies the device and its intended use.

**Step 2:** Self-classifies under Rule 4 of MDR-2017 (Class A/B/C/D).

**Step 3:** Establishes a **Quality Management System (QMS)** compliant with **ISO 13485:2016**.

**Step 4:** Conducts **risk management** per **ISO 14971:2019**.

**Step 5:** Performs **biocompatibility testing** (per ISO 10993) if the device contacts the body.

**Step 6:** Conducts **clinical evaluation** using existing clinical data or new clinical investigation.

**Step 7:** Prepares **Technical File / Common Submission Dossier (CSD)**.

**Step 8:** Applies for licence via **SUGAM portal** to the appropriate authority (State for A/B, CDSCO for C/D/Import).

**Step 9:** Pays the applicable **fee** as per **Schedule II of MDR-2017** (e.g., ₹50,000 for Class A manufacture, ₹1,00,000 for Class C/D).

**Step 10:** CDSCO/State authority conducts **technical review and on-site audit**.

**Step 11:** Licence granted (or rejected with written reasons).

**Step 12:** Post-market surveillance (Materiovigilance, PSURs) begins.

**Step 13:** Re-audits every **2–3 years** for licence retention.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CDSCO Organisational Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid

```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Device Approval Pathway (Block Architecture)

```mermaid

    S1[Step 1 Device Identification and Classification Rule 4 MDR 2017] --> S2[Step 2 QMS Establishment ISO 13485]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Risk Management ISO 14971]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Biocompatibility ISO 10993]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Clinical Evaluation CER]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Technical File Preparation]
    S6 --> S7{Step 7 Class Decision}
    S7 -->|Class A or B| P1[State Drug Controller Form MD-5]
    S7 -->|Class C or D| P2[CDSCO CLAA Form MD-9]
    S7 -->|Import| P3[CDSCO CLAA Form MD-15]
    P1 --> R1[Scrutiny and Audit]
    P2 --> R2[Scrutiny and Central Audit]
    P3 --> R3[Scrutiny and Site Inspection]
    R1 --> L1[Licence Issued]
    R2 --> L2[Licence Issued]
    R3 --> L3[Licence Issued]
    L1 --> M1[Post Market Surveillance MvPI]
    L2 --> M2[Post Market Surveillance MvPI]
    L3 --> M3[Post Market Surveillance MvPI]
    M1 --> N1[PSURs and Recalls]
    M2 --> N1
    M3 --> N1
```

## 4.3 Functional Domain Map of CDSCO

```mermaid
graph LR
    F1[Regulatory Approvals] --> F1a[New Drugs] 
    F1 --> F1b[New Devices]
    F1 --> F1c[Clinical Trials]

    F2 --> F2b[ISO Standards Adoption]
    F3[Post Market Vigilance] --> F3a[Pharmacovigilance PvPI]
    F3 --> F3b[Materiovigilance MvPI]
    F3 --> F3c[Recalls]
    F4[Coordination] --> F4a[State Drug Controllers]
    F4 --> F4b[International Agencies WHO FDA EMA]
    F5[Licensing] --> F5a[Manufacture Licence]
    F5 --> F5b[Import Licence]
    F5 --> F5c[Test Licence]
    F6[Cosmetic Regulation] --> F6a[Schedule S]
    F7[Strategic Functions] --> F7a[NLEM]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** All diagrams use **Mermaid syntax** for direct rendering on GitHub, Obsidian, and Notion. The alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `S1`, `P2`, `F1a`) are safe identifiers that comply with Mermaid's parser rules.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define CDSCO. Mention its headquarters and the title of its head.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO)** is the **apex national regulatory authority** of India for drugs, medical devices, cosmetics, and diagnostics. It is headquartered at **FDA Bhawan, Kotla Road, New Delhi – 110002** and functions under the **Directorate General of Health Services (DGHS)**, Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW). The statutory head of CDSCO is the **Drugs Controller General of India (DCGI)**, who is the final approving authority for new drugs, new medical devices, and clinical trials under the **Drugs & Cosmetics Act, 1940 and the Medical Devices Rules, 2017**.

---

### **Q2. List any six functions of CDSCO.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark per point):**

1. **Approval of new drugs and medical devices** in the country.
2. **Approval and monitoring of clinical trials** for drugs and devices.
3. **Granting import and manufacture licences** for high-risk (Class C and D) medical devices.
4. **Setting pharmacopoeial standards** for drugs and devices in coordination with the Indian Pharmacopoeia Commission (IPC).
5. **Coordination with State Drug Controllers** for uniform regulatory enforcement.
6. **Post-market surveillance** through the Pharmacovigilance Programme of India (PvPI) and Materiovigilance Programme of India (MvPI).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module Internal Choice — Answer **ONE** out of two. Each 14-mark question typically carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**"Explain in detail the functions of CDSCO under the Medical Devices Rules, 2017. Discuss the licensing pathway for Class A, B, C, and D medical devices."**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Functions of CDSCO under MDR-2017**

**Model Answer:**

The **Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO)** discharges a comprehensive set of regulatory functions for medical devices under the **Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (MDR-2017)**, which came into force on **1st January 2018**. The major functions are as follows:

**1. Pre-Market Regulatory Functions:**
- **Approval of new medical devices** based on safety, performance, and clinical data.
- **Approval of clinical investigations** of investigational medical devices (Form MD-22).
- **Classification of medical devices** into four risk-based classes (A, B, C, D) under **Rule 4** and **Schedule I** of MDR-2017.

**2. Licensing Functions:**
- **Import Licences (Form MD-15):** All classes of imported devices require an import licence from the **CLAA** under CDSCO.
- **Manufacture Licences (Form MD-9):** High-risk **Class C and D** devices are licensed centrally by **CDSCO**.
- **Manufacture Licences (Form MD-5):** Low-to-moderate risk **Class A and B** devices are licensed by the **State Licensing Authority** under CDSCO coordination.
- **Test Licences (Form MD-11)** for clinical or investigational use.
- **Registration of foreign manufacturers** (Form MD-13).

**3. Standards and Quality Functions:**
- Adoption of international standards: **ISO 13485 (QMS), ISO 14971 (Risk Management), IEC 60601 (Electrical Safety), and ISO 10993 (Biocompatibility).**
- Coordination with **Indian Pharmacopoeia Commission (IPC)** for standards.

**4. Post-Market Surveillance Functions:**
- **Materiovigilance Programme of India (MvPI):** Reporting of adverse events by manufacturers, importers, and hospitals using **Form MD-14**.
- **Recalls and field safety corrective actions (FSCAs):** Supervised by CDSCO under Rule 75.
- **Periodic Safety Update Reports (PSURs):** Annual submission for Class C and D devices.

**5. Coordination and Advisory Functions:**
- Coordination with **State Drug Controllers** for uniform enforcement.
- **Advisory role** to the Central Government on device policy.
- Liaison with **WHO, IMDRF, US-FDA, EMA, PMDA, MHRA** for global harmonisation.

**6. Strategic and Emergency Functions:**
- **Emergency Use Authorisation (EUA)** — granted during pandemics (e.g., COVID-19 vaccines and devices).
- Coordination of the **National List of Essential Medicines (NLEM)**.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Licensing Pathway for Class A, B, C, D Devices**

**Model Answer:**

The licensing pathway under MDR-2017 is determined by the **risk class** of the device, as illustrated below:

**Class A Devices (Low Risk — e.g., surgical gloves, bandages, examination tables):**
- Apply to the **State Licensing Authority (SLA)** via Form MD-1.
- Manufacture licence granted in **Form MD-5**.
- Audit conducted by **State Drug Inspectors**.

**Class B Devices (Low-Moderate Risk — e.g., hypodermic needles, suction pumps):**
- Apply to the **State Licensing Authority (SLA)** via Form MD-1.
- Manufacture licence granted in **Form MD-5**.
- Audit conducted by **State Drug Inspectors** with possible CDSCO oversight.

**Class C Devices (Moderate-High Risk — e.g., blood bags, orthopaedic implants, ventilators):**
- Apply to **CDSCO (CLAA)** via Form MD-2.
- Manufacture licence granted in **Form MD-9**.
- **Central audit** by CDSCO inspectors.

**Class D Devices (High Risk — e.g., heart valves, stents, implantable pacemakers, IOLs):**
- Apply to **CDSCO (CLAA)** via Form MD-2.
- Manufacture licence granted in **Form MD-9**.
- **Central audit** with extensive clinical data review.

**Import of any class (A, B, C, or D):**
- Apply to **CDSCO (CLAA)** via Form MD-14.
- **Import licence** issued in **Form MD-15**.
- Mandatory appointment of an **Authorised Indian Agent**.

**Workflow Summary:**

$$
\text{Classify} \rightarrow \text{Apply to Authority} \rightarrow \text{Audit} \rightarrow \text{Licence Issued} \rightarrow \text{PMS ongoing}
$$

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice): 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**"Discuss the role of CDSCO in post-market surveillance of medical devices. Explain Pharmacovigilance, Materiovigilance, and the recall procedure under MDR-2017."**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: CDSCO's Role in Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)**

**Model Answer:**

Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) is a **continuing function of CDSCO** under **Chapter VIII (Rules 64–76) of MDR-2017**. It ensures that devices already on the market continue to meet safety and performance standards.

**Key PMS Functions of CDSCO:**

1. **Materiovigilance Programme of India (MvPI):** CDSCO operates MvPI in coordination with the **Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology, Trivandrum** (the National Coordination Centre) to monitor adverse events caused by medical devices.
2. **Adverse Event Reporting (Form MD-14):** Manufacturers, importers, and healthcare professionals must report serious adverse events to CDSCO within **15 days** for serious events and **30 days** for non-serious events.
3. **Periodic Safety Update Reports (PSURs):** Manufacturers of Class C and D devices must submit **PSURs annually**.
4. **Field Safety Corrective Actions (FSCAs):** CDSCO supervises recalls, modifications, and user notifications when a device is found defective.
5. **Trend Analysis and Signal Detection:** CDSCO analyses AE data to identify emerging safety signals and take regulatory action.
6. **Re-audits and Inspections:** Periodic surveillance audits are conducted to ensure continued compliance.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Pharmacovigilance, Materiovigilance, and Recall Procedure**

**Model Answer:**

**Pharmacovigilance (Pv):**
- Pharmacovigilance refers to the **science and activities relating to the detection, assessment, understanding, and prevention of adverse drug reactions (ADRs)**.
- CDSCO operates the **Pharmacovigilance Programme of India (PvPI)** through the **Indian Pharmacopoeia Commission (IPC), Ghaziabad**.
- The **National Coordination Centre (NCC)** for PvPI is located at IPC, with **ADR Monitoring Centres (AMCs)** in over 200 hospitals.
- Reports are submitted via the **VigiFlow / VigiBase** system linked to the **WHO Uppsala Monitoring Centre (UMC)** in Sweden.

**Materiovigilance (Mv):**
- Materiovigilance is the **surveillance system for medical devices** to detect adverse events caused by device failure, misuse, or design defects.
- It is governed by the **MvPI**, launched in **2015** by CDSCO in partnership with **Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute (SCTIMST)**.
- Adverse device events are reported using **Form MD-14** (Medical Device Adverse Event Reporting Form).
- Reports are classified as **serious** (death, life-threatening, hospitalisation, permanent impairment) or **non-serious**.

**Recall Procedure under MDR-2017 (Rule 75):**
- A recall is initiated when a device is found to be **defective, unsafe, or non-compliant** with MDR-2017.
- **Classes of Recall:**
  - **Class I Recall:** Reasonable probability of serious adverse health consequences or death (e.g., faulty pacemaker).
  - **Class II Recall:** May cause temporary or medically reversible harm (e.g., defective syringe).
  - **Class III Recall:** Unlikely to cause harm but violates labelling/manufacturing norms.
- **Procedure:**
  1. Manufacturer identifies the defect (voluntary recall) OR CDSCO issues a recall order (mandatory recall).
  2. Manufacturer notifies CDSCO and submits a **recall strategy** including communication, return, and destruction plans.
  3. Public notification issued by CDSCO.
  4. Post-recall effectiveness check by CDSCO.
  5. Submission of **closure report** within 30 days.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **confuse CDSCO with DCGI** — remember, **DCGI is the head, CDSCO is the organisation**.
> - Do NOT say "CDSCO is under the D&C Act 1940 only" — it also operates under **MDR-2017, Cosmetics Rules 2020, and the New Drugs and Clinical Trials Rules 2019 (NDCTR-2019)**.
> - For "licensing authority" questions, always state **"CDSCO via CLAA for Class C/D and imports; State for Class A/B"** — vague answers like "CDSCO grants licences" lose 2–3 marks.
> - Confusing **Pharmacovigilance (drugs) with Materiovigilance (devices)** is a common error. The nodal agency for MvPI is **SCTIMST, Trivandrum** (Kerala is the answer!).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **High-Density Quick-Revision Block — Re-read this before the exam.**

- **CDSCO = Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation** — India's apex national regulatory body.
- **Head = DCGI (Drugs Controller General of India)** — statutory head.
- **Parent Ministry = MoHFW**, under which sits **DGHS**, which houses **CDSCO**.
- **Parent Legislation = Drugs & Cosmetics Act, 1940 + Rules, 1945**.
- **Device-Specific Legislation = Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (MDR-2017)** — effective **1 Jan 2018**.
- **Headquarters = FDA Bhawan, New Delhi**.
- **18 Core Functions** spread across 7 domains: Approvals, Standards, Vigilance, Coordination, Testing, Cosmetics, Strategic.
- **Risk Classification (Rule 4, MDR-2017):** Class A → B → C → D (low to high risk).
- **Licensing Authority:**
  - **Class A & B → State Drug Controller → Form MD-5**
  - **Class C & D → CDSCO (CLAA) → Form MD-9**
  - **Import of any class → CDSCO (CLAA) → Form MD-15**
  - **Test Licence → Form MD-11**
- **Authorised Indian Agent** is mandatory for all foreign manufacturers.
- **Post-Market Tools:** MvPI (devices), PvPI (drugs), PSURs, FSCAs, Recalls (Rule 75).
- **Nodal Institute for Materiovigilance (MvPI) = SCTIMST, Trivandrum, Kerala** — note its strategic importance.
- **Nodal Centre for Pharmacovigilance (PvPI) = IPC, Ghaziabad**.
- **International Memberships:** WHO, IMDRF, ICH, PIC/S (associated).
- **Standards Adopted:** ISO 13485 (QMS), ISO 14971 (Risk Mgmt), ISO 10993 (Biocompatibility), IEC 60601 (Electrical Safety), IEC 62304 (Software).
- **Online Portal:** All applications submitted via **SUGAM** (sugam.cdsco.gov.in).
- **Common Submission Dossier (CSD)** and **Technical File** are the key documentation deliverables.
- **Declaration of Conformity** is mandatory before placing a device on the market.
- **Licence Validity:** Generally 5 years, renewable.
- **Class D example devices:** Heart valves, coronary stents, implantable cardiac pacemakers, IOLs, hip implants.
- **Class A example devices:** Surgical gloves, dressings, examination tables, tongue depressors.
- **Emergency Use Authorisation (EUA)** was widely used during COVID-19 (e.g., COVAXIN, COVISHIELD, DRDO 2-DG).
- **Recall Classes:** Class I (death-risk), Class II (reversible harm), Class III (labelling/manufacturing issue).
- **Form MD-14** is the **Medical Device Adverse Event Reporting Form**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always cite the **Rule number** (e.g., Rule 4 for classification, Rule 75 for recall) and the **Form number** (e.g., Form MD-9) in your answers — this is the single most effective way to score full marks on KTU theory papers, where examiners reward **statutory precision** over generic paraphrasing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions of State Licensing Authorities - Regulations and Guidelines of Medical Devices in WHO and FDA: Organizational Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Functions of State Licensing Authorities - Regulations and Guidelines of Medical Devices in WHO and FDA: Organizational Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 State Licensing Authority (SLA) – Formal Definition

A **State Licensing Authority (SLA)** is a statutorily empowered regulatory body, constituted under the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 (India)** and operating under the administrative umbrella of the **Central Drugs Standard Control Organization (CDSCO)**, that is responsible for the **issuance, suspension, cancellation, and renewal of manufacturing and sale licenses** for notified medical devices within its respective state or union-territory jurisdiction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In India, medical device regulation operates on a **dual licensing architecture** – the **Central Licensing Authority (CLA / CDSCO)** regulates high-risk Class C & D devices and import licenses, while **State Licensing Authorities (SLAs / State Drug Controllers)** regulate the manufacture, sale, and distribution of Class A & B devices and the operational manufacturing premises.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – "The Inspector General of a Hospital Network"

Think of medical device regulation like a **statewide hospital accreditation system**:

- The **WHO (World Health Organization)** is like the **international medical council** that publishes the *rulebook* (global norms, prequalification standards, nomenclature).
- The **US FDA (Food and Drug Administration)** is like a **national health authority** that has its own *fully developed enforcement machinery* (CDRH, ORA, OPAQ) to inspect, recall, and approve.
- The **State Licensing Authority (SLA)** is like the **district hospital inspector** — it goes to the *local manufacturing unit, audits the production line, checks the quality records, and stamps the operating license on the wall*. It reports upward to a central body but operates locally.

This **three-tier model** (Global → National → State) ensures that medical devices are safe, effective, and of consistent quality *wherever* they are produced or used.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Expansion | Governing Body |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| **SLA** | State Licensing Authority | State Drug Controller (India) |
| **CLA** | Central Licensing Authority | CDSCO (India) |
| **WHO** | World Health Organization | UN specialised agency, Geneva |
| **FDA** | Food and Drug Administration | US Department of Health and Human Services |
| **CDRH** | Center for Devices and Radiological Health | FDA sub-center |
| **WHO PQS** | WHO Performance, Quality and Safety | WHO prequalification programme |
| **IMDRF** | International Medical Device Regulators Forum | Voluntary regulatory convergence body |
| **MDR** | Medical Device Regulation | EU 2017/745 |
| **510(k)** | Premarket Notification | US FDA pathway |
| **PMA** | Premarket Approval | US FDA pathway |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** This topic directly maps to **CO3** of PEBMT861 – *“Understand the global and national regulatory frameworks governing medical devices, including the roles of WHO, FDA, and State Licensing Authorities.”*

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters for B.Tech (Biomedical Engineering)

A biomedical engineer who designs a Class B diagnostic device (say, a glucometer) cannot legally sell it in India without:
1. A **manufacturing license from the SLA** of the state where the factory is located.
2. An **import license from CDSCO** if components are sourced abroad.
3. Conformity to **WHO technical specifications** if the device is procured by UN agencies.
4. Clearance from a **national authority like US FDA** if it is to be exported to the United States.

Hence, **understanding the structural hierarchy of regulators is non-negotiable** for industry-ready biomedical engineers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Functions of State Licensing Authorities (SLAs)

Under **Rule 85 of the Medical Devices Rules, 2017** (India) and the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940**, the SLA discharges the following statutory functions:

### 2.1.1 Licensing Functions
- **Grant of Manufacturing License** for Class A and Class B medical devices.
- **Grant of Loan License** to manufacturers using shared infrastructure.
- **Grant of Wholesale/Retail Sale License** for medical devices.
- **Endorsement of additional products** on existing licenses.
- **Renewal of licenses** (typically every 5 years, with retention fee structure).

### 2.1.2 Inspection & Enforcement Functions
- Conducting **periodic Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP) audits** (per **Schedule 5** of MDR-2017 and **WHO TRS 986, Annex 2**).
- **Drawing samples** for quality testing (under Section 22 of the Act).
- **Investigating complaints** of spurious or substandard devices.
- **Issuing stop-sale notices** and recommending suspension.

### 2.1.3 Coordination Functions
- Acting as a **nodal point between industry and CDSCO**.
- **Forwarding applications** for Class C/D device manufacturing to CLA.
- **Liaising with State Pollution Control Boards, Fire Departments, and BIS** for integrated compliance.
- **Reporting adverse events** to the **Materiovigilance Programme of India (MvPI)**.

### 2.1.4 Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) Functions
- Maintaining **state-level vigilance registers** of adverse events.
- **Monitoring Medical Device Adverse Event (MDAE) reports**.
- Coordinating **field safety corrective actions (FSCA)** initiated by manufacturers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU:** SLAs do **NOT** regulate the *intrinsic safety* of the device (this is the manufacturer's obligation under ISO 13485). SLAs verify the **regulatory compliance of the manufacturing establishment and its operational QMS**.

---

## 2.2 WHO – Regulations and Guidelines for Medical Devices

### 2.2.1 Organizational Position
The WHO operates medical device regulation through its **Health Products Policy and Standards Department (HPS)** and the **Regulation and Prequalification Department (RPQ)**.

### 2.2.2 Core WHO Instruments for Medical Devices

| WHO Instrument | Year | Purpose |
|----------------|------|---------|
| **WHO Global Model Regulatory Framework for Medical Devices (GMRF)** | 2014 (rev. 2017) | Provides a model structure for national regulators |
| **WHO Prequalification Programme** | 2001 (expanded to devices in 2010) | UN-level prequalification of in-vitro diagnostics, male circumcision devices, etc. |
| **WHO TRS 1003, Annex 4** | 2017 | WHO GMP for medical devices |
| **WHO Medical Device Nomenclature (EMDN)** | 2021 (replaced GMDN in WHO context) | European Medical Device Nomenclature adopted by WHO |
| **WHO Essential Diagnostics List (EDL)** | 2018, 3rd ed. 2021 | List of priority IVDs for health systems |
| **WHO Technical Specification Series** | Ongoing | Benchmarks for procurement by UN agencies |
| **WHO Global Surveillance and Monitoring System (GSMS)** | 2017 | Tracks substandard and falsified medical products |

### 2.2.3 Key Functions of WHO in Device Regulation
1. **Setting Global Norms and Standards** – publishes model frameworks, technical specifications.
2. **Prequalification** – assesses devices for UN procurement eligibility.
3. **Capacity Building** – trains regulators in low- and middle-income countries.
4. **International Nomenclature** – maintains EMDN mapping.
5. **Surveillance** – operates the GSMS database for substandard/falsified products.
6. **Convening IMDRF and harmonization forums** – though IMDRF is independent, WHO is an observer.

---

## 2.3 US FDA – Organizational Structure

The FDA is headed by the **Commissioner of Food and Drugs**, who reports to the **Secretary of Health and Human Services (HHS)**.

### 2.3.1 Hierarchical Map of FDA (Top-Down)

1. **Commissioner of Food and Drugs** (political appointee)
2. **Deputy Commissioners** (Operations, Policy, etc.)
3. **Centers and Offices** (operational units)

### 2.3.2 Centers Relevant to Medical Devices

| Center/Office | Acronym | Primary Role in Device Regulation |
|---------------|---------|----------------------------------|
| Center for Devices and Radiological Health | **CDRH** | Premarket review, post-market, compliance, classification |
| Office of Regulatory Affairs | **ORA** | Field inspections, recalls, import operations |
| Office of the Commissioner / CDER | **CDER** | Combination products with drug component |
| Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research | **CBER** | Devices involving biologics |
| Office of Combination Products | **OCP** | Jurisdiction assignment |
| Office of Compliance (within CDRH) | — | Recalls, warning letters, MDR reporting |

### 2.3.3 CDRH Sub-Offices (Device-Specific)
- **Office of Device Evaluation (ODE)** – 510(k), PMA, De Novo review
- **Office of Compliance (OC)** – MDRs, recalls, labelling
- **Office of Surveillance and Biometrics (OSB)** – Post-market signal detection
- **Office of Science and Engineering Laboratories (OSEL)** – Lab research
- **Office of Communication and Education (OCE)** – Industry guidance

### 2.3.4 Statutory Basis of FDA
- **Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act, 1938)**
- **Medical Device Amendments (1976)** – introduced Class I/II/III
- **Safe Medical Devices Act (1990)**
- **FDA Modernization Act (FDAMA, 1997)**
- **21st Century Cures Act (2016)**
- **FDA Reauthorization Act (FDARA, 2017)**

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Reference Sheet – Quick Recap Table

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a regulatory/managerial topic, the "formulas" are actually **classification rules, licensing timelines, and statutory references** that KTU examiners commonly test.

| Concept | Key Parameter | KTU-Standard Value/Rule |
|---------|---------------|------------------------|
| Medical Device Risk Classes (India) | GHTF-based | **Class A (lowest) → D (highest)** |
| SLA jurisdiction (India) | Risk-based | **Class A & B** manufacture license |
| CLA jurisdiction (India) | Risk-based | **Class C & D** manufacture license |
| License validity | MDR-2017 Rule 24 | **5 years** (with retention fee) |
| FDA Device Classes | Risk-based | **Class I → III** |
| 510(k) pathway | Substantially Equivalent | **Class I, II, some III** |
| PMA pathway | Safety + Effectiveness | **Class III (high risk)** |
| WHO Prequalification scope | IVDs, priority devices | **~26 product categories** |
| Timeline for SLA to grant license | MDR-2017 | **45 days** (Class A) / **90 days** (Class B) |
| Timeline for CLA | MDR-2017 | **90 days** (Class C) / **180 days** (Class D) |
| WHO Medical Device GMRF | Risk-based | **4-tier model** (Basic → Advanced) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **Indian risk classification (A/B/C/D)** with the **FDA classification (I/II/III)**. They are **NOT equivalent**; the Indian system is **alphabetical (4 classes)** while the FDA is **roman-numeral (3 classes)**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Activity | SLA / WHO / FDA Function Touched |
|----------------------|----------------------------------|
| Designing a blood pressure monitor | ISO 13485 + State license for Class B |
| Sourcing components from China | Import license from CDSCO + WHO prequalification if UN-funded |
| Conducting clinical trials for a stent | FDA PMA pathway + CLA oversight in India |
| Distributing IVD test kits in Africa | WHO Prequalification Programme |
| Setting up a Class D implant factory | CLA (CDSCO HQ) license, not SLA |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough #1: SLA Licensing Decision Flow

Below is the **decision logic** an SLA follows upon receiving a manufacturing license application for a medical device under **MDR-2017**:

> **Step 1.** Applicant submits Form MD-1 (application for Class A/B) to the SLA of the state where the manufacturing site is located, with **treasury challan fee** and **site master file**.

> **Step 2.** SLA conducts a **pre-licensing inspection** within **30 days** of receipt of application. The audit is conducted against:
> - Schedule 5 of MDR-2017 (Quality Management System)
> - ISO 13485:2016
> - Applicable BIS standards

> **Step 3.** SLA prepares an **inspection report** identifying non-conformities (Critical, Major, Minor).

> **Step 4.** **Decision node:**
> - If **zero critical defects** → SLA grants the license in **Form MD-5** (Class A) or **Form MD-6** (Class B).
> - If **any critical defect** exists → SLA issues a show-cause notice; applicant must respond within **15 days**.

> **Step 5.** If response is **satisfactory** → Re-inspection → License granted.
> If response is **unsatisfactory** → Application rejected; appeal lies with the **State Appellate Authority** within 30 days.

> **Step 6.** Post-licensing, SLA conducts **annual surveillance audits** (or biennial, depending on risk).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough #2: WHO Prequalification Pathway

$$\boxed{\text{Manufacturer Application} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Document Review} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Site Audit} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Laboratory Testing} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Prequalification Listing}}$$

Each step is detailed below:

> **Step 1 – Expression of Interest (EOI):** Manufacturer submits a dossier containing product details, intended use, design dossiers, and QMS certificates.

> **Step 2 – Pre-submission Meeting:** WHO assesses scope and category alignment with PQ program (e.g., IVD, male circumcision device, etc.).

> **Step 3 – Submission of Dossier:** Manufacturer files a comprehensive technical file including:
> - Design and manufacturing information
> - Risk management file (ISO 14971)
> - Performance evaluation data
> - Labelling and instructions for use

> **Step 4 – Document Review:** WHO assessors evaluate the dossier for **completeness and scientific validity**. Deficiencies are communicated via queries; manufacturer has **30–60 days** to respond.

> **Step 5 – Site Audit (if applicable):** A WHO-led team visits the manufacturing site to verify QMS, production controls, and traceability.

> **Step 6 – Performance Evaluation:** Independent **laboratory testing** of product samples at WHO-collaborating labs (e.g., for IVDs against reference methods).

> **Step 7 – Decision:** If all three streams (dossier, audit, lab) are satisfactory → product is **listed on the WHO PQ list**, valid for a fixed term (typically **3–5 years**, renewable).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough #3: FDA Premarket Decision Logic

FDA uses a **risk-based triage** to determine the premarket pathway:

$$
\text{Premarket Pathway} = f(\text{Device Class}, \text{Substantial Equivalence}, \text{Risk Profile})
$$

> **Step 1.** The manufacturer identifies the device's **regulatory class** using **21 CFR Parts 862–892**.

> **Step 2.** Class I devices are **exempt** from premarket notification (with limited exceptions).

> **Step 3.** Class II devices normally require a **510(k) Premarket Notification** demonstrating **substantial equivalence** to a legally marketed predicate device.

> **Step 4.** Class III devices generally require a **Premarket Approval (PMA)** — the most stringent pathway, demanding independent scientific evidence of safety and effectiveness.

> **Step 5.** Devices with no predicate can file a **De Novo Classification Request** under **section 513(f)(2)** of the FD&C Act.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough #4: SLA–CDSCO Coordination Matrix

| Action | SLA Responsibility | CDSCO / CLA Responsibility |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| Class A device manufacture | Full licensing | No involvement |
| Class B device manufacture | Full licensing | Oversight and audit support |
| Class C device manufacture | Only site-level coordination | Full licensing |
| Class D device manufacture | Only site-level coordination | Full licensing |
| Import license | None | CDSCO HQ |
| Clinical investigation (India) | None | CDSCO approval under MDR-2017 |
| Post-market AE reporting | Forward to MvPI | National coordination |
| Recall execution | State-level execution | National coordination |

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analysis: SLA vs. WHO vs. FDA (Indian Context)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Scope}_{\text{SLA}} &= \text{State-bound, Class A/B} \\
\text{Scope}_{\text{WHO}} &= \text{Global norms, no direct licensing power} \\
\text{Scope}_{\text{FDA}} &= \text{US territory, all device classes, enforcement power}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** WHO does **not** directly license any device. WHO publishes *norms*; national regulators (like FDA, CDSCO) *operationalise* them through binding law. This is the **soft-law vs. hard-law** distinction that KTU examiners love to test.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FDA Organizational Structure (Top-Down)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[US Department of Health and Human Services] --> B[FDA Commissioner]
    B --> C[Deputy Commissioner for Policy Legislation and International Affairs]
    B --> D[Deputy Commissioner for Operations]
    B --> E[Deputy Commissioner for Food and Veterinary Medicine]
    B --> F[Deputy Commissioner for Global Regulatory Operations and Policy]
    D --> G1[CDRH Center for Devices and Radiological Health]
    D --> G2[CDER Center for Drug Evaluation and Research]
    D --> G3[CBER Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research]
    D --> G4[CFSAN Center for Food Safety]
    D --> G5[CVM Center for Veterinary Medicine]
    D --> G6[COTP Center for Tobacco Products]
    D --> H1[ORA Office of Regulatory Affairs Field Operations]
    D --> H2[OCP Office of Combination Products]
    G1 --> S1[ODE Office of Device Evaluation]
    G1 --> S2[OSB Office of Surveillance and Biometrics]
    G1 --> S3[OC Office of Compliance]
    G1 --> S4[OSEL Office of Science and Engineering Labs]
    G1 --> S5[OCE Office of Communication and Education]
```

---

## 4.2 Indian Medical Device Regulatory Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ministry of Health and Family Welfare MoHFW] --> B[Central Government under Drugs and Cosmetics Act 1940]
    B --> C[Central Drugs Standard Control Organization CDSCO]
    C --> C1[Drug Controller General India DCGI]
    C1 --> C2[Central Licensing Authority CLA regulates Class C and Class D]
    B --> D[State Governments]
    D --> D1[State Drug Controllers]
    D1 --> D2[State Licensing Authority SLA regulates Class A and Class B]
    D1 --> D3[State Drug Testing Laboratories]
    C1 --> E[Materiovigilance Programme of India MvPI]
    C1 --> F[Medical Device Adverse Event Monitoring Cell]
    E --> G[Indian Pharmacopoeia Commission IPC Adverse Reaction Database]
```

---

## 4.3 WHO Medical Device Regulation Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[World Health Assembly] --> B[WHO Director General]
    B --> C[Science Division]
    B --> D[Regulation and Prequalification Department RPQ]
    B --> E[Health Products Policy and Standards HPS]
    D --> D1[WHO Prequalification Programme]
    D --> D2[WHO Listed Authority WLA]
    D --> D3[Collaborative Registration Procedure]
    E --> E1[Global Model Regulatory Framework GMRF]
    E --> E2[WHO Technical Specification Series]
    E --> E3[Essential Diagnostics List EDL]
    D --> F[GSMS Global Surveillance and Monitoring System]
    D --> G[EMDN European Medical Device Nomenclature adoption]
    C --> H[IMDRF observer and harmonization support]
```

---

## 4.4 SLA Decision Workflow (License Grant Process)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Manufacturer submits application in Form MD-1 to SLA] --> B1[SLA acknowledges receipt within 7 days]
    B1 --> C1[Pre licensing inspection within 30 days]
    C1 --> D1{Inspection result satisfactory}
    D1 -- Yes --> E1[License granted in Form MD-5 or MD-6]
    D1 -- No --> F1[Show cause notice issued to applicant]
    F1 --> G1{Applicant response within 15 days}
    G1 -- Satisfactory --> H1[Re inspection scheduled]
    H1 --> D1
    G1 -- Unsatisfactory --> I1[Application rejected]
    I1 --> J1[Applicant may appeal to State Appellate Authority within 30 days]
    E1 --> K1[Annual surveillance audit by SLA]
```

---

## 4.5 510(k) vs PMA vs De Novo – FDA Premarket Pathway Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Medical Device Manufacturer] --> B{Determine device class 21 CFR}
    B -- Class I --> C[Most exempt from premarket]
    B -- Class II --> D[510k Premarket Notification]
    B -- Class III --> E{New or predicate exists}
    E -- Predicate exists --> D
    E -- No predicate --> F[De Novo Classification Request]
    E -- High risk with prior data --> G[PMA Premarket Approval]
    D --> H[Substantial Equivalence to Predicate]
    F --> I[Risk Benefit Evaluation]
    G --> J[Independent Scientific Evidence]
    H --> K[FDA Clearance]
    I --> K
    J --> L[FDA Approval]
```

## 4.6 Functional Architecture – SLA, WHO, FDA Triad Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Manufacturer or Industry Stakeholder] --> B[State Licensing Authority SLA]
    A --> C[Central Licensing Authority CDSCO]
    A --> D[US FDA for export market]
    A --> E[WHO Prequalification for UN procurement]
    B --> C
    C --> F[National Regulatory Authority NRA assessment by WHO]
    D --> G[International Medical Device Regulators Forum IMDRF]
    E --> H[WHO Listed Authority Framework]
    F --> H
    G --> I[Harmonized Standards ISO IEC]
    H --> I
```
