# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

> **Q:** Define **State Licensing Authority (SLA)** and list **any two** of its key functions under the Medical Devices Rules, 2017.

**Model Answer:**

The **State Licensing Authority (SLA)** is the regulatory body constituted under the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940** (read with **Medical Devices Rules, 2017**) at the state level, headed by the **State Drug Controller**, that is empowered to grant, renew, and cancel **manufacturing and sale licenses** for medical devices of **Class A and Class B** risk categories within its territorial jurisdiction.

**Key Functions (any two):**
1. **Granting manufacturing licenses** for Class A and Class B medical devices under Rule 24 of MDR-2017.
2. **Conducting pre-licensing and periodic GMP inspections** as per Schedule 5 of MDR-2017.
3. **Investigating complaints** of substandard or spurious medical devices and recommending enforcement actions.
4. **Coordinating post-market surveillance activities** including adverse event reporting to **MvPI**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

> **Q:** Mention the **organizational position of CDRH** within the US FDA hierarchy and state its primary mandate in medical device regulation.

**Model Answer:**

The **Center for Devices and Radiological Health (CDRH)** operates as one of the principal product-evaluation centers under the **Office of the Commissioner, US FDA**, within the **Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)** of the United States Government.

**Primary Mandate:**
- To ensure the **safety, effectiveness, and quality** of medical devices and radiation-emitting electronic products marketed in the United States.
- It discharges this mandate through the **premarket review** (510(k), PMA, De Novo), **post-market surveillance** (MDR reporting, recalls), and the issuance of **guidance documents and standards** for industry.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module-Internal Choice)

### Question A – Choice Option 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Module 3]`

> **Q (a)** [7 Marks] Describe in detail the **organizational structure of the US FDA** with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of **CDRH, ORA, and the Office of Compliance (OC)** in medical device regulation.

> **Q (b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **functions of State Licensing Authorities (SLAs)** in India under the Medical Devices Rules, 2017. How does SLA coordinate with the Central Licensing Authority (CDSCO)?

#### Model Solution – Part A(a):

**Organizational Structure of US FDA:**

The **US FDA** is a federal agency of the **United States Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)**. It is headed by the **Commissioner of Food and Drugs**, who is appointed by the President with Senate confirmation.

**Hierarchy:**
1. **Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) – Parent Ministry**
2. **FDA Commissioner** – apex executive
3. **Deputy Commissioners** – for Policy, Operations, Food/Veterinary, and Global Regulatory Operations
4. **Centers and Offices** – operational arms

**Key Centers:**
- **CDRH (Center for Devices and Radiological Health):** the principal center handling medical device premarket and post-market regulation.
- **CDER (Center for Drug Evaluation and Research):** regulates drugs and combination products.
- **CBER (Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research):** regulates devices involving biological products.
- **CFSAN, CVM, CTP** – other centers (food, veterinary, tobacco).

**Role of CDRH:**
- Conducts premarket review of all medical devices through **ODE (Office of Device Evaluation)**.
- Issues **510(k) clearances, PMA approvals, De Novo classifications, and HDE (Humanitarian Device Exemptions)**.
- Develops **guidance documents, recognized consensus standards**, and **device classification panels**.

**Role of ORA (Office of Regulatory Affairs):**
- The **field force** of FDA with **inspectors and investigators** across the US and abroad.
- Conducts **GMP inspections, domestic and foreign establishment inspections**.
- Executes **recalls, import alerts, and detention** of non-compliant products.
- Coordinates with **Customs and Border Protection** for import screening.

**Role of Office of Compliance (OC) within CDRH:**
- Monitors **Medical Device Reporting (MDR)** submissions under 21 CFR Part 803.
- Issues **warning letters** and recommends enforcement actions.
- Oversees **labeling compliance, advertising, and promotional material** review.
- Coordinates **recalls and field safety corrective actions**.

**Block Diagram (Textual Representation):**

> HHS → FDA Commissioner → Deputy Commissioner (Operations) → CDRH → [ODE, OSB, OC, OSEL, OCE]
>
> Deputy Commissioner (Operations) → ORA → [Field Inspectors, Import Operations, Recall Coordinators]
>
> CDRH & ORA jointly execute compliance, enforcement, and surveillance.

#### Model Solution – Part A(b):

**Functions of State Licensing Authorities in India:**

The SLA, headed by the **State Drug Controller**, is established under the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940**, and operationalized through the **Medical Devices Rules, 2017** (notified on **31 January 2017**, effective **1 January 2018**).

**Statutory Functions:**

1. **Licensing Functions:**
   - Issue manufacturing licenses in **Form MD-5** (Class A) and **Form MD-6** (Class B).
   - Endorse additional products in the existing license.
   - Grant **loan licenses** and **wholesale/retail sale licenses** for medical devices.
   - Process **license retention and renewal** (every 5 years).

2. **Inspection Functions:**
   - Conduct **pre-licensing audits** of manufacturing premises within 30 days of application.
   - Verify compliance with **Schedule 5 (QMS)**, **ISO 13485**, and applicable **BIS standards**.
   - Carry out **periodic surveillance inspections** (at least once per year for Class A/B establishments).

3. **Enforcement Functions:**
   - **Draw samples** for testing (under Section 22 of the Act).
   - Issue **stop-sale notices** on non-compliant devices.
   - Prosecute violations under **Section 27/28** of the Act.
   - Coordinate with **police and state agencies** for raids on spurious device units.

4. **Post-Market Functions:**
   - Receive and forward **adverse event reports** to **MvPI** at **IPC, Ghaziabad**.
   - Conduct **state-level vigilance** and traceability audits.
   - Coordinate **field safety corrective actions (FSCA)** at the local level.

5. **Coordination Functions:**
   - Act as the **nodal point** between manufacturers and the **Central Licensing Authority**.
   - Forward applications for **Class C and Class D devices** to CDSCO HQ.
   - Participate in **state-level coordination committees** with BIS, FSSAI, Pollution Control Board, and Customs.

**Coordination with CDSCO (CLA):**

| Mechanism | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Reporting** | SLA forwards Class C/D applications, AE reports, and state-level enforcement data to CDSCO. |
| **Joint Inspections** | For high-risk manufacturers, CDSCO may join SLA inspections. |
| **Centralized Database** | All licenses are entered in the **Sugam online portal** accessible to CDSCO. |
| **Adverse Event Sharing** | State-level AEs flow to **MvPI**; serious AEs trigger **National Coordination** by CDSCO. |
| **Policy Implementation** | CDSCO issues **guidelines and advisories** that SLAs implement at state level. |
| **Capacity Building** | CDSCO conducts **training programs** for state drug inspectors on medical device QMS. |

---

### Question B – Choice Option 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Module 3]`

> **Q (a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **WHO Global Model Regulatory Framework for Medical Devices (GMRF)**. Explain the four-level risk stratification model and the role of WHO in setting international standards.

> **Q (b)** [7 Marks] Compare and contrast the **risk-based classification systems** used in India (CDSCO), the US (FDA), and the European Union (MDR 2017/745). Highlight the regulatory implications for a Class II medical device manufacturer.

#### Model Solution – Part B(a):

**WHO Global Model Regulatory Framework for Medical Devices (GMRF):**

The **GMRF** was first published by WHO in **2014** (revised in 2017) to provide a **unified reference model** for member states to develop, strengthen, and harmonize their national medical device regulatory systems. It is structured around the **full life cycle of a medical device** — from pre-market evaluation to post-market surveillance.

**Guiding Principles:**
- **Proportionality:** regulation should be proportional to the device's risk.
- **Transparency:** regulators must publish clear processes, timelines, and decision criteria.
- **Independence and accountability:** regulatory decisions must be science-based and free from commercial influence.
- **Harmonization:** alignment with **IMDRF, GHTF, ISO/IEC** standards.

**Four-Level Risk Stratification Model:**

WHO classifies regulatory frameworks (not devices) into four increasing levels of maturity:

1. **Level 1 – Basic Controls:** A market entry requirement is in place; the regulator is identified. Minimal structure, mostly voluntary.
2. **Level 2 – Expanded Controls:** Adverse event reporting exists; some form of establishment licensing is operational.
3. **Level 3 – Comprehensive Controls:** Premarket evaluation, registration, and post-market surveillance are mandatory for moderate-to-high risk devices.
4. **Level 4 – Advanced Controls:** All four pillars (registration, premarket, post-market, quality system) are mandatory; the regulator participates in **WHO Listed Authority (WLA)** framework and **IMDRF**.

**Role of WHO in Setting International Standards:**

1. **Publication of Technical Specifications** – WHO Technical Report Series (e.g., **TRS 1003, Annex 4** for GMP).
2. **Prequalification Programme** – Pre-qualifies IVDs and other priority devices for UN procurement.
3. **Nomenclature and Coding** – Adoption and maintenance of **EMDN (European Medical Device Nomenclature)** as a public reference.
4. **Capacity Building** – Conducts training programs for regulators of low- and middle-income countries (e.g., through the **WHO NRA assessment tool**).
5. **Surveillance** – Operates the **Global Surveillance and Monitoring System (GSMS)** to combat substandard and falsified medical products.
6. **Convening Power** – WHO serves as the **observer and convener** in forums like **IMDRF** and **Global Harmonization Working Party (GHWP)**.
7. **Essential Diagnostics List (EDL)** – First published in 2018, the EDL guides member states on priority IVDs, indirectly shaping device development priorities.

#### Model Solution – Part B(b):

**Comparative Risk Classification Systems:**

| Parameter | India (MDR 2017) | USA (FDA) | EU (MDR 2017/745) |
|-----------|------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Total Classes** | 4 (A, B, C, D) | 3 (I, II, III) | 4 (I, IIa, IIb, III) |
| **Basis** | GHTF-derived | 21 CFR Parts 862-892 | Rule-based (Annex VIII) |
| **Authority Issuing License** | SLA (A,B) / CDSCO (C,D) | FDA | Notified Body + Competent Authority |
| **Classification Rule Source** | GHTF | FDA Panel + CFR | EU MDR 2017/745 Annex VIII |
| **Time of Classification** | Before market entry | Before market entry | Before market entry |
| **Examples of Class II** | Hypodermic needles, suction pumps | Infusion pumps, surgical gloves | Contact lenses, hearing aids |

**Detailed Comparison:**

1. **India (MDR-2017):**
   - Adopted the **GHTF (Global Harmonization Task Force)** four-class model.
   - Risk increases from **A** (lowest) to **D** (highest).
   - Class A examples: surgical dressings, alcohol swabs.
   - Class B examples: hypodermic needles, suction pumps.
   - Classification rules are defined in **Schedule I of MDR-2017**.

2. **US (FDA):**
   - **Class I** – Low risk, e.g., elastic bandages, surgical gloves. **Most are 510(k) exempt.**
   - **Class II** – Moderate risk, e.g., infusion pumps, blood pressure monitors. **Most require 510(k).**
   - **Class III** – High risk, e.g., implantable pacemakers, heart valves. **Most require PMA.**

3. **EU (MDR 2017/745):**
   - Four classes: **I, IIa, IIb, III**.
   - Classification is determined by **22 rules in Annex VIII**, considering duration of use, invasiveness, and active vs. non-active.
   - Class I is **non-sterile, non-measuring** and can be **self-declared** by the manufacturer.
   - All other classes require **Notified Body involvement** (a third-party body designated by an EU member state).

**Regulatory Implications for a Class II Device Manufacturer:**

A manufacturer producing a **Class II medical device** (e.g., an electronic blood pressure monitor) faces the following:

- **In India:** Applies for license to the **State Licensing Authority (SLA)** of the manufacturing state. Requires QMS compliant with **ISO 13485:2016 + Schedule 5 of MDR-2017**. License granted in **Form MD-6** within **90 days** of application if compliant.

- **In the US:** Files a **510(k) Premarket Notification** to FDA-CDRH, demonstrating **substantial equivalence** to a legally marketed predicate device. Typical review time is **90–180 days** under the **Substantial Equivalence Review Program**.

- **In the EU:** Engages a **Notified Body** (e.g., TÜV SÜD, BSI) for **conformity assessment** per **Annex IX, X, or XI** of MDR 2017/745. The device must be **CE marked** before marketing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - ❌ Do **NOT** claim that WHO "approves" or "licenses" medical devices. WHO **prequalifies** devices for UN procurement and publishes standards — it does **not** have direct licensing power.
> - ❌ Do **NOT** equate Indian **Class A/B/C/D** with US **Class I/II/III**. They are **different classification philosophies** (Indian: 4-class, alphabetic; US: 3-class, roman numeral).
> - ❌ In SLA questions, do **not** write that SLA licenses Class C/D devices. SLAs handle **Class A and B only**; **Class C and D are under CDSCO HQ (CLA)**.
> - ❌ Do not write "WHO regulates medical devices" — the correct phrasing is "WHO develops **global norms and supports regulatory capacity**."
> - ✅ Always cite the **statutory basis** — *Drugs and Cosmetics Act 1940, MDR-2017, FD&C Act 1938, EU MDR 2017/745*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-Second Recall Map**

- 🔹 **SLA = State Drug Controller** (state level, India). **Class A and B** manufacturing licenses.
- 🔹 **CLA = CDSCO HQ (DCGI)** (central level, India). **Class C and D** manufacturing licenses.
- 🔹 **Indian Risk Classes:** A (lowest) → B → C → D (highest). 4-class, **alphabetical**.
- 🔹 **FDA Risk Classes:** I (lowest) → II → III (highest). 3-class, **roman numeral**.
- 🔹 **CDRH** = apex body within FDA for medical devices. Sub-offices: **ODE, OSB, OC, OSEL, OCE**.
- 🔹 **ORA** = field force for inspections, recalls, imports.
- 🔹 **WHO does NOT license devices**; it publishes **GMRF, TRS, EMDN, EDL, PQ**.
- 🔹 **WHO Prequalification** = UN-level eligibility for procurement; ~26 product categories.
- 🔹 **WHO GMRF** classifies **regulatory systems** (not devices) into 4 maturity levels.
- 🔹 **License validity** in India = **5 years** (with retention fee).
- 🔹 **SLA timelines:** 45 days (Class A) / 90 days (Class B); **CLA timelines:** 90 days (Class C) / 180 days (Class D).
- 🔹 **US FDA premarket pathways:** **510(k)** (Class II, equivalence), **PMA** (Class III, safety+effectiveness), **De Novo** (no predicate).
- 🔹 **EU MDR 2017/745:** 4 classes (I, IIa, IIb, III); **Notified Body** involvement for IIa, IIb, III; CE marking mandatory.
- 🔹 **GHTF** = predecessor; **IMDRF** = current global regulatory harmonization body; **WHO** is an observer.
- 🔹 **MvPI (Materiovigilance Programme of India)** is the national adverse event reporting system coordinated by **IPC, Ghaziabad**.
- 🔹 **Statutes to remember:** *Drugs & Cosmetics Act 1940, MDR 2017, FD&C Act 1938, Medical Device Amendments 1976, 21st Century Cures Act 2016, EU MDR 2017/745, ISO 13485:2016, ISO 14971:2019.*

> **End of Module 3 Notes — PEBMT861 | Medical Device Regulations & Standards**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Purpose and Functions of Regulatory Guidelines - Working Groups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Purpose and Functions of Regulatory Guidelines - Working Groups ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of medical device regulations and standards, **Regulatory Working Groups (WGs)** are formally constituted, multi-stakeholder technical committees established under recognized Standards Development Organizations (SDOs) — such as the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)**, the **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)**, the **International Telecommunication Union (ITU)**, and the **European Committee for Standardization (CEN/CENELEC)** — whose principal purpose is the **drafting, revision, harmonization, and maintenance** of technical standards, guidance documents, and regulatory frameworks that govern the design, manufacture, performance, safety, and post-market surveillance of medical devices. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):** > A **Regulatory Working Group** is a permanent or ad-hoc expert committee constituted by an SDO or regulatory authority, comprising subject-matter experts from industry, academia, regulatory bodies, and notified bodies, mandated to develop consensus-based technical standards that translate broad regulatory objectives (e.g., safety, efficacy, quality) into measurable, testable, and enforceable specifications. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine that building a skyscraper is the medical device, and the **government building codes** are the regulations. The actual written rulebook (e.g., "walls must withstand X wind force, electrical wiring must use Y gauge wire") cannot be drafted by the government alone — it requires **engineers, electricians, material scientists, fire safety experts, and even insurers** to sit together, debate, and write the detailed rules. A **Regulatory Working Group is exactly that "expert round-table"** for medical devices. - The **Government** = Regulatory Body (FDA, CDSCO, EU Commission) - The **Building Code Book** = The Standard (e.g., IEC 60601-1, ISO 13485) - The **Expert Round-Table** = The Working Group - The **Skyscraper** = The Medical Device Without WGs, regulations would be vague, untestable, and unenforceable. WGs turn abstract "the device must be safe" into concrete, measurable specifications like "leakage current shall not exceed **0.1 mA** under single-fault conditions." ### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 > [!NOTE] > Module 3 of PEBMT861 focuses on **how regulations are operationalized through standards development**. Understanding Working Groups is essential because: > 1. Every major medical device standard (ISO 13485, ISO 14971, IEC 60601-1) is born from a Working Group. > 2. The credibility and global acceptance of a standard depends on WG composition and process. > 3. Industry professionals often participate in WGs — KTU expects engineers to understand WG governance. ### 1.4 Key Terminology (Bold Definitions) - **Standards Development Organization (SDO):** A recognized body (ISO, IEC, ITU, CEN) that develops voluntary consensus standards. - **Technical Committee (TC):** A high-level parent committee (e.g., ISO/TC 198 for sterilization of healthcare products). - **Sub-Committee (SC):** A specialized division under a TC handling a sub-domain. - **Working Group (WG):** The smallest, most specialized drafting unit that actually writes the standard. - **Project Leader (PL):** The expert appointed to coordinate a specific standard's drafting. - **Convener:** The chairperson of a Working Group. - **Consensus:** Decision-making principle where all members agree (or none strongly object), as opposed to voting. - **National Mirror Committee:** A country's national body (e.g., BIS in India) that mirrors the international WG and feeds national positions. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### 2.1 The Stated Purpose of Regulatory Working Groups The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to articulate the **purpose** clearly. The purpose can be broken into **five primary mandates**: #### 2.1.1 Purpose 1 — Technical Standardization & Harmonization - WGs develop **uniform technical specifications** so that a device manufactured in Country A can be tested and accepted in Country B. - Example: IEC 60601-1 (Medical electrical equipment — General requirements for basic safety and essential performance) is developed by **IEC/SC 62A** and accepted globally. #### 2.1.2 Purpose 2 — Translation of Regulatory Intent into Testable Criteria - Regulatory laws (e.g., EU MDR 2017/745) state *what* must be achieved (e.g., "devices must be safe and perform as intended"). - WGs define *how* to measure and prove that — e.g., specific tests, pass/fail thresholds, sample sizes. #### 2.1.3 Purpose 3 — Risk Management Integration - WGs embed risk-based thinking (per ISO 14971) into design, manufacturing, and post-market standards. - They specify how manufacturers must identify, evaluate, and control risks. #### 2.1.4 Purpose 4 — Facilitating Regulatory Convergence - By producing standards cited in regulations (e.g., "harmonized standards" under EU MDR), WGs create a **presumed conformity** pathway. - Compliance with a harmonized standard = presumed compliance with the regulation. #### 2.1.5 Purpose 5 — Continuous Updating of the State-of-the-Art - Technology evolves (AI in diagnostics, wearable sensors, 3D-printed implants). WGs continuously revise standards so they remain relevant and reflect current science. ### 2.2 The Core Functions of Regulatory Working Groups KTU examiners typically ask this as a **"list and explain" 7-mark or 14-mark question**. The functions are: | **#** | **Function** | **Description** | **Real-World Example** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Drafting Standards | Writing the technical content of new standards. | Drafting ISO 14708 for active implantable medical devices. | | 2 | Revision of Existing Standards | Periodic review (typically every 5 years) and update. | IEC 60601-1 2nd → 3rd → 4th editions. | | 3 | Interpretation & Guidance | Issuing Technical Reports, Technical Specifications, Interpretations. | ISO/TR 24971 (companion to ISO 14971). | | 4 | Liaison & Coordination | Coordinating with other WGs, TCs, regulatory bodies to avoid conflicts. | IEC/SC 62A liaising with ISO/TC 198. | | 5 | Consensus Building | Resolving technical disagreements among stakeholders. | Debating software lifecycle requirements in IEC 62304. | | 6 | Public Consultation | Managing the enquiry stage where national bodies comment. | ISO DIS (Draft International Standard) ballot. | | 7 | Maintenance & Withdrawal | Issuing amendments, corrigenda, or withdrawing obsolete standards. | Withdrawal of old IEC 60601-1 (2nd edition) after transition period. | | 8 | Training & Dissemination | Conducting workshops, webinars to help industry adopt standards. | ISO training on ISO 13485:2016. | ### 2.3 Composition of a Working Group (Who Sits at the Table?) A KTU-relevant point — WGs are not government-only. They follow the **consensus principle**, meaning all major stakeholder categories must be represented: 1. **Regulatory Authorities** — FDA (USA), MHRA (UK), CDSCO (India), EU Commission. 2. **Industry** — Device manufacturers (Siemens Healthineers, Medtronic, Philips). 3. **Notified Bodies / Certification Bodies** — TÜV SÜD, BSI, DEKRA. 4. **Academia & Research Institutes** — Universities, hospital research centers. 5. **Healthcare Professionals & Clinicians** — Practicing doctors, biomedical engineers. 6. **Patient & Consumer Representatives** — Increasingly included in modern WGs. 7. **National Standards Bodies** — BIS (India), ANSI (USA), BSI (UK), DIN (Germany). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** WGs operate on the principle of **"One Country, One Vote"** at the international level, but within WGs, expert input is weighted by technical merit. This balances national sovereignty with technical excellence. ### 2.4 The Working Group Lifecycle (Stages a Standard Goes Through) A standard's journey from idea to publication is governed by the **ISO/IEC Directives, Part 2** (a meta-standard that governs how all other standards are written). For KTU, know these stages: | **Stage** | **Code** | **Purpose** | **Typical WG Activity** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **NWIP** (New Work Item Proposal) | Propose a new standard | WG drafts business case & scope. | | 2 | **WD** (Working Draft) | Internal drafting | Multiple iterations within WG. | | 3 | **CD** (Committee Draft) | TC/SC level review | WG incorporates SC-level comments. | | 4 | **DIS** (Draft International Standard) | Public enquiry (national bodies vote) | WG responds to hundreds of comments. | | 5 | **FDIS** (Final Draft International Standard) | Final approval vote | Minor edits only. | | 6 | **IS** (International Standard) | Publication | WG disbands or becomes maintenance team. | ### 2.5 Key Operational Documents & Rules (No Math, but Reference) > [!NOTE] > These are **"Standards about Standards"** — the meta-rules: > - **ISO/IEC Directives, Part 1** — Procedures for the technical work (ISO/IEC Directives). > - **ISO/IEC Directives, Part 2** — Rules for the structure and drafting of ISO and IEC documents. > - **WTO TBT Agreement** — Code of Good Practice for the Preparation, Adoption and Application of Standards (Annex 3). Mandates transparency, openness, consensus, and effectiveness. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility For a B.Tech Biomedical Engineer, understanding WGs is critical because: - **Career path:** Many senior regulatory affairs professionals started as WG experts. - **Product compliance:** Knowing which WG governs your product type (e.g., WG 6 of ISO/TC 150 = implants for osteosynthesis) tells you which standard applies. - **Patent & innovation strategy:** WG agendas reveal future regulatory directions, allowing R&D teams to align innovation with upcoming standards. - **Clinical evaluation:** Standards cited in MDR/MDD are produced by WGs — clinical evidence requirements follow WG-developed guidelines (e.g., MDCG guidance). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Detailed Functional Breakdown > [!NOTE] > This topic is **conceptual and procedural**, not mathematical. The "derivation" here is the **systematic logical analysis** of WG purpose and function, with exhaustive step-by-step reasoning as required by KTU board standards. ### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: How a Regulatory Need Becomes a Working Group Output Let us trace the complete logical chain — from a regulatory gap to a published standard — to demonstrate the "why" and "how" of Working Groups. #### Step 1 — Identification of a Regulatory or Technical Gap **Logic:** Either a new technology emerges (e.g., AI/ML in radiology) OR an existing standard is found inadequate (e.g., post-market data shows a recurring failure mode). **Reasoning:** Standards cannot be drafted in a vacuum. A trigger must exist. This trigger comes from: - Adverse event reports (e.g., MAUDE database in US, EUDAMED in EU). - Technological breakthroughs. - Changes in parent regulations (e.g., EU MDR 2017/745 replacing MDD 93/42/EEC). **WG Activity:** No formal WG exists yet at this stage. Instead, a **proposal** is drafted, usually by a national body or TC secretariat. #### Step 2 — Proposal Evaluation at TC Level **Logic:** The parent Technical Committee (TC) evaluates whether the proposal is: - In scope of the TC's domain. - Supported by a market/regulatory need. - Likely to achieve consensus. **Reasoning:** ISO/IEC Directives require a **market relevance** and **stakeholder support** check. If approved, the proposal becomes a **New Work Item Proposal (NWIP)**. **WG Activity:** A WG is **constituted** for the first time. A **Convener** is appointed by the TC. #### Step 3 — WG Constitution & Scope Definition **Logic:** The WG is given a precise scope — for example, "to develop a standard for the application of usability engineering to medical devices." **Reasoning:** Without a defined scope, the WG risks scope creep. The scope statement is approved by the parent TC. **WG Output:** A **scope document** and a **work plan** with target dates. #### Step 4 — Member Nomination & First Meeting **Logic:** National member bodies (e.g., BIS for India) nominate experts. The WG convenes its first meeting (physically or virtually). **Reasoning:** Diversity of expertise ensures the standard is technically sound and globally applicable. A WG with only industry members will be biased; one with only regulators will be impractical. **WG Output:** Meeting minutes, action items, draft structure (called the **"outline"** or **"table of contents"**). #### Step 5 — Drafting the Working Draft (WD) **Logic:** The Project Leader (PL) and a drafting team produce successive versions of the WD. Each clause of the standard is debated, written, and revised. **Reasoning:** Standards are written using a **harmonized template** (ISO/IEC Directives, Part 2). Every clause must follow specific rules (e.g., normative vs. informative content, mandatory vs. optional requirements). **WG Output:** WD 1, WD 2, WD 3, etc., until consensus is reached within the WG. #### Step 6 — Committee Draft (CD) Ballot **Logic:** The WD is promoted to a **CD** and circulated to the parent SC/TC for comments. **Reasoning:** Even if the WG has internal consensus, the broader TC must validate. This is a quality gate. **WG Activity:** Every comment must be **dispositioned** (accepted, rejected, or accepted with modification), with written justification. #### Step 7 — Draft International Standard (DIS) Ballot — The Critical Stage **Logic:** The CD is promoted to DIS. **All national member bodies worldwide** receive the DIS and submit formal votes and comments. **Reasoning:** This is the **public consultation** stage. Voting rules (per ISO/IEC Directives): - Approval requires **≥ 2/3 majority** of P-members voting. - Abstentions are not counted. - Negative votes **must** be accompanied by technical justification. **WG Output:** A **DIS Comment Resolution Report** — often hundreds of pages long. The WG meets (often physically) to resolve every comment. #### Step 8 — Final Draft International Standard (FDIS) **Logic:** The cleaned-up draft is sent for FDIS vote. At this stage, **only editorial corrections** are allowed; technical changes risk rejection. **Reasoning:** The standard is essentially final. FDIS is a formality to ensure no last-minute errors. **WG Activity:** Editorial review, translation coordination. #### Step 9 — Publication as International Standard (IS) **Logic:** Upon FDIS approval, the standard is published. It is now a **referenceable, citable international consensus document**. **Reasoning:** Publication triggers regulatory clockwork — e.g., under EU MDR, harmonized standards are cited in the *Official Journal of the European Union*, granting **presumption of conformity**. **WG Output:** The published standard, plus typically a **maintenance team** (or the WG continues in "maintenance mode") to handle amendments. #### Step 10 — Systematic Review (Every 5 Years) **Logic:** ISO/IEC requires every standard to be reviewed at least every 5 years. The TC decides: confirm, revise, or withdraw. **Reasoning:** Medical technology and science evolve rapidly. A standard from 2005 may be obsolete in 2025. **WG Activity:** If revision is approved, a new WG cycle begins. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Functions to Outcomes | **Function** | **Input** | **Process** | **Output** | **Outcome (Regulatory Impact)** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Drafting | Identified need | WG meetings & drafts | IS / IEC Standard | Basis for presumption of conformity. | | Revision | Systematic review feedback | Re-drafting | New edition | Updated state-of-the-art alignment. | | Interpretation | Industry queries | WG discussion | Technical Report / FAQ | Clarified compliance pathways. | | Liaison | Conflicting requirements | Inter-WG meetings | Joint publication / alignment | Avoiding contradictory standards. | | Public Consultation | DIS ballot | Comment resolution | Improved draft | Transparency & global acceptability. | | Maintenance | Amendments, corrigenda | Fast-track procedures | Updated standard | Continued accuracy. | ### 3.3 Mathematical Representation of Consensus (Conceptual) Although standards are not "solved" with equations, the **consensus threshold** can be formally expressed for KTU conceptual understanding: Let: - $N_p$ = Number of P-member (participating) national bodies that vote. - $N_a$ = Number of P-members voting **approval**. - $N_d$ = Number of P-members voting **disapproval**. **Approval Condition (ISO/IEC Directives):** $$ \frac{N_a}{N_a + N_d} \geq \frac{2}{3} $$ This means: a standard is approved only if at least **two-thirds of the voting P-members (excluding abstentions)** approve it. > [!NOTE] > This single equation is the **formal mathematical anchor** of WG consensus. KTU may include it in 3-mark questions to test if students understand that consensus is a **numerically defined** process, not a vague "agreement." ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (For 3-Mark Clarity) **Problem:** An ISO Working Group circulates a DIS to 30 P-member national bodies. Results: - 18 vote "Approve" - 6 vote "Disapprove" - 6 abstain **Solution (Step-by-Step):** Step 1 — Identify voting members (excluding abstentions): $$ N_a + N_d = 18 + 6 = 24 $$ Step 2 — Compute approval ratio: $$ \text{Approval Ratio} = \frac{N_a}{N_a + N_d} = \frac{18}{24} = 0.75 $$ Step 3 — Compare with the required threshold of **2/3 ≈ 0.6667**: $$ 0.75 \geq 0.6667 \quad \text{? Yes.} $$ Step 4 — Conclusion: $$ \boxed{\text{The DIS is APPROVED. The standard proceeds to FDIS stage.}} $$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hierarchical Structure of Standards Development Organizations (SDO) This diagram shows the governance hierarchy that a Working Group operates within. The **WG sits at the bottom tier** — closest to the actual technical drafting work. ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["ISO Council
and IEC General Assembly"] nodeB["Technical Management Board
TMB and SMB"] nodeC["Technical Committee
e.g. ISO TC 198"] nodeD["Sub Committee
e.g. ISO TC 198 SC 1"] nodeE["Working Group
e.g. WG 3"] nodeF["Project Leader and Drafting Team"] nodeG["National Mirror Committees
BIS ANSI BSI DIN"] nodeH["Published International Standard"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeH nodeC -. "Liaison" .-> nodeG nodeD -. "Liaison" .-> nodeG nodeE -. "National Positions" .-> nodeG nodeG -. "Comments and Votes" .-> nodeE ``` ### 4.2 The Working Group Lifecycle (From Proposal to Publication) This is a **sequential process diagram** showing the stages a standard passes through, with decision gates at each stage. ```mermaid graph TD step1["Stage 1 NWIP
New Work Item Proposal"] step2["Stage 2 WD
Working Draft"] step3["Stage 3 CD
Committee Draft"] step4["Stage 4 DIS
Draft International Standard"] step5["Stage 5 FDIS
Final Draft"] step6["Stage 6 IS
Published International Standard"] step7["Stage 7 Maintenance
5 Year Review"] step1 -->|"WG constituted"| step2 step2 -->|"Internal consensus"| step3 step3 -->|"TC SC approval"| step4 step4 -->|"DIS Comment Resolution"| step5 step5 -->|"FDIS vote"| step6 step6 -->|"Published"| step7 step7 -->|"Confirm Revise Withdraw"| step1 ``` ### 4.3 Stakeholder Composition of a Regulatory Working Group This is a **block-level functional architecture** showing who sits in a typical WG and how their inputs are consolidated. ```mermaid graph TD blockA["Regulatory Authorities
FDA MHRA CDSCO EU Commission"] blockB["Industry
Medical Device Manufacturers"] blockC["Notified Bodies
TUV SUD BSI DEKRA"] blockD["Academia
Universities Research Institutes"] blockE["Healthcare Professionals
Clinicians Hospital Engineers"] blockF["Patient Representatives
Consumer Associations"] blockG["National Standards Bodies
BIS ANSI DIN AFNOR"] blockH["Working Group Convener
Coordinates and Moderates"] blockI["Project Leader
Drafts the Standard"] blockJ["Published International Standard
ISO IEC IS"] blockA --> blockH blockB --> blockH blockC --> blockH blockD --> blockH blockE --> blockH blockF --> blockH blockG --> blockH blockH --> blockI blockI --> blockJ ``` ### 4.4 Consensus Decision Flow (Visualizing the 2/3 Threshold) A flowchart illustrating how DIS votes are tallied and the decision is reached. ```mermaid graph TD flow1["DIS Ballot Initiated
All P-Members Receive Draft"] flow2{"Count Votes"} flow3["Approvals Na"] flow4["Disapprovals Nd"] flow5["Abstentions excluded"] flow6{"Is Na divided by Na plus Nd greater than or equal to 2 by 3?"} flow7["DIS APPROVED
Proceed to FDIS"] flow8["DIS REJECTED
Return to CD Stage"] flow1 --> flow2 flow2 --> flow3 flow2 --> flow4 flow2 --> flow5 flow3 --> flow6 flow4 --> flow6 flow6 -->|"Yes"| flow7 flow6 -->|"No"| flow8 ``` ### 4.5 Functional Block Diagram: Purpose to Outcome Mapping This diagram shows how each *purpose* of a WG leads to a specific *function*, which produces a measurable *outcome*. ```mermaid graph LR pur1["Purpose Harmonization"] pur2["Purpose Testable Criteria"] pur3["Purpose Risk Integration"] pur4["Purpose Regulatory Convergence"] pur5["Purpose State of the Art Update"] fn1["Function Drafting Standards"] fn2["Function Revision"] fn3["Function Interpretation"] fn4["Function Liaison"] fn5["Function Maintenance"] out1["Outcome Global Trade Facilitation"] out2["Outcome Presumed Conformity"] out3["Outcome Reduced Adverse Events"] out4["Outcome Faster Market Access"] out5["Outcome Technological Innovation Enabled"] pur1 --> fn1 --> out1 pur2 --> fn1 --> out2 pur3 --> fn3 --> out3 pur4 --> fn4 --> out2 pur5 --> fn2 --> out5 pur1 --> fn5 --> out4 pur3 --> fn3 --> out3 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Define the term "Regulatory Working Group" in the context of medical device standards development. Mention any two bodies that constitute such working groups.** **Model Answer (Board-Standard):** A **Regulatory Working Group (WG)** is a formally constituted expert committee established under a Standards Development Organization (SDO) such as ISO or IEC, comprising subject-matter experts from regulatory bodies, industry, notified bodies, academia, and national standards bodies. Its primary purpose is the drafting, revision, and maintenance of consensus-based technical standards that translate broad regulatory requirements (e.g., safety, performance, quality) into measurable, testable, and enforceable specifications for medical devices. **Two examples:** 1. **IEC/SC 62A Working Group 1** — responsible for the safety standard IEC 60601-1 (Medical electrical equipment — General requirements). 2. **ISO/TC 198 Working Group 3** — responsible for sterilization standards (e.g., ISO 11135 for ethylene oxide sterilization). **(3 marks — Definition: 1.5 marks, Examples: 1.5 marks)** --- #### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **List any four key functions of a Regulatory Working Group in medical device standards development.** **Model Answer:** The four key functions are: 1. **Drafting of new standards** — Producing consensus-based technical documents (e.g., ISO 13485). 2. **Revision of existing standards** — Periodic review (typically every 5 years) to align with the state-of-the-art. 3. **Coordination and liaison** — Interacting with other WGs, technical committees, and regulatory bodies to avoid duplication and conflict. 4. **Issuing interpretations and guidance** — Publishing technical reports, corrigenda, and FAQs to clarify the application of standards. **(3 marks — 0.75 marks per correctly identified function)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) #### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **five main purposes** of Regulatory Working Groups in the medical device sector. (7 marks) **(b)** Describe the **complete lifecycle of a standard** from NWIP to publication, clearly indicating the role of the Working Group at each stage. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Answer for Question A:** **(a) Five Main Purposes of Regulatory Working Groups (7 marks):** 1. **Technical Standardization and Harmonization (1.5 marks):** WGs develop uniform technical specifications that enable a medical device manufactured in one country to be tested, accepted, and traded globally. For example, IEC 60601-1 created by IEC/SC 62A is accepted in over 100 countries, eliminating duplicative national testing. 2. **Translation of Regulatory Intent into Testable Criteria (1.5 marks):** Regulations state broad goals like "the device must be safe." WGs convert these into specific, measurable, and testable requirements — for example, leakage current limits, mechanical strength thresholds, software validation protocols, and biocompatibility test methods. 3. **Risk Management Integration (1 mark):** WGs embed risk-based thinking (as defined in ISO 14971) into design, manufacturing, and post-market standards. They specify how manufacturers must identify hazards, estimate and evaluate risks, and implement risk control measures. 4. **Facilitating Regulatory Convergence (1.5 marks):** By producing standards cited in regulations (e.g., "harmonized standards" under EU MDR 2017/745), WGs enable a "presumption of conformity" pathway — compliance with the standard is treated as compliance with the regulation, significantly reducing manufacturer burden. 5. **Continuous Updating of the State-of-the-Art (1.5 marks):** WGs systematically review standards every 5 years and revise them to reflect advances in science and technology (e.g., incorporating requirements for AI/ML-based devices, cybersecurity, and 3D-printed implants). --- **(b) Lifecycle of a Standard — Role of the Working Group (7 marks):** | **Stage** | **Description** | **Role of WG** | |---|---|---| | **NWIP** (New Work Item Proposal) | A national body or TC identifies a need and proposes a new standard. | WG is **constituted**; convener and project leader appointed. (1 mark) | | **WD** (Working Draft) | Internal drafting within the WG. | WG drafts and revises the document iteratively. (1 mark) | | **CD** (Committee Draft) | First formal review at TC/SC level. | WG responds to TC/SC comments and updates the draft. (1 mark) | | **DIS** (Draft International Standard) | Public enquiry — all national member bodies vote. | WG **resolves hundreds of comments** and prepares DIS Comment Resolution Report. (2 marks) | | **FDIS** (Final Draft International Standard) | Final approval ballot. | WG performs **editorial review** and coordinates translations. (1 mark) | | **IS** (International Standard) | Publication of the official standard. | WG is disbanded or transitions to a **maintenance team** for 5-yearly reviews. (1 mark) | The WG's role diminishes from primary authorship (WD/CD) to editorial refinement (FDIS) to oversight (maintenance). This phased approach ensures both **technical depth** and **broad stakeholder legitimacy**. --- #### **Question B (Alternative for Internal Choice):** **(a)** Discuss the **composition of a Regulatory Working Group** and explain why diversity of membership is essential for the credibility of a standard. (7 marks) **(b)** Explain the **consensus principle** as applied in ISO/IEC Working Groups. State the numerical threshold for approval and illustrate with a numerical example. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Answer for Question B:** **(a) Composition of a Regulatory Working Group (7 marks):** A regulatory WG typically comprises the following stakeholder categories: 1. **Regulatory Authorities (1 mark):** Representatives from bodies like the US FDA, EU Commission, UK's MHRA, and India's CDSCO. They provide the regulatory perspective and ensure alignment with laws. 2. **Industry Experts (1 mark):** Engineers and regulatory affairs professionals from medical device manufacturers (e.g., Medtronic, Siemens Healthineers, Philips). They contribute practical manufacturing and design knowledge. 3. **Notified Bodies / Certification Bodies (1 mark):** Organizations like TÜV SÜD, BSI, DEKRA that audit manufacturers. They provide the conformity assessment perspective. 4. **Academia and Research Institutes (1 mark):** University professors, hospital research staff, and independent researchers contribute cutting-edge scientific knowledge. 5. **Healthcare Professionals and Patient Representatives (1 mark):** Clinicians provide the user perspective; patient/consumer representatives ensure that the standard serves its ultimate end-user. 6. **National Standards Bodies (1 mark):** Bodies like BIS (India), ANSI (USA), DIN (Germany) represent national positions and ensure global harmonization. 7. **Importance of Diversity (1 mark):** Diversity ensures **credibility, legitimacy, and global acceptance**. A standard drafted solely by industry would be self-serving; one drafted solely by regulators would be impractical. The WTO's *Code of Good Practice* mandates that standards be developed in an **open, transparent, and consensus-based** manner — diversity is the operational manifestation of this principle. --- **(b) Consensus Principle and Numerical Threshold (7 marks):** The **consensus principle** in ISO and IEC decision-making means that decisions are taken when there is **general agreement** among the members, with no sustained opposition from any substantial interest. While not a simple "majority vote," formal votes do occur at the DIS and FDIS stages. **Numerical Threshold for DIS Approval (ISO/IEC Directives, Part 1):** A DIS is approved when at least **two-thirds (2/3)** of the P-member national bodies (those who are participating members) **voting** approve it, **excluding abstentions**. $$ \frac{N_a}{N_a + N_d} \geq \frac{2}{3} $$ Where: - $N_a$ = number of P-members voting **approval**. - $N_d$ = number of P-members voting **disapproval**. **Numerical Example (3 marks):** Suppose 30 P-member national bodies vote on a DIS: - 20 vote Approve - 5 vote Disapprove - 5 Abstain **Step 1 — Exclude abstentions:** $$ N_a + N_d = 20 + 5 = 25 $$ **Step 2 — Compute approval ratio:** $$ \frac{N_a}{N_a + N_d} = \frac{20}{25} = 0.80 $$ **Step 3 — Compare with threshold:** $$ 0.80 \geq 0.6667 \quad \text{? Yes.} $$ **Step 4 — Conclusion:** The DIS is **approved**, and the standard proceeds to FDIS. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to exclude abstentions:** Many students wrongly compute 20/30 = 0.67. This is **incorrect** — abstentions are NOT counted in the denominator. > 2. **Confusing P-members and O-members:** Only **Participating (P) members** vote. Observing (O) members do not vote. > 3. **Negative votes must be justified:** A bare "No" vote without technical justification is **invalid** and not counted in $N_d$. > 4. **Consensus ≠ unanimity:** Consensus does NOT mean everyone agrees. It means no sustained, strong, technically-justified opposition. KTU students often wrongly equate consensus with unanimity. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Working Group (WG):** Smallest, most specialized drafting unit in the ISO/IEC standards hierarchy (sits below SC and TC). - **Parent Bodies:** ISO, IEC, ITU, CEN/CENELEC are the four major SDOs. - **Hierarchy:** Council → TMB/SMB → TC → SC → **WG** → Project Leader → National Mirror Committees. - **Five Purposes:** Standardization, Testable Criteria, Risk Integration, Regulatory Convergence, State-of-the-Art Update. - **Eight Functions:** Drafting, Revision, Interpretation, Liaison, Consensus Building, Public Consultation, Maintenance, Training. - **Stakeholders:** Regulators, Industry, Notified Bodies, Academia, Clinicians, Patients, National Bodies. - **Six-Stage Lifecycle:** NWIP → WD → CD → DIS → FDIS → IS, followed by 5-yearly **Systematic Review**. - **Consensus Rule:** $N_a / (N_a + N_d) \geq 2/3$ — exclude abstentions. - **Key Meta-Standards:** ISO/IEC Directives (Parts 1 & 2); WTO TBT Agreement Annex 3. - **Why WGs Matter:** They create "presumed conformity" pathways that reduce manufacturer burden and enable global market access. - **KTU Hot Words to Use in Answers:** *Consensus-based, harmonized standard, presumption of conformity, state-of-the-art, risk-based approach, national mirror committee, project leader, convener, systematic review.* - **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Do not confuse **Working Group (WG)** with **Technical Committee (TC)** — WGs do the actual drafting; TCs provide strategic oversight. ---

topics.title: Summary Technical Document (STED)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Summary Technical Document (STED)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🩺 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (GHTF SG1/N011R12:2005)**
> The **Summary Technical Documentation (STED)** is a structured, comprehensive, and harmonized compilation of summary information pertaining to the **design, manufacture, intended use, risk management, verification, validation, and clinical evaluation** of a medical device, prepared by the manufacturer to demonstrate conformity of the device to the *Essential Principles of Safety and Performance* during premarket regulatory submissions.

STED was originally conceptualized by the **Global Harmonization Task Force (GHTF)** — a voluntary international consortium comprising regulatory authorities and industry representatives from the **European Union, United States, Canada, Japan, and Australia** — to provide a *common submission dossier format* that could be accepted across multiple jurisdictions, thereby eliminating duplication of technical evidence and reducing time-to-market for medical devices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms & Evolutions of STED (Board-Exam Tip)**
> - **GHTF STED** (original, 2005) → **IMDRF ToC** (Table of Contents, post-2012) → **CECP / Table of Contents Submission Format** adopted by regulators including the **US FDA eSTAR, EU MDR, Health Canada, TGA Australia, PMDA Japan, and CDSCO India**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Inspection" Model

Imagine a manufacturer has constructed a high-rise apartment (the **medical device**) and must convince the municipal building inspector (the **regulatory authority**) that the building is safe for occupancy:

| Element | Real-World Analogy | STED Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| The complete blueprints, structural calculations, soil reports | The "**full library**" stored at the construction site | **Full Technical Documentation / Design Dossier** |
| The well-organized executive summary presented at the inspection desk | The "**presentation folder**" submitted for review | **Summary Technical Document (STED)** |
| The municipal building code (e.g., NBC of India, IBC, Eurocode) | Universal safety principles | **Essential Principles of Safety and Performance** |
| The risk register and safety audit certificates | Documented evidence of risk mitigation | **Risk Management File (ISO 14971)** |
| Fire-drill records and occupancy permits | Practical performance confirmation | **Clinical Evidence & PMCF Data** |

> [!TIP]
> **The Key Insight:** The inspector does **not** have time to read every single drawing. Instead, the construction company prepares a **structured, cross-referenced summary** that points to the underlying evidence on demand. This is precisely what STED accomplishes for the regulator.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical/Conceptual Constants

- **Essential Principles (EP):** A set of approximately **19 fundamental safety and performance requirements** that apply to all medical devices regardless of risk class. These were originally laid down in **GHTF SG1/N68R1**.
- **Technical Documentation (TD):** The complete, granular body of design, manufacturing, and test records retained by the manufacturer under their **ISO 13485 QMS** (referenced in KTU Module 2).
- **STED:** A *curated subset* of the TD — sufficient in depth to permit regulatory review without exposing proprietary or irrelevant granular data.
- **STED-to-TD Ratio (qualitative):** Typically the STED constitutes **15–30% of the full Technical Documentation volume**, focusing on summaries, conclusions, and cross-references rather than raw data.
- **Design Dossier:** The expanded version of TD required for **Class III / high-risk devices** in jurisdictions such as the **EU under MDR 2017/745**.

$$\text{STED} \;\subset\; \text{Technical Documentation (TD)} \;\subset\; \text{Design Dossier (DD)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The STED is **NOT** a substitute for the full Technical Documentation. It is a *gateway document* — the regulatory authority must always have *right of access* to the underlying TD through a documented request mechanism.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — The "Why" Behind STED

### 2.1.1 The Regulatory Harmonization Imperative
Before STED, manufacturers faced a **fragmented global landscape**:
- US FDA required a **510(k) Premarket Notification** or **PMA Application** with its own format.
- EU required a **Technical File** (for Class I, IIa, IIb) or **Design Dossier** (Class III / active implantable).
- Canada required a **License Application** with Health Canada's own structure.
- Japan required a **Pre-market Submission (Todokede / Ninsho)** via PMDA.
- Australia required an **ARTG inclusion application** via TGA.

This created a **regulatory trilemma:**
1. **Duplication of evidence:** Same biocompatibility tests, electrical safety tests, and clinical data had to be re-packaged for each jurisdiction.
2. **Time-to-market delay:** A 24–36 month approval cycle was common.
3. **Cost burden:** SMEs found it economically unviable to launch innovative devices globally.

STED was the GHTF's answer — a **single, harmonized dossier format** that any of the founding regulatory bodies could accept in principle.

### 2.1.2 The "How" — Mechanism of Acceptance
The GHTF SG1(PD)/N011R12:2005 guidance defined a **two-tier documentation model**:

| Tier | Document | Audience | Content Depth | Storage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tier 1** | Full Technical Documentation | Notified Body / RA on request | Granular, complete | Manufacturer's QMS-controlled archive |
| **Tier 2** | **STED** | Regulatory Authority at submission | Summary, cross-referenced | Submitted physically/digitally |

The regulator reviews the **STED** and, if satisfied, may **request specific sections of the underlying TD** for deeper audit. This preserves confidentiality while enabling rigorous assessment.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet — STED Section Map

The following table maps the **mandatory sections of a STED** as defined in GHTF SG1/N011R12 (still the foundational reference for KTU Module 3 and the 2024-scheme syllabus):

| Section No. | Section Title | Purpose / Content Snapshot | Governing Standard(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | **Cover Page & Administrative Information** | Manufacturer name, address, device trade name, model, regulatory class, submission date, document version, signatory | GHTF SG1/N011R12 §5.1 |
| 2.0 | **Table of Contents** | Hierarchical listing of all sections and appendices with page numbers | — |
| 3.0 | **Device Description** | Intended use, indications, contraindications, principles of operation, device variants, accessories, materials of construction, key performance specifications | ISO 13485 §4.2.3, ISO 14971 |
| 4.0 | **Design and Manufacturing Information** | Design controls summary, manufacturing flow, key suppliers, critical processes, sterilization overview (if applicable) | ISO 13485 §7.3, §7.5 |
| 5.0 | **Essential Principles (EP) Checklist & Evidence of Conformity** | A *matrix* mapping each EP to the specific standard, test report, or risk control measure demonstrating compliance | GHTF SG1/N68 (Essential Principles) |
| 6.0 | **Risk Management Summary** | Brief overview of the risk management process, residual risk evaluation, benefit-risk conclusion | **ISO 14971:2019** |
| 7.0 | **Verification and Validation (V&V) Summary** | Tabulated summary of all design V&V activities — biocompatibility, electrical safety, EMC, software, mechanical, packaging, shelf-life, transport | IEC 60601-1, ISO 10993 series, IEC 62366-1, ISO 11607 |
| 8.0 | **Clinical Evidence Summary** | Clinical evaluation report (CER), literature review, clinical study reports, post-market clinical follow-up (PMCF) plan | **ISO 14155, ISO 18969 (MDR CER), MEDDEV 2.7/1 Rev 4** |
| 9.0 | **Labeling and Instructions for Use (IFU)** | Labels (primary, secondary, sterile barrier), IFU, patient implant card, e-IFU justification | ISO 15223-1, IEC 60601-1-11, ISO 20417 |
| 10.0 | **Regulatory Information** | Market history, prior regulatory approvals, regulatory certifications (ISO 13485, MDSAP), declaration of conformity references | ISO 13485, MDSAP |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The **Essential Principles Checklist (Section 5)** is the *most heavily weighted* sub-section in KTU 14-mark questions. It is the single document that *binds* the entire STED to the regulatory requirement framework. Always describe it as a *matrix/table* with three columns: (a) Essential Principle, (b) Applicable Standard/Method, (c) Evidence Document Reference.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Regulatory Utility

The STED framework supports the medical device industry in three critical ways:

1. **Conformity Assessment Pathway:** For Class IIa, IIb, and III devices in jurisdictions that follow GHTF principles (e.g., Australia TGA, Canada Health Canada pre-2019, Japan PMDA), the STED is the *primary* submission document reviewed by the regulator or Notified Body.

2. **MDSAP Audit Foundation:** The **Medical Device Single Audit Program (MDSAP)** — accepted by US FDA, Health Canada, TGA, ANVISA, and MHLW/PMDA — uses a STED-aligned structure as the audit reference document, enabling a single audit to satisfy multiple regulatory authorities.

3. **EU MDR 2017/745 Compatibility:** While the EU has its own structured TD format, the **MDCG 2019-9 Rev. 1** guidance explicitly allows manufacturers to use a STED-style structure, provided all the requirements of Annex II/III of the MDR are met. This makes STED the de facto *global lingua franca* for technical submissions.

$$\text{Effective Market Access} = f(\text{QMS Quality, STED Completeness, EP Conformity, Risk-Benefit Balance})$$

where the **STED Completeness Index (SCI)** is a qualitative measure used internally by regulatory affairs teams:

$$\text{SCI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{10} w_i \cdot c_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{10} w_i}$$

where $w_i$ is the regulatory weight assigned to STED section $i$ (typically 0.5 to 1.5) and $c_i$ is the completeness score of that section (0 to 1). A target $\text{SCI} \geq 0.95$ is considered submission-ready.

> [!NOTE]
> The SCI formula is **illustrative** — used in industry practice for internal document review readiness checks. It is **not** an officially published ISO/GHTF metric, and KTU examiners accept qualitative descriptions of "STED completeness" as equally valid.

# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — STED Preparation Workflow

## 3.1 The STED Compilation Process — Sequential Logic

Since the topic is regulatory and documentary in nature (rather than mathematical), "derivations" here refer to the **logical sequence of document preparation activities** that convert raw engineering outputs into a regulatory-grade STED.

### Step 1: Confirm the Regulatory Pathway & Applicable Guidance

$$\text{Identify: Market} \rightarrow \text{Device Class} \rightarrow \text{Submission Type} \rightarrow \text{Regulator}$$

**Logic:** A Class IIb device targeting the EU follows a different STED preparation sequence (aligned with MDR Annex II) than a Class II device targeting Australia. This determination dictates the depth of each section.

### Step 2: Establish the Document Master List (DML)

The regulatory affairs team prepares a **Document Master List (DML)** — an internal traceability index that maps every requirement of the STED to the source file in the **ISO 13485 QMS documentation set**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The DML ensures *one-to-one traceability* between STED summary statements and the underlying source documents. This is mandatory for **MDR Article 10(4)** and **21 CFR 820.30** design history file requirements.

### Step 3: Author the Device Description (Section 3)

**Logic:** Begin with the *physical* and *functional* identity of the device. This anchors all subsequent sections.

- Nomenclature (GMDN / GUDID / EMDN code)
- Model numbers, variants, accessories
- Materials of construction (with biological risk classification)
- Operating principle and mechanism of action
- Software version (if applicable — SaMD classification)
- Sterile / non-sterile, single-use / reusable status

### Step 4: Develop the Essential Principles (EP) Checklist (Section 5)

This is the most critical step. For each of the ~19 Essential Principles:

1. State the principle verbatim (or paraphrase with citation).
2. Mark it **Applicable (A)** or **Not Applicable (NA)** with justification.
3. Cite the harmonized standard used (e.g., **IEC 60601-1** for electrical safety, **ISO 10993-1** for biocompatibility, **ISO 14971** for risk management).
4. Provide the evidence document reference (e.g., Test Report XYZ-2024-001).

$$\text{EP Conformity Index} = \frac{\text{Number of Applicable EPs with Evidence}}{\text{Total Number of Applicable EPs}}$$

A target conformity index of **1.00 (100%)** is required for submission.

### Step 5: Compile the V&V Summary (Section 7)

For each design verification and validation activity:
- Test name and objective
- Acceptance criteria (predefined in design input)
- Standard / protocol reference
- Pass/Fail result
- Cross-reference to full test report in TD

### Step 6: Draft the Clinical Evidence Summary (Section 8)

Based on **ISO 14155** and **MEDDEV 2.7/1 Rev 4 / MDR Article 61**:
- State the clinical evaluation methodology.
- Summarize literature search strategy and findings.
- Summarize clinical investigation results (if conducted).
- State the benefit-risk conclusion.
- Reference the Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF) plan.

### Step 7: Risk Management Summary (Section 6)

Provide a **tabular summary** referencing the full **ISO 14971:2019 Risk Management File**:
- Identified hazards (electrical, mechanical, biological, software, usability)
- Risk estimation and evaluation
- Risk control measures
- Residual risk evaluation
- Overall benefit-risk conclusion

### Step 8: Labeling, Manufacturing, and Administrative Closure (Sections 1, 4, 9, 10)

Finalize:
- Manufacturer and authorized representative details.
- Manufacturing sites, sterilization subcontractors, critical suppliers.
- IFU, labels, patient information.
- Regulatory history and prior approvals.
- Declaration of conformity draft.

### Step 9: Internal Review & Document Control

$$\text{STED Ready for Submission} \iff \text{Internal QMS Review} \wedge \text{Notified Body Pre-Review (if applicable)} \wedge \text{EP Conformity Index} = 1.00$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — STED vs. Other Submission Formats

This comparative matrix is a **favourite KTU Part B (7-mark) question topic** and is essential for the 14-mark essay answers.

| Dimension | **GHTF STED** | **EU Technical Documentation (MDR Annex II)** | **US FDA 510(k) Premarket Notification** | **US FDA PMA** | **Health Canada Licence Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin / Guidance** | GHTF SG1/N011R12 (2005) | EU MDR 2017/745 Annex II & III | 21 CFR 807.92 | 21 CFR 814 | Canadian Medical Devices Regulations |
| **Structure** | 10 sections (harmonized) | 8 mandated sections (MDR-aligned) | 21 elements (FDA format) | Detailed dossier + 7 modules | 8 schedules |
| **Primary Reviewer** | RA / Notified Body | Notified Body | FDA OPEQ | FDA OPEQ | Health Canada Medical Devices Bureau |
| **Risk Class Applicability** | All classes | All classes (Class III = Design Dossier) | Class I, II (predicate-based) | Class III (highest risk) | All classes |
| **Clinical Evidence Depth** | Summary (CER + PMCF) | Full CER + PMCF (Annex XIV) | Substantial equivalence + summary | Full clinical study data | Summary of clinical data |
| **Harmonization Potential** | **High (global)** | EU-specific | US-specific | US-specific | STED-aligned (Canada accepted STED historically) |
| **Volume (Typical)** | 100–300 pages | 150–500 pages | 50–150 pages | 500–2000+ pages | 100–300 pages |

---

## 3.3 The STED → IMDRF ToC Evolution

The GHTF was **disbanded in 2012** and succeeded by the **International Medical Device Regulators Forum (IMDRF)**. The IMDRF adopted and refined the STED concept into the **IMDRF Table of Contents (ToC)** format, which is now the de facto international standard:

$$\text{GHTF STED (2005)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{IMDRF ToC (2014 onward)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Regulator-specific adoption (FDA eSTAR, EU MDR, TGA, Health Canada, PMDA, ANVISA)}$$

| Generation | Document | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|
| **1st** | GHTF STED (2005) | First harmonized structure |
| **2nd** | IMDRF ToC (2014) | Refined chapter numbering, non-binding "neutral" format |
| **3rd** | Regulator-specific ToCs (2018–2024) | eSTAR (FDA), ToC-based CERs (EU), etc. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Update:** Be prepared to mention that the *current* regulatory parlance is **IMDRF ToC**, but the foundational framework is still referred to as the STED in the context of GHTF legacy. Examiners may use either term and expect consistent understanding.

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the Summary Technical Document (STED). State its origin and primary purpose in medical device regulation.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Summary Technical Document (STED)** is a structured, harmonized compilation of summary information about a medical device's design, manufacture, intended use, risk management, verification, validation, and clinical evaluation, prepared by the manufacturer to demonstrate conformity to the *Essential Principles of Safety and Performance* during premarket regulatory submissions. **(2 marks)**
> It was originally developed by the **Global Harmonization Task Force (GHTF)** in its guidance document **SG1/N011R12 (2005)**, with the primary purpose of providing a **common submission dossier format** accepted across multiple regulatory jurisdictions (EU, US, Canada, Japan, Australia), thereby **eliminating duplication of evidence, reducing time-to-market, and supporting global regulatory harmonization** for medical devices. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List and briefly describe any six key sections of a STED.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 mark per correct section):**
> 1. **Device Description (Section 3):** Trade name, model, variants, materials, operating principle, intended use, accessories. *(0.5)*
> 2. **Design and Manufacturing Information (Section 4):** Design controls, manufacturing flow, critical suppliers, sterilization method. *(0.5)*
> 3. **Essential Principles Checklist (Section 5):** Matrix mapping each EP to applicable standard and evidence document. *(0.5)*
> 4. **Risk Management Summary (Section 6):** Summary of the ISO 14971 risk management process, residual risk evaluation, benefit-risk conclusion. *(0.5)*
> 5. **Verification and Validation Summary (Section 7):** Tabulated summary of design V&V activities — biocompatibility, electrical safety, EMC, software, usability, packaging. *(0.5)*
> 6. **Clinical Evidence Summary (Section 8):** Clinical evaluation report, literature review, clinical investigations, PMCF plan. *(0.5)*
