## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** confuse the STED with the *full* Technical Documentation. Examiners *expect* you to explicitly state the subset relationship: $\text{STED} \subset \text{TD}$.
> - **Do NOT** describe the STED as an *ISO 13485* document. STED is a **regulatory submission** document; ISO 13485 governs the QMS that *produces* the underlying TD. This distinction is a common 1-mark loss.
> - **Do NOT** omit the **Essential Principles (EP) Checklist** when asked about STED structure. It is the *heart* of the document and worth 2–3 marks by itself.
> - **Do NOT** use the phrase "STED replaces the Design Dossier" — the Design Dossier is an *expanded* form of Technical Documentation for Class III devices in the EU, while STED is a *summary* format.
> - **Always** mention the **GHTF origin** and the **IMDRF ToC evolution** for full KTU 2024-scheme credit.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**"Explain in detail the Summary Technical Document (STED) for medical device regulatory submissions. Discuss (a) the origin, purpose, and regulatory significance of STED, and (b) the detailed structure and content of each section."**

#### Part (a) — Origin, Purpose, and Regulatory Significance (7 marks)

The **Summary Technical Document (STED)** originated from the **Global Harmonization Task Force (GHTF)**, an international voluntary consortium formed in 1992 comprising regulatory authorities and industry stakeholders from the EU, US, Canada, Japan, and Australia. The foundational guidance **GHTF SG1/N011R12 (2005)** formally defined the STED format with the explicit objective of **regulatory harmonization**. **(2 marks)**

The **purpose** of STED is fourfold:
1. To provide a **single harmonized dossier format** acceptable across multiple regulatory jurisdictions. **(1 mark)**
2. To **eliminate the duplication** of test reports, clinical data, and risk analyses that were historically required in different formats for different regulators. **(1 mark)**
3. To **accelerate market access** for innovative medical devices by reducing the documentation burden on manufacturers, particularly SMEs. **(1 mark)**
4. To **preserve the manufacturer's right** to retain full technical documentation in their QMS archive while submitting a curated summary for regulatory review. **(1 mark)**

The **regulatory significance** of STED is profound: it forms the foundational structure adopted (or accepted) by:
- **US FDA** (via the modern **eSTAR ToC** and the older 510(k) format).
- **EU Notified Bodies** (under **MDR 2017/745 Annex II**, MDCG 2019-9 Rev. 1 guidance).
- **Health Canada** (historically and currently).
- **TGA Australia** (TGA Australian Regulatory Guidelines for Medical Devices — ARGM).
- **PMDA Japan** (Pre-market submission dossier).
- **MDSAP** (Medical Device Single Audit Program) audit reference. **(1 mark)**

The STED was succeeded and refined by the **IMDRF Table of Contents (ToC)** after the GHTF was disbanded in 2012, but the structural lineage and intent remain identical. **(Bonus / 0.5 mark optional for 7-mark completeness.)**

#### Part (b) — Structure and Content of Each Section (7 marks)

The STED consists of **10 mandatory sections** as follows:

| § | Section | Content (Brief) | Marks Allocation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cover Page** | Manufacturer details, device name, model, classification, signatory, version | 0.5 |
| 2 | **Table of Contents** | Hierarchical index of all sections and appendices | 0.5 |
| 3 | **Device Description** | Intended use, indications, contraindications, variants, materials, operating principle, accessories | 1.0 |
| 4 | **Design and Manufacturing Info** | Design control summary, manufacturing flow, critical suppliers, sterilization method | 0.5 |
| 5 | **Essential Principles Checklist** | EP matrix: clause, applicability, standard, evidence reference — the *most critical* section | 1.5 |
| 6 | **Risk Management Summary** | ISO 14971 summary, residual risk, benefit-risk conclusion | 0.5 |
| 7 | **V&V Summary** | Tabulated test results: biocompatibility (ISO 10993), electrical safety (IEC 60601-1), EMC, software (IEC 62304), usability (IEC 62366-1), packaging (ISO 11607), shelf-life | 1.0 |
| 8 | **Clinical Evidence Summary** | CER, literature review, clinical study data (ISO 14155), PMCF plan, MEDDEV 2.7/1 Rev 4 | 1.0 |
| 9 | **Labeling and IFU** | Primary/secondary labels, sterile barrier labels, IFU, patient implant card, e-IFU rationale, ISO 15223-1 symbols | 0.5 |
| 10 | **Regulatory Info** | Market history, prior approvals, ISO 13485 cert, MDSAP cert, Declaration of Conformity | 0.5 |

*(7 marks total, distributed as above.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**"Compare and contrast the Summary Technical Document (STED) with other major medical device regulatory submission formats. Discuss (a) STED vs. EU Technical Documentation (MDR Annex II) and the Design Dossier, and (b) the role of STED in global regulatory harmonization and its evolution to the IMDRF Table of Contents."**

#### Part (a) — STED vs. EU Technical Documentation and Design Dossier (7 marks)

The **EU Technical Documentation (TD)** under **MDR 2017/745 Annex II** is structurally similar to STED but more **prescriptive** and **EU-specific**. Both share:
- Device description, design & manufacturing info, risk management, V&V, clinical evidence, labeling.
- The *Essential Principles* concept (called *General Safety and Performance Requirements — GSPR* in the EU MDR). **(1 mark)**

Key **differences** between STED and EU TD:

| Aspect | STED (GHTF) | EU TD (MDR Annex II) |
|---|---|---|
| **Format** | 10-section harmonized | 8-section prescriptive (Annex II) |
| **Scope** | Globally acceptable | EU-specific |
| **Risk Class Coverage** | All classes | All classes (Class III → Design Dossier) |
| **GSPR/EP Detail** | ~19 Essential Principles | 23 GSPRs in Annex I + Section 3 of Annex II |
| **Post-Market Section** | Implicit in Section 10 | Explicit PMS/PMCF section required (Annex III) |
| **Notified Body Involvement** | Variable by jurisdiction | Mandatory for Class IIa, IIb, III |

The **Design Dossier** (DD) is the *expanded* version of TD required for **Class III medical devices** and **active implantable medical devices (AIMD)** under the EU MDR. **(1 mark)**

The relationship is hierarchical:
$$\text{STED} \;\subset\; \text{TD (Annex II)} \;\subset\; \text{Design Dossier (Class III)}$$

- **STED** is a *summary* — submitted for review.
- **TD** is the *complete* body of evidence retained by the manufacturer.
- **DD** is the *full Class III dossier* including comprehensive design history, full clinical investigation reports, biocompatibility test reports (not summaries), manufacturing validation protocols, and post-market surveillance plans. **(2 marks)**

Despite the structural similarities, the EU does **not formally call its submission a STED** — it uses the term *Technical Documentation*. However, **MDCG 2019-9 Rev. 1** guidance confirms that manufacturers may use a STED-style structure provided all MDR Annex II/III requirements are met. **(1 mark)**

For **Class I devices (non-sterile, non-measuring, no measuring function)**, neither a TD nor a STED is required for CE marking — only **self-declaration** by the manufacturer is sufficient, although many manufacturers voluntarily prepare a simplified technical file. **(0.5 mark)**

For **Class IIa, IIb devices**, a TD (prepared in STED-compatible format) is reviewed by the **Notified Body**. For **Class III and AIMD**, a **Design Dossier** undergoes rigorous review with possible consultation of the **Medical Device Coordination Group (MDCG)** and expert panels. **(1.5 marks)**

#### Part (b) — STED in Global Harmonization & Evolution to IMDRF ToC (7 marks)

The STED framework was conceived as the *cornerstone of global regulatory harmonization*. Its **three primary contributions** to harmonization are: **(1 mark)**

1. **Common Language:** STED introduced a globally shared vocabulary of "Essential Principles," "Verification & Validation," "Clinical Evidence," and "Risk Management" — replacing the previously fragmented terminologies. **(1 mark)**

2. **Reduced Duplication:** A STED compiled once could (in principle) be submitted to multiple GHTF-member regulators with minimal jurisdiction-specific addenda. This reduced testing redundancy and lowered market entry barriers. **(1 mark)**

3. **Audit Convergence:** The **MDSAP** (Medical Device Single Audit Program) uses a STED-aligned structure, enabling a single audit to satisfy regulatory requirements across US, Canada, Japan, Australia, and Brazil. **(1 mark)**

The **GHTF was disbanded in 2012** and succeeded by the **International Medical Device Regulators Forum (IMDRF)**, comprising regulators from the same core jurisdictions. The IMDRF published the **IMDRF Table of Contents (ToC) structure** in 2014, which **refined the STED format** with: **(1 mark)**

- A standardized **non-binding neutral format** acceptable to all member regulators.
- Refined **chapter numbering** and **preface structure**.
- Explicit alignment with the **ISO 13485:2016 QMS structure**.

The **IMDRF ToC has been adopted** by:
- **US FDA** as the basis of the **eSTAR** (electronic Submission Template And Resource) portal for 510(k) and De Novo submissions.
- **TGA Australia** as the basis of its **ToC-based submission** for inclusion in the **ARTG**.
- **Health Canada** as the basis of its **medical device licence application**.
- **EU** as an *acceptable format* under **MDR Annex II** (per MDCG guidance). **(1 mark)**

The **benefits of STED → IMDRF ToC migration** include:
- **Digital readiness** — the ToC is designed for electronic submission and automated review.
- **Continuous evolution** — IMDRF ToC is updated regularly to reflect new device categories (e.g., SaMD, combination products).
- **Stakeholder inclusivity** — patient and academic representative input is now incorporated.

The **limitations of STED** (which IMDRF ToC attempts to address) include:
- It was *non-binding* and *voluntary* — adoption varied.
- It did not initially cover **software as a medical device (SaMD)** — IMDRF has published separate SaMD guidance.
- It was static — IMDRF ToC is a *living* document with version updates. **(1 mark)**

> **Conclusion (Bonus / 0.5 mark):** STED represents one of the most successful harmonization initiatives in medical device history, with its DNA preserved and enhanced in the IMDRF ToC. KTU engineers entering the medical device industry must understand the STED framework as the *baseline* reference for any technical regulatory submission.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- [x] **STED = Summary Technical Document** — a *summary* subset of the full Technical Documentation, NOT a replacement for it.
- [x] **Origin:** GHTF (Global Harmonization Task Force) guidance **SG1/N011R12 (2005)**.
- [x] **Purpose:** Harmonize submission dossiers across multiple jurisdictions, reduce duplication, accelerate market access.
- [x] **Structure:** 10 mandatory sections, of which the **Essential Principles Checklist (Section 5)** is the most critical.
- [x] **Governing Standards Cross-Referenced Inside STED:** ISO 13485 (QMS), ISO 14971 (Risk), ISO 10993 (Biocompatibility), IEC 60601-1 (Electrical Safety), IEC 62304 (Software), IEC 62366-1 (Usability), ISO 14155 (Clinical Investigation), ISO 11607 (Packaging), ISO 15223-1 (Symbols), MEDDEV 2.7/1 Rev 4 / MDR Article 61 (Clinical Evaluation).
- [x] **Hierarchy:** $\text{STED} \;\subset\; \text{TD} \;\subset\; \text{Design Dossier (Class III EU)}$.
- [x] **Acceptance:** US FDA (eSTAR), EU MDR (MDCG 2019-9 Rev. 1), Health Canada, TGA Australia, PMDA Japan, MDSAP.
- [x] **Evolution:** GHTF STED (2005) → IMDRF ToC (2014) → Regulator-specific ToC-based e-submissions (2018–2024).
- [x] **Difference from 510(k):** 510(k) is a US-specific *premarket notification* relying on substantial equivalence; STED is a *globally harmonized* summary dossier format.
- [x] **Confidentiality:** The full TD remains in the manufacturer's QMS archive; the STED is the public-facing (or regulator-facing) subset.
- [x] **MDSAP Linkage:** STED is the *audit reference* for MDSAP — a single STED-aligned audit can serve multiple regulators.
- [x] **EU Class I Exception:** Non-sterile, non-measuring Class I devices do not require a Notified Body-reviewed TD; a self-declaration suffices.
- [x] **PMCF Linkage:** The PMCF (Post-Market Clinical Follow-up) plan is part of STED Section 8 and is a critical focus of EU MDR.
- [x] **For 14-mark KTU answers:** Always (i) define STED with GHTF origin, (ii) list 10 sections, (iii) elaborate on Section 5 (EP checklist) and Section 7 (V&V), (iv) mention the IMDRF ToC evolution, and (v) cite at least 3 cross-referenced ISO/IEC standards.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global Medical Device Nomenclature (GMDN) - WHO Regulations of Medical Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Global Medical Device Nomenclature (GMDN) - WHO Regulations of Medical Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Global Medical Device Nomenclature (GMDN) — Formal Definition

**Global Medical Device Nomenclature (GMDN)** is the internationally recognized, standardized system of generic descriptors used to identify the **intended purpose**, **functional characteristics**, and **technological classification** of medical devices. It is the official nomenclature endorsed by the **International Medical Device Regulators Forum (IMDRF)** — formerly the Global Harmonization Task Force (GHTF) — and serves as the primary data-exchange vocabulary for regulatory authorities, conformity assessment bodies, manufacturers, and healthcare providers worldwide.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Under the KTU PEBMT861 syllabus (Module 3), GMDN is treated as a **mandatory regulatory data element** for medical device registration, adverse-event reporting, and post-market surveillance. The current operative version is **GMDN 2024 (Membership Edition)**, comprising over **24,000 active Term Names** mapped to approximately **4,000 Collective Terms**.

The nomenclature is **generic** (not brand-specific), **multi-lingual**, and **hierarchical** in nature. Each medical device is identified by a **5-digit numeric GMDN Code** that maps to a single **Term Name** with a structured definition. A corresponding **Collective Term** groups multiple related Term Names under a broader device family (e.g., "Stents" as a collective term containing coronary stents, peripheral stents, biliary stents, etc.).

> [!NOTE] **Key Statistic (Kerala University Board Reference):**
> As of 2024, **>90 regulators** across **>30 countries** (including India's CDSCO, US FDA, EU MDR 2017/745, Japan's PMDA, Australia's TGA, and Brazil's ANVISA) mandate or accept GMDN codes on regulatory submissions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Universal Library Card for Medical Devices"

Imagine walking into the world's largest library containing **hundreds of thousands** of medical devices — pacemakers, infusion pumps, MRI scanners, surgical gloves, contact lenses — each with countless model numbers, trade names, and regional part codes. Without a unified cataloging system, regulators, hospitals, and post-market surveillance teams would be unable to:

- Identify a device when a manufacturer reports an adverse event.
- Compare safety data across similar devices from different companies.
- Trace field-safety corrective actions globally.

**GMDN is the Dewey Decimal System of Medical Devices.**

Just as the Dewey Decimal System assigns every book a standardized number based on its subject, GMDN assigns every medical device a **5-digit code** based on its:
1. **What it is** (generic device type)
2. **What it does** (intended medical purpose)
3. **How it interacts with the body** (invasive vs. non-invasive, active vs. passive, implantable vs. external)

This makes adverse-event reporting, recall management, and UDI (Unique Device Identification) databases universally interoperable.

## 1.3 WHO Regulations of Medical Devices — Formal Definition

The **World Health Organization (WHO)** does not directly regulate medical devices in sovereign nations. Instead, WHO operates as the **global normative authority** that publishes model frameworks, technical guidance, and policy recommendations which member states adapt into binding national law. The principal WHO document for medical device regulation is:

> **WHO Global Model Regulatory Framework for Medical Devices including IVDs (2017, with 2023 revision updates).**

This framework defines four primary regulatory pillars:
1. **Establishing a national regulatory authority (NRA)** for medical devices.
2. **Risk-based classification** of devices (Classes A, B, C, D).
3. **Pre-market evaluation** (registration, conformity assessment).
4. **Post-market surveillance (PMS)** and vigilance.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Critical Distinction (Frequently tested in KTU):**
> - **WHO** → Sets *normative* global standards, no enforcement power.
> - **National Regulators (CDSCO, FDA, MDR, PMDA)** → Enforce *legal* requirements.
> - **IMDRF** → Harmonizes *technical* standards across regulators.
> - **GMDN Agency** → Provides the *nomenclature* (vocabulary) layer.

## 1.4 The WHO 4-Tier Risk-Based Classification Model

| WHO Class | Risk Level | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| A | Low | Surgical gloves, stethoscopes, examination tables |
| B | Low–Moderate | Hypodermic needles, infusion sets, suction equipment |
| C | Moderate–High | Dialysis machines, ventilators, surgical lasers |
| D | High | Implantable pacemakers, heart valves, coronary stents |

## 1.5 Core Term Set — Mandatory KTU Vocabulary

The following terms appear repeatedly in KTU Module 3 questions and must be memorized verbatim:

- **Generic Device Group (GDG):** A broad category of devices with similar intended purpose.
- **Term Name (TN):** The specific generic descriptor for a single device type.
- **Collective Term (CT):** A grouping of multiple Term Names sharing a common attribute.
- **GMDN Code:** A unique 5-digit numeric identifier (e.g., 47317 = "Pacemaker, implantable").
- **Conformity Assessment:** The process verifying a device meets regulatory requirements.
- **Vigilance:** The systematic monitoring of adverse events and field-safety corrective actions.
- **UUDI (Unique Device Identification):** A globally harmonized numeric/alphabetic code for device traceability.

> [!NOTE] **Memory Aid:** "**G**roup → **N**ame → **C**ode" corresponds to **GDG → TN → GMDN Code**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of the GMDN System — Structural Hierarchy

The GMDN operates on a **three-tier structural model** that allows granular identification of medical devices:

### Tier 1 — Category
A broad regulatory grouping (e.g., "Cardiovascular Devices," "Orthopedic Devices," "In-Vitro Diagnostic Devices").

### Tier 2 — Generic Device Group (GDG)
A sub-category within the Category, defined by intended purpose and primary functional mechanism.

### Tier 3 — Term Name (TN)
The most granular level — a single, uniquely defined generic descriptor. **Each Term Name carries a 5-digit GMDN Code** that is **non-hierarchical** (i.e., adjacent codes do not imply family relation).

## 2.2 The GMDN Code Logic — Why Non-Hierarchical?

> [!IMPORTANT] **Critical KTU Concept:**
> Unlike ICD-10 medical codes (which are strictly hierarchical: each digit refines the previous), **GMDN codes are non-hierarchical identifiers**. The digits do NOT carry positional meaning. Code 35162 is not "related to" code 35163.

This design choice ensures:
- **Stability:** Adding new devices does not require renumbering existing ones.
- **Independence:** Manufacturers can reference codes without revealing proprietary design hierarchies.
- **Searchability:** The GMDN Term Browser supports keyword, category, and CT-based searches.

## 2.3 GMDN Membership and Access Model

The GMDN Agency operates on a **paid-membership model** for manufacturers, regulators, and healthcare institutions. Membership tiers include:

| Membership Type | Annual Cost Range (USD) | Privileges |
|---|---|---|
| Regulators (Government) | Free | Full read access, official code lists |
| Manufacturers (Standard) | ~$1,200–$4,500 | Term lookup, new term requests |
| Healthcare Providers | Variable | Browsing and adverse-event coding |
| Conformity Assessment Bodies | Subsidized | Full database for audit purposes |

> [!NOTE] **WHO vs. GMDN Agency — Separation of Roles:**
> The GMDN Agency is an **independent non-profit organization** based in the United Kingdom. It is **not** a WHO subsidiary, though WHO officially **endorses** GMDN as the preferred nomenclature.

## 2.4 WHO's Five Core Regulatory Functions for Medical Devices

WHO's Global Model Regulatory Framework identifies **five essential regulatory functions (ERFs)**:

1. **ERF-1: National Regulatory System** — Establish legal framework, NRA, transparent policies.
2. **ERF-2: Registration & Listing** — Compulsory registration of manufacturers, devices, and IVDs.
3. **ERF-3: Pre-market Evaluation** — Conformity assessment based on risk class.
4. **ERF-4: Market Surveillance & Vigilance** — Post-market monitoring, adverse-event reporting.
5. **ERF-5: Quality Management System (QMS) Enforcement** — ISO 13485 compliance verification.

## 2.5 The WHO Risk-Based Classification Equation (Conceptual)

While not a literal mathematical equation, the WHO classification of a medical device follows a **risk-multiplicative logic**:

$$R_{device} = f\big( P_{harm} \times S_{harm} \times D_{exposure} \big)$$

Where:
- $R_{device}$ → Overall regulatory risk class (A, B, C, D)
- $P_{harm}$ → Probability of harm occurring
- $S_{harm}$ → Severity of harm (negligible → catastrophic)
- $D_{exposure}$ → Duration and mode of exposure (transient < short-term < long-term < permanent implant)

Devices with **longer exposure durations**, **higher invasiveness**, and **life-sustaining function** escalate the class.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Example — A Central Venous Catheter:**
> - Short-term use → Class B
> - Long-term implantable → Class C
> - Drug-eluting (combination product) → Class D

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 3)

| # | Concept | Definition / Key Parameter | Source Authority | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | GMDN Code Length | **5 digits** (non-hierarchical) | GMDN Agency | Very High |
| 2 | Number of Term Names | ~**24,000+** active | GMDN 2024 | High |
| 3 | Number of Collective Terms | ~**4,000** | GMDN 2024 | Medium |
| 4 | WHO Risk Classes | **A, B, C, D** (low → high) | WHO 2017 Framework | Very High |
| 5 | Number of WHO ERFs | **5** essential regulatory functions | WHO 2017 | Very High |
| 6 | GMDN Hierarchy Levels | **Category → GDG → TN** | GMDN Agency | High |
| 7 | WHO Document | Global Model Regulatory Framework | WHO 2017 (+ 2023 updates) | Very High |
| 8 | Key IMDRF Documents | SaMD (2014), UDI (2019), PMS (2020) | IMDRF | High |
| 9 | Combination Product | Drug-device / biologic-device (e.g., drug-eluting stent) | WHO + IMDRF | Medium |
| 10 | Membership Cost (Manufacturer) | ~USD **1,200–4,500/year** | GMDN Agency | Low |
| 11 | Adverse Event Reporting Timeline | **15 days** (serious) → **10 days** (death/unexpected) | EU MDR Article 87 | High |
| 12 | UDI-DI vs UDI-PI | Device Identifier vs. Production Identifier | IMDRF UDI 2019 | High |
| 13 | SaMD Risk Categorization | States I (low) → State IV (high) | IMDRF SaMD N12 | High |
| 14 | WHO Member State Mandate | Article 2(j) of WHO Constitution | WHO | Low |
| 15 | Indian Adoption (CDSCO) | GMDN accepted for registration forms | CDSCO Notice 2022 | High |

> [!WARNING] **Pitfall Note:** Do NOT confuse **GMDN** (generic nomenclature for devices) with **ICD-10/11** (diseases), **ATC** (drugs), or **SNOMED-CT** (clinical terms). Each is a separate terminology system.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of GMDN

For a biomedical engineer, GMDN codes appear in:

- **Regulatory submissions** to CDSCO (India), FDA (US), notified bodies (EU).
- **UDID database** entries in the Indian medical device registry.
- **Hospital inventory systems** (HIS/ERP) for procurement and recall management.
- **Post-market surveillance (PMS) software** for adverse-event coding.
- **Clinical investigation protocols** (ISO 14155) for device description.

GMDN also enables **interoperability** between the European EUDAMED database, the US FDA's AccessGUDID, and India's MDMR (Medical Device Monitoring Registry) — a foundational requirement for **global supply-chain traceability** in the post-COVID medical device ecosystem.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the GMDN Code Assignment Workflow

The GMDN code assignment follows a rigorous, auditable procedure. Below is the step-by-step derivation logic used by manufacturers, regulators, and the GMDN Agency:

### Step 1 — Device Identification (Generic Layer)
**Logic:** Strip away all brand, model, and proprietary information.
**Result:** A pure generic description (e.g., "A battery-powered, implantable, multi-programmable pulse generator that delivers electrical stimulation to cardiac chambers to treat bradyarrhythmia").

### Step 2 — Intended Purpose Determination
**Logic:** Map the device to its regulated medical purpose as defined by the manufacturer (per **ISO 14971:2019** risk management standard).
**Result:** A concise clinical function statement.

### Step 3 — Search the GMDN Term Browser
**Logic:** The GMDN Member Portal allows keyword, category, and CT-based search.
**Result:** A candidate list of Term Names.

### Step 4 — Term Name Selection
**Logic:** Select the most specific TN whose definition **fully matches** the device's generic characteristics, technological features, and intended purpose.
**Result:** A 5-digit GMDN Code (e.g., **47317** → "Pacemaker, implantable, dual-chamber").

### Step 5 — New Term Request (If No Match Exists)
**Logic:** If no TN fits, the manufacturer submits a "New Term Request" to the GMDN Agency.
**Result:** A new code is generated after review (typical lead time: **60–90 days**).

### Step 6 — Code Locking and Regulatory Submission
**Logic:** The code is locked for the device's regulatory lifecycle and travels with the device through conformity assessment, post-market surveillance, and any field-safety corrective actions.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Mathematical Analogy:**
> Treat GMDN as a **bijective mapping** $f: D \to C$, where every device $d \in D$ maps to exactly one code $c \in C$, and no two distinct devices share the same code. Formally:
> $$f(d_1) = f(d_2) \iff d_1 = d_2 \quad \text{(injective and surjective)}$$

## 3.2 Deriving the WHO Risk Class — Step-by-Step Algorithm

WHO's risk-based classification of a medical device can be derived using a **four-question decision matrix**:

### Question 1 — Is the device invasive?

$$R = R_0 + \Delta R_{invasive}$$

If "No" → Class A or B.
If "Yes" → Proceed to Question 2.

### Question 2 — Is the device implantable or in contact with central nervous system / blood?

$$R = R_{invasive} + \Delta R_{implant}$$

If "Yes" → Minimum Class C, possibly D.

### Question 3 — Is the device life-sustaining or life-supporting?

$$R = R_{implant} + \Delta R_{life}$$

If "Yes" → Class C or D.

### Question 4 — Is the device a combination product (drug + device / biologic + device)?

$$R = R_{life} + \Delta R_{combo}$$

If "Yes" → Class D (highest).

### Final Risk Class Calculation:

$$R_{final} = R_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta R_i$$

Where $R_0$ is the baseline risk (low) and each $\Delta R_i$ is a positive increment corresponding to the specific risk factors.

> [!NOTE] **Worked Example — Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator (ICD):**
> 1. Invasive? **Yes** → $+1$
> 2. Implantable + in contact with blood? **Yes** → $+1$
> 3. Life-sustaining? **Yes** → $+1$
> 4. Combination product? **No** (pure electrical device) → $0$
>
> Cumulative risk score = $R_0 + 3$ → **Class D** (highest).

## 3.3 GMDN Mapping for IMDRF SaMD Risk Categorization

For **Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)**, IMDRF/N12 defines four risk categories (State I–IV). GMDN codes apply to SaMD as well, with the following mapping:

$$SaMD_{risk} = f\big( S_{significance} \times I_{state} \big)$$

| SaMD State | Significance of Information | Patient Impact if Wrong | Example GMDN Code |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | Informs clinical management | Non-serious | 64794 — Software, clinical decision support, low-risk |
| II | Drives clinical management | Serious injury | 64795 — Software, infusion dosing, moderate-risk |
| III | Treats or diagnoses | Serious injury | 64796 — Software, cardiac arrhythmia detection |
| IV | Treats or diagnoses | Death or life-threatening | 64797 — Software, autonomous closed-loop insulin delivery |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — WHO ERF Mapping to GMDN Function

The following matrix maps the five WHO Essential Regulatory Functions to their corresponding GMDN-related activities:

| WHO ERF | Primary Activity | GMDN Role | Responsible Party |
|---|---|---|---|
| ERF-1: National Regulatory System | Establish legal framework | Code standard adoption | Government / NRA |
| ERF-2: Registration & Listing | Device registry creation | **GMDN codes as mandatory data field** | NRA + Manufacturer |
| ERF-3: Pre-market Evaluation | Conformity assessment | Code-driven risk class determination | Notified Body |
| ERF-4: Market Surveillance & Vigilance | Adverse-event reporting | **GMDN code enables event aggregation** | NRA + Manufacturer |
| ERF-5: QMS Enforcement | ISO 13485 audits | Code consistency in technical files | Audit Body |

## 3.5 Mathematical Formulation of Adverse-Event Risk Index

The **Adverse-Event Risk Index (AERI)** used in PMS reporting can be expressed as:

$$AERI = \frac{N_{events} \times S_{severity}}{N_{units\_sold} \times T_{exposure}}$$

Where:
- $N_{events}$ → Number of reported adverse events
- $S_{severity}$ → Severity score (1 = minor, 2 = serious, 3 = death)
- $N_{units\_sold}$ → Total units sold/distributed
- $T_{exposure}$ → Average exposure period (patient-years)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Insight:**
> A high AERI triggers **mandatory field-safety corrective action (FSCA)**, which under EU MDR 2017/745 Article 87 must be reported to the competent authority within **15 days** for serious incidents and **10 days** for incidents involving death or unexpected serious deterioration.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GMDN Three-Tier Hierarchy — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CATEGORY Cardiovascular Devices"] --> B["GDG Implantable Pulse Generators"]
    A --> C["GDG Vascular Catheters"]
    A --> D["GDG Heart Valves"]
    
    B --> E["TN Pacemaker single chamber Code 47316"]
    B --> F["TN Pacemaker dual chamber Code 47317"]
    B --> G["TN Pacemaker biventricular Code 47318"]
    B --> H["TN Defibrillator implantable Code 61736"]
    
    C --> I["TN Catheter cardiac balloon Code 35162"]
    C --> J["TN Catheter electrophysiology Code 35164"]
    C --> K["TN Catheter ablation Code 61906"]
    
    D --> L["TN Valve mechanical heart Code 47401"]
    D --> M["TN Valve bioprosthetic heart Code 47402"]
    D --> N["TN Valve transcatheter heart Code 62485"]
    
    E --> O["COLLECTIVE TERM Pacemakers Implantable"]
    F --> O
    G --> O
    H --> P["COLLECTIVE TERM Implantable Defibrillators"]
    L --> Q["COLLECTIVE TERM Heart Valve Prostheses"]
    M --> Q
    N --> Q
```

## 4.2 WHO Five Essential Regulatory Functions (ERF) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WHO["WHO GLOBAL MODEL FRAMEWORK"]
        E1["ERF 1 National Regulatory System"]
        E2["ERF 2 Registration and Listing"]
        E3["ERF 3 Pre market Evaluation"]
        E4["ERF 4 Market Surveillance and Vigilance"]
        E5["ERF 5 QMS Enforcement"]
    end
    
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> E4
    E4 --> E5
    E5 --> E1
    
    E2 -.uses.-> G["GMDN Codes"]
    E3 -.uses.-> G
    E4 -.uses.-> G
    G["GMDN AGENCY Nomenclature Provider"] --> E1
```

## 4.3 GMDN Code Assignment Workflow (Decision Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start Device Identification"] --> S2["Describe generic characteristics"]
    S2 --> S3["Determine intended medical purpose"]
    S3 --> S4["Search GMDN Term Browser"]
    S4 --> S5{Match Found?}
    S5 -->|Yes| S6["Select most specific Term Name"]
    S5 -->|No| S7["Submit New Term Request"]
    S7 --> S8["GMDN Agency Review 60 to 90 days"]
    S8 --> S9["New code issued"]
    S6 --> S10["Lock 5 digit GMDN Code"]
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11["Submit to NRA for regulatory approval"]
    S11 --> S12["End Code active in registries"]
```

## 4.4 WHO Risk-Based Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0["Start Device Analysis"] --> R1{"Is the device non invasive?"}
    R1 -->|Yes| R2["Class A or B"]
    R1 -->|No| R3{"Invasive but not implantable?"}
    R3 -->|Yes| R4["Class B or C"]
    R3 -->|No| R5{"Implantable or CNS contact?"}
    R5 -->|Yes| R6{"Life sustaining or supporting?"}
    R6 -->|No| R7["Class C"]
    R6 -->|Yes| R8{"Combination product drug device?"}
    R8 -->|No| R9["Class D"]
    R8 -->|Yes| R10["Class D with combo review"]
    R2 --> R11["Final Class Assignment"]
    R4 --> R11
    R7 --> R11
    R9 --> R11
    R10 --> R11
```

## 4.5 Global Regulatory Ecosystem — Interoperability Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GLOBAL["GLOBAL STANDARDS LAYER"]
        WHO["WHO Model Framework"]
        IMDRF["IMDRF Guidance"]
        ISO["ISO 13485 14971 Standards"]
    end
    
    subgraph NORM["NOMENCLATURE LAYER"]
        GMDN["GMDN Agency"]
        UDI["IMDRF UDI System"]
    end
    
    subgraph REG["NATIONAL REGULATORS"]
        FDA["US FDA"]
        EU["EU MDR 2017 745"]
        CDSCO["India CDSCO"]
        PMDA["Japan PMDA"]
        TGA["Australia TGA"]
    end
    
    subgraph USER["END USERS"]
        MFR["Manufacturers"]
        HOSP["Hospitals HIS ERP"]
        PMS["Post Market Surveillance Systems"]
    end
    
    WHO --> GMDN
    IMDRF --> GMDN
    IMDRF --> UDI
    GMDN --> REG
    UDI --> REG
    ISO --> REG
    REG --> MFR
    REG --> HOSP
    REG --> PMS
    PMS --> REG
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Reading Tip:** Always start at the **uppermost block** of a regulatory architecture diagram and follow the arrows downward. For a KTU 14-mark question, students are expected to redraw a similar architecture with at least **6–8 labeled nodes**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define GMDN. State its hierarchical structure and the number of active Term Names as of 2024.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**GMDN (Global Medical Device Nomenclature)** is an internationally recognized system of generic descriptors used to identify medical devices for regulatory, post-market surveillance, and UDI purposes. It is maintained by the GMDN Agency, a UK-based non-profit organization, and is endorsed by the International Medical Device Regulators Forum (IMDRF).

**Hierarchical Structure:**
- **Tier 1:** Category (e.g., Cardiovascular Devices)
- **Tier 2:** Generic Device Group (GDG)
- **Tier 3:** Term Name (TN) with a unique 5-digit numeric code

**Number of active Term Names (2024):** Approximately **24,000+ active Term Names**, grouped under ~**4,000 Collective Terms**.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

---

### Question 2: List the five Essential Regulatory Functions (ERFs) defined by the WHO Global Model Regulatory Framework for Medical Devices.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The five ERFs defined by the WHO Global Model Regulatory Framework (2017) are:

1. **ERF-1:** National Regulatory System — establishing legal and institutional frameworks.
2. **ERF-2:** Registration and Listing of medical devices.
3. **ERF-3:** Pre-market Evaluation / Conformity Assessment.
4. **ERF-4:** Market Surveillance and Vigilance (post-market).
5. **ERF-5:** Quality Management System (QMS) Enforcement — based on ISO 13485.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the architecture of the Global Medical Device Nomenclature (GMDN) system. Discuss the differences between Term Names, Collective Terms, and Generic Device Groups, citing at least two examples for each. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Describe the WHO's role in regulating medical devices worldwide. Outline the four-tier risk-based classification used by WHO and explain the risk-multiplicative logic with a worked example. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition and Purpose of GMDN (1 Mark)**

GMDN is a standardized, generic nomenclature for medical devices, used globally for regulatory submissions, UDI labeling, and post-market surveillance. It is endorsed by IMDRF and adopted by over 90 regulators worldwide.

**2. Three-Tier Hierarchy (3 Marks)**

| Tier | Level | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Category | Broadest regulatory grouping | "Cardiovascular Devices" |
| 2 | Generic Device Group (GDG) | A sub-category defined by intended purpose and mechanism | "Implantable Pulse Generators" |
| 3 | Term Name (TN) | Most granular level; unique 5-digit code | TN: "Pacemaker, dual-chamber, implantable" (Code 47317) |

**3. Distinguishing the Three Levels (2 Marks)**

- **Term Name (TN):** A specific, uniquely defined generic descriptor. Each TN has a single 5-digit code. Codes are **non-hierarchical** (e.g., 47316 and 47317 do not imply family relationship).
  - *Example 1:* TN 61736 — Defibrillator, implantable, single-chamber.
  - *Example 2:* TN 61906 — Catheter, ablation, cardiac.

- **Collective Term (CT):** A grouping of multiple Term Names sharing a common attribute. Used for high-level regulatory reporting and adverse-event aggregation.
  - *Example 1:* CT "Pacemakers, implantable" — contains multiple pacemaker TNs.
  - *Example 2:* CT "Heart valve prostheses" — groups mechanical, bioprosthetic, and transcatheter valves.

- **Generic Device Group (GDG):** A mid-level descriptor that groups Term Names by their broad functional mechanism or intended purpose.
  - *Example 1:* GDG "Vascular Catheters."
  - *Example 2:* GDG "Imaging Diagnostic Equipment."

**4. Key Properties (1 Mark)**

- Non-hierarchical coding ensures code stability across device lifecycles.
- Multi-lingual (English, French, Spanish, German, Italian, Portuguese, Chinese, Japanese).
- Maintained by the GMDN Agency (UK) on a paid-membership model.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**1. WHO's Role in Medical Device Regulation (2 Marks)**

The World Health Organization (WHO) operates as the **global normative authority** for medical devices. It does not enforce laws in sovereign nations. Instead, it publishes:

- The **Global Model Regulatory Framework for Medical Devices including IVDs (2017, updated 2023).**
- Technical guidance documents, normative standards, and capacity-building toolkits.
- Recommendations for establishing or strengthening National Regulatory Authorities (NRAs).

WHO's role is to harmonize **legal frameworks, technical standards, and PMS practices** across member states, particularly supporting low- and middle-income countries (LMICs).

**2. WHO's Four-Tier Risk-Based Classification (2 Marks)**

| Class | Risk Level | Device Examples |
|---|---|---|
| A | Low | Surgical gloves, stethoscopes, examination tables |
| B | Low–Moderate | Hypodermic needles, infusion sets, suction equipment |
| C | Moderate–High | Dialysis machines, surgical lasers, ventilators |
| D | High | Implantable pacemakers, heart valves, drug-eluting stents |

**3. Risk-Multiplicative Logic (2 Marks)**

The overall risk class is derived from three factors:

$$R_{device} = f(P_{harm} \times S_{harm} \times D_{exposure})$$

- $P_{harm}$ → Probability of harm
- $S_{harm}$ → Severity of harm
- $D_{exposure}$ → Duration of exposure

**4. Worked Example: Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator (ICD) (1 Mark)**

- $P_{harm}$ = moderate (electrical/lead complications possible)
- $S_{harm}$ = high (failure can cause death)
- $D_{exposure}$ = permanent implant
- **Result:** Risk class = **D** (highest), requiring extensive pre-market evaluation and post-market surveillance.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the relationship between WHO, IMDRF, the GMDN Agency, and national regulators. Explain why GMDN was adopted as the international nomenclature, with reference to its key advantages over proprietary systems. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Describe the application of GMDN codes in post-market surveillance (PMS), adverse-event reporting, and Unique Device Identification (UDI). Provide two regulatory scenarios illustrating code use. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Multi-Tier Regulatory Ecosystem (3 Marks)**

| Body | Role | Authority Type |
|---|---|---|
| **WHO** | Normative global framework, capacity building | Advisory |
| **IMDRF** | Technical harmonization, guidance documents | Voluntary consensus |
| **GMDN Agency** | Nomenclature provider, code database | Vocabulary authority |
| **National Regulators (CDSCO, FDA, EU MDR, PMDA)** | Enforce device registration and PMS in their jurisdiction | Legal mandate |

**Flow:** WHO publishes the model framework → IMDRF develops technical standards → GMDN Agency provides the naming vocabulary → National Regulators implement mandatory requirements for manufacturers in their country.

**2. Why GMDN Was Adopted (3 Marks)**

- **Interoperability:** Enables data exchange between EUDAMED (EU), GUDID (US), UDID (India), and other national registries.
- **Genericity:** Hides proprietary information while allowing device identification.
- **Non-hierarchical coding:** Prevents disruption when new devices are added.
- **Multi-lingual support:** Allows global use without translation errors.
- **Stability:** Codes are locked to a device's regulatory lifecycle, ensuring longitudinal traceability.

**3. Advantages Over Proprietary Systems (1 Mark)**

- Reduces regulatory burden (one code globally instead of multiple).
- Enables faster adverse-event identification and field-safety corrective action (FSCA) management.
- Supports UDI compliance under EU MDR 2017/745 Article 27.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**1. GMDN in Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) (2 Marks)**

When an adverse event is reported (e.g., a malfunctioning infusion pump), the manufacturer and NRA must:
- Log the event with the **GMDN Term Name and 5-digit code** (e.g., 13218 — Infusion pump, single-channel, electronic).
- Aggregate similar events across manufacturers using the same code.
- Trigger FSCA if the AERI threshold is exceeded.

**2. GMDN in Adverse-Event Reporting (2 Marks)**

Under EU MDR Article 87:
- Serious incidents → report within **15 days**.
- Incidents involving death or unexpected serious deterioration → **10 days**.
- The GMDN code forms the **primary key** linking the event to device family statistics.

**3. GMDN in UDI (1.5 Marks)**

The UDI system (IMDRF 2019) has two components:
- **UDI-DI (Device Identifier):** Identifies the device model — linked to the GMDN code.
- **UDI-PI (Production Identifier):** Identifies the production batch/serial/lot/expiry.

**4. Regulatory Scenarios (1.5 Marks)**

- **Scenario 1:** A US hospital reports 12 incidents of surgical stapler misfires. FDA's GUDID allows aggregating all staplers (GMDN code 61822) regardless of manufacturer, triggering a Class II recall.
- **Scenario 2:** CDSCO's UDID database identifies 200 cardiac stent events (GMDN code 47566) within 6 months, leading to PMS audit under the new Medical Devices Rules 2017.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing GMDN with ICD/ATC/SNOMED-CT:** GMDN is for *devices*, not diseases or drugs. (-1 Mark)
> 2. **Treating GMDN codes as hierarchical:** They are NON-hierarchical. Do not infer relationships between adjacent codes. (-1 Mark)
> 3. **Wrongly assigning WHO the role of enforcement:** WHO is normative, NOT regulatory. Enforcement is by national authorities. (-1 Mark)
> 4. **Missing the year of the WHO document:** Always cite "WHO Global Model Regulatory Framework 2017" or "with 2023 updates." (-0.5 Mark)
> 5. **Omitting examples in 7-mark sub-parts:** Always provide at least TWO examples in 7-mark answers. (-1 to -2 Marks)
> 6. **Forgetting the UDI-DI vs UDI-PI distinction in 14-mark questions.** (-1 Mark)
> 7. **Writing GMDN as a WHO product:** GMDN Agency is an **independent** non-profit. (-0.5 Mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3 — GMDN & WHO Regulations):**

- **GMDN** = Generic nomenclature, NOT a regulatory body, NOT a WHO department.
- **GMDN codes** = 5 digits, NON-hierarchical, mapped to Term Names.
- **Hierarchy:** Category → Generic Device Group (GDG) → Term Name (TN).
- **Active TNs:** ~24,000+ | **Collective Terms:** ~4,000+ (as of 2024).
- **GMDN Agency** = UK-based, independent, paid-membership.
- **WHO Model Framework (2017 + 2023)** = Normative, not legally binding.
- **WHO Risk Classes:** A (low) → B → C → D (high).
- **Five WHO ERFs:** Regulatory System, Registration, Pre-market, PMS, QMS.
- **Risk Equation (Conceptual):** $R = f(P \times S \times D)$.
- **UAE EU MDR Article 87 Timeline:** 15 days (serious) / 10 days (death).
- **UDI System:** UDI-DI (device) + UDI-PI (production), linked to GMDN.
- **SaMD Risk:** States I–IV per IMDRF/N12.
- **Combination Products:** Drug-device or biologic-device → Class D.
- **Adoption:** Mandatory in EU MDR, US FDA, accepted by CDSCO (India).
- **Interoperability:** EUDAMED ↔ GUDID ↔ UDID via GMDN.
- **AERI Formula:** $AERI = (N_{events} \times S_{severity}) / (N_{units} \times T_{exposure})$.
- **Key IMDRF Documents:** SaMD N12 (2014), UDI (2019), PMS N47 (2020).
- **Indian Context:** Medical Devices Rules 2017, CDSCO as NRA, UDID as central registry.
- **Membership Cost:** ~USD 1,200–4,500/year for manufacturers.
- **Master Mnemonic:** "**C**ategory, **G**roup, **N**ame" → CGN = **3 levels**.
- **Master Mnemonic:** "**A B C D**" → "**A**void **B**ad **C**are, **D**anger ahead" (low→high risk).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FDA Regulations of Medical Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** FDA Regulations of Medical Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 FDA (U.S. Food and Drug Administration)

The **U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA)** is the federal agency of the United States Department of Health and Human Services responsible for protecting public health by ensuring the safety, efficacy, and security of human and veterinary drugs, biological products, medical devices, food supply, cosmetics, and products that emit radiation. Within the FDA, the **Center for Devices and Radiological Health (CDRH)** is the specific branch that oversees the regulation of medical devices and radiation-emitting products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Under the *Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) of 1938* and its amendments, every medical device marketed in the United States **must** comply with FDA regulations codified primarily in **21 CFR (Code of Federal Regulations) Parts 1–58, 800–1299**.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of a Medical Device (FDA)

Per **Section 201(h) of the FD&C Act**, a "device" is defined as:

> "An instrument, apparatus, implement, machine, contrivance, implant, in vitro reagent, or other similar or related article, including a component part or accessory which is:
> 1. Recognized in the official National Formulary, or the United States Pharmacopeia (USP), or any supplement to them,
> 2. Intended for use in the diagnosis of disease or other conditions, or in the cure, mitigation, treatment, or prevention of disease, in man or other animals, or
> 3. Intended to affect the structure or any function of the body of man or other animals, and which does not achieve its primary intended purposes through chemical action within or on the body of man or other animals and which is not dependent upon being metabolized for the achievement of its primary intended purposes."

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Building Permit System"**
> 
> Imagine the FDA's medical device framework as a city’s **building permit and inspection system**:
> - **Classification (Class I, II, III)** is like categorizing buildings: a garden shed (Class I — minimal risk), a residential home (Class II — moderate risk, needs standard plans review), and a hospital or skyscraper (Class III — high risk, needs full engineering review).
> - **510(k) Premarket Notification** is like showing that your new house design is *substantially equivalent* to an already-approved one — you don't need to re-prove that gravity works, but you must show the design is comparable.
> - **PMA (Premarket Approval)** is like building a brand-new skyscraper — you must submit complete structural, safety, and clinical data before a single brick is laid.
> - **Post-market surveillance** is the periodic inspection of the building after occupancy — checking for cracks, faulty wiring, or reported accidents.

## 1.4 Key Statutory and Regulatory Foundation

| Instrument | Year | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) | **1938** | Foundational U.S. law giving FDA authority over devices |
| Medical Device Amendments to FD&C Act | **1976** | Introduced device classification (Class I, II, III) and 510(k) |
| Safe Medical Devices Act (SMDA) | **1990** | Strengthened post-market surveillance, MDR, tracking |
| FDA Modernization Act (FDAMA) | **1997** | Introduced FDA Third Party Review, expedited review |
| Medical Device User Fee and Modernization Act (MDUFMA) | **2002** | User fees, establishment registration |
| Food and Drug Administration Safety and Innovation Act (FDASIA) | **2012** | UDI system, De Novo pathway formalized |
| 21st Century Cures Act | **2016** | Breakthrough device designation, software regulation |
| FDA Reauthorization Act (FDARA) | **2017** | User fees, regulatory improvements |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code of Federal Regulations (CFR)** — The 21 CFR is divided into:
> - **Parts 1–99** – General regulations, food, drugs
> - **Parts 100–169** – Food labelling, food standards
> - **Parts 170–199** – Food additives, color additives
> - **Parts 200–299** – Drugs (notably Part 211 for GMP)
> - **Parts 800–898** – Medical devices (Part 820 = Quality System Regulation)
> - **Parts 900–1299** – Specific product regulations

---

## 1.5 Classification of Medical Devices (FDA)

The FDA classifies medical devices into **three regulatory classes** based on the level of risk and the controls necessary to provide reasonable assurance of safety and effectiveness:

### 1.5.1 Class I — General Controls

- **Risk Level:** Low to moderate
- **Examples:** Elastic bandages, stethoscopes, surgical gloves, tongue depressors, enema kits
- **Regulatory Pathway:** Most Class I devices are **exempt** from Premarket Notification 510(k)
- **Controls:** General controls (registration, listing, labelling, GMP)
- **% of all devices:** Approximately **47%**
- **% of all marketed devices cleared:** Approximately **95%**

### 1.5.2 Class II — General Controls + Special Controls

- **Risk Level:** Moderate to high
- **Examples:** Infusion pumps, surgical drapes, pregnancy test kits, blood pressure cuffs, catheters, contact lens cleaners
- **Regulatory Pathway:** Most require **510(k) Premarket Notification**
- **Controls:** General + Special controls (performance standards, post-market surveillance, special labelling, patient registries, guidelines)
- **% of all devices:** Approximately **43%**

### 1.5.3 Class III — General Controls + Premarket Approval (PMA)

- **Risk Level:** High (life-sustaining, life-supporting, implantable, or significant risk devices)
- **Examples:** Heart valves, implantable pacemakers, coronary stents, deep brain stimulators, HIV diagnostic tests
- **Regulatory Pathway:** **Premarket Approval (PMA)** — most stringent
- **Controls:** General + Special + PMA (clinical data demonstrating safety and effectiveness)
- **% of all devices:** Approximately **10%**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Some students confuse the **% of devices** (Class I ≈ 47%, Class II ≈ 43%, Class III ≈ 10%) with the **% of FDA review workload**. Class III devices, though only 10% in number, consume the majority of FDA review time and resources due to the depth of clinical evidence required.

## 1.6 Risk-Based Classification Triangle

```
        CLASS III
       (High Risk)
        PMA Required
       Clinical Trials
          /\
         /  \
        /    \
       /      \
      /________\
     /          \
    /  CLASS II  \
   / (Moderate)   \
  /  510(k) +      \
 /   Special        \
/    Controls        \
----------------------
   CLASS I
  (Low Risk)
  General Controls
  Often 510(k) Exempt
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major FDA Regulatory Pathways (Step-by-Step Logic)

### Pathway 1: 510(k) Premarket Notification (21 CFR Part 807, Subpart E)

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin:** Section 510(k) of the FD&C Act (1976)
> 
> **Purpose:** Demonstrate that the device to be marketed is **"substantially equivalent" (SE)** to a legally marketed device (the "predicate device") that does not require PMA.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Identify Predicate Device:** Find a legally marketed Class I or II device (or preamendment Class III device) with the same intended use and technological characteristics.
2. **Prepare 510(k) Submission:** Include device description, intended use, comparison to predicate, performance data (bench, animal, clinical if needed), biocompatibility, sterilization, software documentation, labelling.
3. **User Fee Payment:** Pay applicable MDUFA user fee.
4. **FDA Review (90 days standard, 30 days for Special 510(k)):**
   - **Substantially Equivalent (SE):** Cleared for marketing.
   - **Not Substantially Equivalent (NSE):** Device is automatically Class III; must file PMA or reclassify via De Novo.
5. **Post-Clearance:** Manufacturing must comply with **21 CFR Part 820 (QSR)**; must register establishment, list device, comply with UDI, MDR, labelling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Types of 510(k):**
> - **Traditional 510(k)** — Full submission with all elements
> - **Special 510(k)** — Modifications to own cleared device; FDA review ~**30 days**
> - **Abbreviated 510(k)** — Reliance on FDA-recognized consensus standards, guidance documents, or special controls

### Pathway 2: Premarket Approval (PMA) (21 CFR Part 814)

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin:** Section 515 of the FD&C Act
> 
> **Purpose:** Provide reasonable assurance of **safety and effectiveness** for Class III devices.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Pre-Submission (Pre-Sub) Meeting:** Optional but strongly recommended; free Q&A with FDA.
2. **Investigational Device Exemption (IDE)** — If clinical study is needed: 21 CFR Part 812.
3. **Conduct Clinical Trials:** Designed in accordance with **Good Clinical Practice (GCP)**; statistically powered for safety and effectiveness endpoints.
4. **Prepare PMA Application:** Contains technical, performance, manufacturing, sterilisation, biocompatibility, software, electromagnetic compatibility, animal, and clinical data. Use of **Modular PMA** is permitted.
5. **User Fee (MDUFA):** Significantly higher than 510(k).
6. **FDA Review — 180 days (statutory):**
   - **Filing Review** (45 days)
   - **Substantive Review** (up to 180 days total)
   - **Advisory Panel Review** (if needed)
   - **Decision:** Approve, Approve with conditions, or Denied.
7. **Post-Approval:** Annual reports, post-approval studies, recall, MDR, corrections & removals.

### Pathway 3: De Novo Classification Request (21 CFR Part 860, Subpart D)

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose:** For novel, low-to-moderate risk devices with **no predicate** device. After De Novo grant, the device becomes a Class I or II device and serves as a future predicate.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. Receive **NSE** determination on 510(k) submission.
2. File De Novo request (or directly file De Novo without prior 510(k) under FDASIA 2012).
3. FDA reviews risk profile, benefits, controls necessary.
4. If granted: device is classified, becomes predicate, special controls established.

### Pathway 4: Humanitarian Device Exemption (HDE) (21 CFR Part 814, Subpart H)

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose:** Devices for rare diseases/disorders affecting **≤ 8,000 individuals/year** in the U.S. Effectiveness is "probable" (not required to be demonstrated), but safety must be shown.

### Pathway 5: Emergency Use Authorization (EUA)

> [!NOTE]
> **Authority:** Section 564 of FD&C Act. Used during declared emergencies (e.g., COVID-19). Allows unapproved devices or unapproved uses of approved devices.

### Pathway 6: Breakthrough Device Designation

> [!NOTE]
> **Authority:** 21st Century Cures Act (2016). Devices that provide **more effective treatment** of life-threatening or irreversibly debilitating conditions. Provides prioritized review and interactive engagement with FDA.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Term | Definition | Citation |
|---|---|---|
| **SE (Substantially Equivalent)** | Same intended use AND either same technological characteristics, OR different technological characteristics that do not raise different questions of safety and effectiveness, AND demonstrate SE | 21 CFR 807.92(a)(3) |
| **Predicate Device** | A legally marketed device (Class I, II, or preamendment Class III) used as basis for SE comparison | FD&C Act § 513(i) |
| **510(k) Review Clock** | Traditional: 90 days FDA review; Special: 30 days | 21 CFR 807 |
| **PMA Review Clock** | 180 days statutory; 45 days filing | 21 CFR 814 |
| **IDE** | Investigational Device Exemption — authorizes investigational use | 21 CFR 812 |
| **QSR / cGMP** | Quality System Regulation — design controls, CAPA, etc. | 21 CFR Part 820 |
| **MDR** | Medical Device Reporting (post-market adverse events) | 21 CFR Part 803 |
| **UDI** | Unique Device Identification | 21 CFR Part 830 |
| **HDE Limit** | ≤ **8,000** individuals/year (US) | FD&C Act § 520(m) |
| **MDUFA** | Medical Device User Fee Amendments | Reauthorized every 5 years |
| **Establishment Registration** | Mandatory annual (now per 21 CFR Part 807) | 21 CFR 807 |
| **Device Listing** | All marketed devices must be listed with FDA | 21 CFR 807 |

## 2.3 Quality System Regulation (QSR) — 21 CFR Part 820

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **On a KTU exam, you MUST be able to enumerate the subparts of 21 CFR 820 and explain at least 2–3 in detail.** This is a **favourite KTU question**.

| Subpart | Title | Key Highlights |
|---|---|---|
| A | General Provisions | Scope, definitions, abbreviations |
| B | Quality System Requirements | Quality policy, organisation, management review, design controls |
| C | Design Controls | Design and development planning, design input/output, reviews, verification, validation, design transfer, design changes, design history file (DHF) |
| D | Document Controls | Document approval, distribution, changes |
| E | Purchasing Controls | Supplier evaluation, purchasing data |
| F | Identification and Traceability | Lot/serial number system |
| G | Production and Process Controls | Equipment, environmental, contamination control |
| H | Acceptance Activities | Incoming, in-process, finished device inspection |
| I | Nonconforming Product | Identification, segregation, disposition |
| J | Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA) | Investigation, root cause, action verification |
| K | Labelling and Packaging Controls | Label integrity, storage |
| L | Handling, Storage, Distribution, Installation | Controls throughout chain |
| M | Records | General records, complaint files, service reports |
| N | Servicing | Service procedures, reports |
| O | Statistical Techniques | Sampling plans, trend analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modernization Note:** The FDA is transitioning from **QSR (21 CFR 820)** to the **Quality Management System Regulation (QMSR)** harmonized with **ISO 13485:2016**, with the final rule effective **February 2, 2026**. As of the KTU 2024 syllabus, both frameworks are examinable.

## 2.4 Design Controls (Subpart C) — Heart of QSR

The **Design Controls** apply to most **Class II and Class III devices** (and some Class I). Required elements (per 21 CFR 820.30):

1. **Design and Development Planning** — Plan activities, responsibilities, timelines
2. **Design Input** — Physical, performance, regulatory, user needs
3. **Design Output** — Specifications, drawings, software, manufacturing instructions
4. **Design Review** — Formal, documented, multi-disciplinary
5. **Design Verification** — "Did we make the product right?" — confirms output meets input
6. **Design Validation** — "Did we make the right product?" — confirms device meets user needs and intended use
7. **Design Transfer** — Transition of design to production
8. **Design Changes** — Controlled change management
9. **Design History File (DHF)** — Compiled record proving design was developed per plan

## 2.5 Post-Market Requirements (Real-World Utility)

| Requirement | Citation | Trigger / Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **MDR (Medical Device Reporting)** | 21 CFR Part 803 | Report death, serious injury, malfunction to FDA within **30 days** (5 days if remedial action needed to prevent unreasonable risk) |
| **Recalls** | 21 CFR Part 806 | Voluntary or FDA-mandated; classification Class I (most serious) to Class III |
| **Corrections & Removals** | 21 CFR Part 806 | 10 working day report to FDA |
| **Tracking** | 21 CFR Part 821 | For life-sustaining/implantable devices |
| **UDI** | 21 CFR Part 830 | Device Identifier (DI) + Production Identifier (PI) |
| **Post-Approval Studies** | PMA conditions | Continued evaluation |
| **Post-Market Surveillance Studies** | 522 PS | Section 522 of FD&C Act, as amended |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Correct FDA Pathway

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** KTU frequently tests the student's ability to decide *which pathway* applies for a given device description. Use the decision tree below.

```
START: New device to be marketed in U.S.
    │
    ▼
Has a legally marketed predicate device (Class I, II, or 
preamendment Class III) with same intended use?
    │
    ├── YES ──► Does it raise different Qs of safety/effectiveness?
    │                 │
    │                 ├── NO ──► 510(k) ► SE ► Marketing cleared ✓
    │                 │
    │                 └── YES ──► NSE ──► File PMA or De Novo
    │
    └── NO (no predicate)
              │
              ├── Is it high risk / life-supporting / implantable?
              │         │
              │         ├── YES ──► Class III ► PMA required
              │         │
              │         └── NO ──► Class I/II (low-to-moderate risk) 
              │                      │ 
              │                      └── De Novo (request low/moderate 
              │                          risk classification)
              │
              └── HDE? (≤ 8000 patients/yr US) ──► HDE pathway
              
              └── Emergency? (declared emergency) ──► EUA pathway
              
              └── Breakthrough? (more effective Tx for 
                    life-threatening condition) ──► Breakthrough 
                    Designation + Priority review
```

## 3.2 Step-by-Step — 510(k) Substantial Equivalence Determination

Per **21 CFR 807.92(b)**, a 510(k) submission must include:

1. **Cover letter** (Form FDA 3514) — submitter, contact, device name, predicate
2. **Indications for Use Statement** (Form FDA 3881)
3. **510(k) Summary** (Form FDA 3514) OR **510(k) Statement** (Form FDA 3514)
4. **Truthful and Accurate Statement** (Form FDA 3514)
5. **Class III Certification** (if applicable) (Form FDA 3514)
6. **Financial Certification/Disclosure** (Form FDA 3454/3455) — for studies of human subjects
7. **Device Description** — pictures, engineering drawings, materials, energy used
8. **Substantial Equivalence Comparison Table** — side-by-side with predicate
9. **Performance Testing** — bench, animal, sterilization (per ISO 11135, 11137), biocompatibility (per ISO 10993), electrical safety (per IEC 60601-1), EMC (per IEC 60601-1-2), software (per IEC 62304)
10. **Proposed Labelling** — including package insert, IFU, label

**SE Decision Logic (per 21 CFR 807.92(a)(3)):**

$$
\text{SE} = \begin{cases} \text{Same Intended Use} \; \wedge \; \left[ \text{Same Technological Characteristics} \; \vee \right. \\ \left. \left( \text{Different Technological Characteristics} \; \wedge \; \text{No different Qs} \; \wedge \; \text{Demonstrated SE} \right) \right] \end{cases}
$$

If **NSE** determination, the device is automatically classified into **Class III** (per Section 513(f) of FD&C Act), requiring PMA.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step — PMA Application Structure (per 21 CFR 814.20)

| Section | Element | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cover Letter | Submission type, applicant, table of contents |
| 2 | Table of Contents | With page numbers |
| 3 | Summary | Indications, device description, studies, conclusions |
| 4 | Complete Device Description | Materials, components, energy source, principles of operation |
| 5 | Performance Specifications | Bench, in-vitro, in-vivo, animal |
| 6 | Software Documentation | Per IEC 62304, software level of concern (LOC) |
| 7 | Biocompatibility | Per ISO 10993 |
| 8 | Sterilization & Shelf Life | Per ISO 11135/11137, ASTM F1980 |
| 9 | Electrical Safety & EMC | Per IEC 60601-1, IEC 60601-1-2 |
| 10 | Manufacturing Information | QSR compliance, facilities |
| 11 | Labelling | Draft labels, IFU, package insert |
| 12 | Environmental Assessment | Per 21 CFR 25 |
| 13 | Financial Disclosure | Per Form FDA 3454/3455 |
| 14 | Clinical Studies | Protocol, IRB, informed consent, statistical analysis, results |
| 15 | Bibliography | Peer-reviewed literature |
| 16 | User Fee Cover Sheet | MDUFA |
| 17 | Other | Pediatric subpopulation, post-approval study plan |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step — Determining Device Classification (Analytical Walkthrough)

**Method:** When in doubt, use the FDA's official classification panels (21 CFR Parts 862–892, organized by medical specialty).

For each device:
- **Step 1:** Identify the panel (e.g., Cardiovascular = 21 CFR 870, Neurology = 21 CFR 882, Orthopedic = 21 CFR 888)
- **Step 2:** Look up the regulation number in the panel
- **Step 3:** Check the classification number (I, II, or III)
- **Step 4:** Apply corresponding controls

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick analytical relationship:**

$$
\text{Risk Level} \;\propto\; \text{Class Number} \;\propto\; \text{Regulatory Control Stringency} \;\propto\; \text{Data Requirements}
$$

This can be expressed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Class I} &: \;\; \text{General Controls Only} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Minimal regulatory burden} \\
\text{Class II} &: \;\; \text{General + Special Controls} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{510(k) and performance data} \\
\text{Class III} &: \;\; \text{General + Special + PMA} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Clinical evidence mandatory}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Calculating QSR 510(k) User Fee

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** Fees are updated annually by MDUFA. The following is a representative structure for exam purposes only.

Suppose a company submits a **Traditional 510(k)** in **FY 2024**. The FDA user fee is approximately **$24,335** for small businesses (reduced) and approximately **$54,000–60,000** for standard. The company has **50 employees** and is a small business.

Since the small business rate applies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{User Fee} &= \text{Small Business Rate (FY 2024 estimate)} \\
&= \$24{,}335 \text{ (approximate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **PMA submission**, the fee is substantially higher (e.g., ~$540,000 range in recent years).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not use outdated user fees in answers. Always state "as per the latest MDUFA fee schedule."

## 3.6 Comparative Analysis Table — 510(k) vs. PMA vs. De Novo

| Attribute | 510(k) | PMA | De Novo |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Statute** | § 510(k) FD&C Act | § 515 FD&C Act | § 513(f)(2) FD&C Act |
| **CFR** | 21 CFR 807 | 21 CFR 814 | 21 CFR 860.200–.260 |
| **Risk Level** | Class I/II (moderate) | Class III (high) | Novel low/moderate |
| **Predicate Required?** | Yes | No (standalone) | No (no predicate exists) |
| **Clinical Data** | Usually not for SE; sometimes required | Always required (safety + effectiveness) | Sometimes required |
| **FDA Review Clock** | 90 days (Traditional) | 180 days | 150 days (FDARA target) |
| **User Fee** | Lower | Highest | Moderate |
| **Resulting Marketing Status** | Cleared | Approved | Granted (becomes Class I/II; new predicate) |
| **Documentation Rigor** | Moderate | Very High | Moderate–High |

## 3.7 Calculation — Substantial Equivalence Matrix (Worked Example)

Suppose a new **glucose meter** (Device A) is being developed. Predicate Device B is a cleared, substantially equivalent glucose meter.

| Attribute | Predicate Device B | New Device A | Same/Different | SE Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Intended Use | Quantitative measurement of blood glucose | Quantitative measurement of blood glucose | Same | ✓ OK |
| Patient Population | Adult diabetic patients | Adult diabetic patients | Same | ✓ OK |
| Energy Source | Battery | Battery | Same | ✓ OK |
| Measurement Technology | Electrochemical biosensor | Electrochemical biosensor | Same | ✓ OK |
| Software Algorithm | v1.0 | v2.0 with new averaging | Different | Need verification |
| Sample Volume | 0.6 μL | 0.5 μL | Different | Need biocompatibility, performance |

**Conclusion:** Intended use is the same. Different technological characteristics (sample volume, software). New questions: Do differences raise different Qs of safety/effectiveness? If testing shows no different Qs, then **SE is achievable**.
