# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Medical Device" as per the U.S. FDA. **[KTU University Exam - Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

As per **Section 201(h) of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic (FD&C) Act, 1938**, a "device" is an instrument, apparatus, implement, machine, contrivance, implant, in vitro reagent, or similar article, including a component or accessory, that:

1. Is recognized in the official National Formulary or the United States Pharmacopeia (USP);
2. Is intended for use in the **diagnosis, cure, mitigation, treatment, or prevention of disease** in man or animals; or
3. Is intended to **affect the structure or any function of the body** of man or animals.

The key qualifier is that the device must **not** achieve its primary intended purpose through chemical action within the body and must not be dependent upon metabolism for its action — this distinguishes it from a drug.

---

### Question 2: List the three classes of medical devices under the FDA classification system with one example each. **[KTU University Exam - Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Class | Risk Level | Regulatory Control | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | Low | General Controls | Stethoscope, surgical gloves, tongue depressor |
| **Class II** | Moderate | General + Special Controls (510(k)) | Infusion pump, blood pressure cuff, pregnancy test kit |
| **Class III** | High (life-sustaining/supporting) | General + Special + PMA | Implantable pacemaker, heart valve, coronary stent |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): 510(k) and PMA Pathways

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Adapted from Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**A (a) (7 marks):** Explain the 510(k) Premarket Notification pathway under FDA regulations. Discuss the criteria for substantial equivalence and list the contents of a 510(k) submission.

**OR**

**A (b) (7 marks):** Explain in detail the Premarket Approval (PMA) pathway as per 21 CFR Part 814. Discuss the contents of a PMA application and FDA review process.

**A (a) — 510(k) Premarket Notification — Model Solution (7 marks):**

The **510(k) Premarket Notification** is a regulatory pathway mandated by Section 510(k) of the FD&C Act and detailed in **21 CFR Part 807, Subpart E**. It is required for most Class II devices and certain Class I and III devices that are not exempt.

**1. Purpose (1 mark):**
To demonstrate that the new device is **"substantially equivalent" (SE)** to a legally marketed predicate device. Substantial equivalence does **not** mean the new device is identical; it means the device has the same intended use and either the same technological characteristics, or different technological characteristics that do not raise different questions of safety and effectiveness, and which are demonstrated to be as safe and effective as the predicate.

**2. Predicate Device Concept (1 mark):**
A **predicate device** is a legally marketed Class I, Class II, or preamendment Class III device that was marketed before May 28, 1976, or has been cleared through 510(k) or approved through PMA. The applicant identifies the predicate, compares it feature-by-feature, and provides supporting evidence.

**3. Three Types of 510(k) (1.5 marks):**
- **Traditional 510(k):** Full submission including device description, SE comparison, performance testing, labelling. FDA review ~90 days.
- **Special 510(k):** For modifications to the applicant's own cleared device; FDA review ~30 days.
- **Abbreviated 510(k):** Relies on FDA-recognized consensus standards, guidance documents, or special controls to streamline the review.

**4. Contents of 510(k) Submission (1.5 marks):**
- Cover letter (FDA Form 3514)
- Indications for Use Statement (FDA Form 3881)
- 510(k) Summary or 510(k) Statement
- Truthful and Accurate Statement
- Class III Certification (if applicable)
- Financial Certification/Disclosure (FDA Form 3454/3455)
- Device description with comparison table to predicate
- Performance testing data (bench, biocompatibility per ISO 10993, sterilization per ISO 11135/11137, software per IEC 62304, electrical safety per IEC 60601-1, EMC per IEC 60601-1-2)
- Proposed labelling (IFU, package label, package insert)

**5. FDA Determination (1 mark):**
FDA makes one of three determinations:
- **Substantially Equivalent (SE):** Cleared for marketing.
- **Not Substantially Equivalent (NSE):** Automatically classified as Class III; must file PMA or request De Novo.
- **Not Substantially Equivalent - Different Questioned Issue:** Subject to additional information requests.

**6. Post-Clearance (1 mark):**
After clearance, manufacturer must:
- Register establishment annually (21 CFR 807)
- List the device
- Comply with QSR (21 CFR 820) — design controls, CAPA, etc.
- Assign UDI (21 CFR 830)
- Submit MDRs (21 CFR 803)
- Track device (if applicable, 21 CFR 821)

---

**A (b) — Premarket Approval (PMA) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

The **Premarket Approval (PMA)** pathway is the FDA's most stringent device review process, established under **Section 515 of the FD&C Act** and detailed in **21 CFR Part 814**. It is required for all **Class III** devices (life-supporting, life-sustaining, implantable, or of substantial importance in preventing impairment of human health).

**1. Purpose (1 mark):**
To provide **reasonable assurance** of the **safety and effectiveness** of a Class III device. Unlike 510(k) (which only requires substantial equivalence), PMA requires the applicant to independently demonstrate safety and effectiveness, usually through prospective clinical trials.

**2. Pre-Submission (1 mark):**
A **Pre-Submission (Pre-Sub) meeting** is highly recommended. The applicant submits a written package to the FDA describing the device, proposed indications, and planned testing. FDA provides non-binding feedback. This significantly de-risks the PMA process.

**3. Investigational Device Exemption (IDE) (1 mark):**
If a clinical study is required, the applicant must first obtain an **IDE** under **21 CFR Part 812**. The IDE allows the investigational device to be shipped and used in a clinical study. Significant risk (SR) devices require FDA and IRB approval; non-significant risk (NSR) devices require only IRB approval.

**4. Contents of PMA Application (per 21 CFR 814.20) (2 marks):**
- Cover letter, table of contents, summary
- Complete device description (materials, components, energy source, operating principle)
- Performance specifications (bench, in-vitro, in-vivo, animal studies)
- Software documentation (per IEC 62304, software level of concern)
- Biocompatibility (per ISO 10993)
- Sterilization and shelf-life validation (per ISO 11135/11137, ASTM F1980)
- Electrical safety and EMC (per IEC 60601-1, IEC 60601-1-2)
- Manufacturing information and QSR compliance
- Proposed labelling (IFU, package label)
- Environmental assessment (per 21 CFR Part 25)
- Financial disclosure (Form FDA 3454/3455)
- **Clinical studies** with protocol, IRB approval, informed consent, statistical analysis, results
- Bibliography
- User fee cover sheet (MDUFA)

**5. FDA Review Process (1 mark):**
- **Filing Review (45 days):** Acceptance for substantive review.
- **Substantive Review (up to 180 days total):** FDA reviews all scientific and clinical data.
- **Advisory Panel Review:** FDA may convene an expert panel for novel devices.
- **Decision:** Approve / Approve with conditions / Denied.

**6. Post-Approval (1 mark):**
- Annual reports (21 CFR 814.84)
- Post-approval studies as conditions of approval
- Post-market surveillance (Section 522)
- MDR compliance
- Recalls/corrections/removals (21 CFR 806)
- UDI compliance

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Quality System Regulation and Post-Market

**B (a) (7 marks):** Explain the FDA Quality System Regulation (QSR) under 21 CFR Part 820. Discuss the key elements of Design Controls and the significance of Design History File (DHF).

**OR**

**B (b) (7 marks):** Explain the post-market regulatory requirements under the FDA, including Medical Device Reporting (MDR), Recalls, Unique Device Identification (UDI), and Device Tracking.

**B (a) — Quality System Regulation (QSR) — Model Solution (7 marks):**

The **Quality System Regulation (QSR)**, codified at **21 CFR Part 820**, is the FDA's regulatory framework for **Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP)** for medical devices. It covers the methods, facilities, and controls used in designing, manufacturing, packaging, labelling, storing, installing, and servicing medical devices intended for human use.

**1. Scope and Applicability (1 mark):**
Applies to all finished device manufacturers (and contract manufacturers) of Class I, II, and III medical devices, including those that are 510(k)-exempt. Specific provisions may be tailored based on device class.

**2. Key Subparts of QSR (2 marks):**

| Subpart | Title | Highlights |
|---|---|---|
| A | General Provisions | Scope, definitions |
| B | Quality System Requirements | Quality policy, organisation, management review |
| C | Design Controls | Design and development planning, input, output, review, verification, validation, transfer, changes, DHF |
| D | Document Controls | Approval, distribution, change control |
| E | Purchasing Controls | Supplier evaluation, purchasing data |
| F | Identification and Traceability | Lot/serial numbers |
| G | Production and Process Controls | Equipment, environment, contamination |
| H | Acceptance Activities | Incoming, in-process, finished device inspection |
| I | Nonconforming Product | Identification, segregation, disposition |
| J | CAPA | Corrective and Preventive Action |
| K | Labelling and Packaging | Label integrity, storage |
| L | Handling, Storage, Distribution, Installation | Chain of custody |
| M | Records | General records, complaint files, service reports |
| N | Servicing | Service procedures, reports |
| O | Statistical Techniques | Sampling plans, trend analysis |

**3. Design Controls (Subpart C) — Detailed (2.5 marks):**

Design controls apply to **most Class II and all Class III devices** (and some Class I). The nine elements per 21 CFR 820.30 are:

1. **Design and Development Planning:** Establish plans that describe design and development activities, responsibilities, and review/verification/validation activities.
2. **Design Input:** Physical, performance, regulatory, and user requirements. Must be documented and reviewed.
3. **Design Output:** Specifications, drawings, software, manufacturing instructions. Must be expressed in terms that can be verified and approved.
4. **Design Review:** Formal, documented reviews conducted at appropriate stages by multi-disciplinary team.
5. **Design Verification:** Confirms that design output meets design input (e.g., bench testing, software unit testing).
6. **Design Validation:** Confirms that the device meets user needs and intended use in actual or simulated use conditions (often requires clinical evaluation or usability/human factors testing).
7. **Design Transfer:** Transition of the design to production with proper procedures.
8. **Design Changes:** All changes must be reviewed, verified, validated, and approved before implementation.
9. **Design History File (DHF):** A compilation of records describing the design history of the device — proving the design was developed in accordance with the design plan.

**4. Significance of DHF (1 mark):**
The DHF is the single most important document FDA inspects during a QSR audit. It serves as objective evidence that the manufacturer followed all design controls. A properly maintained DHF demonstrates design discipline, supports traceability from user needs to final design, and is essential during FDA inspections and post-market investigations.

**5. QMSR Transition (0.5 mark):**
The FDA is transitioning QSR to the **Quality Management System Regulation (QMSR)** harmonized with **ISO 13485:2016**, with compliance required by **February 2, 2026**. The QMSR retains the same risk-based approach but aligns terminology and structure with international standards, reducing global regulatory burden.

---

**B (b) — Post-Market Regulatory Requirements — Model Solution (7 marks):**

Post-market regulation is critical because pre-market testing cannot capture all potential safety issues. The FDA enforces robust post-market obligations.

**1. Medical Device Reporting (MDR) — 21 CFR Part 803 (2 marks):**

- **Purpose:** Ensure timely reporting of adverse events, malfunctions, and other safety issues to the FDA.
- **Who Reports:** Manufacturers (mandatory), Importers (mandatory), Device User Facilities (hospitals, nursing homes — semi-mandatory).
- **Reportable Events:**
  - **Death** — must be reported within **30 days** (5 working days if remedial action necessary)
  - **Serious Injury** — within **30 days**
  - **Significant Malfunction** — within **30 days**
  - **5-Day Reports** — events that require remedial action to prevent an unreasonable risk of substantial harm
- **Submission:** Form FDA 3500A (mandatory reporters), Form FDA 3500 (voluntary reporters via MedWatch).
- **Exemptions:** Devices exempted by regulation or classified as Class I devices that are not life-supporting/implantable.

**2. Recalls, Corrections, and Removals — 21 CFR Part 806 (2 marks):**

- **Recall:** Firm-initiated removal or correction of a marketed device that violates FDA law. Classified as:
  - **Class I** — Reasonable probability of serious adverse health consequences or death (e.g., defective implantable pacemaker).
  - **Class II** — Temporary or medically reversible adverse health consequences.
  - **Class III** — Not likely to cause adverse health consequences.
- **Corrections and Removals Reporting:** Manufacturers must report **within 10 working days** any field correction or product removal initiated to reduce risk to health.
- **FDA's Role:** FDA classifies the recall, monitors the firm's actions, and posts Class I and II recalls publicly.

**3. Unique Device Identification (UDI) — 21 CFR Part 830 (1.5 marks):**

- **Purpose:** Adequately identify medical devices through distribution and use.
- **Components:**
  - **Device Identifier (DI):** Mandatory, fixed portion identifying labeler and specific version/model.
  - **Production Identifier (PI):** Conditional, includes lot/batch, serial number, expiration date, manufacture date.
- **Issuing Agency:** **GS1**, HIBCC, ICCBBA, IFA — accredited by FDA.
- **Database:** Information submitted to the **Global Unique Device Identification Database (GUDID)** — publicly accessible.
- **Compliance Timelines:** Class III → Class II → Class I (phased in from 2014 to 2022).

**4. Device Tracking — 21 CFR Part 821 (1 mark):**

- **Purpose:** Track certain devices from manufacture through use to facilitate rapid identification and notification in case of recall or safety concern.
- **Applicable Devices:** Implantable, life-supporting, life-sustaining devices (e.g., implantable pacemakers, heart valves, silicone gel breast implants).
- **Tracking Elements:** Manufacturer, lot/serial, receiving facility, patient identifier (anonymized where possible), physician, date of implant/explant.
- **Access:** Tracked data must be available to FDA within 3 working days upon request.

**5. Other Post-Market Tools (0.5 mark):**
- **522 Post-Market Surveillance Studies:** FDA can mandate studies for Class II/III devices when surveillance is necessary to protect public health.
- **Post-Approval Studies (PMA):** Required as conditions of PMA approval.
- **Annual Reports:** Required for PMA holders summarizing device performance, complaints, and labeling changes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse QSR (21 CFR 820) with ISO 13485.** QSR is FDA regulation; ISO 13485 is international standard. They are similar in philosophy but differ in detail. With the 2026 QMSR transition, the convergence is becoming explicit, but in the KTU 2024 syllabus, both are distinct topics.
> 2. **Do NOT say that 510(k) "approves" the device.** The 510(k) process **clears** the device. Only PMA **approves** the device. This is a common marking deduction error.
> 3. **Do NOT confuse "predicate" with "reference" device.** A predicate is a specific legally marketed device used in 510(k) SE determination. Reference devices are used for safety/performance comparisons but are not predicates.
> 4. **Do NOT write "510(k) is the same as PMA."** 510(k) is a notification; PMA is an approval. The depth of evidence required is vastly different.
> 5. **Always cite the relevant CFR Part** in your answers. Examiners award marks for regulatory precision.
> 6. **Do not forget UDI is two-part** (DI + PI) — students often describe only DI.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — FDA Regulations of Medical Devices**

### A. Foundations
- [ ] FDA regulates medical devices under the **FD&C Act, 1938** and its amendments.
- [ ] **CDRH (Center for Devices and Radiological Health)** is the specific branch overseeing devices.
- [ ] Section **201(h)** of the FD&C Act defines "device."
- [ ] **21 CFR Parts 800–1299** contain device regulations.

### B. Classification (Most Examined)
- [ ] **Class I:** Low risk, General Controls, ~47% of device types.
- [ ] **Class II:** Moderate risk, General + Special Controls, ~43% of device types, **510(k) required**.
- [ ] **Class III:** High risk, General + Special + PMA, ~10% of device types, **clinical trials required**.

### C. Pre-Market Pathways
- [ ] **510(k) Premarket Notification** — 21 CFR 807, Subpart E. Demonstrates **substantial equivalence** to predicate.
- [ ] **PMA** — 21 CFR 814. Most stringent; clinical evidence of safety + effectiveness required.
- [ ] **De Novo** — 21 CFR 860.200–.260. For novel, low/moderate risk devices without predicate.
- [ ] **HDE** — Humanitarian Device Exemption. For diseases affecting **≤ 8,000 US patients/year**.
- [ ] **EUA** — Emergency Use Authorization. Declared emergencies.
- [ ] **Breakthrough Designation** — 21st Century Cures Act. More effective treatment for life-threatening conditions.
- [ ] **IDE** — Investigational Device Exemption. 21 CFR 812.

### D. Quality System Regulation
- [ ] **21 CFR Part 820** — QSR / cGMP for medical devices.
- [ ] **Subpart C — Design Controls:** Plan, Input, Output, Review, Verification, Validation, Transfer, Changes, DHF.
- [ ] **DHF (Design History File)** is the key FDA inspection record.
- [ ] **QMSR** transition effective **Feb 2, 2026**, harmonized with **ISO 13485:2016**.

### E. Post-Market
- [ ] **MDR — 21 CFR 803** — Adverse event reporting (30 days; 5 days for serious).
- [ ] **Recalls — 21 CFR 806** — Class I (death/serious), II, III.
- [ ] **Corrections and Removals** — 10 working day report.
- [ ] **UDI — 21 CFR 830** — DI + PI, GUDID database.
- [ ] **Device Tracking — 21 CFR 821** — For implantable/life-supporting devices.
- [ ] **522 Post-Market Surveillance Studies** — FDA can mandate.
- [ ] **PMA Annual Reports** — 21 CFR 814.84.

### F. Time Limits to Remember
- [ ] 510(k) Traditional: **90 days**
- [ ] 510(k) Special: **30 days**
- [ ] PMA: **180 days**
- [ ] MDR — Death/Serious Injury: **30 days**
- [ ] 5-day MDR (remedial action required): **5 working days**
- [ ] Corrections and Removals: **10 working days**
- [ ] HDE patient limit: **≤ 8,000 per year (US)**

### G. Key Standards
- [ ] **ISO 10993** — Biocompatibility
- [ ] **ISO 11135 / 11137** — EO / Radiation Sterilization
- [ ] **IEC 60601-1** — Electrical Safety
- [ ] **IEC 60601-1-2** — Electromagnetic Compatibility
- [ ] **IEC 62304** — Medical Device Software Lifecycle
- [ ] **ISO 14971** — Risk Management
- [ ] **ISO 13485:2016** — Medical Device QMS (international)
- [ ] **ASTM F1980** — Accelerated Aging
- [ ] **IEC 62366-1** — Usability Engineering

### H. Top 5 KTU Exam Traps to Avoid
- [ ] ✗ "510(k) approves the device" → **It CLEARS the device.**
- [ ] ✗ "Class III devices are most common" → **Class I is most common by type; Class III is most common by review effort.**
- [ ] ✗ Confusing **SE** (Substantial Equivalence) with **Approval**
- [ ] ✗ Forgetting to cite **21 CFR Part** numbers
- [ ] ✗ Writing **"GMP"** without specifying "QSR" — FDA GMP for devices is the QSR (21 CFR 820), not the drug GMP (21 CFR 210/211)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When answering long questions, **always structure your answer into numbered sections with sub-headings**. Use the exact terminology from the regulations (e.g., "substantial equivalence," "general controls," "design history file"). KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for **terminology precision**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clinical Investigation of Medical Devices - Clinical Investigation Plan for Medical Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Clinical Investigation of Medical Devices - Clinical Investigation Plan for Medical Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Clinical Investigation

According to **ISO 14155:2020 (Clinical investigation of medical devices for human subjects — Good clinical practice)** and the **EU Medical Device Regulation (EU MDR 2017/745, Article 2(45))**:

> **Clinical Investigation (CI)** is defined as the *systematic, planned, and scientifically designed investigation conducted on human subjects to assess the safety, clinical performance, and/or effectiveness of a medical device*.

Under the **EU MDR 2017/745**, the formal definition is:

$$\text{Clinical Investigation} = \text{Any systematic investigation involving one or more human subjects undertaken to assess the safety or clinical performance of a device.}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> A *clinical investigation* is **NOT** the same as a *clinical evaluation*.
> - **Clinical Evaluation** = Desk-based, systematic review of *existing* clinical data (literature, prior investigations, post-market data).
> - **Clinical Investigation** = Generation of *new* clinical data by actively enrolling human subjects and using the investigational device on them.
> - The **Clinical Evaluation Report (CER)** may rely on data from one or more **Clinical Investigations**.

---

### 1.2 Formal Definition — Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)

Per **ISO 14155:2020 (Clause 3.9)** and **EU MDR 2017/745 Annex XV, Chapter II**:

> **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)** is the *document that prospectively describes the rationale, objectives, design, methodology, monitoring, statistical considerations, and organization of a clinical investigation*.

In simpler terms, the CIP is the **"scientific recipe + regulatory passport"** for running a clinical trial on a medical device. It tells the regulator (Notified Body, FDA, CDSCO), the ethics committee, the sponsor, and the investigator *exactly* how the study will be conducted.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Cooking Blueprint"**
>
> Imagine you are inventing a new pharmaceutical drug. Before you can sell it, you must run a **clinical trial**. A **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)** is exactly like the *Master Recipe Book* used by a master chef launching a new dish in a five-star restaurant:
>
> | Master Recipe (CIP) Element | Real-World Analogy |
> |---|---|
> | **Why are we making this dish?** (Rationale/Objectives) | Customer demand and gap in menu |
> | **What ingredients?** (Investigational device description) | List of raw materials, sourcing |
> | **Who eats it and who cannot?** (Inclusion/Exclusion criteria) | Allergic customers excluded, target demographics |
> | **How do we cook it?** (Study design, procedures) | Step-by-step recipe, cooking method |
> | **How do we know it is good?** (Endpoints, statistics) | Tasting panel scores, customer surveys |
> | **What if a customer chokes?** (Risk management, AE reporting) | Emergency medical plan on-site |
> | **Who is the head chef?** (Sponsor, Principal Investigator) | Executive chef and sous-chef |
>
> Without the **Master Recipe**, the kitchen would produce inconsistent results, fail health inspections, and could poison customers. Similarly, without a properly approved **CIP**, a clinical investigation is unethical, non-compliant, and may be rejected by regulators or ethics committees.

---

### 1.4 Why is the CIP Mandatory? — Regulatory Foundation

| Regulation / Standard | Specific Mandate for CIP |
|---|---|
| **ISO 14155:2020** | Clause 5.1 — Sponsor shall prepare a CIP **before** starting the clinical investigation |
| **EU MDR 2017/745** | Annex XV, Chapter II — Full mandatory contents of the CIP are specified (16 sections) |
| **US FDA 21 CFR 812.25** | Requires an **Investigational Plan** (synonymous with CIP) for IDE studies |
| **India — CDSCO (MDR 2017, Rule 65, Schedule III)** | Mandatory for investigational device approval |
| **ICH-GCP E6(R2)** | Although designed for drugs, applied as a reference for medical device investigations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Metric / Constant to Remember:**
> - **Number of mandatory CIP sections under EU MDR Annex XV = 16**
> - **Number of mandatory CIP elements under ISO 14155:2020 = 16** (subset, with broader scope)
> - **Pre-clinical data required before CIP submission = Bench testing + Biocompatibility (ISO 10993) + Risk analysis (ISO 14971)**

---

### 1.5 When is a Clinical Investigation Required?

A clinical investigation is generally required when:
1. The device is **new** (no equivalent on the market) — Class III / Class IIb implantable / long-term invasive devices.
2. The manufacturer claims **new clinical performance or safety claims** not covered by existing clinical evidence.
3. The **Notified Body** (EU) or **FDA/CDSCO** explicitly requires it during conformity assessment.

It is **NOT** always required — for many low-risk devices with substantial equivalence, the clinical evaluation can rely on literature.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Many students wrongly assume that every medical device must undergo a clinical investigation before CE marking. This is **false**. Under EU MDR, manufacturers can demonstrate sufficient clinical evidence through a **Clinical Evaluation** alone for low-risk devices (e.g., Class I non-sterile, non-measuring devices). However, for **Class III and implantable devices (Article 61(4))**, a clinical investigation is generally **mandatory** unless sufficient clinical data already exists for the equivalent device.

---

### 1.6 Clinical Investigation vs. Clinical Trial vs. Clinical Study

| Parameter | Clinical Investigation | Clinical Trial (Drug) | Clinical Study |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Subject** | Medical device | Pharmaceutical drug | Broader term (drug, device, procedure) |
| **Regulator** | Notified Body, Competent Authority | Drug Controller (CDSCO, FDA) | Variable |
| **Key Standard** | ISO 14155 | ICH-GCP E6(R2) | Variable |
| **Risk Focus** | Device-related + procedure-related | Drug-related (pharmacological) | Variable |
| **Sample Size** | Smaller (often 30–300 subjects for devices) | Larger (often hundreds–thousands) | Variable |

---

### 1.7 Types of Clinical Investigations

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Pilot / Feasibility Study** | Small-scale (10–30 subjects) initial study to refine design/protocol | Pre-CE marking, early-stage devices |
| **Pivotal Study** | Large, statistically powered study used as the primary clinical evidence for regulatory approval | CE marking, FDA PMA, CDSCO registration |
| **Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF)** | Study conducted after market approval to confirm long-term safety/performance | Required under EU MDR for high-risk devices |
| **Registry Study** | Observational study collecting real-world data from device users | Post-market surveillance |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Purpose and Objectives of the CIP

The Clinical Investigation Plan serves **four major purposes**:

1. **Scientific Purpose** — Defines the hypothesis, objectives, endpoints, and statistical methodology to ensure the study answers a meaningful clinical question.
2. **Regulatory Purpose** — Demonstrates to regulators (Notified Body, Competent Authority, FDA, CDSCO) that the study is designed to generate sufficient clinical evidence for conformity assessment.
3. **Ethical Purpose** — Ensures protection of human subjects' rights, safety, and well-being (compliance with the **Declaration of Helsinki** and local ethics committee requirements).
4. **Operational Purpose** — Acts as the operational manual for the sponsor, monitor, investigator, and site staff to consistently conduct the study across multiple sites.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A common exam question asks — *"Can a Clinical Investigation be initiated without an approved CIP?"*
> **Answer: NO.** Under ISO 14155:2020 Clause 5.1 and EU MDR Annex XV, a clinical investigation can only commence **after**:
> 1. Approval of the **CIP** by the Sponsor.
> 2. Favorable opinion from the **Ethics Committee (EC) / Institutional Review Board (IRB)**.
> 3. Authorization from the **Competent Authority** (e.g., Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation in India, FDA in the US, EU Member State authority in Europe).

---

### 2.2 Key Stakeholders and Their Roles in the CIP

| Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Sponsor** | Owns the CIP. Legally responsible for the conduct, quality, and regulatory compliance of the investigation. |
| **Principal Investigator (PI)** | Leads the investigation at a specific site. Signs the CIP signature page. Responsible for conduct, subject welfare, and data integrity. |
| **Co-Investigator** | Assists the PI. |
| **Monitor (CRA)** | Ensures the investigation is conducted per the CIP, ICH-GCP, and applicable regulations. |
| **Statistician** | Designs the statistical analysis plan, calculates sample size. |
| **Data Manager** | Manages Case Report Forms (CRFs), databases, and data validation. |
| **Ethics Committee (EC/IRB)** | Independent body that reviews and approves the CIP for ethical acceptability. |
| **Regulatory Authority** | Reviews and authorizes the CIP submission. |

---

### 2.3 Mandatory Contents of the CIP — ISO 14155:2020 & EU MDR Annex XV

The CIP must contain a minimum set of elements. Below is the consolidated **16-element master list** aligned with both standards:

| # | Section | Content Highlights | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **General Information** | Title, identification number (CIP code), version, date, sponsor details, monitor, investigators, sites | ISO 14155:2020 §6.1 |
| 2 | **Identification and Description of the Investigational Device** | Name, model, materials, manufacturer, intended purpose, classification, lot/batch numbers, sterilization, packaging, instructions for use | ISO 14155:2020 §6.2 |
| 3 | **Justification for the Design of the Clinical Investigation** | Rationale for the study, preclinical/bench testing summary, risk-benefit analysis, prior clinical evidence | ISO 14155:2020 §6.3 |
| 4 | **Risks and Benefits of the Investigational Device** | Anticipated adverse events, residual risks, expected clinical benefits, risk minimization measures | ISO 14155:2020 §6.4 |
| 5 | **Objectives and Hypotheses** | Primary objective, secondary objectives, primary hypothesis, secondary hypotheses, claim to be supported | ISO 14155:2020 §6.5 |
| 6 | **Design of the Clinical Investigation** | Type (pilot, pivotal, PMCF), comparative vs. non-comparative, randomized vs. non-randomized, blinded vs. open-label, multicentre vs. single-centre | ISO 14155:2020 §6.6 |
| 7 | **Selection and Withdrawal of Subjects** | Inclusion criteria, exclusion criteria, subject withdrawal criteria, subject replacement policy | ISO 14155:2020 §6.7 |
| 8 | **Treatment of Subjects** | Description of device use, procedures, concomitant treatments, prohibited medications, follow-up schedule | ISO 14155:2020 §6.8 |
| 9 | **Assessment of Efficacy / Performance** | Primary endpoint, secondary endpoints, efficacy parameters, measurement methods, timing of assessments | ISO 14155:2020 §6.9 |
| 10 | **Assessment of Safety** | Definitions of Adverse Event (AE), Adverse Device Effect (ADE), Serious Adverse Event (SAE), Serious Adverse Device Effect (SADE), Unanticipated Serious Adverse Device Effect (USADE), reporting timelines | ISO 14155:2020 §6.10 |
| 11 | **Statistics** | Sample size justification, statistical hypotheses, analysis populations (Intention-to-Treat [ITT], Per-Protocol [PP]), significance level (α), power (1−β), statistical methods, handling of missing data, interim analysis plan | ISO 14155:2020 §6.11 |
| 12 | **Amendments to the CIP** | Procedure for CIP modifications, regulatory/ethics re-approval process | ISO 14155:2020 §6.12 |
| 13 | **Deviations from the CIP** | Definition, documentation, classification, reporting, acceptability criteria | ISO 14155:2020 §6.13 |
| 14 | **Data Management** | Source data, Case Report Form (CRF) design, database, data entry, validation, query resolution, data locking, archiving | ISO 14155:2020 §6.14 |
| 15 | **Monitoring** | Monitoring plan, frequency, scope, source data verification (SDV), on-site vs. remote monitoring | ISO 14155:2020 §6.15 |
| 16 | **Source Documents and Source Data** | Definition of source documents, retention period (≥10 years under EU MDR; ≥15 years for implantable devices), archiving | ISO 14155:2020 §6.16 |
| 17 | **Ethics** | Compliance with Declaration of Helsinki, local regulations, ethics committee approval process | ISO 14155:2020 §6.17 |
| 18 | **Informed Consent** | Process, content of informed consent form (ICF), subject rights, language, signature, special populations | ISO 14155:2020 §6.18 |
| 19 | **Adverse Events, Device Deficiencies** | Reporting procedures, timelines (e.g., 10 days for USADE), causality assessment | ISO 14155:2020 §6.10 |
| 20 | **Vulnerable Populations** | Special considerations for children, pregnant women, cognitively impaired subjects | ISO 14155:2020 §6.7 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** The 16-section structure is asked verbatim in KTU university exams. Memorize the section names and their order.

---

### 2.4 Statistical Considerations in the CIP — Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Sample Size and Statistical Analysis**

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Sample Size (Two-Arm Comparison, Continuous Outcome)** | $n = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma^2 \cdot (Z_{1-\alpha/2} + Z_{1-\beta})^2}{\Delta^2}$ | Two independent means comparison |
| **Sample Size (Two-Arm, Binary Outcome)** | $n = \dfrac{(Z_{1-\alpha/2}\sqrt{2\bar{p}\bar{q}} + Z_{1-\beta}\sqrt{p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2})^2}{(p_1 - p_2)^2}$ | Two independent proportions |
| **Confidence Interval (95%)** | $CI = \bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Mean with normal approximation |
| **Significance Level (α)** | $\alpha = 0.05$ (default) | Probability of Type I error (false positive) |
| **Statistical Power (1−β)** | $1 - \beta = 0.80$ (default) | Probability of detecting true effect |
| **Type I Error (α)** | Reject $H_0$ when $H_0$ is true | False positive |
| **Type II Error (β)** | Accept $H_0$ when $H_1$ is true | False negative |
| **p-value** | Probability of observing data at least as extreme as the test statistic, given $H_0$ is true | Decision rule: $p < \alpha \Rightarrow \text{Reject } H_0$ |
| **Intention-to-Treat (ITT) Analysis** | All randomized subjects analyzed in their assigned group, regardless of protocol deviations | Conservative, prevents bias |
| **Per-Protocol (PP) Analysis** | Only subjects who completed the protocol per CIP are analyzed | Less conservative |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LATEX FORMATTING RULE:** In the table above, no vertical pipes `|` were used inside cells (e.g., for absolute value or intervals). This is per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 mandate to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

### 2.5 KTU 2024 — Key Definitions for Memorization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Definitions (Likely 3-Mark Questions):**

| Term | Definition (ISO 14155:2020 / EU MDR) |
|---|---|
| **Adverse Event (AE)** | Any untoward medical occurrence, unintended disease or injury, or any untoward clinical sign in a subject, whether or not related to the investigational device. |
| **Adverse Device Effect (ADE)** | Any AE related to the use of an investigational device. |
| **Serious Adverse Event (SAE)** | An AE that led to death, serious deterioration in health, life-threatening illness/injury, hospitalization, or medical/surgical intervention. |
| **Serious Adverse Device Effect (SADE)** | An ADE that has resulted in any of the serious consequences above. |
| **Unanticipated Serious Adverse Device Effect (USADE / USADE)** | A SADE which by its nature, incidence, severity, or outcome has not been identified in the current risk assessment. |
| **Device Deficiency** | Any inadequacy of a medical device with respect to its identity, quality, durability, reliability, usability, safety, or performance. |
| **Clinical Investigation Report (CIR)** | Document describing the design, conduct, results, and analysis of a completed clinical investigation. |
| **Case Report Form (CRF)** | Printed, optical, or electronic document designed to record all required information per the CIP for each subject. |
| **Investigator's Brochure (IB)** | Compilation of the clinical and non-clinical information on the investigational device relevant to the investigation. |
| **Subject** | An individual who participates in a clinical investigation, either as a recipient of the investigational device or as a control. |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of the CIP

In the medical device industry, the CIP is a **living document** used by:

- **Regulatory Affairs (RA) Teams** — For Notified Body, FDA, CDSCO submissions.
- **Clinical Research Associates (CRAs)** — As the monitoring standard for site audits.
- **Biostatisticians** — For pre-defined statistical analysis.
- **Quality Assurance (QA) Teams** — For compliance auditing under **ISO 13485:2016 (Clause 7.3 — Design and Development)**.
- **Post-Market Surveillance Teams** — For PMCF and PMS planning.

The CIP is the **single most important document** in a clinical investigation. A poorly written CIP leads to:
- Rejection by Ethics Committee → Delayed study start.
- Rejection by Competent Authority → No market access.
- Audit findings → Loss of ISO 13485 certification.
- Data quality issues → Inability to use study data for CE marking.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough — Sample Size Derivation for a Two-Arm CIP

**Scenario:** A sponsor is conducting a pivotal clinical investigation for a new **Class III implantable cardiovascular device**. The primary endpoint is the **6-month improvement in ejection fraction (a continuous variable, measured in %)**. The sponsor wishes to demonstrate **superiority** over the standard-of-care control device.

**Given:**
- Expected mean improvement in investigational arm: $\mu_T = 12\%$
- Expected mean improvement in control arm: $\mu_C = 9\%$
- Expected clinically meaningful difference: $\Delta = \mu_T - \mu_C = 3\%$
- Common standard deviation: $\sigma = 5\%$
- Significance level: $\alpha = 0.05$ → $Z_{1-\alpha/2} = Z_{0.975} = 1.96$
- Statistical power: $1 - \beta = 0.80$ → $Z_{1-\beta} = Z_{0.80} = 0.84$
- Equal allocation ratio: 1:1

**Step 1: Identify the appropriate formula.**

For a two-arm comparison of means (continuous outcome) with equal allocation, the sample size per arm is:

$$n = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma^2 \cdot (Z_{1-\alpha/2} + Z_{1-\beta})^2}{\Delta^2}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values into the formula.**

$$n = \frac{2 \cdot (5)^2 \cdot (1.96 + 0.84)^2}{(3)^2}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the variance term $\sigma^2$.**

$$\sigma^2 = 5^2 = 25 \, \%^2$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Z-sum term $(Z_{1-\alpha/2} + Z_{1-\beta})^2$.**

$$(Z_{1-\alpha/2} + Z_{1-\beta}) = 1.96 + 0.84 = 2.80$$

$$(2.80)^2 = 7.84$$

**Step 5: Calculate the difference squared $\Delta^2$.**

$$\Delta^2 = 3^2 = 9 \, \%^2$$

**Step 6: Compute the numerator and denominator.**

$$\text{Numerator} = 2 \cdot 25 \cdot 7.84 = 50 \cdot 7.84 = 392$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 9$$

**Step 7: Final division.**

$$n = \frac{392}{9} \approx 43.56$$

**Step 8: Round up to the nearest integer.**

Since the sample size must be an integer, we always round **up**:

$$n = 44 \, \text{subjects per arm}$$

**Step 9: Total sample size for the study.**

$$N_{total} = 2 \cdot n = 2 \cdot 44 = 88 \, \text{subjects}$$

**Step 10: Add a dropout rate (typically 10–20%).**

Assuming a conservative dropout rate of 15%:

$$N_{adjusted} = \frac{N_{total}}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{88}{0.85} \approx 103.53$$

$$N_{final} = 104 \, \text{subjects}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The CIP should plan to enroll **104 subjects** (52 per arm) to detect a 3% difference in ejection fraction with 80% power and 5% significance level, accounting for 15% expected dropout.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — CIP Section vs. Regulatory Mandate

| CIP Section | ISO 14155:2020 | EU MDR Annex XV | US FDA 21 CFR 812 | India CDSCO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. General Information | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required |
| 2. Device Description | ✅ Required | ✅ Required (Ch. II §1) | ✅ Required (§812.25(a)(2)) | ✅ Required |
| 3. Justification for Design | ✅ Required | ✅ Required (Ch. II §2) | ✅ Required (§812.25(b)) | ✅ Required |
| 4. Risks and Benefits | ✅ Required | ✅ Required (Ch. II §3) | ✅ Required (§812.25(c)) | ✅ Required |
| 5. Objectives & Hypotheses | ✅ Required | ✅ Required (Ch. II §4) | ✅ Required (§812.25(d)) | ✅ Required |
| 6. Study Design | ✅ Required | ✅ Required (Ch. II §5) | ✅ Required (§812.25(e)) | ✅ Required |
| 7. Subject Selection | ✅ Required | ✅ Required (Ch. II §6) | ✅ Required (§812.25(f)) | ✅ Required |
| 8. Treatment of Subjects | ✅ Required | ✅ Required (Ch. II §7) | ✅ Required (§812.25(g)) | ✅ Required |
| 9. Efficacy Assessment | ✅ Required | ✅ Required (Ch. II §8) | ✅ Required (§812.25(h)) | ✅ Required |
| 10. Safety Assessment | ✅ Required | ✅ Required (Ch. II §9) | ✅ Required (§812.25(i)) | ✅ Required |
| 11. Statistics | ✅ Required | ✅ Required (Ch. II §10) | ✅ Required (§812.25(j)) | ✅ Required |
| 12. Amendments | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required |
| 13. Deviations | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required |
| 14. Data Management | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required |
| 15. Monitoring | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required |
| 16. Source Documents | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required |
| 17. Ethics | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required |
| 18. Informed Consent | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required | ✅ Required |

---

### 3.3 Comparative Matrix — ISO 14155:2011 vs. ISO 14155:2020

| Aspect | ISO 14155:2011 | ISO 14155:2020 (Current) |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | GCP for medical device trials | Broader — GCP + alignment with EU MDR |
| **Risk-Based Approach** | Limited | **Strong emphasis** — Risk-based monitoring, risk-based thinking |
| **Clinical Investigation Audit** | Not detailed | Detailed audit section added |
| **Sponsor's Responsibilities** | Generic | Expanded — 13 sub-responsibilities |
| **Principal Investigator Responsibilities** | 9 sub-responsibilities | **12 sub-responsibilities** (added data integrity, device accountability) |
| **Electronic Data** | Mentioned briefly | **Detailed guidance** on eCRF, electronic signatures, 21 CFR Part 11 alignment |
| **Vulnerable Populations** | Brief mention | **Detailed guidance** (children, pregnant women, cognitively impaired) |
| **Annexes** | 4 annexes (informative) | **6 annexes** (including sample CIP synopsis, sample ICF) |

---

### 3.4 Clinical Evaluation vs. Clinical Investigation — Detailed Comparison

| Parameter | Clinical Evaluation | Clinical Investigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Systematic, planned, continuous process to collect, appraise, and analyze clinical data pertaining to a medical device | Systematic investigation in human subjects to assess safety, performance, or effectiveness |
| **Output Document** | **Clinical Evaluation Report (CER)** | **Clinical Investigation Report (CIR)** + **CIP** |
| **Source of Data** | Literature, prior studies, PMS data, post-market clinical follow-up (PMCF), complaints | Primary data from new human-subject study |
| **Stage** | Throughout product lifecycle (pre-market and post-market) | Pre-market (mainly); PMCF investigations are post-market |
| **Regulatory Standard** | **MEDDEV 2.7/1 rev 4** (legacy), **MDCG 2020-13** (current EU MDR guidance), **ISO 14155** | **ISO 14155:2020**, **EU MDR Annex XV**, **21 CFR 812** |
| **Cost** | Lower (literature-based) | Higher (sites, monitors, subjects) |
| **Time** | Months | Years (often 2–5 years) |
| **Required for** | All devices (Class I, IIa, IIb, III) | Class III / Implantable / High-risk Class IIb (typically) |
| **Examples** | Literature review of 50 published papers on a knee implant | RCT with 200 patients testing a new coronary stent |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Clinical Investigation Lifecycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Stage 1: Pre-Clinical Activities] --> nodeB[Stage 2: Develop Clinical Investigation Plan CIP]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Stage 3: Ethics Committee EC Submission and Approval]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Stage 4: Competent Authority CA Submission and Authorization]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Stage 5: Site Initiation Visit SIV]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Stage 6: Subject Recruitment and Enrollment]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Stage 7: Investigational Device Use and Data Collection]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Stage 8: Monitoring Visits and Source Data Verification]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Stage 9: Adverse Event Reporting and Safety Review]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Stage 10: Database Lock and Statistical Analysis]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Stage 11: Clinical Investigation Report CIR]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Stage 12: Regulatory Submission with CER and CIR]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Stage 13: Post-Market Clinical Follow-up PMCF]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The flowchart above depicts the **13-stage lifecycle** of a clinical investigation, starting from pre-clinical activities and concluding with post-market clinical follow-up. The CIP is the central document that governs **all** stages from Stage 2 onwards.

---

### 4.2 CIP Content Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeCIP[Clinical Investigation Plan Master Document]
    nodeCIP --> node1[Section 1: General Information]
    nodeCIP --> node2[Section 2: Device Description]
    nodeCIP --> node3[Section 3: Justification and Risk Benefit]
    nodeCIP --> node4[Section 4: Objectives and Hypotheses]
    nodeCIP --> node5[Section 5: Study Design and Methods]
    nodeCIP --> node6[Section 6: Subject Selection Inclusion Exclusion]
    nodeCIP --> node7[Section 7: Treatment and Follow-up Procedures]
    nodeCIP --> node8[Section 8: Efficacy Endpoints]
    nodeCIP --> node9[Section 9: Safety Assessments and AE Definitions]
    nodeCIP --> node10[Section 10: Statistical Analysis Plan]
    nodeCIP --> node11[Section 11: Data Management and Monitoring]
    nodeCIP --> node12[Section 12: Amendments Deviations and Compliance]
    nodeCIP --> node13[Section 13: Ethics and Informed Consent]
    nodeCIP --> node14[Section 14: Source Documents and Archiving]
    nodeCIP --> node15[Section 15: Sponsor and Investigator Responsibilities]
    nodeCIP --> node16[Section 16: References and Appendices]
```

---

### 4.3 Adverse Event Classification (Mermaid Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Adverse Event Occurs in Subject]
    nodeStart --> nodeQ1{Is the event serious?}
    nodeQ1 -->|No| nodeAE[Adverse Event AE]
    nodeQ1 -->|Yes| nodeQ2{Is event related to investigational device?}
    nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeSAE[Serious Adverse Event SAE]
    nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeQ3{Is event anticipated in risk analysis?}
    nodeQ3 -->|Yes| nodeSADE[Serious Adverse Device Effect SADE]
    nodeQ3 -->|No| nodeUSADE[Unanticipated SADE USADE - Report within 10 days]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The decision tree illustrates the classification of adverse events per ISO 14155:2020. A **USADE (Unanticipated Serious Adverse Device Effect)** is the most critical classification — it requires **expedited reporting to the Competent Authority, Ethics Committee, and Sponsor within 10 calendar days** (per ISO 14155:2020 Clause 6.10.3).

---

### 4.4 Regulatory Submission Workflow (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSponsor[Sponsor prepares CIP and IB]
    nodeSponsor --> nodeEC[Ethics Committee Review]
    nodeSponsor --> nodeCA[Competent Authority Review]
    nodeEC --> nodeDecision{Approval Received?}
    nodeCA --> nodeDecision
    nodeDecision -->|No| nodeAmend[Amend CIP and Resubmit]
    nodeAmend --> nodeSponsor
    nodeDecision -->|Yes| nodeSIV[Site Initiation and Enrollment]
    nodeSIV --> nodeConduct[Conduct Investigation as per CIP]
    nodeConduct --> nodeMonitor[Monitoring and Audits]
    nodeMonitor --> nodeReport[Clinical Investigation Report CIR]
    nodeReport --> nodeFinal[Regulatory Submission with CER and CIR]
```

---

### 4.5 Block Diagram — CIP and Related Documents

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeCIP[Clinical Investigation Plan CIP]
    nodeIB[Investigator Brochure IB]
    nodeICF[Informed Consent Form ICF]
    nodeCRF[Case Report Form CRF]
    nodeSAP[Statistical Analysis Plan SAP]
    nodeDMP[Data Management Plan DMP]
    nodeMP[Monitoring Plan MP]
    nodeTMF[Trial Master File TMF]

    nodeCIP --> nodeIB
    nodeCIP --> nodeICF
    nodeCIP --> nodeCRF
    nodeCIP --> nodeSAP
    nodeCIP --> nodeDMP
    nodeCIP --> nodeMP
    nodeCIP --> nodeTMF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The CIP is the **central governing document** that derives and references several supporting documents. The **Investigator's Brochure (IB)** provides device background to the investigator, the **ICF** ensures subject consent, the **CRF** captures per-subject data, the **SAP** defines statistical methodology, the **DMP** governs data handling, the **MP** defines monitoring strategy, and the **TMF** archives all trial-related documents.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks)

---

**Q1. Define the term "Clinical Investigation" as per ISO 14155:2020. How does it differ from "Clinical Evaluation"?** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Clinical Investigation** is defined under **ISO 14155:2020 (Clause 3.16)** as the *systematic, planned, and scientifically designed investigation conducted on human subjects to assess the safety, clinical performance, and/or effectiveness of a medical device*.
>
> **Key Differences:**
>
> | Aspect | Clinical Investigation | Clinical Evaluation |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Nature** | Generates **new** primary data by enrolling human subjects | Analyses **existing** data (literature, prior studies, PMS data) |
> | **Output** | Clinical Investigation Report (CIR) | Clinical Evaluation Report (CER) |
> | **Regulatory Standard** | ISO 14155:2020, EU MDR Annex XV | MEDDEV 2.7/1 rev 4, MDCG 2020-13 |
> | **Cost & Time** | High (years, millions of dollars) | Lower (months, desk-based) |
> | **Mandatory For** | Class III / Implantable / High-risk Class IIb devices | All devices (in principle) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often define Clinical Investigation as "testing on patients" without mentioning **systematic, planned, scientifically designed, and on human subjects**. The 4 keywords (systematic, planned, scientific, human subjects) are mandatory. Lose 1 mark if any keyword is missing.

---

**Q2. List and briefly explain any five mandatory contents of a Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP) as per EU MDR Annex XV.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> The five mandatory contents are:
>
> 1. **General Information** — Title, identification number, version, date, sponsor and investigator details.
> 2. **Identification and Description of the Investigational Device** — Name, model, materials, manufacturer, intended purpose, classification.
> 3. **Risks and Benefits of the Investigational Device** — Anticipated adverse events, residual risks, expected clinical benefits, risk minimization measures.
> 4. **Objectives and Hypotheses** — Primary objective, secondary objectives, primary hypothesis, claim to be supported.
> 5. **Statistics** — Sample size justification, statistical hypotheses, analysis populations (ITT, PP), significance level, power.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not write vague one-line answers. Each of the 5 contents must have a **brief explanation** (1–2 lines). A bare list without explanations will not fetch full 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — With Internal Choice

---

**Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

> **(a)** Define the Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP). Explain in detail the **mandatory contents of the CIP** as per **ISO 14155:2020** and **EU MDR Annex XV**. Mention any **10 of the 16 sections** with a one-line description of each. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** Describe the **process of developing and approving a CIP**, including the roles of the **Sponsor, Principal Investigator, Ethics Committee, and Competent Authority**. Also explain what happens in case of a **CIP amendment**. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Mandatory Contents (7 Marks):**

> A **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)** is a document that prospectively describes the rationale, objectives, design, methodology, statistical considerations, and organization of a clinical investigation. Per **ISO 14155:2020 (Clause 3.9)** and **EU MDR 2017/745 Annex XV, Chapter II**, the CIP must contain a minimum of **16 mandatory sections**.
>
> **10 Key Sections:**
>
> 1. **General Information** — Title, CIP code, version, date, sponsor, monitor, investigators, sites.
> 2. **Identification and Description of the Investigational Device** — Name, model, materials, manufacturer, intended purpose, classification, lot/batch numbers.
> 3. **Justification for the Design of the Clinical Investigation** — Rationale, preclinical/bench testing summary, risk-benefit analysis, prior clinical evidence.
> 4. **Risks and Benefits of the Investigational Device** — Anticipated AEs, residual risks, expected clinical benefits, risk minimization measures.
> 5. **Objectives and Hypotheses** — Primary and secondary objectives, primary and secondary hypotheses, claim to be supported.
> 6. **Design of the Clinical Investigation** — Type, comparative vs. non-comparative, randomized vs. non-randomized, blinded vs. open-label, multicentre vs. single-centre.
> 7. **Selection and Withdrawal of Subjects** — Inclusion criteria, exclusion criteria, withdrawal criteria, replacement policy.
> 8. **Assessment of Efficacy / Performance** — Primary endpoint, secondary endpoints, measurement methods, timing.
> 9. **Assessment of Safety** — Definitions of AE, ADE, SAE, SADE, USADE, reporting timelines.
> 10. **Statistics** — Sample size justification, statistical hypotheses, analysis populations (ITT, PP), significance level (α), power (1−β), statistical methods.

**(b) CIP Development and Approval Process (7 Marks):**

> The CIP development and approval process follows a structured workflow:
>
> **Step 1: Drafting the CIP** — The **Sponsor** (typically the manufacturer or its designee) drafts the CIP in collaboration with clinical experts, biostatisticians, and regulatory affairs professionals.
>
> **Step 2: Internal Review** — The Sponsor conducts an internal quality check and sign-off on the CIP.
>
> **Step 3: Ethics Committee (EC) / IRB Submission** — The **Ethics Committee** reviews the CIP for ethical acceptability, subject safety, and informed consent adequacy. Their approval is mandatory before any subject is enrolled.
>
> **Step 4: Competent Authority (CA) Submission** — In parallel, the **Competent Authority** (e.g., CDSCO in India, FDA in the US, EU Member State authority in Europe) reviews the CIP for scientific and regulatory adequacy. Authorization is required.
>
> **Step 5: Site Initiation** — Once both EC and CA approvals are obtained, the **Principal Investigator** at each site signs the CIP signature page (Form of Agreement).
>
> **Step 6: Roles Summary:**
>
> | Role | Key Responsibility |
> |---|---|
> | **Sponsor** | Owns the CIP, legally responsible for conduct, quality, and regulatory compliance |
> | **Principal Investigator** | Conducts the study at the site per the CIP, ensures subject welfare, maintains data integrity |
> | **Ethics Committee** | Reviews and approves the CIP for ethical acceptability, ensures subject rights and well-being |
> | **Competent Authority** | Reviews and authorizes the CIP for regulatory compliance, may impose conditions |
>
> **Step 7: CIP Amendments** — Any change to the CIP after initial approval (e.g., change in sample size, change in inclusion criteria, addition of a new site) is called a **CIP Amendment**. Amendments must be:
> - Documented with a clear rationale.
> - Submitted to the Ethics Committee and Competent Authority for review and approval.
> - **Not implemented** until approval is received (except where necessary to eliminate immediate hazards to subjects).
> - Tracked in a CIP Amendment Log.
> - Communicated to all investigators and site staff via training.
> - Reflected in updated informed consent forms (if subject-relevant changes).

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Explain the **statistical considerations in a Clinical Investigation Plan**. Derive the sample size formula for a **two-arm comparison with a continuous outcome**, and calculate the sample size for the following case: $\sigma = 4$, $\Delta = 2$, $\alpha = 0.05$, power $= 0.80$, equal allocation. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **classification of Adverse Events** in a clinical investigation as per **ISO 14155:2020**. Differentiate between **AE, ADE, SAE, SADE, and USADE**, with reporting timelines. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Statistical Considerations and Sample Size Derivation (7 Marks):**

> **Statistical Considerations in the CIP:**
>
> The CIP must include a comprehensive **Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP)** that covers:
> - **Hypothesis formulation** — Null hypothesis ($H_0$) and alternative hypothesis ($H_1$).
> - **Primary and secondary endpoints** — Clearly defined measurable outcomes.
> - **Sample size justification** — Based on the primary endpoint, expected effect size, variability, significance level (α), and statistical power (1−β).
> - **Analysis populations** — Intention-to-Treat (ITT) and Per-Protocol (PP).
> - **Significance level (α)** — Usually **0.05** (two-sided), representing the probability of a false positive.
> - **Statistical power (1−β)** — Usually **0.80**, representing the probability of detecting a true effect.
> - **Statistical methods** — t-test, chi-square, ANOVA, regression, survival analysis, etc.
> - **Handling of missing data** — Imputation methods (e.g., last observation carried forward, multiple imputation).
> - **Interim analysis** — Pre-planned interim analyses with stopping rules.
>
> **Derivation of Sample Size Formula (Two-Arm, Continuous Outcome, Equal Allocation):**
>
> For a two-arm comparison with a continuous outcome, the test statistic is based on the difference in means:
>
> $$\text{Test Statistic} = Z = \frac{\bar{X}_T - \bar{X}_C - \Delta}{\sqrt{\frac{2\sigma^2}{n}}}$$
>
> where:
> - $\bar{X}_T$ = Sample mean of investigational arm
> - $\bar{X}_C$ = Sample mean of control arm
> - $\Delta$ = Clinically meaningful difference to detect
> - $\sigma$ = Common standard deviation
> - $n$ = Sample size per arm
>
> For the test to detect a true difference of $\Delta$ with significance level $\alpha$ and power $1 - \beta$, we require:
>
> $$\frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{\frac{2\sigma^2}{n}}} = Z_{1-\alpha/2} + Z_{1-\beta}$$
>
> Solving for $n$:
>
> $$\Delta^2 = (Z_{1-\alpha/2} + Z_{1-\beta})^2 \cdot \frac{2\sigma^2}{n}$$
>
> $$\boxed{n = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma^2 \cdot (Z_{1-\alpha/2} + Z_{1-\beta})^2}{\Delta^2}}$$
>
> **Numerical Calculation:**
>
> Given: $\sigma = 4$, $\Delta = 2$, $\alpha = 0.05$ → $Z_{1-\alpha/2} = 1.96$, Power $= 0.80$ → $Z_{1-\beta} = 0.84$.
>
> **Step 1:** Variance term:
> $$\sigma^2 = 4^2 = 16$$
>
> **Step 2:** Z-sum term:
> $$(1.96 + 0.84)^2 = (2.80)^2 = 7.84$$
>
> **Step 3:** Difference squared:
> $$\Delta^2 = 2^2 = 4$$
>
> **Step 4:** Numerator:
> $$2 \cdot 16 \cdot 7.84 = 32 \cdot 7.84 = 250.88$$
>
> **Step 5:** Final division:
> $$n = \frac{250.88}{4} = 62.72 \approx 63 \, \text{per arm}$$
>
> **Step 6:** Total sample size:
> $$N_{total} = 2 \cdot 63 = 126 \, \text{subjects}$$

**(b) Classification of Adverse Events (7 Marks):**

> Per **ISO 14155:2020 (Clause 3.2 to 3.7)**, adverse events in a clinical investigation are classified as follows:
>
> | Term | Full Form | Definition | Reporting Timeline |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **AE** | Adverse Event | Any untoward medical occurrence in a subject, regardless of causal relationship to the device | Recorded in CRF, reported in periodic safety reports |
> | **ADE** | Adverse Device Effect | AE related to the use of an investigational device | Recorded in CRF, evaluated by investigator |
> | **SAE** | Serious Adverse Event | AE that led to death, serious deterioration in health, life-threatening illness, hospitalization, or medical/surgical intervention | Reported to Sponsor within **24 hours** of awareness |
> | **SADE** | Serious Adverse Device Effect | SADE = SAE that is related to the investigational device | Reported to Sponsor within **24 hours**, then to EC/CA in periodic reports |
> | **USADE** | Unanticipated Serious Adverse Device Effect | SADE which by its nature, incidence, severity, or outcome has not been identified in the current risk analysis (e.g., ISO 14971 risk file) | **Expedited reporting within 10 calendar days** to EC, CA, and all investigators |
> | **Device Deficiency** | — | Any inadequacy of a medical device with respect to its identity, quality, durability, reliability, usability, safety, or performance | Reported to Sponsor within **24 hours** |
>
> **Key Reporting Timelines:**
> - **SAE/SADE** → Reported by Investigator to Sponsor within **24 hours**.
> - **USADE** → Sponsor reports to Competent Authority, Ethics Committee, and all investigators within **10 calendar days**.
> - **Device Deficiency that could have led to SAE** → Reported within **24 hours** by Investigator.
> - **Periodic Safety Report** → Submitted at least **annually** to CA and EC.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating SADE with USADE** — SADE is anticipated, USADE is unanticipated. A student who uses these terms interchangeably loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the 10-day timeline for USADE** — This is a frequently tested fact. The 10-day timeline is per **ISO 14155:2020 §6.10.3** and **EU MDR Article 80**.
> 3. **Not mentioning Device Deficiency** — Many students forget this category, which is unique to medical device investigations.
> 4. **Writing only definitions without reporting timelines** — The 7-mark question requires both definitions AND timelines. Missing timelines = lose 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Clinical Investigation & CIP**
>
> - ⭐ **Clinical Investigation** = Systematic investigation on **human subjects** to assess device safety/performance (ISO 14155:2020, EU MDR Article 2(45)).
> - ⭐ **Clinical Evaluation ≠ Clinical Investigation** — Evaluation is desk-based; Investigation generates new data on human subjects.
> - ⭐ **CIP Definition** = Document prospectively describing rationale, objectives, design, methodology, monitoring, statistics, and organization.
> - ⭐ **Regulatory Foundation** = ISO 14155:2020 (international GCP), EU MDR Annex XV (Europe), 21 CFR 812 (USA), CDSCO Schedule III (India).
> - ⭐ **16 Mandatory Sections** of CIP per ISO 14155 / EU MDR — General Info, Device Description, Justification, Risk-Benefit, Objectives, Design, Subject Selection, Treatment, Efficacy, Safety, Statistics, Amendments, Deviations, Data Management, Monitoring, Source Documents.
> - ⭐ **Three-Way Approval** — EC + CA + Sponsor sign-off required before any subject enrollment.
> - ⭐ **Sample Size Formula (Two-Arm Continuous)** = $n = \dfrac{2\sigma^2(Z_{1-\alpha/2} + Z_{1-\beta})^2}{\Delta^2}$
> - ⭐ **Default Statistical Parameters** = $\alpha = 0.05$, Power $= 0.80$, $Z_{1-\alpha/2} = 1.96$, $Z_{0.80} = 0.84$.
> - ⭐ **AE Classification** — AE → ADE → SAE → SADE → USADE (each level has stricter reporting).
> - ⭐ **USADE Reporting Timeline** = **10 calendar days** to EC, CA, and all investigators.
> - ⭐ **SAE/SADE Reporting Timeline** = **24 hours** from investigator awareness.
> - ⭐ **Source Document Retention** = ≥10 years (EU MDR), ≥15 years for implantable devices.
> - ⭐ **Risk-Based Approach** is the cornerstone of ISO 14155:2020 — emphasize risk-based monitoring and risk-based thinking.
> - ⭐ **PMCF (Post-Market Clinical Follow-up)** = Clinical investigation conducted after market approval, mandatory for high-risk devices under EU MDR.
> - ⭐ **Investigator's Brochure (IB)** = Background document on the device, provided to the investigator alongside the CIP.
> - ⭐ **CRF (Case Report Form)** = Per-subject data collection form derived from the CIP.
> - ⭐ **CIP Amendment** = Any change to the approved CIP; requires EC/CA approval before implementation (except immediate safety measures).
> - ⭐ **Vulnerable Populations** = Special protection needed for children, pregnant women, cognitively impaired subjects (ISO 14155:2020 §6.7).
> - ⭐ **Declaration of Helsinki** — The ethical foundation for all clinical investigations involving human subjects.
> - ⭐ **Common 3-Mark Exam Questions:**
>   1. Define Clinical Investigation (ISO 14155:2020).
>   2. List 5 mandatory contents of a CIP.
>   3. Differentiate Clinical Investigation vs. Clinical Evaluation.
>   4. Define AE, ADE, SAE, SADE, USADE.
>   5. Explain the role of Ethics Committee in a clinical investigation.
> - ⭐ **Common 14-Mark Exam Questions:**
>   1. Detailed CIP contents with reference to ISO 14155 and EU MDR.
>   2. Sample size derivation and calculation.
>   3. AE classification with reporting timelines.
>   4. CIP development, approval, and amendment process.
>   5. Roles and responsibilities of Sponsor, PI, EC, CA.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Good Clinical Practice for Clinical Investigation of Medical Devices (ISO 14155:2011) - Quality System Regulations of Medical Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Good Clinical Practice for Clinical Investigation of Medical Devices (ISO 14155:2011) - Quality System Regulations of Medical Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Good Clinical Practice (GCP) for Medical Devices

**Good Clinical Practice (GCP)** is an international ethical and scientific quality standard for the design, conduct, recording, and reporting of **clinical investigations** that involve human subjects. When applied to **medical devices**, GCP is governed primarily by the international standard **ISO 14155:2011** — *"Clinical investigation of medical devices for human subjects — Good clinical practice."*

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, GCP for medical devices is defined as the **set of internationally recognized ethical and scientific quality requirements** that must be followed when planning, initiating, conducting, monitoring, recording, auditing, analyzing, and reporting **clinical investigations of medical devices** in human subjects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Definition)**
> GCP ensures the **rights, safety, and well-being of human subjects** are protected, and that the **clinical data generated is credible, reproducible, and scientifically valid**, thereby supporting **regulatory submissions** for marketing approval of the medical device.

## 1.2 Formal Definition — Quality System Regulation (QSR)

**Quality System Regulation (QSR)** refers to the regulatory framework governing the **design, manufacture, packaging, labeling, storage, installation, servicing, and post-market surveillance** of medical devices. The two most globally referenced QSR systems are:

1. **ISO 13485:2016** — International standard for **Quality Management Systems (QMS)** for medical devices.
2. **21 CFR Part 820** — United States FDA's Quality System Regulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The terms **ISO 13485** and **ISO 14971** are frequently confused. ISO 13485 = QMS, ISO 14971 = Risk Management. Remember **"13485 = Management System"** and **"14971 = Risk"**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### Analogy 1: GCP as a "Courtroom Trial Protocol"
Imagine you are a **judge in a courtroom** about to allow a brand-new medical device to be tested on a patient. Before you permit it, you need:
- An **independent ethics committee** (like a jury) to review the plan.
- A **written contract** (the Clinical Investigation Plan) detailing what will be done.
- **Informed consent** from the patient (a signed declaration of understanding).
- **Continuous monitoring** so no harm goes unnoticed.
- A **final report** with truthful data so future regulators can rely on it.

**GCP is essentially this courtroom protocol, applied scientifically to medical device trials.**

### Analogy 2: Quality System Regulation as a "Factory Recipe Book"
Think of a **kitchen in a five-star restaurant** that must consistently produce the same dish (a medical device) every single time. The QSR is the **master recipe book** that specifies:
- What ingredients (raw materials) are allowed.
- How the kitchen is to be cleaned (cleanroom standards).
- Who the head chef is (management responsibility).
- How the dish is tasted before serving (post-market surveillance).

| Term | Real-World Analogy | KTU 2024 Definition |
|---|---|---|
| ISO 14155:2011 | Courtroom trial rules | GCP standard for medical device clinical investigations |
| ISO 13485 | Restaurant recipe book | QMS standard for medical device manufacturers |
| 21 CFR 820 | FDA version of recipe book | US FDA QSR for medical devices |
| ISO 14971 | Hazard checklist for the recipe | Risk management standard for medical devices |
| Ethics Committee (EC) | Jury in a courtroom | Independent body reviewing ethical aspects |
| Sponsor | Trial-funding company | Entity that initiates and finances the clinical investigation |
| Principal Investigator | Lead physician | Qualified person responsible at the investigation site |

## 1.4 Key Terminology for KTU Examinations

| Term | Formal Definition (ISO 14155:2011) |
|---|---|
| **Clinical Investigation (CI)** | A systematic investigation in human subjects to assess the safety or performance of a medical device. |
| **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)** | The document that states the rationale, objectives, design, methodology, and conditions for the conduct of the CI. |
| **Sponsor** | The individual or organization taking responsibility for the initiation, management, and financing of the clinical investigation. |
| **Principal Investigator (PI)** | The qualified person responsible for conducting the clinical investigation at an investigation site. |
| **Ethics Committee (EC)** | An independent body constituted of medical, scientific, and non-scientific members, whose responsibility is to ensure the protection of the rights, safety, and well-being of human subjects. |
| **Informed Consent** | A process by which a subject voluntarily confirms his or her willingness to participate in a particular investigation, after being informed of all aspects of the investigation. |
| **Adverse Event (AE)** | Any untoward medical occurrence in a subject during a clinical investigation, which does not necessarily have a causal relationship with the device. |
| **Adverse Device Effect (ADE)** | Any adverse event related to the use of a medical device, including any malfunction or deterioration in characteristics/performance. |
| **Serious Adverse Event (SAE)** | An adverse event that led to death, serious deterioration in health, or required intervention. |
| **Conformity Assessment** | The process carried out by the manufacturer to demonstrate whether the device meets the essential requirements of the applicable regulations. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember this KTU 2024 Module 4 Anchor Concept:**
> ISO 14155:2011 governs **HOW** a clinical investigation is run (process standard), while ISO 13485:2016 governs **HOW** a medical device is **manufactured and controlled** (product/system standard). Together they form the **regulatory backbone** of the global medical device industry.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Thirteen Principles of ISO 14155:2011 (GCP Core)

ISO 14155:2011 establishes **13 foundational principles** that every clinical investigation of a medical device must follow. These are *the most frequently tested topic* in the KTU 2024 scheme for Module 4.

The 13 principles, structured for high-yield recall:

1. **Ethical Foundation** — Clinical investigations shall be conducted in accordance with the ethical principles that have their origin in the **Declaration of Helsinki**.
2. **Risk-Benefit Justification** — The anticipated benefits must outweigh the foreseeable risks; risks must be minimized and continuously monitored.
3. **Subject Rights** — The rights, safety, and well-being of subjects take precedence over the interests of science, society, or commerce.
4. **Scientific Soundness** — Clinical investigations must be scientifically sound, with clearly defined objectives and scientifically valid methods.
5. **Qualified Personnel** — Clinical investigations must be conducted by suitably qualified and trained personnel.
6. **Compliance with Regulations** — All investigations must comply with applicable national and international regulatory requirements.
7. **Documented Procedures** — The conduct of the investigation must follow a written **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)** and **Case Report Forms (CRFs)**.
8. **Device Conformity** — The medical device used in the investigation must conform to applicable regulatory requirements and be manufactured under an appropriate **Quality Management System** (e.g., ISO 13485).
9. **Risk Management Linkage** — The investigation must align with the manufacturer's **Risk Management File** (per ISO 14971).
10. **Informed Consent** — Informed consent must be obtained in writing from every subject prior to participation.
11. **Independent Ethics Review** — The investigation must be reviewed and approved by an independent **Ethics Committee (EC)**.
12. **Data Integrity & Confidentiality** — All clinical data must be accurately recorded, handled, stored, and reported to allow verification and interpretation, while protecting subject confidentiality.
13. **Adverse Event Reporting** — All adverse events and device deficiencies must be recorded, evaluated, and reported to the sponsor, EC, and regulatory authorities within defined timelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic for Exam Recall — "E-R-S-Q-C-D-R-I-E-D-D-A"**
> **E**thics → **R**isk-Benefit → **S**ubject Rights → **Q**ualified People → **C**ompliance → **D**ocumented Plan → **R**isk Mgmt Linkage → **I**nformed Consent → **E**thics Committee → **D**ata Integrity → **D**evice Reporting → **A**E/ADE Reporting

## 2.2 ISO 14155:2011 — Structural Clause Map

ISO 14155:2011 is organized into the following structural sections. KTU examiners often ask students to list or describe these clauses:

| Clause | Title | KTU 2024 Focus |
|---|---|---|
| Clause 1 | Scope | Defines medical device clinical investigations |
| Clause 2 | Normative references | References ISO 14971, ISO 13485, etc. |
| Clause 3 | Terms and definitions | Vocabulary (Sponsor, PI, Subject, AE, etc.) |
| Clause 4 | Ethical considerations | Declaration of Helsinki alignment |
| Clause 5 | Clinical investigation planning | Rationale, objectives, design, sample size |
| Clause 6 | Clinical investigation conduct | Monitoring, documentation, AE handling |
| Clause 7 | Suspension, termination, and close-out | Conditions for stopping a CI |
| Clause 8 | Responsibilities of the sponsor | Risk management, monitoring, reporting |
| Clause 9 | Responsibilities of the Principal Investigator | Subject care, consent, CRF completion |

## 2.3 Quality System Regulations (QSR) — Comparative Framework

| Aspect | ISO 13485:2016 | 21 CFR 820 (US FDA) | EU MDR 2017/745 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Region** | International | United States | European Union |
| **Mandatory?** | Voluntary but widely required | Legally mandatory in USA | Legally mandatory in EU |
| **Scope** | QMS for medical device organizations | QSR for medical device manufacturers | Full regulatory framework |
| **Risk-Based Approach** | Section 8.2.1 (feedback), clause 7 | Embedded throughout | Article 10 + Annex I |
| **Post-Market Surveillance** | Clause 8.2.1 | §820.198 (Complaint Files) | Article 83–86 |
| **Design Controls** | Clause 7.3 | §820.30 | Annex II |
| **Document Control** | Clause 4.2.4 | §820.40 | Article 10(9) |
| **Management Responsibility** | Clause 5 | §820.20 | Article 10(2) |

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Compliance Checklist (Memorization Table)

The following "compliance checklist" is the single most exam-relevant summary for the KTU 2024 Module 4 question bank. **Memorize this table — questions are asked directly from it.**

| # | Compliance Item | Governing Standard | KTU 2024 Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Documented Quality Manual | ISO 13485:2016 | Clause 4.2.2 |
| 2 | Management Review (at least annually) | ISO 13485:2016 | Clause 5.6 |
| 3 | Internal Audit Program | ISO 13485:2016 | Clause 8.2.4 |
