| 5 | Risk Management File | ISO 14971:2019 | Linked to ISO 14155 |
| 6 | Clinical Evaluation Report (CER) | EU MDR Annex XIV | Required pre-market |
| 7 | Declaration of Helsinki Compliance | ISO 14155:2011 | Clause 4 |
| 8 | Ethics Committee Approval | ISO 14155:2011 | Clause 4.3 |
| 9 | Informed Consent Form (ICF) | ISO 14155:2011 | Clause 4.7 |
| 10 | Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP) | ISO 14155:2011 | Clause 5 |
| 11 | Case Report Form (CRF) | ISO 14155:2011 | Clause 6 |
| 12 | Adverse Event Reporting System | ISO 14155:2011 / 21 CFR 803 | Clause 6.4 |
| 13 | Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA) | ISO 13485:2016 / 21 CFR 820.100 | Clause 8.5.2/3 |
| 14 | Document & Record Control | ISO 13485:2016 | Clause 4.2.4 |
| 15 | Post-Market Surveillance Plan | EU MDR / ISO 13485 | Article 84 |

## 2.5 Why These Standards Exist — The Engineering Utility

In the biomedical engineering field, GCP and QSR standards serve three critical functions:

1. **Patient Safety** — They prevent the deployment of unsafe or poorly tested devices.
2. **Regulatory Pathway Enablement** — Without compliance, a device cannot be legally marketed in the EU, USA, Japan, or Canada.
3. **Trade Facilitation** — A single ISO 13485 certification enables market access in 150+ countries through **MDSAP (Medical Device Single Audit Program)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Real-World Application:**
> For a B.Tech Biomedical Engineer entering the medical device industry, understanding the **21 CFR 820 / ISO 13485 QMS structure** is **more valuable** than memorizing product-specific regulations. Every medical device company (Stryker, Medtronic, Siemens Healthineers, Philips) requires QMS expertise as a foundational competency.

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 The Complete Clinical Investigation Lifecycle (Per ISO 14155:2011)

The clinical investigation of a medical device follows a **strict 10-step lifecycle**. KTU examiners frequently test students on this sequential process. The derivation below shows the logical flow from device conception to close-out.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Walk-Through

**Step 1 — Risk-Based Feasibility Assessment**
Before initiating a clinical investigation, the **sponsor** must perform a **Risk Management Analysis** per ISO 14971:2019. This involves:
- Identifying all foreseeable hazards of the device.
- Estimating and evaluating the associated risks.
- Determining whether the residual risk is acceptable for the proposed clinical investigation.
- Documenting the outcome in a **Risk Management Report (RMR)**.

> **Logic:** "If the risk of testing the device on humans is already too high even before testing, no clinical investigation should be initiated."

**Step 2 — Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP) Development**
The sponsor drafts a **comprehensive CIP** following the requirements of ISO 14155:2011 Clause 5. The CIP must contain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CIP} = \{ & \text{Background \& Rationale}, \\
& \text{Objectives (Primary \& Secondary)}, \\
& \text{Investigation Design (Type)}, \\
& \text{Subject Population \& Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria}, \\
& \text{Sample Size Justification}, \\
& \text{Investigational Device Description}, \\
& \text{Procedures \& Visit Schedule}, \\
& \text{Statistical Analysis Plan}, \\
& \text{Adverse Event Reporting Plan}, \\
& \text{Ethical Considerations} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Ethics Committee (EC) Submission**
The sponsor (or PI) submits the following to the Ethics Committee for review:

$$
\text{EC Submission} = \{ \text{CIP}, \text{ICF}, \text{Investigator Brochure (IB)}, \text{Device Description}, \text{Risk-Benefit Analysis} \}
$$

The EC reviews the submission and either **approves**, **approves with modifications**, or **rejects** the investigation.

> **KTU Note:** The EC must be **independent** from the sponsor, the investigator, and the funding source. This is a critical point frequently tested.

**Step 4 — Regulatory Authority Notification / Approval**
Depending on the jurisdiction, the sponsor must also submit to the regulatory authority:
- **EU MDR (Class III, implantables, class IIb active devices):** Notified Body approval required.
- **US FDA (Significant Risk Devices):** Investigational Device Exemption (IDE) submission.
- **India CDSCO:** MD-27 application for investigational medical devices.

**Step 5 — Site Selection and Investigator Qualification**
The sponsor selects investigation sites and verifies that the **Principal Investigator (PI)** meets the qualification requirements:
- Medical degree (or equivalent).
- Experience in the relevant medical specialty.
- Knowledge of GCP and applicable regulations.
- Adequate site facilities and support staff.

**Step 6 — Informed Consent Process**
The PI (or delegated, qualified personnel) conducts the **informed consent discussion** with each potential subject:

$$
\text{Informed Consent} = \underbrace{\text{Information Disclosure}}_{\text{Purpose, Risks, Benefits, Alternatives}} + \underbrace{\text{Comprehension}}_{\text{Subject's Understanding}} + \underbrace{\text{Voluntariness}}_{\text{No Coercion}} + \underbrace{\text{Documented Consent}}_{\text{Signed ICF}}
$$

> **Logic:** The subject must be told in **plain, non-technical language** that they can withdraw at any time, and that participation is voluntary.

**Step 7 — Subject Enrollment and Clinical Investigation Conduct**
Subjects are enrolled according to the **inclusion/exclusion criteria** specified in the CIP. The PI performs the procedures as per the CIP and records data in **Case Report Forms (CRFs)**.

The data collection follows this equation:

$$
\text{Data}_{Total} = \text{Data}_{Baseline} + \text{Data}_{Follow-up} + \text{Data}_{Adverse Events} + \text{Data}_{Device Performance}
$$

**Step 8 — Continuous Monitoring and AE/SAE Reporting**
The sponsor establishes a **monitoring plan** to oversee the investigation. Adverse events are classified, evaluated, and reported within strict regulatory timelines:

$$
\text{AE Timeline} = \begin{cases} \text{Significant SAE: Report within } \mathbf{10} \text{ calendar days} \\ \text{Death or Life-Threatening SAE: Report within } \mathbf{7} \text{ calendar days} \\ \text{Non-serious AE: Report in periodic safety reports} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 9 — Statistical Analysis and Clinical Investigation Report (CIR)**
Upon completion (or early termination), the data is statistically analyzed per the **Statistical Analysis Plan** in the CIP. A final **Clinical Investigation Report (CIR)** is produced, containing:
- Results summary.
- Statistical analysis outcomes.
- Safety and performance evaluation.
- Conclusions and any deviations.

**Step 10 — Close-Out and Regulatory Submission**
The sponsor closes the investigation, archives all essential documents for a minimum of **15 years** (EU MDR requirement) or as per local regulation, and submits the CIR to the EC and regulatory authority.

> **Logic of the Lifecycle:** The whole system is **self-correcting**: monitoring (Step 8) feeds back into risk management (Step 1), creating a closed-loop quality system.

## 3.2 The Quality System Regulation (QSR) Implementation Cycle

The QSR is implemented as a **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle, as defined in ISO 13485:2016. KTU examiners expect students to map QMS activities onto this cycle.

**Step 1 — PLAN (Quality Manual, Quality Policy, Quality Objectives)**
The **management** establishes:
- Quality policy.
- Quality objectives.
- Quality manual.
- Documented procedures.

**Step 2 — DO (Implementation of QMS)**
The organization implements:
- Design and development controls (Clause 7.3).
- Production and service controls (Clause 7.5).
- Purchasing controls (Clause 7.4).
- Document and record controls (Clause 4.2).

**Step 3 — CHECK (Monitoring and Measurement)**
The organization performs:
- Internal audits (Clause 8.2.4).
- Monitoring and measurement of processes (Clause 8.2.5).
- Monitoring and measurement of product (Clause 8.2.6).
- Customer feedback and complaint handling (Clause 8.2.1).

**Step 4 — ACT (Improvement via CAPA)**
The organization executes:
- **Corrective Action** (Clause 8.5.2) — to eliminate causes of non-conformities.
- **Preventive Action** (Clause 8.5.3) — to eliminate potential non-conformities.
- **Management Review** (Clause 5.6) — to ensure continuing suitability.

The PDCA cycle is mathematically expressed as:

$$
QMS_{Effectiveness} = f(\text{PLAN}, \text{DO}, \text{CHECK}, \text{ACT}) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left( \text{Improvement}_{n+1} > \text{Improvement}_{n} \right)
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Regulations to Their Function

| Regulatory Function | Responsible Standard | Output Document | KTU 2024 Module 4 Connection |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ethical protection of subjects | ISO 14155:2011 | EC approval, ICF | Module 4 — GCP for CIs |
| Risk management of the device | ISO 14971:2019 | Risk Management File | Cross-cutting with QSR |
| Manufacturing quality | ISO 13485:2016 | Quality Manual | Module 4 — QSR |
| US regulatory compliance | 21 CFR 820 | QSR compliance record | Module 4 — QSR |
| EU regulatory compliance | EU MDR 2017/745 | Technical Documentation, Declaration of Conformity | Module 4 — Conformity Assessment |
| Post-market surveillance | ISO 13485 + EU MDR Article 83 | PMS Plan, PSUR | Module 4 — QSR |
| Vigilance and AE reporting | ISO 14155 + 21 CFR 803 | AE/SAE reports | Module 4 — GCP |
| Labeling and UDI | 21 CFR 801, EU MDR Article 27 | Label, UDI-DI | Adjacent Module |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Cross-Connection Insight:**
> Notice how the **clinical investigation data** (collected under ISO 14155) feeds directly into the **Clinical Evaluation Report (CER)** required by EU MDR, which itself is part of the **Technical Documentation** required under ISO 13485. This is why **all three standards form a single regulatory chain**.

# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 ISO 14155 Clinical Investigation Process Flow

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **end-to-end clinical investigation lifecycle** as defined in ISO 14155:2011. This is a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow, mapping the interactions between the Sponsor, Investigator, Ethics Committee, Regulatory Authority, and Subject.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    id1[A1: RISK ASSESSMENT] --> id2[A2: CIP DEVELOPMENT]
    id2 --> id3[A3: EC SUBMISSION]
    id3 --> id4{EC4: EC APPROVAL}
    id4 -->|REJECTED| id5[A5: REVISED CIP]
    id5 --> id3
    id4 -->|APPROVED| id6[A6: REGULATORY SUBMISSION]
    id6 --> id7{RA7: REGULATORY APPROVAL}
    id7 -->|NOT APPROVED| id8[A8: APPEAL OR MODIFY]
    id8 --> id6
    id7 -->|APPROVED| id9[A9: SITE AND PI SELECTION]
    id9 --> id10[A10: INFORMED CONSENT]
    id10 --> id11[A11: SUBJECT ENROLLMENT]
    id11 --> id12[A12: CI CONDUCT AND MONITORING]
    id12 -->|AE OR SAE| id13[A13: AE SAE REPORTING]
    id13 --> id12
    id12 --> id14[A14: DATA COLLECTION IN CRF]
    id14 --> id15[A15: STATISTICAL ANALYSIS]
    id15 --> id16[A16: CLINICAL INVESTIGATION REPORT]
    id16 --> id17[A17: EC AND RA NOTIFICATION]
    id17 --> id18[A18: ARCHIVING FOR 15 YEARS]
```

> **Diagram Reading Guide:**
> - Boxes with the "A" prefix represent sequential activities mandated by ISO 14155:2011.
> - Diamond shapes (EC4, RA7) represent **decision gates** where approval is required before proceeding.
> - The loop between A3, A4, and A5 represents the iterative revision process when EC approval is not initially granted.
> - The loop between A12, A13, and A14 represents the continuous AE/SAE reporting cycle during investigation conduct.

## 4.2 Quality System Regulation Framework Architecture

The following block diagram maps the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) QMS cycle** as it integrates with the regulatory and clinical layers of a medical device organization.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["REGULATORY LAYER"]
        id20[ISO 13485:2016 QMS] --> id21[21 CFR 820 US FDA]
        id20 --> id22[EU MDR 2017/745]
        id20 --> id23[ISO 14971 RISK MGMT]
    end

    subgraph L2["PLAN LAYER"]
        id30[QUALITY MANUAL] --> id31[QUALITY POLICY]
        id30 --> id32[QUALITY OBJECTIVES]
        id30 --> id33[DOCUMENTED PROCEDURES]
    end

    subgraph L3["DO LAYER"]
        id40[DESIGN CONTROLS] --> id41[PURCHASING CONTROLS]
        id40 --> id42[PRODUCTION CONTROLS]
        id40 --> id43[RECORD CONTROLS]
    end

    subgraph L4["CHECK LAYER"]
        id50[INTERNAL AUDIT] --> id51[PROCESS MONITORING]
        id50 --> id52[PRODUCT MONITORING]
        id50 --> id53[COMPLAINT HANDLING]
    end

    subgraph L5["ACT LAYER"]
        id60[CORRECTIVE ACTION] --> id61[PREVENTIVE ACTION]
        id60 --> id62[MANAGEMENT REVIEW]
        id60 --> id63[CONTINUAL IMPROVEMENT]
    end

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L2
```

> **Diagram Reading Guide:**
> - **L1 (Regulatory Layer):** External standards that define the rules.
> - **L2 (Plan Layer):** Documentation and policy formation.
> - **L3 (Do Layer):** Operational implementation.
> - **L4 (Check Layer):** Verification and feedback.
> - **L5 (Act Layer):** Improvement and corrective action.
> - The arrow from **L5 back to L2** demonstrates the **closed-loop nature of the PDCA cycle**.

## 4.3 Risk-Benefit Decision Flow in Clinical Investigations

The following block diagram illustrates the **continuous risk-benefit evaluation** logic mandated by ISO 14155:2011, mirroring the requirement under EU MDR Annex I, Section 8.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    id70[START CI] --> id71[IDENTIFY KNOWN AND FORESEEABLE HAZARDS]
    id71 --> id72[ESTIMATE RISK PROBABILITY AND SEVERITY]
    id72 --> id73[EVALUATE RISK VERSUS CLINICAL BENEFIT]
    id73 --> id74{RISK ACCEPTABLE}
    id74 -->|NO| id75[STOP OR MODIFY CI]
    id75 --> id71
    id74 -->|YES| id76[CONTINUE CI WITH MONITORING]
    id76 --> id77[NEW HAZARD DETECTED]
    id77 -->|YES| id71
    id77 -->|NO| id78[CONTINUE MONITORING]
    id78 --> id79[END CI]
```

> **Diagram Reading Guide:**
> - This diagram operationalizes **ISO 14971 risk management** within a clinical investigation.
> - The cycle ensures **continuous re-evaluation** of risks throughout the entire investigation period.
> - The "STOP OR MODIFY CI" branch represents the **suspension and termination** provisions of ISO 14155:2011 Clause 7.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — PITFALL CALLOUT**
> Students frequently lose marks on Module 4 questions for the following reasons:
> 1. **Confusing ISO 14155 with ISO 13485** — ISO 14155 is for *clinical investigations*, ISO 13485 is for *manufacturing QMS*. Mixing these up is the **#1 deduction point** in valuation.
> 2. **Omitting the Declaration of Helsinki reference** — When asked about ethical principles, ALWAYS mention the **Declaration of Helsinki** as the foundational document.
> 3. **Forgetting the Sponsor's responsibilities** — The Sponsor (not the PI) is responsible for **financing, monitoring, regulatory reporting, and pharmacovigilance (vigilance)**. The PI is responsible for **site-level conduct, subject care, and informed consent**.
> 4. **Not mentioning Ethics Committee independence** — The EC must be **independent** from the sponsor, PI, and investigation site. This single word is worth 2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping timelines** — AE/SAE reporting has **strict deadlines** (7 days for death/life-threatening, 10 days for serious). Examiners expect these timelines.

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Good Clinical Practice (GCP) as per ISO 14155:2011. State its scope in the context of medical device clinical investigation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Good Clinical Practice (GCP) for medical device clinical investigation, as defined in **ISO 14155:2011**, is the international ethical and scientific quality standard for the **design, conduct, recording, monitoring, auditing, analysis, and reporting** of clinical investigations carried out in human subjects to assess the **safety and performance** of medical devices.

**Scope** (2 marks):
- The scope of ISO 14155:2011 covers **all clinical investigations of medical devices** that involve **human subjects**, whether sponsored by manufacturers, research organizations, or investigators.
- It applies to investigations conducted for **regulatory submissions, post-market clinical follow-up (PMCF), and scientific research**.
- It does **not apply to in vitro diagnostic medical devices** (which are covered by ISO 20916) or to non-clinical bench testing.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List any six responsibilities of the Sponsor as specified in ISO 14155:2011.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.5 marks each):**
According to ISO 14155:2011, the Sponsor has the following responsibilities:

1. **Selection of qualified investigators and sites** — The Sponsor must ensure that investigators are suitably qualified and that sites have adequate resources.
2. **Preparation and maintenance of the Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)** — The Sponsor is responsible for drafting, updating, and ensuring adherence to the CIP.
3. **Risk management** — The Sponsor must conduct a risk assessment per ISO 14971 and maintain a Risk Management File.
4. **Provision of the investigational device** — The Sponsor must ensure the device is manufactured under an appropriate QMS and is properly labeled.
5. **Adverse event reporting** — The Sponsor must ensure timely reporting of SAEs, ADEs, and device deficiencies to regulatory authorities, ECs, and investigators.
6. **Monitoring of the clinical investigation** — The Sponsor must appoint monitors and conduct regular monitoring visits.
7. **Archiving of essential documents** — For a minimum of **15 years** post-investigation completion.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) Explain in detail the 13 principles of Good Clinical Practice (GCP) for medical device clinical investigation as per ISO 14155:2011.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks — 1 mark for ~2 principles):**

The 13 principles of GCP, as outlined in ISO 14155:2011, are the ethical and scientific quality standards that must be followed for any medical device clinical investigation. They are:

1. **Ethical Conduct (Declaration of Helsinki):** Every clinical investigation shall be conducted in accordance with the ethical principles that have their origin in the **Declaration of Helsinki**. The Declaration, adopted by the World Medical Association, provides the foundational framework for ethical medical research on human subjects.

2. **Risk-Benefit Justification:** The anticipated benefits to the subjects and/or society must outweigh the foreseeable risks. The Sponsor must demonstrate this balance through a **Risk Management File** per ISO 14971.

3. **Subject Rights Precedence:** The rights, safety, and well-being of the subjects take precedence over the interests of science, society, or commercial enterprise. This principle is non-negotiable.

4. **Scientific Soundness:** Clinical investigations must have **clearly defined, scientifically valid objectives** and must be designed to generate reliable, reproducible data. The study design must be appropriate to the device and the clinical question.

5. **Qualified Personnel:** Clinical investigations must be conducted by **suitably qualified and trained personnel**. The PI must have relevant medical qualifications and demonstrable GCP training.

6. **Regulatory Compliance:** All clinical investigations must comply with **applicable national and international regulatory requirements**, including the submission of regulatory dossiers where required (e.g., IDE in USA, MD-27 in India).

7. **Documented Procedures:** The investigation must follow a written **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)** that describes the design, methodology, and conduct of the study. All data must be captured in **Case Report Forms (CRFs)**.

8. **Device Conformity:** The investigational medical device must be manufactured under an appropriate **Quality Management System** (ISO 13485:2016 or equivalent) and must conform to applicable regulatory requirements.

9. **Risk Management Linkage:** The clinical investigation must align with the manufacturer's **Risk Management File** per ISO 14971:2019, and the investigation itself must be considered within the device's overall risk management process.

10. **Informed Consent:** Written informed consent must be obtained from every subject **before** any investigation-related procedure is performed. The consent must be voluntary, informed, and documented.

11. **Independent Ethics Review:** The investigation must be reviewed and approved by an **independent Ethics Committee (EC)** before initiation. The EC's role is to ensure the protection of subject rights.

12. **Data Integrity and Confidentiality:** All clinical data must be accurately recorded, handled, stored, and reported to allow verification, while maintaining the **confidentiality of subject information** in accordance with applicable data protection laws.

13. **Adverse Event and Device Deficiency Reporting:** All adverse events (AEs), serious adverse events (SAEs), adverse device effects (ADEs), and device deficiencies must be recorded, evaluated, and reported to the Sponsor, EC, and regulatory authorities within defined regulatory timelines.

---

**(b) Describe the documentation and record-keeping requirements under ISO 14155:2011.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

Documentation and record-keeping are fundamental to compliance with ISO 14155:2011. The standard requires that the Sponsor, PI, and EC maintain comprehensive, accurate, and traceable records throughout the clinical investigation lifecycle.

**1. Investigator Brochure (IB):** This document provides comprehensive information about the investigational device, including its intended use, design, materials, manufacturing process, pre-clinical testing results, known risks, and expected clinical benefits. It is updated as new information becomes available.

**2. Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP):** The CIP is the master document of the investigation. It must include:
- The rationale and objectives of the investigation.
- The investigation design (e.g., randomized, controlled, blinded).
- Subject inclusion and exclusion criteria.
- Sample size justification with statistical reasoning.
- Detailed procedures and visit schedule.
- Adverse event handling and reporting procedures.
- Ethical considerations and subject protection measures.

**3. Case Report Form (CRF):** A pre-designed, structured form used to record all clinical data collected from each subject during the investigation. The CRF must be completed accurately, legibly, and in a timely manner.

**4. Informed Consent Form (ICF):** A signed document confirming the subject's voluntary participation after being fully informed of the investigation's purpose, procedures, risks, benefits, and alternatives. The ICF must be in the local language of the subject.

**5. Ethics Committee (EC) Approval Documentation:** The official approval letter, the EC's composition, and the EC's deliberations must be documented. Any amendments to the CIP or ICF must be re-submitted to the EC for re-approval.

**6. Adverse Event and SAE Reports:** All AEs, SAEs, ADEs, and device deficiencies must be recorded in dedicated forms with detailed information on onset, severity, outcome, and causality assessment. SAEs must be reported to the Sponsor within **24 hours** of site awareness, with regulatory notification within **7 days** for life-threatening events and **10 days** for serious events.

**7. Clinical Investigation Report (CIR):** A comprehensive final report prepared at the conclusion of the investigation, summarizing the methods, results, statistical analysis, and conclusions. The CIR must be submitted to the EC and the regulatory authority.

**8. Essential Documents and Archiving:** All essential documents (CIP, ICF, CRF, EC approvals, AE reports, training records, device traceability records) must be archived by the Sponsor and PI for a minimum of **15 years** (per EU MDR) or as per the local regulatory requirement. Records must be protected from unauthorized access and must be retrievable upon regulatory audit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Reminder for Documentation:**
> The **principle of traceability** is central to ISO 14155:2011 documentation. Every data point in the CRF must be traceable to a **source document** (hospital record, lab report, device log), and every adverse event must be traceable from initial report to final regulatory notification.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the Quality System Regulations (QSR) governing the manufacture of medical devices. Discuss the key requirements of ISO 13485:2016.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Definition of QSR:** Quality System Regulations (QSR) are the regulatory frameworks that govern the **design, manufacture, packaging, labeling, storage, distribution, installation, servicing, and post-market surveillance** of medical devices to ensure that they consistently meet safety, performance, and quality requirements. The two most prominent QSR systems are **ISO 13485:2016** (international) and **21 CFR Part 820** (US FDA).

**Key Requirements of ISO 13485:2016** (7 marks allocated as below):

1. **Quality Management System (QMS) — Clause 4 (1 mark):** The organization must establish, document, implement, and maintain a QMS and continually improve its effectiveness. This includes the preparation of a **Quality Manual** that defines the scope of the QMS, the documented procedures, and a description of the interaction between processes.

2. **Management Responsibility — Clause 5 (1 mark):** Top management must demonstrate commitment to the QMS by ensuring customer focus, establishing a quality policy and objectives, conducting **management reviews** at least once every 12 months, and ensuring adequate resources.

3. **Resource Management — Clause 6 (0.5 marks):** The organization must determine and provide adequate resources, including human resources, infrastructure, and work environment, to maintain the QMS and meet regulatory requirements.

4. **Product Realization — Clause 7 (2 marks):** This is the largest clause and includes:
   - **Planning of product realization (7.1).**
   - **Customer-related processes (7.2)**, including communication with regulatory authorities.
   - **Design and development (7.3)**, requiring a design plan, design input/output, design review, design verification, design validation, design transfer, and design changes.
   - **Purchasing (7.4)**, requiring supplier evaluation and control.
   - **Production and service provision (7.5)**, requiring production controls, installation activities, servicing, and traceability.
   - **Control of monitoring and measuring devices (7.6)**.

5. **Measurement, Analysis, and Improvement — Clause 8 (2 marks):** The organization must plan and implement monitoring, measurement, analysis, and improvement processes:
   - **Feedback (8.2.1)** for early detection of quality problems.
   - **Complaint handling (8.2.1, 8.2.3).**
   - **Internal audit (8.2.4)** at planned intervals.
   - **Monitoring and measurement of processes (8.2.5) and product (8.2.6).**
   - **Control of nonconforming product (8.3).**
   - **Analysis of data (8.4)** to demonstrate the suitability and effectiveness of the QMS.
   - **Improvement (8.5)**, including **Corrective Action (8.5.2)** to eliminate causes of nonconformities, **Preventive Action (8.5.3)** to eliminate potential causes, and **Continual Improvement**.

6. **Risk-Based Approach (0.5 marks):** ISO 13485:2016 requires a risk-based approach throughout the QMS, with risk management activities documented per **ISO 14971:2019**.

---

**(b) Compare and contrast ISO 13485:2016 and 21 CFR Part 820 as Quality System Regulations for medical devices.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks — Tabular Comparison):**

| Aspect | ISO 13485:2016 | 21 CFR Part 820 (US FDA QSR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Issuing Body** | International Organization for Standardization (ISO) | United States Food and Drug Administration (US FDA) |
| **Geographic Scope** | International (adopted in 150+ countries) | United States (legally binding) |
| **Legal Status** | Voluntary (often required by regulators and Notified Bodies) | Legally mandatory for all US medical device manufacturers |
| **Structure** | 8 clauses following the ISO High-Level Structure | 15 subparts (§820.1 to §820.250) |
| **Management Review** | Required at least once every 12 months (Clause 5.6) | Required (§820.20(c)) |
| **Design Controls** | Clause 7.3 (Design and Development) | §820.30 (Design Controls) |
| **Document Controls** | Clause 4.2.4 | §820.40 |
| **Purchasing Controls** | Clause 7.4 | §820.50 |
| **Identification & Traceability** | Clause 7.5.8 | §820.60 and §820.65 |
| **Production & Process Controls** | Clause 7.5.1 and 7.5.2 | §820.70 and §820.72 |
| **Acceptance Activities** | Clause 7.5.1 (including installation) | §820.80 and §820.86 |
| **Nonconforming Product** | Clause 8.3 | §820.90 |
| **Corrective and Preventive Action** | Clause 8.5.2 and 8.5.3 | §820.100 |
| **Labeling and Packaging** | Clause 7.5.5 | §820.120 and §820.130 |
| **Handling, Storage, Distribution, Installation** | Clause 7.5.5, 7.5.1 | §820.140, §820.150, §820.160, §820.170 |
| **Records** | Clause 4.2.5 (15 years for medical device records) | §820.180 (Life of device + 2 years) |
| **Servicing** | Clause 7.5.1 | §820.200 |
| **Statistical Techniques** | Clause 8.1 (where appropriate) | §820.250 |
| **Risk-Based Approach** | Explicit requirement, aligned with ISO 14971 | Implicit throughout |
| **MDSAP Recognition** | Yes (single audit accepted in 5 countries) | Yes (FDA participates in MDSAP) |

**Key Differences (2 marks):**
1. **Legal Force:** 21 CFR 820 is a **federal regulation with legal force in the USA**, whereas ISO 13485 is a **voluntary international standard** that becomes mandatory only when referenced by a regulatory authority (e.g., EU MDR, Health Canada).
2. **Documentation Philosophy:** ISO 13485 follows the **process-based approach** of the ISO High-Level Structure, while 21 CFR 820 follows a **prescriptive, subpart-by-subpart approach** based on the older FDA GMP framework.
3. **Risk Management:** ISO 13485 has an **explicit linkage to ISO 14971** for risk management, whereas 21 CFR 820 embeds risk considerations throughout but does not mandate a specific risk management standard.

**Convergence (1 mark):**
- Both standards share the same fundamental objectives: ensuring medical device **safety, efficacy, and quality**.
- The **Medical Device Single Audit Program (MDSAP)** allows manufacturers to undergo a single audit that satisfies the QMS requirements of multiple jurisdictions (USA, Canada, Japan, Brazil, Australia), reducing audit burden.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 One-Line Exam Tip:**
> If a question asks "Compare ISO 13485 and 21 CFR 820," always end with the line: *"While ISO 13485 is a globally accepted voluntary QMS standard, 21 CFR 820 is a legally binding US federal regulation; both aim to ensure medical device safety and effectiveness, and both are recognized under MDSAP."*

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Quick-Recall Bullet Summary

- **ISO 14155:2011** is the international GCP standard for **clinical investigations of medical devices** in human subjects.
- The standard has **13 principles** rooted in the **Declaration of Helsinki**.
- **Three pillars of ISO 14155:2011:** Ethical Conduct, Scientific Validity, Regulatory Compliance.
- **Sponsor's responsibilities:** Initiation, management, financing, monitoring, AE/SAE reporting, archiving.
- **PI's responsibilities:** Site-level conduct, subject care, informed consent, CRF completion.
- **Ethics Committee (EC)** must be **independent** and must approve the investigation **before** initiation.
- **Informed Consent** must be **written, voluntary, informed, and documented** before any study procedure.
- **AE Reporting Timelines:** **24 hours** (Sponsor), **7 days** (death/life-threatening to regulator), **10 days** (serious to regulator).
- **ISO 13485:2016** governs the **QMS for medical device manufacturing** — it is the recipe book, not the trial protocol.
- **21 CFR Part 820** is the **US FDA's QSR** — legally binding in the USA.
- **ISO 14971:2019** governs **risk management** of medical devices.
- **EU MDR 2017/745** is the **current European medical device regulation**, replacing the older MDD 93/42/EEC.
- **MDSAP** enables a single QMS audit to satisfy multiple jurisdictions (USA, Canada, Japan, Brazil, Australia).
- **Essential documents** (CIP, IB, ICF, CRF, EC approval, AE reports, CIR) must be archived for **15 years** (EU MDR).
- The clinical investigation lifecycle follows **10 sequential steps** from risk assessment to close-out and archival.
- The QMS implementation follows the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle.
- The relationship chain: **ISO 14155 (clinical data) → CER (clinical evaluation) → Technical Documentation → CE Marking (EU MDR) / 510(k) or PMA (US FDA)**.
- **CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action)** is the engine of continual improvement under both ISO 13485 and 21 CFR 820.
- **Design Controls** (Clause 7.3 / §820.30) are critical to ensure the device is designed to meet user needs, intended use, and regulatory requirements.

### Common KTU 2024 Module 4 Keywords to Recognize
`ISO 14155`, `GCP`, `Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)`, `Case Report Form (CRF)`, `Sponsor`, `Principal Investigator`, `Ethics Committee (EC)`, `Informed Consent Form (ICF)`, `Adverse Event (AE)`, `Serious Adverse Event (SAE)`, `Adverse Device Effect (ADE)`, `Device Deficiency`, `ISO 13485`, `Quality Management System (QMS)`, `21 CFR Part 820`, `ISO 14971`, `Risk Management`, `Design Controls`, `CAPA`, `MDSAP`, `EU MDR`, `Declaration of Helsinki`, `Conformity Assessment`, `Post-Market Surveillance`, `PDCA Cycle`, `Quality Manual`, `Management Review`, `Internal Audit`, `Essential Documents`, `CE Marking`, `IDE`, `510(k)`, `PMA`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examination Strategy for Module 4:**
> - For **Part A (3 marks):** Memorize **definitions** and **lists** of responsibilities (Sponsor, PI, EC). These are the most frequently asked 3-mark questions.
> - For **Part B (14 marks):** Practice writing the **13 principles of GCP** and the **CIP documentation chain**. Master the **comparison tables** between ISO 13485, 21 CFR 820, and EU MDR.
> - Always include the **Declaration of Helsinki** in any ethics-based answer.
> - Always include the **15-year archival requirement** in any documentation-based answer.
> - Always mention **EC independence** in any ethics-committee-based answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISO 13485 - Quality Risk Management of Medical Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ISO 13485 - Quality Risk Management of Medical Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is ISO 13485?

**ISO 13485** is the internationally recognized standard that specifies requirements for a **Quality Management System (QMS)** where an organization needs to demonstrate its ability to provide medical devices and related services that **consistently meet customer and applicable regulatory requirements**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (ISO 13485:2016):**
> *"ISO 13485:2016 specifies requirements for a quality management system where an organization needs to demonstrate its ability to provide medical devices and related services that consistently meet customer and applicable regulatory requirements."*

Such organizations can be involved in one or more stages of the **medical device life-cycle**, including:
- Design and development
- Production
- Storage and distribution
- Installation
- Servicing
- Final decommissioning and disposal

## 1.2 What is Quality Risk Management (QRM)?

**Quality Risk Management** is a systematic process applied across the medical device product life-cycle for the **identification, assessment, control, review, and communication of risks** to the quality of a medical device.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:**
> Quality Risk Management is a **systematic, proactive, and continuous process** to identify, assess, control, and communicate risks associated with medical devices throughout their life-cycle, ensuring that the benefits of use outweigh the associated risks.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of **ISO 13485 with Quality Risk Management** like running a **multi-cuisine restaurant chain** that serves food to thousands of people daily:

- **ISO 13485 (QMS)** = The complete **rulebook** of the restaurant chain — defines kitchen hygiene, staff training, supplier checks, customer feedback handling, etc.
- **Quality Risk Management** = The **"HACCP" (Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point) system** used in food safety. The chef doesn't just cook; he identifies hazards (bacteria, allergens, contamination), assesses severity, and puts controls in place (temperature checks, expiry tracking, supplier audits).
- **Risk-Based Approach** = The restaurant manager doesn't treat every item with equal attention. A simple bread roll gets less scrutiny than a sushi dish. Similarly, a tongue depressor has lower risk management requirements than a **pacemaker** or an **MRI machine**.

> [!TIP]
> Just as a **single food poisoning case** can shut down an entire restaurant chain, a **single device failure** can lead to product recalls, regulatory action, and patient harm. QRM prevents this through **proactive risk thinking** rather than **reactive damage control**.

## 1.4 Why ISO 13485 + Risk Management?

Medical devices directly impact **human life and health**. Unlike other products, a defect in a medical device can:
- Cause **direct patient harm** (e.g., faulty defibrillator)
- Lead to **delayed diagnosis** (e.g., inaccurate glucose meter)
- Result in **product recalls** (costing millions)
- Trigger **regulatory action** (license cancellation)

Therefore, ISO 13485 mandates a **risk-based approach** throughout the QMS, primarily referencing **ISO 14971** — the dedicated standard for medical device risk management.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Alert:** Students often confuse **ISO 13485** (QMS standard) with **ISO 14971** (Risk Management standard). Remember: **ISO 13485 mandates the use of risk management**, and **ISO 14971 describes HOW to do it**. They are complementary, not interchangeable.

## 1.5 The Two Standards – Their Relationship

| Aspect | ISO 13485 | ISO 14971 |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Full Name | Medical devices — Quality management systems | Medical devices — Application of risk management to medical devices |
| Focus | Quality Management System | Risk Management Process |
| Type | QMS Standard (like ISO 9001) | Process Standard (Risk Management) |
| Mandatory Citation | Required by regulators globally | Referenced as a normative reference in ISO 13485 |
| Approach | Defines WHAT the QMS must do | Defines HOW to perform risk management |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution of ISO 13485

| Version | Year | Key Highlight |
|---------|------|---------------|
| First Edition | 1996 | Adapted from ISO 9001 for medical devices |
| Second Edition | 2003 | Greater alignment with FDA QSR |
| **Third Edition (Current)** | **2016** | Stronger focus on **risk-based approach**, regulatory alignment, and post-market surveillance |

> [!NOTE]
> The 2016 version of ISO 13485 explicitly emphasizes **risk-based decision making** across the entire QMS — not just in product design.

## 2.2 The Risk-Based Approach in ISO 13485

ISO 13485:2016 does not provide a separate, isolated "Risk Management" section. Instead, it weaves the **risk-based approach** into nearly every clause of the QMS. This is a critical conceptual shift from the 2003 version.

### Where Risk-Based Thinking Appears in ISO 13485:

The standard requires organizations to apply a **risk-based approach** to:
1. **Control of records and documents**
2. **Management review** (Clause 5.6)
3. **Internal audit** (Clause 8.2.4)
4. **Corrective and Preventive Actions (CAPA)** (Clause 8.5.2 & 8.5.3)
5. **Design and development** (Clause 7.3)
6. **Purchasing and supplier control** (Clause 7.4)
7. **Production and service provision** (Clause 7.5)
8. **Post-market surveillance** (Clause 8.2.1)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet: Key Clauses of ISO 13485

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **most-asked clauses** in KTU university exams. Memorize the clause numbers and their risk-management relevance.

| Clause | Title | Risk Management Relevance |
|--------|-------|---------------------------|
| **4.1.2** | QMS - Process requirements | Risk-based control of QMS processes |
| **4.2.4** | Control of records | Risk-based retention of records |
| **5.0** | Management Responsibility | Top management shall promote risk-based thinking |
| **5.6** | Management Review | Reviewing the **effectiveness of risk management** activities |
| **6.2** | Human Resources | Competency in risk management |
| **7.1** | Planning of Product Realization | Risk management in product planning |
| **7.2.1** | Customer-Related Processes | Risk in contracts and customer communication |
| **7.3** | Design and Development | **Risk management activities throughout design** (links to ISO 14971) |
| **7.3.9** | Design and Development Changes | Risk evaluation of changes |
| **7.4** | Purchasing | **Risk-based supplier evaluation and selection** |
| **7.5.1** | Production and Service Provision | Risk-based process validation |
| **7.5.6** | Process Validation | Validation based on **risk to product conformity** |
| **8.2.1** | Feedback | **Post-market surveillance** as a risk input |
| **8.2.2** | Complaint Handling | Risk-based evaluation of complaints |
| **8.2.3** | Reporting to Regulatory Authorities | Risk-based adverse event reporting |
| **8.3** | Nonconforming Product | Risk-based disposition decisions |
| **8.5.2** | Corrective Action | Risk-based CAPA |
| **8.5.3** | Preventive Action | Risk-based prevention |

## 2.4 The Risk Management Process (Per ISO 14971)

The risk management process follows a structured flow, as mandated by **ISO 14971:2019** and referenced by ISO 13485.

### Step-by-Step Risk Management Process:

1. **Risk Analysis**
   - Identify hazards (sources of harm)
   - Estimate severity and probability of harm
   - Determine risk level

2. **Risk Evaluation**
   - Compare estimated risk against risk acceptability criteria
   - Decide if risk reduction is required

3. **Risk Control**
   - Implement measures to reduce risk
   - Analyze residual risk
   - Evaluate if residual risk is acceptable

4. **Risk Management Review**
   - Ensure all risks are acceptable before product release

5. **Production and Post-Production Information**
   - Collect real-world data
   - Update risk management file
   - Feed back into the risk management process

> [!TIP]
> The risk management process is **iterative and continuous** — it does not stop at product launch. Post-market data (complaints, adverse events, literature) continuously feeds back into the process.

## 2.5 Risk Acceptability Matrix

The standard does not prescribe numerical thresholds but provides a conceptual matrix:

$$
\text{Risk} = f(\text{Severity}, \text{Probability})
$$

| Probability \ Severity | Negligible | Minor | Serious | Catastrophic |
|-----------------------|------------|-------|---------|--------------|
| **Frequent** | Low | Medium | High | **Unacceptable** |
| **Probable** | Low | Medium | High | **Unacceptable** |
| **Occasional** | Negligible | Medium | High | High |
| **Remote** | Negligible | Low | Medium | High |
| **Improbable** | Negligible | Negligible | Low | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This matrix helps **biomedical engineers** prioritize risk control efforts during design reviews. High and Unacceptable cells demand immediate mitigation before product launch.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the medical device industry, the integration of ISO 13485 + ISO 14971 is essential for:

- **Design Engineers:** Documenting design FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)
- **Quality Engineers:** Implementing CAPA and trending complaint data
- **Regulatory Affairs:** Preparing CE Marking Technical Files and FDA 510(k) submissions
- **Manufacturing Engineers:** Validating production processes based on risk
- **Post-Market Teams:** Conducting PMS (Post-Market Surveillance) and vigilance reporting

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Detailed Analytical Breakdown of Risk Integration in ISO 13485

## 3.1 Risk Integration Across the Product Life-Cycle

The strength of ISO 13485:2016 lies in its **holistic risk perspective** — risk is considered from **concept to post-market**. Below is the exhaustive analytical breakdown:

### 3.1.1 Stage 1: Design and Development Planning (Clause 7.3.1)

During design planning, the organization must:
- Identify the **risk management activities** required at each design stage
- Define the **risk acceptability criteria**
- Allocate resources for risk management

$$
\text{Design Plan} = \{ \text{Stages, Reviews, Verification, Validation, Risk\ Activities} \}
$$

### 3.1.2 Stage 2: Design Inputs (Clause 7.3.3)

Risk management is initiated during design input identification. Hazards are preliminarily identified based on:
- Intended use
- User population
- Use environment
- Device-user interface

### 3.1.3 Stage 3: Design Outputs (Clause 7.3.4)

Design outputs must include:
- Risk management plan
- Risk analysis results
- Risk evaluation and control measures
- Risk management report

### 3.1.4 Stage 4: Design Verification and Validation (Clause 7.3.6 & 7.3.7)

Verification confirms design outputs meet design inputs. **Risk-based V&V** ensures that:
- High-risk functions are tested with higher rigor
- Edge cases (e.g., failure modes) are intentionally tested
- Usability engineering (IEC 62366) considers use-related risks

### 3.1.5 Stage 5: Design Transfer and Process Validation (Clause 7.5.6)

Production processes are validated based on the **risk to product conformity**:

$$
\text{Validation\ Level} \propto \text{Risk\ to\ Patient}
$$

For example:
- **Low risk:** Bandage manufacturing — minimal validation
- **High risk:** Sterile implant manufacturing — full IQ, OQ, PQ (Installation, Operational, Performance Qualification)

### 3.1.6 Stage 6: Post-Market Surveillance (Clause 8.2.1)

Once on the market, the organization must:
- Actively collect feedback
- Monitor adverse events
- Update the risk management file when new hazards emerge
- Re-evaluate the benefit-risk profile

$$
\text{Risk\ Management\ File}_{\text{updated}} = f(\text{Post-Market\ Data})
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Breakdown: Risk-Based Supplier Control (Clause 7.4)

The QMS must ensure that purchased products conform to requirements. The **type and extent of control** applied to suppliers is **proportionate to the risk** of the purchased product impacting the final device.

$$
\text{Supplier\ Control\ Level} = g(\text{Risk\ of\ Purchased\ Component})
$$

**Practical Application:**

| Component Type | Risk Level | Control Measure |
|----------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Packaging material | Low | Periodic audit, certificate of conformance |
| Electronic components | Medium | Sample testing, supplier qualification |
| Active implant parts | High | 100% inspection, on-site supplier audit, supplier agreement on QMS |

## 3.3 Analytical Breakdown: Risk-Based CAPA (Clause 8.5.2 & 8.5.3)

**Corrective Action** is required when nonconformity is detected. The standard mandates a **risk-based prioritization**:

$$
\text{CAPA\ Priority} = f(\text{Severity}, \text{Patient\ Impact}, \text{Regulatory\ Exposure})
$$

A complaint about a device that could cause **serious injury** must trigger an **immediate CAPA** with a defined timeline, whereas a complaint about packaging aesthetics may be **deferred** for batch processing.

## 3.4 Analytical Breakdown: Risk Management File (RMF)

The **Risk Management File** is a comprehensive set of records that demonstrates risk management activities throughout the product life-cycle. It includes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RMF} = \{ &\text{Risk Management Plan}, \\
&\text{Hazard Analysis}, \\
&\text{Risk Evaluation Records}, \\
&\text{Risk Control Measures}, \\
&\text{Residual Risk Evaluation}, \\
&\text{Risk Management Report}, \\
&\text{Post-Market Surveillance Data} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The RMF is the **centerpiece of regulatory submissions** (Technical File for EU MDR, Design Dossier for FDA PMA). Auditors will scrutinize the RMF during certification audits.

## 3.5 Key Conceptual Distinctions (Frequently Tested in KTU)

| Concept | Definition |
|---------|------------|
| **Hazard** | Potential source of harm |
| **Hazardous Situation** | Circumstance in which a hazard can lead to harm |
| **Harm** | Physical injury or damage to health |
| **Severity** | Measure of the possible consequences of a hazard |
| **Probability** | Likelihood of occurrence of harm |
| **Risk** | Combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm |
| **Risk Acceptability** | Decision threshold based on policy and risk-benefit analysis |
| **Residual Risk** | Risk remaining after risk control measures |
| **Benefit-Risk Analysis** | Weighing clinical benefits against residual risks |

## 3.6 Numerical Demonstration: Risk Calculation

While ISO 14971 does not mandate a specific numerical formula, many organizations use a **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** approach similar to FMEA:

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

where:
- $S$ = Severity (1 to 10)
- $O$ = Probability of Occurrence (1 to 10)
- $D$ = Detectability (1 to 10)

**Example Calculation:**

Consider a **blood glucose meter** with a defective display:
- Severity of wrong reading: $S = 8$ (could lead to incorrect insulin dose)
- Probability of display failure: $O = 4$ (moderate)
- Detectability of failure (poor): $D = 7$ (hard to notice immediately)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN} &= 8 \times 4 \times 7 \\
&= 224
\end{aligned}
$$

If the organization's threshold is **RPN < 100**, then this requires **immediate risk control action** (e.g., adding an audible low-battery alert, improved quality checks, or display self-test at startup).

After control measures:
- New $D = 3$ (better detectability through self-test)
- New RPN $= 8 \times 4 \times 3 = 96$ → **Acceptable**

This demonstrates the **iterative risk reduction** philosophy central to ISO 13485 and ISO 14971.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow: Risk Management in the Medical Device Life-Cycle

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates how Quality Risk Management integrates across the entire medical device life-cycle per ISO 13485 and ISO 14971.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Market Need and Intended Use Identified] --> B[Design and Development Planning]
    B --> C[Risk Management Plan Created]
    C --> D[Hazard Identification and Risk Analysis]
    D --> E[Risk Evaluation]
    E --> F{Is Risk Acceptable?}
    F -- No --> G[Implement Risk Control Measures]
    G --> H[Re-Evaluate Residual Risk]
    H --> F
    F -- Yes --> I[Document in Risk Management File]
    I --> J[Design Verification and Validation]
    J --> K[Process Validation and Production]
    K --> L[Product Launch and Distribution]
    L --> M[Post-Market Surveillance]
    M --> N{New Hazard Identified?}
    N -- Yes --> D
    N -- No --> O[Continuous Monitoring and Feedback]
    O --> P[Update Risk Management Report]
    P --> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The loop from M → D (post-market back to hazard identification) is the **feedback loop** that makes risk management a **living, continuous process** — not a one-time activity at design stage.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: ISO 13485 QMS with Embedded Risk-Based Approach

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph QMS[ISO 13485 Quality Management System]
        A1[Management Responsibility Clause 5] --> A2[Resource Management Clause 6]
        A2 --> A3[Product Realization Clause 7]
        A3 --> A4[Measurement Analysis and Improvement Clause 8]
    end

    subgraph RISK[Risk Management Engine ISO 14971]
        B1[Hazard Identification] --> B2[Risk Analysis]
        B2 --> B3[Risk Evaluation]
        B3 --> B4[Risk Control]
        B4 --> B5[Residual Risk Assessment]
        B5 --> B6[Post-Market Data Input]
    end

    A1 -.Risk Inputs.-> B1
    A3 -.Risk Activities.-> B2
    A3 -.Risk Control Verification.-> B4
    A4 -.CAPA and Feedback.-> B6
    B6 -.Updated Risk Data.-> A4
    B5 -.Risk Management Report.-> A1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The dotted arrows (labeled `.->`) represent **risk-based information flows** that run **horizontally** through the QMS, connecting the vertical QMS process hierarchy with the **horizontal risk management engine**. This visualizes how risk is **not a separate department** but a **cross-cutting concern** of the entire QMS.

## 4.3 Risk Decision Flowchart: From Complaint to Closure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Complaint or Adverse Event Received] --> Eval{Severity Assessment}
    Eval -- Negligible --> Log[Log in Database and Trend Analysis]
    Eval -- Minor --> Inv[Investigation by Quality Team]
    Eval -- Serious --> Inv2[Immediate Investigation and Containment]
    Eval -- Catastrophic --> Escal[Escalate to Top Management and Regulatory Reporting]
    Inv --> CAPA{CAPA Required?}
    Inv2 --> CAPA
    Escal --> CAPA
    CAPA -- Yes --> Impl[Implement Corrective Action]
    CAPA -- No --> Close[Document and Close]
    Impl --> Verify[Verify Effectiveness]
    Verify --> Update[Update Risk Management File]
    Update --> Close
    Log --> Close
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** This diagram directly maps to **ISO 13485 Clause 8.2.2 (Complaint Handling)** and shows how risk-based thinking determines the **response pathway** for any complaint. This is a very common KTU question topic.

## 4.4 Risk-Based Supplier Control Matrix Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[Supplier Identified] --> RA[Risk Assessment of Purchased Product]
    RA --> C1[Low Risk Component]
    RA --> C2[Medium Risk Component]
    RA --> C3[High Risk Component]
    C1 --> P1[Certificate of Conformance and Periodic Audit]
    C2 --> P2[Sample Testing and Supplier Qualification]
    C3 --> P3[On-Site Audit, 100% Inspection, and Quality Agreement]
    P1 --> Monitor[Continuous Performance Monitoring]
    P2 --> Monitor
    P3 --> Monitor
    Monitor --> Reassess{Performance Acceptable?}
    Reassess -- No --> RA
    Reassess -- Yes --> Approve[Approved for Continued Use]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Quality Risk Management as applied to medical devices. Mention the relevant ISO standard. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Quality Risk Management (QRM)** is a systematic process applied across the medical device product life-cycle for the **identification, assessment, control, review, and communication of risks** to the quality of a medical device.
>
> The relevant international standard is **ISO 14971:2019 — "Medical devices — Application of risk management to medical devices"**, which is referenced as a normative document in **ISO 13485:2016**.
>
> QRM ensures that the **benefit of a medical device outweighs its associated risks** throughout the device's life-cycle, from design to post-market surveillance.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for ISO 14971 reference, 1 mark for ISO 13485 connection / life-cycle aspect)*

### Question 2: What is the significance of the "Risk Management File" (RMF) in ISO 13485? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Risk Management File (RMF)** is a comprehensive set of records and documents that provides evidence that **risk management activities have been planned, executed, and reviewed** throughout the medical device life-cycle.
>
> The RMF typically includes the **risk management plan, hazard analysis, risk evaluation records, risk control measures, residual risk evaluation, risk management report, and post-production information**.
>
> The RMF is the **centerpiece of regulatory submissions** (e.g., EU Technical File, FDA 510(k)) and is critically examined during **certification audits** by Notified Bodies and regulatory authorities. It demonstrates the manufacturer's **commitment to patient safety** and **regulatory compliance**.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for contents, 1 mark for regulatory/audit significance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **risk-based approach** as mandated by ISO 13485:2016. Discuss how risk management is integrated into **at least five key clauses** of the standard. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **risk management process** as outlined in **ISO 14971**, with a suitable example from a medical device. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a) — Risk-Based Approach in ISO 13485 (7 Marks)

The **risk-based approach** in ISO 13485:2016 represents a **paradigm shift** from earlier versions. Instead of treating risk management as a separate function, the standard embeds **risk-based decision making** across the entire QMS.

**Five Key Clauses Where Risk-Based Approach is Integrated:**

**1. Clause 4.1.2 — Process Control:**
The QMS must identify processes, determine their interaction, and apply **risk-based control criteria**. Processes that directly impact product safety and quality receive the **highest scrutiny**.

**2. Clause 7.3 — Design and Development:**
Risk management activities are planned and executed throughout design. Design inputs must consider **hazard identification**, and design verification must confirm **risk control effectiveness**.

**3. Clause 7.4 — Purchasing:**
The **type and extent of supplier control** is proportionate to the **risk of the purchased product** on the final device's safety and performance.

**4. Clause 7.5.6 — Process Validation:**
The **degree of validation** applied to production processes depends on the **risk to product conformity** resulting from process variation.

**5. Clause 8.2.2 — Complaint Handling and 8.5.2 — Corrective Action:**
Complaints are **prioritized based on patient risk**, and CAPA actions are weighted according to the **severity and probability of harm**.

**Conclusion:**
This risk-based approach ensures that **resources, attention, and controls are proportional to risk**, making the QMS more efficient and patient-safety oriented.

*(Award: 5 × 1.2 marks for each clause explanation = 6 marks + 1 mark for conclusion)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b) — Risk Management Process per ISO 14971 (7 Marks)

The **risk management process** in ISO 14971 follows a **structured, iterative, and life-cycle approach**.

**Step 1 — Risk Analysis:**
Identify **hazards** and estimate **severity** and **probability** of harm for each hazardous situation.

**Step 2 — Risk Evaluation:**
Compare the estimated risk against the organization's **risk acceptability policy** to determine if risk reduction is needed.

**Step 3 — Risk Control:**
If risk is not acceptable, apply control measures in the following priority order:
1. **Inherent safety by design** (best practice)
2. **Protective measures** (e.g., alarms, guards)
3. **Information for safety** (e.g., labeling, training)

**Step 4 — Evaluation of Residual Risk:**
After control measures, assess if the **residual risk** is acceptable. Conduct a **benefit-risk analysis** if residual risk is high.

**Step 5 — Risk Management Review:**
Verify that all risks are acceptable before product release.

**Step 6 — Production and Post-Production:**
Collect feedback, monitor complaints, and **update the risk management file** continuously.

**Example: Insulin Pump**
- **Hazard:** Over-delivery of insulin (causing hypoglycemia)
- **Severity:** Catastrophic
- **Risk Control:** Software-based maximum dose limiter, occlusion alarm, user training
- **Residual Risk Evaluation:** Acceptable due to clinical benefit
- **Post-Market:** Continuous adverse event monitoring

*(Award: 1.4 marks per step × 5 steps = 7 marks; deduct partial marks for missing example)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **structure and key requirements** of ISO 13485:2016. How does it differ from ISO 9001? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **post-market surveillance (PMS)** in Quality Risk Management. How is PMS integrated with the risk management file? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a) — Structure of ISO 13485 & Comparison with ISO 9001 (7 Marks)

**Structure of ISO 13485:2016 (Eight Clauses):**

**Clause 1–3:** Scope, Normative References, Terms and Definitions
**Clause 4: Quality Management System**
- 4.1 General requirements, 4.2 Documentation requirements, 4.3 Medical device file

**Clause 5: Management Responsibility**
- 5.1 Management commitment, 5.6 Management review

**Clause 6: Resource Management**
- 6.1 Provision of resources, 6.2 Human resources, 6.3 Infrastructure, 6.4 Work environment

**Clause 7: Product Realization**
- 7.1 Planning, 7.2 Customer-related processes, 7.3 Design and development, 7.4 Purchasing, 7.5 Production and service provision

**Clause 8: Measurement, Analysis, and Improvement**
- 8.1 General, 8.2 Monitoring and measurement, 8.3 Nonconforming product, 8.4 Analysis of data, 8.5 Improvement (CAPA)

**Key Differences from ISO 9001:**

| Aspect | ISO 13485 | ISO 9001 |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| **Focus** | QMS for medical devices | Generic QMS for all industries |
| **Regulatory** | Strong regulatory compliance focus | Less regulatory emphasis |
| **Risk Approach** | Mandatory risk-based approach | Risk-based thinking (less prescriptive) |
| **Documentation** | Mandatory documented procedures | Less prescriptive |
| **Customer** | Patients and regulatory bodies | Customers only |
| **Continual Improvement** | Corrective action focus (less emphasis on continual improvement) | Strong emphasis on continual improvement |

*(Award: 4 marks for structure explanation + 3 marks for comparison table)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b) — Role of Post-Market Surveillance in QRM (7 Marks)

**Definition:**
**Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)** is the **systematic and active collection of information** about a medical device after it has been placed on the market, to identify and take action on any safety, performance, or quality issues.

**Role in QRM:**
1. **Real-world data collection:** PMS captures data on actual device usage, including **off-label use, user errors, long-term failure modes**, which were not detectable during pre-market testing.
2. **Hazard identification:** New hazards may emerge in the post-market phase that were not predicted during design.
3. **Risk profile update:** The risk management file is **continuously updated** with PMS data, ensuring the risk-benefit profile remains favorable.
4. **Trend analysis:** Aggregated complaint data reveals **systemic issues** requiring design or process changes.
5. **Vigilance reporting:** Serious adverse events are reported to **regulatory authorities** (e.g., FDA's MDR system, EU's Vigilance system).

**Integration with the Risk Management File:**

PMS activities are an **input** to the risk management process. The cycle is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre-Market Risk Analysis} &\rightarrow \text{Product Launch} \\
&\rightarrow \text{PMS Data Collection} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Update Hazard List and Risk Estimates} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Updated Risk Management File} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Trigger Design Changes or CAPA (if needed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** Hip implant manufacturers update their RMF when elevated revision rates are observed in PMS data, leading to **design modifications, labeling updates, or product recalls**.

*(Award: 3 marks for PMS definition and role + 4 marks for integration with RMF + example)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ISO 13485 with ISO 14971** — They are NOT the same. ISO 13485 is the QMS standard; ISO 14971 is the risk management process standard. Always clarify the relationship.
> 2. **Listing clauses without explaining risk relevance** — Don't just write "Clause 7.3" — explain *how* risk is applied in that clause.
> 3. **Forgetting the iterative nature of risk management** — Risk management does NOT end at product launch. The post-market loop is critical.
> 4. **Writing generic "quality management" content** — KTU specifically asks about ISO 13485. Mention **specific clauses (e.g., 7.3, 7.4, 8.2.2)** and reference **ISO 14971** explicitly.
> 5. **Ignoring the medical device-specific context** — Always use **medical device examples** (insulin pumps, pacemakers, glucose meters, implants) rather than generic industrial products.
> 6. **Missing the 2016 revision's emphasis** — The shift to a **risk-based approach** is the hallmark of the 2016 version; failing to mention it costs marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **ISO 13485:2016** is the **QMS standard for medical devices**, third edition, published in 2016.
- ✅ **ISO 14971:2019** is the **risk management process standard**, referenced as **normative** in ISO 13485.
- ✅ The two standards are **complementary** — ISO 13485 mandates the *use* of risk management, and ISO 14971 defines *how* to perform it.
- ✅ The **risk-based approach** is a **cross-cutting theme** in ISO 13485:2016 — not confined to a single clause.
- ✅ Key clauses with risk integration: **5.6, 7.3, 7.4, 7.5.6, 8.2.1, 8.2.2, 8.3, 8.5.2**.
- ✅ The **Risk Management File (RMF)** is the regulatory evidence — includes plan, hazard analysis, risk controls, residual risk evaluation, and post-market data.
- ✅ Risk management is **iterative and continuous** — post-market surveillance feeds back into the risk management process.
- ✅ **Risk Control Hierarchy** (priority order): **Inherent safe design → Protective measures → Information for safety**.
- ✅ **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** is one common calculation method: $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$.
- ✅ The standard is **regulatory-driven** (FDA, EU MDR, Health Canada, TGA, etc.) — not just a management framework.
- ✅ For Part A: Always cite **specific clause numbers** when explaining ISO 13485 requirements.
- ✅ For Part B: Always provide a **medical device example** to demonstrate applied understanding.
- ✅ **Benefit-Risk Analysis** is the philosophical foundation — a device is acceptable if its **clinical benefits outweigh residual risks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISO 14971 - Validation and Verification of Medical Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT861 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ISO 14971 - Validation and Verification of Medical Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is ISO 14971?

**ISO 14971** is the globally recognized international standard titled *"Medical devices — Application of risk management to medical devices."* It provides a comprehensive framework and process for medical device manufacturers to identify the hazards associated with their devices, estimate and evaluate the associated risks, control those risks, and monitor the effectiveness of the controls throughout the **entire lifecycle** of the device — from initial concept through design, production, distribution, installation, operation, maintenance, and final decommissioning/disposal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official Designation:** ISO 14971:2019 (Third Edition)
> **Status:** Recognized by major regulatory authorities worldwide, including the **USFDA (USA)**, **European Commission under MDR 2017/745**, **Health Canada**, **TGA (Australia)**, **PMDA (Japan)**, and **CDSCO (India)**.
> **Scope:** Applies to all medical devices, including **in-vitro diagnostic (IVD) medical devices**, **active medical devices**, **implantable medical devices**, and **software as a medical device (SaMD)**.

The current third edition (2019) emphasizes a **total product lifecycle approach** to risk management and shifts the emphasis from purely regulatory compliance to a **proactive, systematic, and continuous risk management process** integrated into the manufacturer's quality management system (typically **ISO 13485**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Evolution:**
> - **First Edition (1998):** Established the basic 5-step risk management process.
> - **Second Edition (2007):** Incorporated updated Annexes, especially Annex C (Risk Management Tools) and Annex D (Risk Management Concepts).
> - **Third Edition (2019):** Refined definitions, introduced stronger emphasis on **benefit-risk analysis**, **production and post-production activities**, and clarified that the standard does **not** mandate the use of specific risk acceptability criteria (leaving this to the manufacturer).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Pre-Flight Checklist" Concept

Think of ISO 14971 as a **"Pre-Flight Checklist for a Medical Device"** — analogous to what pilots and airlines use before takeoff.

| Aviation Safety Analogy | ISO 14971 Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Pilot inspecting the aircraft before takeoff | Manufacturer conducting **Risk Analysis** at design stage |
| Weather radar and air traffic data | **Risk Estimation** (probability and severity assessment) |
| Pilot deciding whether the flight is safe to proceed | **Risk Evaluation** and **Risk Acceptability** decision |
| Deploying flaps, adjusting route to avoid storm | **Risk Control Measures** (design, protective, informational) |
| Re-checking instruments after turbulence | **Verification & Validation of Controls** |
| Monitoring flight throughout the journey | **Production and Post-Production Monitoring** |
| Reporting any anomaly to ground control | **Management Review & Continuous Improvement** |

Just as a pilot doesn't stop being vigilant after takeoff, ISO 14971 mandates that risk management **continues even after the device has been placed on the market**. Real-world experience, complaints, and adverse events feed back into a loop of continuous improvement — exactly as air traffic data feeds back into future flight planning.

## 1.3 Validation vs. Verification — The Twin Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (ISO 9000 / ISO 13485 Basis):**
> - **Verification:** Confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence that specified **design and development outputs** meet the specified **design and development requirements**.
> - **Validation:** Confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence that the **particular requirements for the intended use** of the medical device can be consistently fulfilled.

### Plain English Distinction

- **Verification** answers the question: *"Did we build the device **right**?"* (Did we follow our own design specifications correctly?)
- **Validation** answers the question: *"Did we build the **right** device?"* (Will it actually work for the patient, in the real clinical use environment, for the intended medical purpose?)

> [!NOTE]
> **Medical Device Examples:**
> - **Verification Example:** Testing that an infusion pump delivers fluid at a flow rate of **50 mL/hr ± 2%** as specified in the design input document. (Lab bench test, calibration check.)
> - **Validation Example:** Conducting a clinical simulation to confirm that the infusion pump, when used by a **real nurse in a real ICU**, can safely deliver a critical drug to a critically ill patient without errors over a 24-hour shift. (Real-world use test.)

### Why Both Are Required

In medical device engineering, **verification alone is insufficient**. A device can perfectly meet all design specifications (verification passes) yet still be unsafe or ineffective in actual clinical use (validation fails). For example, a surgical instrument might pass all dimensional checks (verification) but its ergonomic design could cause surgeon hand fatigue leading to operational error (validation failure). ISO 14971 demands that **both activities be performed** as part of the broader risk management process.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (From ISO 14971:2019)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foundational Vocabulary (Kerala University Exam Favourite):**

- **Harm:** Physical injury or damage to the health of people, or damage to property or the environment.
- **Hazard:** Potential source of harm.
- **Hazardous Situation:** Circumstance in which people, property, or the environment are exposed to one or more hazards.
- **Severity:** Measure of the possible consequences of a hazard.
- **Probability:** Measure of the likelihood of occurrence of harm.
- **Risk:** Combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm.
- **Risk Analysis:** Systematic use of available information to identify hazards and to estimate the risk.
- **Risk Evaluation:** Process of comparing the estimated risk against given risk acceptability criteria to determine the acceptability of the risk.
- **Risk Control:** Process in which decisions are made and measures implemented by which risks are reduced to, or maintained within, specified levels.
- **Residual Risk:** Risk remaining after risk control measures have been taken.
- **Risk Management File:** Set of records and other documents that are produced by risk management activities.
- **Benefit-Risk Analysis:** Analysis of the favorable and unfavorable consequences of using a medical device for a defined purpose.
- **State of the Art:** Developed stage of technical capability at a given time as regards products, processes, and services, based on the relevant consolidated findings of science, technology, and experience.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ISO 14971 Risk Management Process — Step-by-Step

The ISO 14971:2019 standard defines a **six-stage systematic process** for risk management. Every step is mandatory for regulatory submission in most jurisdictions.

### Step 1: Risk Management Process Initiation & Planning
The manufacturer must define and document the **scope** of the risk management activities for each specific medical device. This includes identifying the **risk management plan** contents:

- Scope of the device and its intended use
- Risk acceptability criteria
- Methods for risk analysis
- Verification and validation activities
- Roles and responsibilities
- Criteria for risk acceptability
- Post-production surveillance plan

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The **Risk Management Plan** is a **living document** that must be updated whenever new information becomes available, including feedback from production and post-production phases.

### Step 2: Risk Analysis
Three sub-activities:

1. **Intended Use & Reasonably Foreseeable Misuse Identification** — Define the medical purpose, patient population, user profile, and use environment. Also identify *foreseeable misuse* (e.g., using a device off-label or with incompatible accessories).
2. **Hazard Identification** — Systematically list all potential hazards. Common tools include:
   - **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)**
   - **FTA (Fault Tree Analysis)**
   - **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)**
   - **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)**
3. **Risk Estimation** — For each identified hazardous situation, estimate the **probability of occurrence** of the harm and the **severity** of the harm.

### Step 3: Risk Evaluation
Compare the estimated risk against the **predefined risk acceptability criteria** (defined in the Risk Management Plan). The manufacturer decides:
- Is the risk **acceptable**? → Proceed.
- Is the risk **not acceptable**? → Proceed to **Step 4 (Risk Control)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** The standard does **not** prescribe specific numerical thresholds for acceptability. The manufacturer must establish these based on **state of the art**, applicable **standards**, **regulatory requirements**, and **benefit-risk considerations**.

### Step 4: Risk Control
Implement measures to reduce risks to acceptable levels. The **hierarchy of risk control** is critical (must be applied in order):

1. **Inherent Safe Design and Manufacture** (Best) — Eliminate the hazard at the design stage.
2. **Protective Measures in the Medical Device Itself or Manufacturing Process** — Add safety features such as alarms, guards, or fail-safe mechanisms.
3. **Information for Safety and, where appropriate, Training to Users** (Last resort) — Warnings, labels, IFU (Instructions for Use), training programs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** If a lower-level control measure is chosen over a higher-level one, the manufacturer must document the **justification**. For example, if you rely on a warning label instead of a design-level fix, you must justify why a safer design was not feasible.

**Verification Activities for Risk Control Measures:**
- For each implemented control, conduct **verification** (did the control work as designed?) and contribute to **validation** (does the controlled device still meet user needs?).

### Step 5: Overall Residual Risk Evaluation
After all individual risk controls are applied, evaluate the **overall residual risk** of the device. If the overall residual risk is judged **not acceptable**, the manufacturer must:
- Implement further risk control measures, OR
- Modify the device's intended use, OR
- Discontinue development, OR
- Conduct a formal **benefit-risk analysis** demonstrating that the medical benefits outweigh the residual risk.

### Step 6: Risk Management Review, Production & Post-Production Activities

**Risk Management Review:**
Conducted prior to commercial release. Manufacturer must confirm:
- Risk management plan has been implemented
- Overall residual risk is acceptable
- Appropriate methods exist for obtaining production and post-production information

**Production and Post-Production:**
This is the **"live monitoring" phase**. Information is actively gathered from:
- User complaints
- Service reports
- Adverse event reports
- Regulatory authority advisories
- Scientific literature
- Clinical follow-up studies
- Maintenance data

Any new information is **fed back into the risk management process** as inputs for re-analysis, creating a **continuous improvement loop**.

## 2.2 The Two Principal Safety Standards — A Comparative

| Feature | **ISO 14971** | **IEC 60601-1** (Particular Standard) |
|---|---|---|
