# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]

**Q: Define IMDRF and explain its role in harmonizing medical device regulations globally.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **IMDRF (International Medical Device Regulators Forum)** is a voluntary group of medical device regulators established in **2011**, succeeding the **GHTF (Global Harmonization Task Force)**. It includes regulators from major jurisdictions such as the **USA (FDA), EU (European Commission), Japan (PMDA), Canada (Health Canada), Australia (TGA), China (NMPA), and India (CDSCO as affiliate)**.
>
> **Role in harmonization (3 key points):**
> 1. Develops **harmonized guidance documents** (e.g., Essential Principles, SaMD framework, UDI guidance) that serve as templates for national regulations.
> 2. Promotes **convergence** of regulatory systems through a common vocabulary, risk-based classification, and lifecycle approach.
> 3. Enables **mutual recognition and reliance** (e.g., MDSAP) — reducing duplicate testing and audit burdens on manufacturers.
>
> *Examiner's note: A diagram-free, definition-plus-three-points answer is sufficient for 3 marks.*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]

**Q: List any three emerging trends in the use of standards for medical device regulation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. **AI/ML-Specific Standards:** Adoption of ISO/IEC 42001 (AI Management System) and ISO/IEC 23894 (AI Risk Management) for adaptive algorithms, alongside FDA's **Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP)** framework.
> 2. **Unique Device Identification (UDI):** Global implementation across USA, EU, China, India — standardized through ISO/IEC 15459, with **EUDAMED** and the **AccessGUDID** databases linking premarket and post-market data.
> 3. **Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) Lifecycle:** Use of **IMDRF SaMD risk categorization** (IMDRF/SaMD WG/N12) with IEC 62304 and IEC 82304-1 to define premarket evidence requirements by state of healthcare significance.

> *Examiner's note: Naming the trend + citing one standard/document = 1 mark each.*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE]** — Answer **ANY ONE** from the two alternatives below. Each question carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### ✍️ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Discuss the role of consensus standards (ISO, IEC, ASTM) in the FDA's 510(k) and De Novo regulatory pathways. How does the "FDA Recognized Consensus Standards" database function as a regulatory tool?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The U.S. FDA's regulatory framework **strongly relies on consensus standards** as a means of demonstrating substantial equivalence (510(k)) or safety and effectiveness (De Novo).

**1. Foundation — 21st Century Cures Act (Section 3061) (1 mark):**
This 2016 legislation **codified** the use of consensus standards, allowing FDA to accept declarations of conformity to recognized standards in lieu of submitting complete test data, **shortening review timelines**.

**2. Types of Standards Recognized (1 mark):**
FDA recognizes standards from:
- **AAMI, ANSI, ASTM, IEC, ISO, CLSI, EN** (European)
- Each standard is assigned a **Recognition Number** and a **status** (e.g., complete, partial, withdrawn).

**3. Functioning of the Recognized Consensus Standards Database (3 marks):**
- Maintained at: `accessdata.fda.gov/scripts/cdrh/cfdocs/cfStandards`
- Searchable by: standard number, organization, device area, FR citation.
- Each entry contains: **Standard Title, Recognition Number, FR Notice, Extent of Recognition** (full, partial, or specific to a particular test method), and the **Device Class** applicable.
- Manufacturers can submit a **Declaration of Conformity (DoC)** stating which tests were performed per which standard — FDA then relies on this in lieu of full test reports.

**4. Limitations and Trends (2 marks):**
- **Partial recognition** is common — FDA may recognize only specific clauses of a standard.
- **Standards lag behind technology** — for AI/ML, FDA has issued **Cross-cutting Guidance (2024)** in lieu of dedicated standards.
- For SaMD, IEC 62304 is recognized; newer **IEC 81001-5-1 (cybersecurity)** is increasingly cited in submissions.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the Medical Device Single Audit Program (MDSAP). List its participating regulators, the audit model, and how MDSAP demonstrates compliance with **ISO 13485:2016** plus country-specific requirements.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. What is MDSAP? (1 mark):**
MDSAP is a program that allows a **single regulatory audit** of a medical device manufacturer's **Quality Management System (QMS)** to satisfy the requirements of **multiple participating regulators** simultaneously.

**2. Participating Regulators (1 mark):**
- 🇺🇸 **U.S. FDA** (replaces routine FDA inspections for eligible firms)
- 🇨🇦 **Health Canada** (mandatory since 2019 for Class II–IV)
- 🇯🇵 **Japan's PMDA** (MHLW Ordinance)
- 🇧🇷 **Brazil's ANVISA**
- 🇦🇺 **Australia's TGA**
- 🌍 **WHO** (as observer)

**3. The MDSAP Audit Model — "Four-Process Approach" (3 marks):**
The audit is organized around four interrelated QMS processes:
1. **Management** (Cl 4–6 of ISO 13485)
2. **Measurement, Analysis, and Improvement** (Cl 8)
3. **Design and Development** (Cl 7.3)
4. **Production and Service Controls** (Cl 7 + sterilization, etc.)

Each process is audited using **task-based worksheets** that incorporate country-specific regulatory requirements (e.g., 21 CFR Part 820 for FDA, RDC 16/2013 for ANVISA, Medical Devices Regulations SOR/98-282 for Health Canada).

**4. Role of ISO 13485:2016 (1 mark):**
ISO 13485 is the **base standard**; MDSAP audits verify compliance to it AND add **country-specific regulatory audits**. A single MDSAP audit report is shared with all participating regulators.

**5. Outcomes and Trends (1 mark):**
- Health Canada: MDSAP certificate **mandatory** for Class II–IV licenses (since 2019).
- FDA: MDSAP certificate can substitute for a routine inspection.
- **Trend:** EU MDR transition is making EU regulators consider **MDSAP recognition** (discussions ongoing within MDCG).

---

### ✍️ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main] — **ALTERNATIVE**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Discuss the current trends in using standards for **AI/ML-enabled medical devices**. Cover the FDA's Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) approach, ISO/IEC 42001, and the IMDRF AIMD WG/N67 framework.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Background — Why AI/ML Devices Need New Standards (1 mark):**
Traditional medical device standards assume a **"locked" algorithm** validated once at premarket. AI/ML models can **learn and adapt post-deployment**, which violates the "no-change without re-submission" paradigm. New frameworks are required to balance **innovation** with **patient safety**.

**2. FDA's Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) — 2024 Guidance (2 marks):**
The PCCP is a regulatory mechanism allowing manufacturers to pre-specify:
- **Anticipated modifications** to the model (e.g., retraining cadence, data drift corrections)
- **Associated risk control measures** (e.g., performance bounds, monitoring thresholds)
- **Methods for validating** modifications (e.g., silent vs. shadow deployment)
Once approved, modifications within the PCCP scope **do not require a new 510(k)**, enabling rapid iteration. PCCP is the **cornerstone of FDA's 2021 AI/ML Action Plan**.

**3. ISO/IEC 42001:2023 — AI Management System (2 marks):**
- First international **AI management system standard**.
- Aligns structurally with ISO 9001 and ISO 13485.
- Specifies controls for: AI policy, risk management, data quality, transparency, accountability, lifecycle management.
- Provides a **certifiable** framework that manufacturers can integrate with their existing ISO 13485 QMS.

**4. IMDRF AIMD WG/N67 — Good Machine Learning Practice (GMLP) (1 mark):**
- Published by IMDRF's AI/ML Working Group.
- Lists **10 GMLP principles** (e.g., training data relevance, clinical relevance, performance across subgroups, robustness, transparency).
- It is the **IMDRF harmonized baseline** that regulators from multiple jurisdictions are aligning with.

**5. Other Supporting Standards (1 mark):**
- **ISO/IEC TR 24027:2021** — Bias in AI systems
- **ISO/IEC 23894:2023** — AI risk management
- **IEC 62304** — software lifecycle (still applies as the foundation)
- **ISO/IEC TS 4213:2022** — AI performance evaluation methodology

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the **Unique Device Identification (UDI)** system as a current trend. Compare its implementation in the USA (FDA) and the EU (EUDAMED). What is its impact on post-market surveillance?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Definition of UDI (1 mark):**
A **Unique Device Identifier** is a numeric or alphanumeric code that allows **unambiguous identification** of a specific medical device on the market. It consists of:
- **Device Identifier (DI):** Mandatory, fixed — identifies the labeler and specific version/model of the device.
- **Production Identifier (PI):** Conditional, variable — includes lot/batch, serial number, expiration date, manufacture date.

**2. Foundation Standard (1 mark):**
**ISO/IEC 15459** — Information technology — Unique identifiers. Issuing agencies include **GS1, HIBCC, ICCBBA, IFA**.

**3. FDA UDI System (USA) — 2 marks:**
- Established under **21 CFR Part 830** (final rule 2013, phased implementation completed 2022).
- **AccessGUDID** database contains DI records.
- Compliance by class: Class III → Class II → Class I.
- For most Class I and certain Class II devices, only DI is required; others require full UDI (DI + PI) on the label.
- **Direct Marking** required for devices that are intended to be used more than once and re-sterilized between uses.

**4. EU UDI System (EUDAMED) — 2 marks:**
- Mandated by **EU MDR Article 27** and **EU IVDR Article 24**.
- **EUDAMED** has modules for: UDI Device Registration, Notified Body & Certificates, Clinical Investigations, Vigilance, Market Surveillance.
- UDI-DI is linked to the device's **Basic UDI-DI** (grouping of devices sharing core design, manufacturing, and intended purpose).
- **Rollout status (2024):** UDI module is functional; full EUDAMED transition is ongoing (with partial mandatory dates).

**5. Impact on Post-Market Surveillance (1 mark):**
- **Traceability** from manufacturer → distributor → user.
- **Recall efficiency:** Single UDI enables targeted field actions.
- **Trend detection:** PMS data can be aggregated by DI.
- **Clinical linkage:** UDI in electronic health records (EHRs) enables real-world evidence generation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **Common mistakes students make in Module 4 questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing IMDRF and GHTF:** GHTF ended in 2011. Mentioning it as the "current" forum costs marks. Always use **IMDRF** (post-2011).
> 2. **Naming standards without context:** Writing "ISO 13485" is half a mark. You must say *what* it is for (e.g., "QMS for regulatory purposes") and *which clause* matters.
> 3. **Ignoring country-specific nuances:** For 14-mark questions, always contrast **at least two jurisdictions** (typically FDA + EU MDR).
> 4. **Treating SaMD like hardware:** Software standards (IEC 62304, IEC 82304-1) have **different verification** paradigms — be explicit about software lifecycle.
> 5. **Skipping the TPLC:** Trends are *lifecycle* trends, not just premarket. Always mention **post-market** (PMS, PMCF, RWE).
> 6. **Forgetting EU MDR/IVDR transition deadlines:** EU MDR Date of Application is **26 May 2021** (full effect from 2024 for legacy devices). EUDAMED is in **gradual rollout**. Citing wrong dates = -1 mark.
> 7. **Mixing up MDSAP scope:** MDSAP is **not** a CE Mark pathway — it covers QMS audit, not product certification.
> 8. **Ignoring Indian context (CDSCO):** For KTU Kerala, mentioning **India's Medical Devices Rules 2017** adds contextual value.
> 9. **AI/ML answers without PCCP:** For 2024+ questions on AI, mentioning **PCCP** is almost mandatory.
> 10. **Not showing a "Traceability Matrix":** Examiner looks for **Essential Principle → Standard → Evidence** chain. Drawing or describing a matrix in words earns 2+ extra marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **IMDRF** (since 2011) is the global regulatory forum — replaced GHTF.
- ✅ **Essential Principles of Safety and Performance** (IMDRF/GRRP WG/N47) are the modern foundation, mapping to consensus standards.
- ✅ **ISO 13485:2016** is the QMS base for regulators worldwide.
- ✅ **ISO 14971:2019** drives risk management throughout the TPLC.
- ✅ **IEC 60601-1** (electrical safety), **IEC 62304** (software), **IEC 62366-1** (usability), **ISO 10993** (biocompatibility) are the "big four" product-specific standards.
- ✅ **MDSAP** enables single-audit access to USA, Canada, Japan, Brazil, Australia.
- ✅ **EU MDR 2017/745** is the current EU regulation (replaced MDD); EU IVDR 2017/746 covers IVDs.
- ✅ **EU Harmonized Standards** (OJEU list) provide presumption of conformity to EU MDR.
- ✅ **UDI** = DI + PI; **GS1/HIBCC/ICCBBA/IFA** are issuing agencies.
- ✅ **EUDAMED** is the EU medical device database; rollout is gradual.
- ✅ **SaMD** is governed by **IMDRF/SaMD WG/N12**; **IEC 62304** is the software lifecycle standard.
- ✅ **AI/ML devices** need **ISO/IEC 42001 (AIMS)**, **ISO/IEC 23894 (AI risk)**, and **FDA PCCP** for pre-specified changes.
- ✅ **Cybersecurity** in health software → **IEC 81001-5-1** + **IEC 62443**.
- ✅ **PMS standards:** **ISO/TR 20416:2020**, **ISO 13485 Cl 8**, **EU MDR Articles 84–86**.
- ✅ **PMCF** (Post-Market Clinical Follow-up) is mandatory for higher-risk EU devices.
- ✅ **RWE/RWD** are accepted by FDA (2018 framework) and increasingly by EU.
- ✅ **MDR transition:** Legacy devices needed compliance by **26 May 2024** (certificates under MDD expired).
- ✅ **India's Medical Devices Rules 2017** (CDSCO) align with IMDRF, classify into Class A–D (risk-based).
- ✅ **Combination products** in USA → **21 CFR Part 4**; drug-led vs. device-led designation.
- ✅ **Cybersecurity** is now a **Core Essential Principle** in EU MDR Annex I.
- ✅ **TPLC** = Design → V&V → Premarket → Manufacturing → PMS → End-of-Life, with continuous feedback loops.
- ✅ **Single most important concept:** The trend is **harmonization + risk-based + lifecycle-driven + AI-ready** regulation.

> 🎯 **Top Exam Tip:** Always frame your answer using the **"Standard + Scope + Trend"** triplet. Example: *"IEC 62304 (Standard) governs medical device software lifecycle (Scope); the current trend is to extend it with AI/ML overlays and PCCP for adaptive algorithms (Trend)."* This is exactly the structure KTU 2024 examiners reward.
